topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Non-Linear Scattering Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Scattering Losses in Optical Fibers?

In an optical fiber, scattering losses refer to the redirection or dissipation of optical energy out of the guided modes due to microscopic and macroscopic material inhomogeneities, as well as nonlinear interactions between the propagating light and the silica lattice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Linear & Non-Linear Scattering Losses):**
> Scattering losses are mechanisms by which the propagating optical signal loses power because a fraction of the photons is deflected out of the guided mode (linear) or because the high-intensity field excites molecular vibrations of the fiber material, converting some signal energy into new frequency-shifted photons (non-linear).

Formally, scattering is classified into two regimes based on the dependence of the attenuation on input optical power:
- **Linear Scattering Losses:** Independent of signal power; caused by static material imperfections.
- **Non-Linear Scattering Losses:** Power-dependent; caused by interaction of intense light with vibrational modes of silica.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Cars on a Foggy Highway":**
> Imagine driving a high-beam car headlight through a foggy night. The beam appears dimmer ahead because a portion of photons collide with tiny water droplets (Rayleigh/Mie scattering) and bounce sideways — this represents **linear** scattering. If you turn the headlight to maximum intensity, the powerful beam begins to heat the air molecules, which in turn shake and emit their own secondary light in different directions (Raman/Brillouin scattering) — this represents **non-linear** scattering.

In simpler words:
- **Linear Scattering** = "Static fog" — present at any power level.
- **Non-Linear Scattering** = "Heat-induced turbulence" — only kicks in above a threshold intensity.

---

## 1.3 Classification Overview

| Category | Type | Physical Origin | Threshold |
|----------|------|-----------------|-----------|
| Linear | Rayleigh Scattering | Sub-wavelength density fluctuations in SiO₂ | None |
| Linear | Mie Scattering | Macroscopic index/geometric imperfections | None |
| Non-Linear | Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) | Electrostriction → acoustic phonons | ~ 1–10 mW (CW) |
| Non-Linear | Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS) | Molecular vibrational phonons | ~ 500 mW – 1 W (CW) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two fundamental material constants you must memorize for KTU exams are:
> - **Brillouin gain coefficient:** $g_B \approx 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W (in silica)
> - **Raman gain coefficient:** $g_R \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W (in silica)
>
> Note: $g_B$ is approximately **1000× larger** than $g_R$, which is why SBS occurs at a much lower threshold than SRS.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rayleigh Scattering (The Dominant Linear Loss)

### Origin Mechanism
Rayleigh scattering arises from random, **frozen-in** density fluctuations of the silica glass network. These fluctuations occur because the molten SiO₂ structure, when rapidly quenched, never achieves a perfect crystalline order. The fluctuation dimensions are **far smaller than the operating wavelength** ($\lambda$).

The scattering loss exhibits a strong wavelength dependence:

$$\alpha_R \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\lambda^4}$$

This $\frac{1}{\lambda^4}$ dependence is the primary reason why modern long-haul optical communication systems operate near **1550 nm** rather than the historical **850 nm**.

### Rayleigh Attenuation Coefficient (Governing Formula)

$$\boxed{\alpha_R \;=\; \frac{8\pi^3}{3\lambda^4}\,\bigl(n^8\,p^2\bigr)\,\bigl(k_B\,T_f\bigr)\,\beta_c}$$

where the symbols are defined in the table below.

### Physical Interpretation
- The factor $n^8$ reveals that **scattering is highly sensitive to refractive index** — small changes in composition (e.g., doping with GeO₂ or B₂O₃) significantly alter $\alpha_R$.
- $T_f$ is the **fictive temperature** — a measure of the disorder frozen in during fiber drawing. Lower drawing temperatures yield lower Rayleigh loss.

---

## 2.2 Mie Scattering

### Origin Mechanism
Mie scattering is caused by structural imperfections whose size is **comparable to the wavelength** $\lambda$. These include:
- Core–cladding boundary irregularities
- Diameter fluctuations along the fiber length
- Strain birefringence and microbends
- Bubbles, dust, and foreign inclusions

$$\alpha_{Mie} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\lambda^2}$$

This **weaker wavelength dependence** allows engineers to distinguish Mie loss from Rayleigh loss experimentally (slope of the loss-vs-wavelength curve).

> [!NOTE]
> Mie scattering is largely a **manufacturing defect** and is typically reduced to $< 0.1$ dB/km in modern MCVD (Modified Chemical Vapor Deposition) fibers. Rayleigh scattering, however, is a **fundamental material property** and cannot be eliminated.

---

## 2.3 Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) — Non-Linear

### Origin Mechanism
When the optical intensity exceeds a threshold, the electric field exerts an **electrostrictive force** on the silica molecules, generating a coherent **acoustic wave** (acoustic phonon). This acoustic wave acts as a moving Bragg grating, scattering a fraction of the forward-propagating light **backwards** with a Doppler downshift.

- **Direction:** Predominantly **backward** (opposite to signal propagation).
- **Frequency shift:** $\nu_B \approx 11$ GHz for silica at 1550 nm.
- **Bandwidth:** Extremely narrow — $\Delta\nu_B \approx 10$–$30$ MHz.

### SBS Threshold Power

$$\boxed{P_{th}^{B} \;\approx\; \frac{21\,A_{eff}}{g_B\,L_{eff}}}$$

where:
- $A_{eff}$ = effective core area (m²)
- $g_B$ = Brillouin gain coefficient $\approx 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W
- $L_{eff}$ = effective interaction length $\approx \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha}$

For a typical single-mode fiber at 1550 nm with $A_{eff} = 80 \,\mu m^2$ and $L_{eff} = 20$ km:

$$P_{th}^{B} \;\approx\; \frac{21 \times 80 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-11} \times 20 \times 10^3} \;\approx\; 1.68 \text{ mW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often compute SBS threshold with $L$ (actual fiber length) instead of $L_{eff}$. Always use $L_{eff}$ which accounts for fiber attenuation.

---

## 2.4 Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS) — Non-Linear

### Origin Mechanism
SRS arises from the interaction of light with **optical phonons** (molecular vibrations) of the silica lattice. The incident photon (pump) loses energy to the lattice, generating a **Stokes-shifted** photon (lower frequency) plus an optical phonon. Unlike SBS, SRS occurs **both forward and backward** with comparable efficiency.

- **Direction:** **Bidirectional** (forward + backward).
- **Frequency shift:** $\nu_R \approx 13.2$ THz (very wide, $\sim 40$ nm in 1550 nm region).
- **Bandwidth:** $\Delta\nu_R \approx 10$–$15$ THz.

### SRS Threshold Power

$$\boxed{P_{th}^{R} \;\approx\; \frac{16\,A_{eff}}{g_R\,L_{eff}}}$$

For the same fiber with $g_R = 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W:

$$P_{th}^{R} \;\approx\; \frac{16 \times 80 \times 10^{-12}}{6.5 \times 10^{-14} \times 20 \times 10^3} \;\approx\; 0.98 \text{ W} \approx 1 \text{ W}$$

This confirms $P_{th}^{R} \gg P_{th}^{B}$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Rayleigh loss vs wavelength | $\alpha_R = \dfrac{C}{\lambda^4}$ | dB/km | Find $\alpha_R$ at a new wavelength given a reference. |
| 2 | Rayleigh coefficient (physical) | $\alpha_R = \dfrac{8\pi^3}{3\lambda^4}\,n^8 p^2\,k_B T_f\,\beta_c$ | 1/m | Derivation / 14-mark problems. |
| 3 | Mie loss slope | $\alpha_{Mie} \propto \dfrac{1}{\lambda^2}$ | dB/km | Distinguish from Rayleigh. |
| 4 | SBS threshold | $P_{th}^{B} = \dfrac{21\,A_{eff}}{g_B\,L_{eff}}$ | W | Compute CW threshold power. |
| 5 | SRS threshold | $P_{th}^{R} = \dfrac{16\,A_{eff}}{g_R\,L_{eff}}$ | W | Compute CW threshold power. |
| 6 | Effective length | $L_{eff} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha}$ | m | Convert physical length to effective length. |
| 7 | Brillouin frequency shift | $\nu_B = \dfrac{2 n V_a}{\lambda_p}$ | Hz | Derive acoustic velocity. |
| 8 | Raman Stokes shift | $\Delta\nu_R \approx 13.2$ THz (silica) | Hz | Qualitative Q/A. |
| 9 | Brillouin gain | $g_B \approx 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W | m/W | Standard constant. |
| 10 | Raman gain | $g_R \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W | m/W | Standard constant. |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** SBS limits the maximum launch power in **narrow-linewidth, long-haul analog links** (e.g., CATV). SRS, although higher threshold, is exploited constructively in **Raman amplifiers** and is also the dominant inter-channel crosstalk mechanism in **WDM systems**.

---

## 2.6 Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Feature | Rayleigh | Mie | SBS | SRS |
|---------|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Type | Linear | Linear | Non-linear | Non-linear |
| Loss $\propto$ | $1/\lambda^4$ | $1/\lambda^2$ | Power-dependent | Power-dependent |
| Threshold | None | None | $\sim 1$–$10$ mW | $\sim 0.5$–$1$ W |
| Direction | All | All | Backward | Bi-directional |
| Phonon | None | None | Acoustic | Optical |
| Bandwidth | Broadband | Broadband | $\sim 10$ MHz | $\sim 10$ THz |
| Frequency shift | Elastic (no shift) | Elastic | $\sim 11$ GHz | $\sim 13$ THz |
| Control | Material design | Manufacturing | Phase modulation, $A_{eff}$ | WDM channel spacing |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rayleigh Scattering Coefficient

We start with the classical theory of scattering from a small dielectric sphere (Rayleigh regime: particle size $\ll \lambda$). For an induced dipole moment $p = \alpha_{pol} E$, the radiated power per solid angle is:

$$\frac{dP_{rad}}{d\Omega} = \frac{\pi^2}{\epsilon_0 \lambda^4} \vert p \vert^2 \sin^2\theta$$

**Step 1 — Relate dipole moment to refractive index fluctuation:**
The polarizability of a small volume $V$ is $\alpha_{pol} = \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) V$, where $\epsilon_r = n^2$. The fluctuation in $\epsilon_r$ arises from **density fluctuations** $\Delta\rho$:

$$\Delta\epsilon_r = \frac{\partial\epsilon_r}{\partial\rho}\,\Delta\rho = \frac{2n}{\rho_0}\,\frac{dn}{d\rho}\,(n^2-1)\,\Delta\rho$$

Equivalently, in terms of the photoelastic coefficient $p$:

$$\Delta\epsilon_r \;\approx\; 2 n^2 p \,\frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho_0}$$

**Step 2 — Express the scattered power in terms of $\Delta\epsilon_r$:**
Substituting $p = \tfrac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 \Delta\epsilon_r V$ into the dipole radiation equation and integrating over all solid angles (assuming unpolarized incident light):

$$P_{sca} = \frac{\pi^2 V^2}{2 \epsilon_0 \lambda^4} \langle \vert \Delta\epsilon_r \vert^2 \rangle$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\langle \vert \Delta\rho \vert^2 \rangle$ to compressibility & temperature:**
Density fluctuations obey thermodynamics:

$$\langle \vert \Delta\rho \vert^2 \rangle = \frac{\rho_0^2 \, k_B T_f}{V \beta_c}$$

**Step 4 — Combine and simplify:**

$$P_{sca} = \frac{\pi^2 V}{2 \epsilon_0 \lambda^4} \cdot 4 n^4 p^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_0^2 k_B T_f}{V \beta_c} \cdot n^4$$

The fractional scattered power (scattering coefficient times length) is:

$$\alpha_R = \frac{P_{sca}}{P_{inc}\,L} = \frac{8\pi^3 n^8 p^2 k_B T_f}{3 \lambda^4 \beta_c}$$

> This is the **fundamental Rayleigh scattering formula** that KTU examiners love to derive step by step in 14-mark questions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Wavelength Scaling of Rayleigh Loss

A standard SMF-28 single-mode fiber has a measured Rayleigh loss of $\alpha_R(1310) = 0.32$ dB/km. Find the loss at **1550 nm** using the $\lambda^{-4}$ law.

**Given:** $\alpha_R(1310) = 0.32$ dB/km, $\lambda_1 = 1310$ nm, $\lambda_2 = 1550$ nm.

**Formula:**

$$\frac{\alpha_R(\lambda_2)}{\alpha_R(\lambda_1)} = \left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}\right)^4$$

**Computation:**

$$\left(\frac{1310}{1550}\right)^4 = \left(\frac{1310}{1550}\right)^4 = (0.8452)^4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
0.8452^2 &= 0.7144 \\
0.8452^4 &= 0.7144^2 = 0.5104
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:**

$$\alpha_R(1550) = 0.32 \times 0.5104 = 0.163 \text{ dB/km} \approx 0.17 \text{ dB/km}$$

> This matches the standard Corning SMF-28 datasheet value, validating the $\lambda^{-4}$ scaling.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: SBS Threshold Power

A single-mode fiber link has the following parameters:
- Effective area $A_{eff} = 80 \,\mu\text{m}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-12}$ m²
- Fiber length $L = 50$ km
- Linear attenuation $\alpha = 0.2$ dB/km $= 0.0461$ /km $= 4.61 \times 10^{-5}$ /m
- $g_B = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W

**Step 1 — Compute effective length:**

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha}$$

Convert $\alpha$ to Np/m: $\alpha_{Np} = 0.2 / 4.343 = 0.0461$ /km $= 4.61 \times 10^{-5}$ /m.

$$\alpha L = 4.61 \times 10^{-5} \times 50 \times 10^3 = 2.305$$

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1 - e^{-2.305}}{4.61 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1 - 0.0996}{4.61 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.9004}{4.61 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 19{,}530 \text{ m} \approx 19.5 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2 — Compute SBS threshold:**

$$P_{th}^{B} = \frac{21 \times 80 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-11} \times 19.53 \times 10^3}$$

$$P_{th}^{B} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-9}}{9.765 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 1.72 \text{ mW}$$

**Conclusion:** Launch power must be kept **below ~1.7 mW** to avoid significant SBS in this link.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: SRS Threshold Power

Using the **same fiber** as Section 3.3, with $g_R = 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W:

$$P_{th}^{R} = \frac{16 \times 80 \times 10^{-12}}{6.5 \times 10^{-14} \times 19.53 \times 10^3}$$

$$P_{th}^{R} = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-9}}{1.269 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.008 \text{ W} \approx 1 \text{ W}$$

**Comparison:** $\dfrac{P_{th}^{R}}{P_{th}^{B}} = \dfrac{1.008}{0.00172} \approx 586 \times$

> This **numerical ratio ~ 500–1000** is a key KTU takeaway: SBS threshold is roughly **three orders of magnitude lower** than SRS threshold.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cause → Effect → Mitigation

| Phenomenon | Cause | Effect on Signal | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|-------|------------------|---------------------|
| Rayleigh | Frozen-in density fluctuations | Power loss $\propto 1/\lambda^4$ | Use longer wavelengths (1550 nm); novel low-loss glasses |
| Mie | Macroscopic defects | Excess loss & modal noise | Improve manufacturing (MCVD); tight geometric tolerance |
| SBS | Electrostrictive acoustic phonons | Backward Stokes, signal depletion, noise | Phase modulation, larger $A_{eff}$, shorter $L$, temperature gradient |
| SRS | Vibrational optical phonons | Forward/backward Stokes, inter-channel crosstalk in WDM | Limit per-channel power, increase WDM channel spacing |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Rayleigh scattering loss? State its wavelength dependence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rayleigh scattering is a **linear** loss mechanism in optical fibers caused by **microscopic density fluctuations** in the silica glass, which are frozen in during the fiber drawing process. These fluctuations have dimensions much smaller than the operating wavelength and act as scattering centers for the propagating light.

The attenuation coefficient has the wavelength dependence:

$$\alpha_R \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\lambda^4}$$

This means scattering loss is **very high at short wavelengths** (e.g., 850 nm) and decreases rapidly at longer wavelengths. This dependence is the fundamental reason why modern long-haul systems operate near **1550 nm**, where $\alpha_R$ is minimized.

*(Keyword: linear, density fluctuation, $\lambda^{-4}$ — 1 mark each)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) and Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SBS | SRS |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Phonon type | Acoustic | Optical (vibrational) |
| Direction | Backward dominant | Bi-directional |
| Frequency shift | $\sim 11$ GHz | $\sim 13.2$ THz |
| Bandwidth | $\sim 10$–$30$ MHz (narrow) | $\sim 10$–$15$ THz (broad) |
| Threshold | $\sim 1$–$10$ mW | $\sim 0.5$–$1$ W |
| Gain coefficient | $\sim 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W | $\sim 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W |

*(Comparison table-style answer — full marks for listing 3 correct differences.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for Rayleigh scattering loss coefficient in an optical fiber. State clearly the physical meaning of each term. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The Rayleigh scattering coefficient is derived from classical dipole radiation theory.

**Step 1 — Dipole radiation from induced dipole:**
When an EM wave $E$ impinges on a small dielectric volume $V$, it induces a dipole moment $p = \alpha_{pol} E$ where $\alpha_{pol} = \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1)V$ and $\epsilon_r = n^2$.

**Step 2 — Density fluctuation source:**
For silica, the dominant fluctuation is in density $\rho$:

$$\Delta\epsilon_r = 2n^2 p \cdot \frac{\Delta\rho}{\rho_0}$$

**Step 3 — Total scattered power (integrate over $4\pi$ steradians):**

$$P_{sca} = \frac{\pi^2 V}{2\epsilon_0 \lambda^4}\langle \vert \Delta\epsilon_r \vert^2 \rangle$$

**Step 4 — Thermodynamic density variance:**

$$\langle \vert \Delta\rho \vert^2 \rangle = \frac{\rho_0^2\,k_B T_f}{V\,\beta_c}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to get attenuation coefficient:**

$$\boxed{\alpha_R = \frac{8\pi^3}{3\lambda^4}\,n^8\,p^2\,k_B\,T_f\,\beta_c}$$

**Meaning of each term:**
- $n$ = refractive index of silica
- $p$ = photoelastic coefficient (Pockels coefficient)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant
- $T_f$ = fictive temperature
- $\beta_c$ = isothermal compressibility

---

**(b) A single-mode silica fiber has a Rayleigh loss of 0.35 dB/km at 1310 nm. Calculate (i) the loss at 850 nm, and (ii) the loss at 1550 nm. State one important conclusion from the results. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

The Rayleigh loss follows $\alpha_R \propto 1/\lambda^4$, therefore:

$$\frac{\alpha_R(\lambda)}{\alpha_R(\lambda_0)} = \left(\frac{\lambda_0}{\lambda}\right)^4$$

**Part (i) — Loss at 850 nm:**

$$\alpha_R(850) = 0.35 \times \left(\frac{1310}{850}\right)^4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1310}{850} &= 1.5412 \\
1.5412^2 &= 2.3753 \\
1.5412^4 &= 5.6421
\end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha_R(850) = 0.35 \times 5.6421 = 1.975 \text{ dB/km}$$

**Part (ii) — Loss at 1550 nm:**

$$\alpha_R(1550) = 0.35 \times \left(\frac{1310}{1550}\right)^4$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1310}{1550} &= 0.8452 \\
0.8452^4 &= 0.5104
\end{aligned}$$

$$\alpha_R(1550) = 0.35 \times 0.5104 = 0.179 \text{ dB/km}$$

**Conclusion:** Rayleigh scattering loss decreases by approximately **an order of magnitude** as the wavelength increases from 850 nm to 1550 nm. This is why optical communication systems prefer the **1550 nm** window (C-band) for long-haul, low-loss transmission.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) Explain Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS) and Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS) in optical fibers. Compare their threshold powers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Stimulated Brillouin Scattering (SBS):**
SBS arises from the **electrostrictive interaction** between the optical field and the silica medium. The strong electric field of the light wave generates density variations in the medium, producing a **coherent acoustic phonon**. This acoustic wave behaves as a moving Bragg grating and scatters light predominantly **backward** with a Doppler frequency shift $\nu_B \approx 11$ GHz (at 1550 nm). The interaction bandwidth is narrow, only about **10–30 MHz**.

**Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS):**
SRS arises from the interaction of light with **optical phonons** (molecular vibrations) of the silica lattice. The incident photon (pump) loses energy to the lattice, creating a **Stokes photon** of lower frequency. SRS occurs **in both forward and backward directions** with comparable efficiency and has a much larger bandwidth of about **10–15 THz**.

**Threshold Power Comparison:**

$$\boxed{P_{th}^{B} = \frac{21\,A_{eff}}{g_B\,L_{eff}} \quad ; \quad P_{th}^{R} = \frac{16\,A_{eff}}{g_R\,L_{eff}}}$$

For silica: $g_B = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W, $g_R = 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W.

Therefore:

$$\frac{P_{th}^{B}}{P_{th}^{R}} = \frac{16\,g_R}{21\,g_B} \approx \frac{16 \times 6.5 \times 10^{-14}}{21 \times 5 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 9.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

This shows $P_{th}^{R} \approx 1000 \times P_{th}^{B}$. So **SBS is the dominant non-linear scattering concern** in single-channel long-haul systems.

---

**(b) A single-mode fiber link of length 40 km has an effective area of 70 μm² and a linear attenuation of 0.22 dB/km. Calculate the SBS threshold power at 1550 nm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $L = 40$ km, $A_{eff} = 70 \times 10^{-12}$ m², $\alpha = 0.22$ dB/km, $g_B = 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W.

**Step 1 — Convert $\alpha$ to Np/m:**

$$\alpha_{Np} = \frac{0.22}{4.343} = 0.05066 \text{ /km} = 5.066 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective length:**

$$\alpha L = 5.066 \times 10^{-5} \times 40 \times 10^3 = 2.0264$$

$$L_{eff} = \frac{1 - e^{-2.0264}}{5.066 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{1 - 0.1318}{5.066 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.8682}{5.066 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 17{,}138 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply SBS threshold formula:**

$$P_{th}^{B} = \frac{21 \times 70 \times 10^{-12}}{5 \times 10^{-11} \times 17{,}138}$$

$$P_{th}^{B} = \frac{1.47 \times 10^{-9}}{8.569 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.715 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 1.72 \text{ mW}$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum continuous-wave (CW) launch power must be kept **below 1.72 mW** to avoid significant SBS-induced signal degradation.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Scattering Loss Questions:**
> 1. **Using $L$ instead of $L_{eff}$:** Many students substitute the actual fiber length directly into the threshold formula. This is wrong — for long fibers the effective length saturates near $1/\alpha$. Always compute $L_{eff} = (1 - e^{-\alpha L})/\alpha$ first.
> 2. **Mixing dB and Np units:** Threshold formulas use attenuation in **Np/m** (natural units), not dB/km. Convert: $\alpha_{Np} = \alpha_{dB} / 4.343$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\lambda^{-4}$ scaling constant:** You cannot use $\alpha_R$ values from one wavelength to predict values at another without first establishing the constant $C$ from the reference.
> 4. **Confusing SBS with SRS directionality:** SBS is **backward**, SRS is **bidirectional** — examiners test this specific distinction.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always write the units (dB/km, mW, W) explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting physical meaning in derivations:** A 7-mark derivation of $\alpha_R$ without the explanation of each term loses at least 2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Rayleigh scattering** is the **dominant intrinsic loss** in silica fibers; loss $\propto 1/\lambda^4$.
- ⭐ **Mie scattering** arises from **manufacturing defects**; loss $\propto 1/\lambda^2$.
- ⭐ **Linear scattering losses are power-independent**; **non-linear scattering losses are power-dependent** (with thresholds).
- ⭐ **SBS** uses **acoustic phonons**, is **backward**, and has threshold of **~1–10 mW**.
- ⭐ **SRS** uses **optical phonons**, is **bidirectional**, and has threshold of **~1 W**.
- ⭐ Brillouin gain $g_B \approx 5 \times 10^{-11}$ m/W is **~1000× larger** than Raman gain $g_R \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ m/W.
- ⭐ SBS threshold formula: $P_{th}^{B} = \dfrac{21\,A_{eff}}{g_B\,L_{eff}}$.
- ⭐ SRS threshold formula: $P_{th}^{R} = \dfrac{16\,A_{eff}}{g_R\,L_{eff}}$.
- ⭐ Effective length: $L_{eff} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha}$.
- ⭐ Ray design wavelength (silica) $\lambda_d \approx 1300$ nm — where Rayleigh loss = UV absorption tail.
- ⭐ Modern systems operate near **1550 nm** to minimize Rayleigh loss and utilize EDFA gain.
- ⭐ Always convert **dB/km → Np/m** before applying threshold formulas (divide by 4.343).
- ⭐ WDM systems are limited by **SRS crosstalk**, not SRS loss — inter-channel power transfer is the key concern.
- ⭐ Rayleigh loss cannot be eliminated; it is a fundamental material property.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bend Losses; Dispersion - Intermodal Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bend Losses; Dispersion - Intermodal Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Bend Losses — Formal Definition

**Bend losses** are the optical power losses that occur in an optical fiber when the fiber is subjected to any form of physical curvature. When light travels through a curved fiber section, the guided modes are perturbed and a portion of the optical power is no longer totally internally reflected — it **radiates (leaks) out** of the core into the cladding. Bend loss is wavelength-dependent, mode-dependent, and a critical parameter in fiber link design (especially in access networks, FTTH bends, and tight cable routing).

Bend losses are classified into two fundamental types per the **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus (OEECT721 — Module 1)**:

| Type | Cause | Typical Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Macrobending Loss** | Large-scale bends (R ≫ fiber diameter) — e.g., cable loops, splicing coils | Radius R in mm to cm |
| **Microbending Loss** | Microscopic (µm-scale) deformations due to cabling stress, coating irregularities, lateral pressure | Distortion period in µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition:** *Bend loss is the excess attenuation observed when an optical fiber is bent. Macrobends occur for bend radii comparable to the fiber diameter; microbends are small periodic distortions of the fiber axis that couple power from guided modes to radiation (cladding/leaky) modes.*

### 1.2 Intermodal Dispersion — Formal Definition

**Intermodal dispersion** (also called *modal dispersion*) is the **pulse broadening** that arises in **multimode fibers** because different guided modes travel along paths of different effective lengths. The lowest-order (axial) mode travels the shortest geometric path, while higher-order modes zigzag through the core at steeper angles, taking longer to traverse the same axial distance. This **path-length difference** translates into a temporal spread at the receiver, limiting the **bit rate × distance** product.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition:** *Intermodal dispersion is the temporal spreading of an optical pulse caused by the difference in propagation time between various guided modes in a multimode fiber. It is the dominant dispersion mechanism in step-index multimode (SI-MM) fibers and is significantly reduced (but not eliminated) in graded-index multimode (GI-MM) fibers.*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Marathon Runners on a Curved Track":**
>
> Imagine 50 runners starting simultaneously on a circular athletics track. Runners in the **innermost lane** (axial mode) cover the shortest path. Runners in the **outer lanes** (higher-order modes) must run a longer arc. Even though all runners have the same speed, they **finish at different times** — this *time spread* is **intermodal dispersion**.
>
> Now imagine the track is **bent into a tight curve** at one section. Some runners in the outer lanes, being pushed outward by centrifugal tendency, **stumble off the track** into the grass — these are the **radiated modes lost to bend loss**. The tighter the curve, the more runners fall out. This is exactly what happens in an optical fiber: the curvature distorts the effective refractive index profile, and modes near cutoff radiate away.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- Free-space wavelength: $\lambda$ in **µm** (typical: 0.85, 1.31, 1.55 µm)
- Fiber core radius: $a$ in **µm** (typical: 25, 50, 62.5 µm for MM; 4–10 µm for SM)
- Cladding radius: $b$ in **µm** (typical: 62.5, 125 µm)
- Relative refractive index difference: $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ (typical: 0.002 to 0.02 for MM)
- Numerical Aperture: $NA = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ (typical: 0.1 to 0.3 for MM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Bend loss increases **exponentially** as the bend radius decreases below a critical value $R_c$. It is negligible for $R > 10$ cm in standard SMF-28 fibers but can exceed **100 dB/m** for $R < 5$ mm in bend-insensitive single-mode fibers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Macrobending Loss — Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

1. **Bending Distorts the Refractive Index Profile:** When a straight fiber of radius $a$ is bent with radius of curvature $R$ (with $R \gg a$), the local effective index seen by a mode becomes position-dependent.
2. **Outer Edge Becomes Critical:** On the **outer side of the bend**, the guided mode experiences an effectively lower potential barrier. The condition for total internal reflection breaks down.
3. **Radiation of Higher-Order Modes First:** Lower-order (more tightly bound) modes survive small bends; higher-order modes near cutoff are the **first to leak out**.
4. **Field Tails Extend Beyond Core:** The evanescent field in the cladding can no longer be confined because the cladding boundary on the outer side moves closer to the mode field center.
5. **Exponential Sensitivity to Radius:** The loss coefficient follows approximately an **$\exp(-R)$** law — a tiny change in $R$ near the critical value can cause orders-of-magnitude change in loss.

The standard expression derived by **Marcatili & Miller (1969)** and **Gloge (1972)** for the macrobending loss coefficient in a step-index fiber is:

$$\alpha_M = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{a W^3 R}} \cdot \frac{U^2}{V^2 K_\nu^2(Wa)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{2 W^3 R}{3 a \beta^2}\right)$$

where:
- $W$ = cladding decay parameter (transverse wavenumber in cladding)
- $U$ = core transverse parameter
- $V$ = normalized frequency $V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$
- $K_\nu$ = modified Bessel function of the second kind (order $\nu$)
- $\beta$ = axial propagation constant
- $R$ = bend radius

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Simplified Form:** In most practical design, the bend loss is approximated as $\alpha_M = A_1 \exp(-B_1 R)$ where $A_1, B_1$ are constants determined by fiber parameters ($a$, $NA$, $\lambda$).

### 2.2 Microbending Loss — Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

1. **External Mechanical Pressure:** During cabling, the fiber is pressed against irregular surfaces (other fibers, rough coatings, thermal contraction mismatches).
2. **Small Periodic Axis Distortions:** The fiber axis develops small random or periodic displacements with amplitude $\sigma$ and characteristic spatial frequency $\Omega$.
3. **Mode Coupling to Radiation Modes:** These distortions act as a **long-period grating**, coupling power from guided modes to **cladding (radiation) modes**.
4. **Power Loss Without Attenuation in the Core:** The power leaks into the cladding and is absorbed by the coating/jacket, appearing as additional attenuation.

The **Gloge microbend loss formula** (1972) gives the additional attenuation:

$$\alpha_{micro} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{n_1^2 k^4 a^6}{b^2 \Delta^3} \cdot \sigma^2 \Omega^2$$

where:
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the free-space wavenumber
- $\sigma$ = RMS amplitude of microbends
- $\Omega$ = RMS spatial frequency of microbends

### 2.3 Intermodal Dispersion — Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

1. **Multiple Guided Modes Exist:** A multimode fiber supports hundreds of modes, each with a different propagation constant $\beta_{lm}$.
2. **Different Path Lengths:** The axial mode ($l = 1, m = 1$) travels a straight path of length $L$. Higher-order modes zigzag at angle $\theta_l$ to the axis.
3. **Group Delay Per Mode:** The time taken by mode with angle $\theta$ to traverse length $L$ is $t(\theta) = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c \cos\theta}$.
4. **Maximum Delay Difference:** Between the axial mode ($\theta = 0$) and the highest-angle guided mode ($\theta = \theta_{max}$), the time difference is the **pulse broadening** $\Delta\tau_{modal}$.
5. **Limit on Bit-Rate-Distance Product:** This broadening limits the **bandwidth-distance product** $B \cdot L \leq 1/(2 \Delta\tau)$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Fiber |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Macrobend loss coefficient (full) | $\alpha_M = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{aW^3R}} \cdot \dfrac{U^2}{V^2 K_\nu^2(Wa)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2W^3R}{3a\beta^2}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Step-index, SM/MM |
| 2 | Macrobend loss (engineering) | $\alpha_M = A_1 \exp(-B_1 R)$ | dB/m | All fibers |
| 3 | Microbend loss | $\alpha_{micro} = \dfrac{1}{8} \cdot \dfrac{n_1^2 k^4 a^6}{b^2 \Delta^3} \cdot \sigma^2 \Omega^2$ | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Step-index MM |
| 4 | Relative index difference | $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ | dimensionless | All |
| 5 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless | Step-index |
| 6 | Critical bend radius (approx.) | $R_c \approx \dfrac{3 a \beta^2}{2 W^3}$ | m | Step-index |
| 7 | Intermodal dispersion (SI) | $\Delta\tau_{modal} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\dfrac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$ | s | Step-index MM |
| 8 | Intermodal dispersion (SI, NA form) | $\Delta\tau_{modal} = \dfrac{(NA)^2 L}{2 n_1 c}$ | s | Step-index MM |
| 9 | Intermodal dispersion (GI optimal) | $\Delta\tau_{GI} = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta^2}{8c}$ | s | Graded-index MM |
| 10 | RMS pulse width from mode-coupling | $\sigma_{rms} = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c\sqrt{12}}$ | s | Step-index MM (coupled) |
| 11 | Bandwidth-distance product (SI) | $B \cdot L \leq \dfrac{c}{n_1 \Delta}$ | Hz·m | Step-index MM |
| 12 | Bandwidth-distance product (GI) | $B \cdot L \leq \dfrac{8 c}{n_1 \Delta^2}$ | Hz·m | Graded-index MM |
| 13 | V-number | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$ | dimensionless | All |
| 14 | Max mode angle (acceptance half-angle) | $\sin\theta_{max} = NA$ | rad | Step-index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Intermodal dispersion in step-index MM fiber is **~1000× worse** than in optimally graded-index MM fiber because $\Delta^2 \ll \Delta$. This is precisely why **GI-MMF (50/125 µm, OM2/OM3/OM4)** replaced SI-MMF in LAN backbones (FDDI, 100BASE-FX, 1 Gbit Ethernet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Intermodal Dispersion in Step-Index Multimode Fiber

**Goal:** Derive the pulse broadening $\Delta\tau$ between the axial mode and the highest-angle guided mode in a step-index fiber of length $L$.

**Step 1 — Define the path of an arbitrary guided mode.**

Consider a meridional ray propagating at angle $\theta$ to the fiber axis. The axial distance covered per internal reflection is $d_{axial} = d \cos\theta$, where $d$ is the path length of one zigzag segment. For a fiber of length $L$, the number of zigzag segments is $N = L / (d\cos\theta)$, and the **total physical path length** traveled is:

$$P(\theta) = N \cdot d = \frac{L}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the propagation time per mode.**

The light travels with speed $v = c/n_1$ inside the core. Hence the time taken by the mode at angle $\theta$ to traverse length $L$ is:

$$t(\theta) = \frac{P(\theta)}{v} = \frac{L}{(c/n_1)\cos\theta} = \frac{n_1 L}{c \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Identify minimum and maximum delay.**

- **Minimum delay** (lowest-order axial mode): $\theta = 0 \Rightarrow t_{min} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}$
- **Maximum delay** (highest-angle guided mode): $\theta = \theta_{max}$ where $\sin\theta_{max} = NA$ (from Snell's law at the core-cladding interface, $n_1 \sin\theta_{max} = n_2 \sin 90° \Rightarrow \sin\theta_{max} = n_2/n_1 = \sqrt{2\Delta}$ for small $\Delta$).

At the maximum, $\cos\theta_{max} = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_{max}} = \sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the difference (pulse broadening).**

$$\Delta\tau_{modal} = t_{max} - t_{min} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\frac{1}{\cos\theta_{max}} - 1\right)$$

With $\cos\theta_{max} = n_2/n_1$ (using $n_1 \sin\theta_{max} = n_2$):

$$\frac{1}{\cos\theta_{max}} = \frac{n_1}{n_2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$\Delta\tau_{modal} = \frac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\frac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \frac{n_1 L}{c} \cdot \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_2}$$

**Step 6 — Express in terms of $\Delta$.**

Since $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$, we have $n_1 - n_2 = n_1 \Delta$. For small $\Delta$, $n_2 \approx n_1$ (denominator approximation):

$$\Delta\tau_{modal} \approx \frac{n_1 L}{c} \cdot \frac{n_1 \Delta}{n_1} = \frac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for step-index intermodal dispersion. $\blacksquare$

**Step 7 — Alternative form in terms of NA.**

Using $NA = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta} \Rightarrow \Delta = NA^2/(2n_1^2)$:

$$\Delta\tau_{modal} = \frac{n_1 L}{c} \cdot \frac{NA^2}{2n_1^2} = \frac{NA^2 L}{2 n_1 c} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Intermodal Dispersion in Graded-Index Fiber (Optimal Parabolic Profile)

**Goal:** Show that the optimal refractive index profile $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta(r/a)^2}$ reduces the dispersion to $\Delta\tau = n_1 L \Delta^2/(8c)$.

**Step 1 — Refractive index profile.**

$$n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta \left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^\alpha}$$

For the optimal case $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic), the mode delay difference reduces drastically.

**Step 2 — Group delay in a GRIN fiber.**

For an $\alpha$-profile fiber, the time delay for a mode with normalized radial constant is:

$$\tau \approx \frac{n_1 L}{c}\left[1 + \frac{\Delta^2}{2}\left(\frac{\alpha - 2}{\alpha + 2}\right)m + \text{const}\right]$$

**Step 3 — The optimal $\alpha$ value.**

Setting $\alpha = 2$ makes the **first-order dispersion term vanish**, leaving only the second-order term:

$$\Delta\tau_{GI} = \frac{n_1 L \Delta^2}{8 c} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is a **$\Delta^2$** dependence — for $\Delta = 0.01$, this is **100× smaller** than the step-index case.

### 3.3 Derivation: Macrobend Loss — Effective Index Approach

**Goal:** Show the exponential dependence of $\alpha_M$ on $R$.

**Step 1 — Refractive index transformation under bending.**

When a straight fiber is bent with radius $R$, the equivalent straight fiber has an effective transverse refractive index distribution. In polar coordinates $(r, \phi)$ centered on the bend axis:

$$n_{eff}(r, \phi) = n(r) \left(1 + \frac{r \cos\phi}{R}\right)$$

where $n(r) = n_1$ for $r < a$ and $n(r) = n_2$ for $r > a$.

**Step 2 — Confinement condition on the outer side.**

On the **outer edge** ($\phi = 0$), the effective index is **enhanced** by the factor $(1 + a/R)$. On the **inner edge** ($\phi = \pi$), it is reduced by $(1 - a/R)$. The local NA on the outer side becomes effectively:

$$NA_{local}(r) \approx NA \cdot \sqrt{1 - \frac{2r}{R}}$$

**Step 3 — Evanescent field attenuation.**

The mode field extends into the cladding as $\exp(-Wr)$. Beyond a critical radius $r_{crit}$ (where $NA_{local} \to 0$), the mode is no longer bound. The radiative decay length in the cladding gives the **exponential** factor in the loss coefficient.

**Step 4 — Final form (Marcatili-Miller).**

Combining the geometric-optic and waveguide analysis:

$$\alpha_M \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{R}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{2 W^3 R}{3 a \beta^2}\right)$$

For $R$ near the critical radius, the exponential dominates and:

$$\alpha_M(R) \approx A_1 \exp(-B_1 R) \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU Board Standard

> **Problem:** A step-index multimode fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $L = 1$ km. Compute the intermodal pulse broadening and the bandwidth-distance product.

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $L = 1$ km $= 10^3$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta$:**

$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.48 - 1.46}{1.48} = \frac{0.02}{1.48} = 0.01351$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{n_1 L \Delta}{c} = \frac{1.48 \times 10^3 \times 0.01351}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{20.00}{3 \times 10^8} = 6.667 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 66.67 \text{ ns/km}$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth-distance product:**

$$B \cdot L \approx \frac{1}{2 \Delta\tau} = \frac{1}{2 \times 66.67 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 7.5 \text{ MHz·km}$$

This matches the textbook result for standard 50/125 µm SI-MMF at 850 nm. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When numerical values are given, examiners expect you to **substitute and compute** — partial credit is awarded for correct formula application even if arithmetic slips. Always show the formula, then substitute, then compute, then **box the answer with units**.

### 3.5 Comparison Matrix: SI-MMF vs GI-MMF vs SMF

| Property | SI-MMF | GI-MMF | SMF |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core diameter | 50–100 µm | 50–62.5 µm | 4–10 µm |
| Profile | Step | Parabolic ($\alpha = 2$) | Step |
| Modes | Hundreds | Hundreds | Single (HE₁₁) |
| Intermodal dispersion | **Dominant** ($\sim$50 ns/km) | **Small** ($\sim$0.5 ns/km) | **None** (fundamentally absent) |
| Dispersion formula | $\Delta\tau = n_1 L \Delta / c$ | $\Delta\tau = n_1 L \Delta^2 / (8c)$ | Only chromatic |
| Typical B·L | 10–50 MHz·km | 500–2000 MHz·km | > 500 GHz·km |
| Bend sensitivity | Low | Low | High (without BI-SMF) |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Power Flow Through a Bent Fiber

    A -- SMF --> D[No Intermodal Dispersion Only Chromatic]
