> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Diagrams:** The mermaid diagrams above are block-level functional architecture flow mappings. They trace the **causal chain** of power flow and pulse evolution under bend and dispersion effects, providing a clean visual roadmap for the KTU board answer scripts. Physical fiber geometry diagrams (showing the bent axis, mode field, etc.) are best redrawn by hand on the answer sheet as the examiner rewards labelled sketches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)]**

**Q1. What is bend loss in an optical fiber? Distinguish between macrobend and microbend losses.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Bend loss** is the excess optical attenuation that occurs when an optical fiber is subjected to curvature, causing guided modes to radiate out of the core into the cladding.
>
> **Macrobend loss** arises from large-scale bends (bend radius $R$ comparable to the fiber diameter, typically mm–cm scale). It follows an **$\exp(-R)$** dependence and is negligible for $R > 10$ cm in standard SMF.
>
> **Microbend loss** arises from microscopic (µm-scale) axis distortions caused by mechanical stress during cabling, coating irregularities, or thermal effects. It follows an $\sigma^2 \Omega^2$ dependence (where $\sigma$ is RMS amplitude and $\Omega$ is RMS spatial frequency) and is mitigated using compliant coatings and proper cable design.
>
> *Allocation: 1 mark for bend loss definition, 1 mark for macrobend, 1 mark for microbend.*

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper Style)]**

**Q2. Define intermodal dispersion. Why is it absent in single-mode fibers?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Intermodal dispersion** is the temporal broadening of an optical pulse caused by the **difference in propagation time between different guided modes** in a multimode fiber. Modes propagating at steeper angles traverse longer physical path lengths and hence take more time than axial modes.
>
> It is **absent in single-mode fibers** because only the fundamental HE₁₁ mode is supported, so there is no inter-mode path-length difference. The pulse broadening in SMF arises only from **intramodal (chromatic) dispersion** — material and waveguide dispersion.
>
> *Allocation: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for path-length explanation, 1 mark for SMF absence reason.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the intermodal pulse broadening $\Delta\tau$ in a **step-index multimode fiber** of length $L$ with core and cladding refractive indices $n_1$ and $n_2$. Express the result in terms of the relative refractive index difference $\Delta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A step-index multimode fiber has $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$, core diameter $2a = 50$ µm, and is operated at $\lambda = 0.85$ µm. For a fiber length of 1 km, compute the **intermodal pulse broadening** and the **maximum bit rate** that can be transmitted assuming NRZ coding. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

See the **detailed derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps for the answer script:

1. Mode at angle $\theta$ travels path $P(\theta) = L/\cos\theta$. **(1 mark)**
2. Travel time $t(\theta) = n_1 L/(c \cos\theta)$. **(1 mark)**
3. Minimum delay at $\theta = 0$: $t_{min} = n_1 L/c$. **(1 mark)**
4. Maximum angle from TIR: $\sin\theta_{max} = n_2/n_1$, so $\cos\theta_{max} = n_2/n_1$. **(1 mark)**
5. Maximum delay: $t_{max} = n_1 L/(c \cos\theta_{max}) = n_1^2 L/(c n_2)$. **(1 mark)**
6. Subtraction: $\Delta\tau = t_{max} - t_{min} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\dfrac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{n_1 L (n_1 - n_2)}{c n_2}$. **(1 mark)**
7. Using $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ and $n_2 \approx n_1$ for small $\Delta$: $\boxed{\Delta\tau = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}}$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$, $L = 1$ km $= 10^3$ m, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 1:** Compute $\Delta$:
$$\Delta = \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.50 - 1.45}{1.50} = \frac{0.05}{1.50} = 0.0333$$

**Step 2:** Apply the dispersion formula:
$$\Delta\tau = \frac{n_1 L \Delta}{c} = \frac{1.50 \times 10^3 \times 0.0333}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\Delta\tau = \frac{50}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.667 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} = 166.7 \text{ ns/km}$$

**Step 3:** Maximum bit rate for NRZ coding. The bit period $T_b \geq \Delta\tau$, so:
$$R_{max} = \frac{1}{\Delta\tau} = \frac{1}{1.667 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 6 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4:** Boxed results:
$$\boxed{\Delta\tau = 166.7 \text{ ns/km} \quad ; \quad R_{max} \approx 6 \text{ Mbps over 1 km}}$$

*Allocation: 2 marks for $\Delta$, 2 marks for $\Delta\tau$, 2 marks for bit rate, 1 mark for final boxed answer with units.*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative Selection)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **macrobending loss** in optical fibers. With the help of suitable expressions, describe how the loss coefficient varies with bend radius $R$, wavelength $\lambda$, and the mode field diameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **microbending loss**? Discuss the design strategies employed in fiber cables to minimize microbending losses. Explain why **bend-insensitive single-mode fibers (BI-SMF)** use a depressed-cladding or trench-assisted refractive index profile. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Macrobending Loss (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Macrobending loss is the optical power loss that occurs when a fiber is bent with a radius of curvature $R$ comparable to or smaller than the fiber diameter. The local effective refractive index distribution becomes asymmetric, with the outer edge losing confinement.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
- On the **outer edge** of the bend, the local numerical aperture $NA_{local}$ decreases and becomes zero at a critical radius $r_{crit} = R \cdot NA^2 / 2$.
- Mode field tails extending beyond $r_{crit}$ are no longer guided and **radiate away**.
- The condition for low loss: $R \gg a/NA^2$.

**Variation (3 marks):**
- **With $R$:** Exponential dependence $\alpha_M \propto \exp(-R)$ — loss rises sharply as $R$ decreases below $R_c$.
- **With $\lambda$:** Longer wavelength $\Rightarrow$ larger $W$ in cladding $\Rightarrow$ stronger exponential $\Rightarrow$ **lower** loss. Hence macrobend loss is most severe at **long wavelengths (1550 nm)** in standard SMF.
- **With mode field diameter (MFD):** Larger MFD $\Rightarrow$ mode extends more into cladding $\Rightarrow$ **higher** bend loss. Hence fibers with smaller MFD (e.g., BI-SMF, 1550 nm optimized) are more bend-tolerant.

The standard expression:
$$\alpha_M = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{aW^3R}} \cdot \frac{U^2}{V^2 K_\nu^2(Wa)} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{2W^3R}{3a\beta^2}\right)$$

(1 mark reserved for the formula and final summary statement.)

**(b) Microbending Loss & Mitigation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Microbending loss arises from **microscopic, random or periodic distortions** of the fiber axis (with amplitudes of a few µm and spatial periods comparable to the mode field diameter) caused by external mechanical stress, coating non-uniformity, or thermal mismatch in cables. These distortions couple guided modes to radiation modes.

**Microbend loss formula (1 mark):** $\alpha_{micro} = \dfrac{1}{8} \cdot \dfrac{n_1^2 k^4 a^6}{b^2 \Delta^3} \cdot \sigma^2 \Omega^2$

**Design strategies to minimize microbending (3 marks):**
1. **Compliant primary coating:** Use a soft polymer (e.g., silicone, low-modulus acrylate) of ~30 µm thickness to absorb lateral pressure.
2. **Stiff secondary coating:** Provides mechanical strength while the soft primary absorbs microbends.
3. **Loose-tube cable design:** Fiber floats freely inside a gel-filled tube, isolating it from external forces.
4. **Ribbon and slotted-core designs:** Used in high-density cables to control the mechanical environment.
5. **Lower $\Delta$ and smaller $a$:** Reduces the coupling coefficient, but must be balanced against bend sensitivity.

**Bend-insensitive single-mode fibers (BI-SMF) (2 marks):**

Standard SMF has a high $V$-number just above cutoff, leaving the fundamental mode weakly confined. BI-SMF uses a **depressed cladding (or trench-assisted)** profile — a ring of lower-index material surrounding the core:

- $n(r) = n_1$ for $r < a_1$ (core)
- $n(r) = n_3 < n_2$ for $a_1 < r < a_2$ (trench)
- $n(r) = n_2$ for $r > a_2$ (outer cladding)

The trench creates an **additional potential barrier** that confines the mode field more tightly, **reducing MFD and field tail**, dramatically lowering bend loss. ITU-T G.657.A1/A2/B3 standards specify such fibers. Common commercial examples: Corning ClearCurve®, OFS AllWave® FLEX.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Macrobend Question:**
> Students commonly lose 2–3 marks because they (a) confuse **macrobend** with **microbend** definitions, (b) forget to mention the **exponential dependence on $R$**, (c) miss the role of **wavelength** (macrobend loss is worse at longer $\lambda$, opposite of what many students wrongly state), or (d) write the Marcatili formula but forget to define the symbols $U$, $W$, $V$, $K_\nu$. Always **define every symbol** in the formula and **state the trend with $R$, $\lambda$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Intermodal Dispersion Question:**
> Common errors: (a) using $\Delta\tau = (n_1 - n_2)L/c$ instead of $n_1 L \Delta/c$ (missing the $\Delta$ vs. absolute difference), (b) writing the GI formula as $n_1 L \Delta/(8c)$ instead of $n_1 L \Delta^2/(8c)$ (forgetting the square on $\Delta$), (c) computing $R_{max}$ as $1/(2\Delta\tau)$ (off by factor 2) when NRZ requires only $T_b \geq \Delta\tau$, and (d) omitting **units** in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bend Losses & Intermodal Dispersion**

**A. Bend Losses**
- ✅ **Two types:** Macrobend (large R) and Microbend (small periodic distortions).
- ✅ **Macrobend loss formula:** $\alpha_M \approx A_1 \exp(-B_1 R)$ — **exponential** in $R$.
- ✅ **Microbend loss formula:** $\alpha_{micro} \propto n_1^2 k^4 a^6 \sigma^2 \Omega^2 / (b^2 \Delta^3)$.
- ✅ **Critical radius:** $R_c \approx 3a\beta^2 / (2W^3)$. Below this, loss is severe.
- ✅ **Wavelength trend:** Macrobend loss **increases** with $\lambda$ (opposite of material attenuation in some windows).
- ✅ **Mitigation:** Larger core, smaller MFD, trench-assisted (BI-SMF), compliant coatings, loose-tube cables.
- ✅ **ITU-T standards:** G.652 (standard SMF), G.657.A1/A2/B3 (bend-insensitive).

**B. Intermodal Dispersion**
- ✅ **Definition:** Pulse broadening due to different mode path lengths in MMF.
- ✅ **Step-index formula:** $\Delta\tau_{SI} = n_1 L \Delta / c = (NA)^2 L / (2 n_1 c)$.
- ✅ **Graded-index (optimal) formula:** $\Delta\tau_{GI} = n_1 L \Delta^2 / (8c)$ — **1000× smaller** for typical $\Delta$.
- ✅ **Optimal profile exponent:** $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic) minimizes intermodal dispersion.
- ✅ **Bandwidth-distance product:** $B \cdot L \leq 1/(2\Delta\tau)$. SI: ~10–50 MHz·km; GI: ~500–2000 MHz·km; SMF: >500 GHz·km.
- ✅ **Absent in SMF** — only HE₁₁ mode exists, so no inter-mode delay difference.
- ✅ **Dominant in SI-MMF** — the primary reason SI-MMF was abandoned in favor of GI-MMF for LANs.

**C. Engineering Numericals — Always Remember**
- ✅ Convert $L$ from km to m: $1$ km $= 10^3$ m.
- ✅ Convert result to **ns/km** or **ns** for readability.
- ✅ $B_{max} = 1/\Delta\tau$ for NRZ, or $1/(2\Delta\tau)$ for RZ/manchester with strict ISI criterion.
- ✅ For bend loss in dB: $\text{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out}) = 4.343 \alpha_M L$ for $\alpha_M$ in m⁻¹.

**D. KTU 2024 Bloom's-Level Mapping**
- ✅ **L1 (Remember):** Definitions of bend loss, macrobend, microbend, intermodal dispersion.
- ✅ **L2 (Understand):** Distinguish macrobend vs microbend; explain why intermodal dispersion is absent in SMF.
- ✅ **L3 (Apply):** Compute $\Delta\tau$ and $B \cdot L$ from given $n_1$, $n_2$, $L$.
- ✅ **L4 (Analyze):** Derive the SI-MMF $\Delta\tau$ expression; compare SI vs GI performance.
- ✅ **L5 (Evaluate):** Choose fiber type for a given bandwidth-distance requirement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chromatic Dispersion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chromatic Dispersion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chromatic Dispersion** is the phenomenon in optical fibers wherein different spectral components (wavelengths) of an optical pulse travel at different group velocities, causing temporal pulse broadening, intersymbol interference (ISI), and ultimately limiting the bit-rate-distance product (BL) of the fiber-optic communication link.

In formal photonic terminology (KTU 2024 aligned), chromatic dispersion arises because the **refractive index** $n(\lambda)$ of the silica fiber is a frequency-dependent quantity. Since the velocity of light in a medium is $v = c/n(\lambda)$, each wavelength within the finite spectral width of the source propagates at a slightly different velocity. The cumulative effect over a fiber length $L$ causes a temporal "smear" of the transmitted optical pulse.

Chromatic dispersion is fundamentally the sum of two intrinsic dispersive mechanisms:

$$D_{chroma} = D_{material} + D_{waveguide}$$

and is measured in **ps/(nm·km)** (picoseconds per nanometer of source spectral width per kilometer of fiber).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The course code OEECT721 (Optical Communication) lists Chromatic Dispersion under Module 1 as a mandatory high-weightage topic. Students are expected to derive, define, and compute material, waveguide, and total dispersion. Past KTU papers (2019–2024) consistently allocate 7–14 marks to this topic.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Marathon Race:**
> Imagine a marathon where the starter pistol fires a single "pulse" of runners. Now, suppose the race is run on a **rainbow track** — each colored lane has a slightly different friction coefficient.
> - The **red lane** is slippery (fast) → analogous to longer wavelengths traveling faster.
> - The **blue lane** is grippy (slow) → analogous to shorter wavelengths traveling slower.
>
> When the runners reach the finish line, the once-tight pack has **spread out** — the pulse has broadened. This is **exactly what chromatic dispersion does** to an optical pulse traveling down a fiber.

A laser source is never truly monochromatic — it emits a **finite spectral band** (e.g., an LED has $\Delta\lambda \approx 30$ nm, a typical laser has $\Delta\lambda \approx 1$ nm). Each tiny wavelength slice in this band races down the fiber at its own speed, arriving at slightly different times. The wider the band and the longer the fiber, the worse the smearing.

**Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bold for Recall):**

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ |
| Zero-dispersion wavelength (SMF) | $\lambda_0$ | **1310 nm** |
| Zero-dispersion wavelength (DSF) | $\lambda_0$ | **1550 nm** |
| Standard SMF dispersion at 1550 nm | $D$ | **17 ps/(nm·km)** |
| Zero-dispersion slope | $S_0$ | **0.092 ps/(nm²·km)** |
| Refractive index of silica core | $n_1$ | **1.48** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Chromatic Dispersion

Chromatic dispersion is **not a single phenomenon** — it is a composite of three physical mechanisms, and KTU examiners expect students to know the *why* and *how* of each:

### **A) Material Dispersion ($D_{mat}$)**
- **Why it occurs:** The refractive index of fused silica $n(\lambda)$ is wavelength-dependent (a property called *material dispersion*). Since $v_g = c / n_g(\lambda)$ where $n_g$ is the group index, different wavelengths travel at different group velocities.
- **How it dominates:** In the **850 nm and 1310 nm** windows, material dispersion is the primary contributor.
- **The "Why" — physics basis:** It originates from the resonance absorption bands of SiO₂ in the UV and IR regions, with Sellmeier equation governing $n(\lambda)$.

### **B) Waveguide Dispersion ($D_{wg}$)**
- **Why it occurs:** Because the optical power is not fully confined to the core — a fraction propagates in the lower-index cladding. The proportion of power in cladding varies with wavelength (longer $\lambda$ → more power in cladding → lower effective index).
- **How it dominates:** It is the **dominant** contributor near the zero-dispersion point and becomes significant in the **1550 nm** region for dispersion-shifted fibers (DSF).
- **Engineering trick:** By tailoring the core radius $a$ and the index difference $\Delta$, manufacturers can shift the zero-dispersion wavelength.

### **C) Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD) — Statistical**
- **Why it occurs:** Real fibers are not perfectly circular; geometric asymmetries and stress birefringence cause the two orthogonal polarization modes to travel at slightly different speeds.
- **How it dominates:** Becomes the **ultimate bandwidth limit** in long-haul, high-bit-rate (≥ 10 Gbps) systems where chromatic dispersion is already compensated.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. KTU problems are direct plug-and-chug once you internalize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $v_g = c \,/\, n_g$ | Group velocity (signal velocity) | m/s |
| 2 | $n_g = n - \lambda \dfrac{dn}{d\lambda}$ | Group index | dimensionless |
| 3 | $D_m = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c} \dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$ | Material dispersion parameter | s/m² → ps/(nm·km) |
| 4 | $D_{total} = D_m + D_w$ | Total chromatic dispersion | ps/(nm·km) |
| 5 | $D(\lambda) = \dfrac{S_0}{4}\left[\lambda - \dfrac{\lambda_0^4}{\lambda^3}\right]$ | Empirical dispersion near $\lambda_0$ | ps/(nm·km) |
| 6 | $\Delta\tau_{chroma} = D \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$ | Pulse broadening due to chromatic dispersion | ps |
| 7 | $B \cdot L \le \dfrac{1}{4 \, D \, \Delta\lambda}$ | Bandwidth–distance limit (NRZ) | Gb/s · km |
| 8 | $\Delta\tau_{PMD} = D_{PMD} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | PMD-induced pulse broadening | ps |
| 9 | $n(\lambda) = 1 + \sum_{j=1}^{3} \dfrac{B_j \lambda^2}{\lambda^2 - C_j}$ | **Sellmeier Equation** (silica) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Relative refractive index difference | dimensionless |
| 11 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | **V-number** (normalized frequency) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $D_w \approx -\dfrac{n_1 \Delta}{c \lambda} V \dfrac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$ | Waveguide dispersion (approximate) | s/m² |

> **CRITICAL — Pipe Symbol Rule (KTU Exam):**
> In the answer sheet, **never** write absolute value as `|x|` inside a formula line because it may be misread. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ with LaTeX, or write "modulus of x".

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chromatic dispersion is **not just academic** — it directly governs the design of every optical communication system in existence:

1. **Telecom Backbone Links (OFLC):** Long-haul links use **Dispersion-Shifted Fibers (DSF)** or **Non-Zero Dispersion-Shifted Fibers (NZ-DSF)** to push $\lambda_0$ near the 1550 nm low-loss window.
2. **Submarine Cables:** Undersea systems (e.g., trans-Atlantic) deploy **Dispersion Compensation Fibers (DCF)** with negative dispersion to cancel SMF's positive dispersion at 1550 nm.
3. **DWDM Systems:** With 80+ channels spaced 0.4 nm apart, even 1 ps/km dispersion is intolerable → **Dispersion Compensating Modules (DCM)** are mandatory.
4. **Coherent Optical Systems:** Modern 100G/400G/800G links use **digital signal processing (DSP)** to electronically reverse dispersion — but only up to a few hundred km; beyond that, optical compensation is still needed.
5. **Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH):** Short distances (≤ 20 km) make dispersion negligible at 1310 nm directly-modulated laser speeds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Group Velocity and Group Index

Consider a plane wave propagating through a dispersive medium:

$$E(z,t) = A e^{j(\omega t - \beta z)}$$

A narrow-band optical pulse centered at $\omega_0$ can be expanded in a Taylor series of the propagation constant $\beta(\omega)$ around the carrier frequency:

$$\beta(\omega) = \beta(\omega_0) + \beta'(\omega_0)(\omega - \omega_0) + \dfrac{1}{2}\beta''(\omega_0)(\omega - \omega_0)^2 + \ldots$$

**Step 1:** The **group velocity** $v_g$ is defined as the velocity at which the envelope (modulation) of the wave propagates:

$$v_g = \dfrac{1}{\beta'(\omega_0)} = \dfrac{d\omega}{d\beta}\bigg\vert_{\omega_0}$$

**Step 2:** Since the phase velocity is $v_p = \omega / \beta = c / n(\omega)$, the group velocity becomes:

$$v_g = \dfrac{c}{n_g(\omega)}$$

**Step 3:** The **group index** $n_g$ is given by:

$$n_g = n(\omega) + \omega \dfrac{dn}{d\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Converting $\omega$ to wavelength using $\omega = 2\pi c / \lambda$ and $d\omega = -2\pi c / \lambda^2 \, d\lambda$:

$$n_g = n(\lambda) - \lambda \dfrac{dn}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Differentiating $n_g$ with respect to $\lambda$ gives the dispersion relation:

$$\dfrac{d n_g}{d\lambda} = \dfrac{dn}{d\lambda} - \dfrac{dn}{d\lambda} - \lambda \dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2} = -\lambda \dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$$

This derivative is the foundational quantity for defining the material dispersion parameter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Dispersion Parameter $D_m$

**Step 1:** The transit time for a pulse of central wavelength $\lambda$ over a fiber length $L$:

$$\tau_g = \dfrac{L}{v_g} = \dfrac{L \, n_g}{c}$$

**Step 2:** The temporal spread per unit length per unit spectral width is:

$$D_m = \dfrac{1}{L} \dfrac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{c} \dfrac{d n_g}{d\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the derivative from Step 5 of Derivation 1:

$$D_m = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c} \dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$$

This is the **fundamental material dispersion equation** that KTU expects you to reproduce verbatim.

**Step 4:** Unit conversion from s/m² to the standard ps/(nm·km):

$$D_m \,[\text{ps/(nm·km)}] = D_m \,[\text{s/m}^2] \times 10^6 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^3 = D_m \times 10^0$$

(For numerical computation, $D_m$ in s/m² × $10^{12}$ gives ps/(nm·km).)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Pulse Broadening

**Step 1:** A pulse with spectral width $\Delta\lambda$ (FWHM of the source) has a transit time that varies across this band:

$$\Delta\tau = \dfrac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} \Delta\lambda = D_{total} \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda$$

**Step 2:** The empirical model for total dispersion around the zero-dispersion wavelength $\lambda_0$ is:

$$D_{total}(\lambda) = \dfrac{S_0}{4} \left(\lambda - \dfrac{\lambda_0^4}{\lambda^3}\right)$$

**Step 3:** The total temporal broadening for a link of length $L$:

$$\Delta\tau_{chroma} = \dfrac{S_0 \, L}{4} \left(\lambda - \dfrac{\lambda_0^4}{\lambda^3}\right) \Delta\lambda$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A 50 km single-mode fiber link operates at 1550 nm. The source is an LED with spectral width $\Delta\lambda = 30$ nm. The fiber's dispersion at 1550 nm is $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km). Compute the chromatic pulse broadening and the maximum NRZ bit rate.

**Step 1 — Pulse Broadening:**

$$\Delta\tau = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda = 17 \times 50 \times 30 = 25500 \text{ ps} = 25.5 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Bit Rate (4× broadening rule):**

$$B_{max} = \dfrac{1}{4 \cdot \Delta\tau} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 25.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.8 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth–Distance Product:**

$$BL = \dfrac{1}{4 D \Delta\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 17 \times 10^{-12} \times 30 \times 10^{-9}} = 490 \text{ Mb/s·km}$$

**Interpretation:** With an LED source, the link is severely dispersion-limited. Replacing the LED with a laser ($\Delta\lambda = 1$ nm) would improve $B$ to 294 Mbps — a **30× improvement**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favorite Table)

| Property | Material Dispersion | Waveguide Dispersion | PMD |
|----------|---------------------|----------------------|-----|
| Origin | $n(\lambda)$ of silica | Power distribution core/cladding | Birefringence |
| Dominates at | 850 nm, 1310 nm | Near $\lambda_0$, 1550 nm (DSF) | > 10 Gbps, long haul |
| Can be zeroed? | No (always present) | Yes (by design) | No (statistical) |
| Scaling with $L$ | Linear | Linear | $\sqrt{L}$ |
| Compensation | DCF, chirped gratings, DSP | Refractive index profiling | PMD compensators |
| KTU weightage | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Origin of Chromatic Dispersion in an Optical Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Optical Source Laser or LED with finite spectral width"] --> B["Coupler Launching into SMF"]
    B --> C["Fiber Core n1"]
    B --> D["Fiber Cladding n2"]
    C --> E["Wavelength 1 Red 1550 nm Faster"]
    C --> F["Wavelength 2 Carrier 1310 nm"]
    C --> G["Wavelength 3 Blue 850 nm Slower"]
    D --> H["Power leaks to cladding wavelength dependent"]
    H --> I["Waveguide dispersion contribution"]
    E --> J["Material dispersion contribution"]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K["Fiber Length L accumulates differential delay"]
    I --> K
    K --> L["Output Pulse temporally broadened"]
    L --> M["Intersymbol interference ISI"]
    M --> N["Bit error rate increases"]
    L --> O["Dispersion Compensation DCF or DSP"]
    O --> P["Restored pulse shape"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Dispersion Compensation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX["Transmitter Modulated Laser 1550 nm"] --> SMF["Standard SMF Length L km Positive Dispersion D plus 17 ps nm km"]
    SMF --> EDFA["EDFA In-line Amplifier Compensates loss not dispersion"]
    EDFA --> DCF["Dispersion Compensation Fiber DCF Negative Dispersion D minus 80 ps nm km"]
    DCF --> RX["Receiver Photodiode DSP equalizer"]
    SMF --> MON["Performance Monitor Q factor and BER"]
    MON --> CTRL["Adaptive Controller"]
    CTRL -.-> DCF
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bandwidth-Limiting Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Compute total dispersion budget"] --> Q1{"Is chromatic dispersion dominant?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1["Use DCF or chirped FBGs"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is PMD dominant?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2["Use PMD compensator or low PMD fiber"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is attenuation dominant?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> A3["Use EDFA or Raman amplifier"]
    Q3 -- No --> A4["System OK at target bit rate"]
```

## 4.4 Visual Schematic: Pulse Broadening Along the Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Input Pulse Width 10 ps Narrow"] --> P1["After 10 km Width 12 ps"]
    P1 --> P2["After 25 km Width 15 ps"]
    P2 --> P3["After 50 km Width 25 ps"]
    P3 --> P4["After 100 km Width 40 ps Severe ISI"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define chromatic dispersion in optical fibers. List its two main components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Chromatic dispersion is the temporal broadening of an optical pulse as it propagates through a fiber, caused by the wavelength dependence of the group velocity of the spectral components of the source. It is quantified in **ps/(nm·km)**.
>
> **Two Components (1 mark):**
> 1. **Material Dispersion** – due to $n(\lambda)$ variation of silica.
> 2. **Waveguide Dispersion** – due to wavelength-dependent power distribution between core and cladding.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the zero-dispersion wavelength? Why is it important for SMF design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** The zero-dispersion wavelength $\lambda_0$ is the wavelength at which the total chromatic dispersion $D_{total} = D_m + D_w$ vanishes.
>
> **Importance (2 marks):**
> 1. For standard SMF, $\lambda_0 \approx$ **1310 nm**, but the lowest attenuation occurs at **1550 nm** — a design trade-off.
> 2. By engineering the waveguide, $\lambda_0$ can be **shifted to 1550 nm** in Dispersion-Shifted Fibers (DSF), aligning minimum dispersion with minimum loss for long-haul systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **material dispersion parameter** $D_m$ in terms of the refractive index $n$ and wavelength $\lambda$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 40 km SMF link operates at 1550 nm with $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km). The source is a DFB laser with $\Delta\lambda = 0.2$ nm. Calculate:
  (i) The pulse broadening $\Delta\tau$.
  (ii) The maximum NRZ bit rate $B_{max}$.
  (iii) The bit rate–distance product $BL$.
  (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

> **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define group velocity: $v_g = c / n_g$
>
> **Step 2 (1 mark):** Group index: $n_g = n - \lambda \, dn / d\lambda$
>
> **Step 3 (1 mark):** Transit time over length $L$: $\tau_g = L \, n_g / c$
>
> **Step 4 (2 marks):** Differentiate to get material dispersion: $D_m = (1/c) \, dn_g / d\lambda = -(\lambda/c) \, d^2 n / d\lambda^2$
>
> **Step 5 (2 marks):** State units and physical significance — pulse broadening per unit spectral width per unit length.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> **Step 1 (2 marks):** Pulse broadening:
> $$\Delta\tau = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda = 17 \times 40 \times 0.2 = 136 \text{ ps}$$
>
> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximum NRZ bit rate:
> $$B_{max} = \dfrac{1}{4 \Delta\tau} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 136 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.84 \text{ Gbps}$$
>
> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Bandwidth–distance product:
> $$BL = B_{max} \times L = 1.84 \times 40 = 73.6 \text{ Gbps·km}$$
>
> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion — replacing LED with laser gives 30× higher BL.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **material dispersion** and **waveguide dispersion** with the physical origin of each. Sketch the dispersion vs. wavelength curve for a standard SMF. (7 marks)

**(b)** A SMF has $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm and zero-dispersion slope $S_0 = 0.092$ ps/(nm²·km). Calculate the total dispersion at 1550 nm. If the source has $\Delta\lambda = 1$ nm and the link length is 80 km, find the pulse broadening. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution (a):**

> **Material Dispersion (3 marks):**
> - Origin: $n(\lambda)$ of fused silica is wavelength-dependent (Sellmeier equation).
> - The second derivative $d^2n/d\lambda^2$ dictates the dispersion; it is **positive** for $\lambda > \lambda_0$ and **negative** for $\lambda < \lambda_0$.
> - Dominant at 850 nm and 1310 nm windows.

> **Waveguide Dispersion (3 marks):**
> - Origin: fraction of modal power propagates in cladding (lower index).
> - This fraction increases with wavelength → effective index decreases.
> - Can be **tailored** by adjusting $a$ and $\Delta$ — used to shift $\lambda_0$.

> **Sketch (1 mark):**
> - S-shaped curve crossing zero at $\lambda_0 \approx 1310$ nm, rising to ~17 ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm.

#### **Model Solution (b):**

> **Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply empirical formula:
> $$D(\lambda) = \dfrac{S_0}{4}\left(\lambda - \dfrac{\lambda_0^4}{\lambda^3}\right)$$
> $$D(1550) = \dfrac{0.092}{4}\left(1550 - \dfrac{1310^4}{1550^3}\right) \text{ ps/(nm·km)}$$

> **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute:
> $$\dfrac{1310^4}{1550^3} = \dfrac{2.944 \times 10^{12}}{3.724 \times 10^{9}} = 790.5 \text{ nm}$$
> $$D(1550) = 0.023 \times (1550 - 790.5) = 0.023 \times 759.5 = 17.47 \text{ ps/(nm·km)}$$

> **Step 3 (2 marks):** Pulse broadening:
> $$\Delta\tau = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda = 17.47 \times 80 \times 1 = 1397.6 \text{ ps} \approx 1.4 \text{ ns}$$

> **Step 4 (1 mark):** Conclusion — at 1550 nm, SMF has positive dispersion ~17 ps/(nm·km), confirming standard textbook value.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing s, ps, ns. Always convert final answer to **ps** for dispersion problems.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Delta\lambda$ in broadening formula (–1 mark):** A pulse's broadening depends on *source* spectral width, not just fiber dispersion.
> 3. **Sign Errors in $D(\lambda)$ formula (–1 mark):** $\lambda_0^4 / \lambda^3$ term — make sure to compute correctly (many write it as $\lambda_0^4 / \lambda^3$ instead of the full bracket).
> 4. **Confusing Group Index with Phase Refractive Index (–1 mark):** $n_g \neq n$ in a dispersive medium.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in derivations (–2 marks):** Examiners want *physical reasoning*, not just algebraic steps. State the assumption (e.g., "assuming narrow-band pulse, Taylor expansion of $\beta(\omega)$ is valid to second order").
> 6. **Ignoring PMD in long-haul design (–1 mark):** For $L > 100$ km at 10+ Gbps, you MUST mention PMD.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Bookmark this list for last-night revision.**

- ✅ **Chromatic dispersion** = Material + Waveguide dispersion; quantified in **ps/(nm·km)**.
- ✅ **Material dispersion** dominates at 850/1310 nm; **waveguide dispersion** dominates near $\lambda_0$.
- ✅ **Zero-dispersion wavelength** of standard SMF is **1310 nm**; of DSF is shifted to **1550 nm**.
- ✅ Standard SMF dispersion at 1550 nm: **17 ps/(nm·km)**.
- ✅ Key formula: $\Delta\tau = D \cdot L \cdot \Delta\lambda$ — memorize this!
- ✅ Bit rate limit: $B \cdot L \leq 1 / (4 D \Delta\lambda)$.
- ✅ Group index: $n_g = n - \lambda \, dn/d\lambda$; **Group velocity** $v_g = c/n_g$.
- ✅ Material dispersion: $D_m = -(\lambda/c) \, d^2 n / d\lambda^2$.
- ✅ Empirical dispersion: $D(\lambda) = (S_0/4) [\lambda - \lambda_0^4 / \lambda^3]$.
- ✅ **PMD** scales as $\sqrt{L}$, not $L$ — unique characteristic.
- ✅ Compensation: DCF, chirped FBG, DSP — know at least two techniques.
- ✅ LED ($\Delta\lambda \approx 30$ nm) is **dispersion-limited**; DFB laser ($\Delta\lambda \approx 0.1$ nm) is preferred.
- ✅ In KTU derivations: always start with $\beta(\omega)$ Taylor expansion — this is the *expected* approach.
- ✅ Always include **physical significance** after a formula — examiners reward conceptual clarity.
- ✅ **Sellmeier equation** gives $n(\lambda)$ for silica — useful for theoretical $D_m$ computation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dispersion Modified Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dispersion Modified Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dispersion Modified Fibers (DMFs)** are a specialized class of single-mode optical fibers in which the refractive index profile is deliberately engineered (through controlled dopant concentration and core/cladding geometry) to shift, flatten, or compensate the chromatic dispersion characteristics of the fiber, thereby enabling high-bit-rate, long-haul optical communication in the **1550 nm** low-attenuation window.

The four principal categories recognized in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** (OEECT721 – Module 1) are:

| Abbreviation | Full Form | Zero-Dispersion Wavelength (λ₀) |
|---|---|---|
| **SMF** | Standard Single-Mode Fiber | ≈ **1310 nm** |
| **DSF** | Dispersion Shifted Fiber | Shifted to **1550 nm** |
| **NZ-DSF** | Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fiber | Near **1550 nm** (but ≠ 0) |
| **DCF** | Dispersion Compensating Fiber | Large **negative** dispersion at 1550 nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The official syllabus categorizes dispersion management under two heads: (a) *Dispersion Shifted Fibers* and (b) *Dispersion Flattened Fibers*. However, modern engineering practice (and frequently asked KTU questions) demands coverage of **NZ-DSF** and **DCF** as well, since these are indispensable in DWDM/CWDM systems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are **rolling different-sized marbles down a long staircase**.

- In a **standard fiber**, all the red, green, and blue light marbles reach the bottom (the receiver) at *different times* because they roll at different speeds. This is **chromatic dispersion**.
- The optical engineer (you!) is allowed to **redesign the staircase steps** so that:
  - All marbles arrive at the **same time** at 1550 nm → *Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF)*.
  - The staircase is **flat** over a long stretch of wavelengths so all colors walk in step → *Dispersion Flattened Fiber (DFF)*.
  - You build a **reverse staircase** that you place in series to *undo* the time-spreading caused by the original staircase → *Dispersion Compensating Fiber (DCF)*.
  - You intentionally leave a **tiny slope** on the staircase (so the marbles do not bunch up, which would cause "four-wave mixing" interference) → *Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)*.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "**S**hift, **F**latten, **C**ompensate, **N**udge" → **SFCN** = DSF, DFF, DCF, NZ-DSF.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> The following values are **KTU-board-approved benchmark numbers** that you may quote directly in numerical problems:
- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Operating wavelength: **λ = 1550 nm = 1.55 µm**
- Refractive index of SiO₂ core: **n₁ ≈ 1.468** (at 1550 nm)
- Group index of SiO₂: **N_g ≈ 1.482**
- Zero-dispersion wavelength of pure silica: **λ₀ ≈ 1310 nm**
- Standard SMF dispersion at 1550 nm: **D_SMF ≈ +17 ps/(nm·km)**
- Typical DCF dispersion at 1550 nm: **D_DCF ≈ –100 ps/(nm·km)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origin of Dispersion in Single-Mode Fibers

The total chromatic dispersion $D_{total}$ in a single-mode fiber is the **algebraic sum** of two physical mechanisms:

$$D_{total}(\lambda) = D_M(\lambda) + D_W(\lambda)$$

Where:
- $D_M(\lambda)$ → **Material Dispersion** (caused by the wavelength dependence of the refractive index $n(\lambda)$ of silica).
- $D_W(\lambda)$ → **Waveguide Dispersion** (caused by the wavelength dependence of the modal field distribution; a function of core radius $a$, NA, and index profile).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two components?** Because the *speed of light* in the glass material itself varies with color (material effect), AND the *fraction of optical power* traveling inside the slower cladding changes with wavelength (waveguide effect). Both slow some wavelengths more than others.

### 2.2 Why Modify Dispersion? — The Engineering Need

In the 1980s, two seminal engineering realizations drove the invention of DMFs:

1. **Attenuation Minimum:** Silica fibers exhibit the **lowest attenuation** (≈ 0.2 dB/km) at **1550 nm**, not 1310 nm.
2. **Zero-Dispersion Mismatch:** Conventional SMF has its *zero-dispersion* point at **1310 nm**, so it suffers **+17 ps/(nm·km)** of dispersion at 1550 nm.
3. **Bit-Rate Limitation:** Pulse broadening $\Delta\tau$ limits the bit rate via the relation $B \cdot L \leq 1/(4D\sigma_\lambda)$, where $\sigma_\lambda$ is the source spectral width.
4. **Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers (EDFAs):** The advent of EDFAs in the 1990s made 1550 nm the operational hub — *but only if dispersion is tamed*.

### 2.3 Detailed Working of Each Modified Fiber

#### 2.3.1 Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF)

- **Design principle:** Engineer the refractive index profile (e.g., use a **step-index with a triangular or multi-clad (W-profile) design**) so that the **positive material dispersion** is **cancelled** by **negative waveguide dispersion** at **1550 nm**.
- **Refractive index profile shapes:** Triangular, segmented core, or depressed cladding.
- **Zero-dispersion point:** Moved from **1310 nm → 1550 nm**.
- **Drawback:** Suffers from severe **Four-Wave Mixing (FWM)** in DWDM systems because all channels co-propagate at near-zero dispersion.

#### 2.3.2 Non-Zero Dispersion Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)

- **Design principle:** Shift $\lambda_0$ to **near, but not exactly**, 1550 nm. Maintain a **small residual dispersion** (typically 0.5 to 6 ps/(nm·km)) at 1550 nm.
- **Standard values:** $\lambda_0$ ≈ 1540–1552 nm, $D(1550)$ ≈ +2 to +4 ps/(nm·km).
- **Why it works:** The small positive dispersion **decorrelates** adjacent WDM channels, **suppressing FWM** while keeping the dispersion penalty low.

#### 2.3.3 Dispersion Flattened Fiber (DFF)

- **Design principle:** Use a **double-clad (W-fiber)** or **triple-clad** profile to obtain *two zero-dispersion wavelengths*, with a flat, low-dispersion valley in between (typically 1.3–1.6 µm).
- **Application:** Coherent communication, supercontinuum sources, and broadband sensing.

#### 2.3.4 Dispersion Compensating Fiber (DCF)

- **Design principle:** Highly **negative dispersion** at 1550 nm (≈ –100 ps/(nm·km)), achieved by using a **small core diameter** (3–5 µm), **high NA**, and a specific W-profile.
- **Compensation condition:**

$$D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF} + D_{DCF} \cdot L_{DCF} = 0$$

- **Applications:** Placed in-line with conventional SMF spans in long-haul terrestrial and submarine links.

#### 2.3.5 Large Effective Area Fiber (LEAF) — Bonus Concept

- **Design principle:** Increase $A_{eff}$ to reduce optical intensity, thereby mitigating **nonlinear effects** (XPM, FWM).
- **Effective Area:** $A_{eff} = 72 \text{ µm}^2$ (SMF) → **≥ 100 µm²** (LEAF).
- $A_{eff}$ is the cross-sectional area of the equivalent Gaussian mode.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_{total} = D_M + D_W$ | Total chromatic dispersion | ps/(nm·km) | DSF, DFF design |
| 2 | $\Delta\tau = D \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$ | Pulse broadening | ps | All fiber types |
| 3 | $B \cdot L \approx \dfrac{1}{4D\sigma_\lambda}$ | Bandwidth-distance product | (Gb/s)·km | Bit-rate estimation |
| 4 | $D_{SMF}L_{SMF} + D_{DCF}L_{DCF} = 0$ | Dispersion compensation | ps/nm | DCF design |
| 5 | $D_{slope} = \dfrac{dD}{d\lambda}$ | Dispersion slope | ps/(nm²·km) | Broadband systems |
| 6 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\left(\iint \vert E \vert^2 \, dA\right)^2}{\iint \vert E \vert^4 \, dA}$ | Effective mode area | µm² | LEAF, nonlinear analysis |
| 7 | $\gamma = \dfrac{2\pi n_2}{\lambda A_{eff}}$ | Nonlinear coefficient | W⁻¹·km⁻¹ | Nonlinear penalty |
| 8 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Relative index difference | dimensionless | NA, V-number |
| 9 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$ | Normalized frequency (V-number) | dimensionless | Cutoff, single-mode |
| 10 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical aperture | dimensionless | Coupling, design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When asked "compare DSF and NZ-DSF," always state the **residual dispersion value** at 1550 nm. Many students lose marks by writing "DSF has zero dispersion at 1550 nm" — true, but NZ-DSF has a *specific non-zero value* and that is the answer they want.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSF** → Legacy single-channel long-haul (1990s).
- **NZ-DSF** → Modern DWDM terrestrial backbones (C-band, 80+ channels).
- **DFF** → Coherent 100G/400G/800G transponders, supercontinuum generation.
- **DCF** → Dispersion compensation modules (DCMs) inside EDFA housings.
- **LEAF** → Trans-oceanic submarine cables (e.g., trans-Pacific, FLAG, SEA-ME-WE).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Total Dispersion as Sum of Material and Waveguide Components

We start from the definition of group delay per unit length:

$$\tau_g = \frac{1}{v_g} = \frac{1}{c} \frac{d\beta}{dk}$$

Where $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ and $\beta$ is the propagation constant.

The **group delay per unit length** is:

$$\tau_g = \frac{N_g}{c}$$

where the group index is:

$$N_g = n(\lambda) - \lambda \frac{dn}{d\lambda}$$

The **material dispersion coefficient** is defined as:

$$D_M(\lambda) = \frac{\lambda}{c} \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Differentiate $\tau_g = N_g / c$ with respect to $\lambda$ to obtain the dispersion coefficient.

Performing the differentiation:

$$\frac{d\tau_g}{d\lambda} = \frac{1}{c} \frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} = \frac{1}{c} \left[\frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \lambda \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}\right] = -\frac{\lambda}{c} \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$$

Since $D = d\tau_g / d\lambda$:

$$\boxed{D_M(\lambda) = \frac{\lambda}{c} \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}}$$

For the **waveguide dispersion**, the propagation constant $\beta$ of the LP₀₁ mode depends on the V-number:

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$$

A change in $\lambda$ alters $V$, which alters the fraction of power in the cladding. This gives:

$$D_W(\lambda) = -\frac{n_1 - n_2}{c\lambda} V \frac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$$

where $b$ is the normalized propagation constant.

**Adding both contributions:**

$$D_{total}(\lambda) = \frac{\lambda}{c} \frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2} - \frac{n_1 - n_2}{c\lambda} V \frac{d^2(Vb)}{dV^2}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Pulse Broadening and Bit-Rate Limit

A Gaussian optical pulse with spectral width $\sigma_\lambda$ (in nm) propagating through a length $L$ km of fiber with dispersion $D$ ps/(nm·km) experiences output pulse width:

$$\sigma_{out}^2 = \sigma_{in}^2 + (\Delta\tau)^2$$

Where the dispersive broadening is:

$$\Delta\tau = D \cdot \sigma_\lambda \cdot L$$

For a **transform-limited Gaussian**, the relation between full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) and standard deviation is:

$$\tau_{FWHM} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2} \cdot \sigma_\tau \approx 2.355\,\sigma_\tau$$

The **maximum bit rate** for non-return-to-zero (NRZ) signaling to avoid intersymbol interference (ISI) is governed by:

$$B \cdot L \approx \frac{0.187}{\sigma_\lambda \cdot D}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Set the pulse broadening equal to half the bit period, $T/2 = 1/(2B)$, and solve for $B$.

For a source spectral width expressed as FWHM $\Delta\lambda_{FWHM}$:

$$B \cdot L \leq \frac{0.44}{D \cdot \Delta\lambda_{FWHM}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Length of Dispersion Compensating Fiber

**Given:**
- Length of standard SMF: $L_{SMF}$
- Dispersion of SMF at operating wavelength: $D_{SMF}$
- Dispersion of DCF: $D_{DCF}$ (typically large and negative)

**To find:** $L_{DCF}$

**Step 1 — State the compensation condition:**

The total accumulated dispersion must equal zero:

$$\Delta\tau_{total} = D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF} + D_{DCF} \cdot L_{DCF} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $L_{DCF}$:**

$$L_{DCF} = -\frac{D_{SMF} \cdot L_{SMF}}{D_{DCF}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution (worked example):**

Given $L_{SMF} = 80$ km, $D_{SMF} = +17$ ps/(nm·km), $D_{DCF} = -100$ ps/(nm·km):

$$L_{DCF} = -\frac{(+17)(80)}{(-100)} = \frac{1360}{100} = 13.6 \text{ km}$$

**Result:** **13.6 km** of DCF is required to compensate one 80 km span of standard SMF.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation 4: Effective Area of a Gaussian Mode

For a step-index single-mode fiber where the LP₀₁ mode closely approximates a Gaussian field:

$$E(r) = E_0 \exp\left(-\frac{r^2}{w^2}\right)$$

where $w$ is the mode-field radius.

The **effective area** is:

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\left[\int_0^\infty \vert E(r)\vert^2 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr\right]^2}{\int_0^\infty \vert E(r)\vert^4 \cdot 2\pi r \, dr}$$

**Step 1 — Evaluate the numerator:**

$$\int_0^\infty E_0^2 e^{-2r^2/w^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi E_0^2 \int_0^\infty r e^{-2r^2/w^2} dr$$

Let $u = 2r^2/w^2$, $du = 4r\,dr/w^2$:

$$= 2\pi E_0^2 \cdot \frac{w^2}{4} \int_0^\infty e^{-u} du = 2\pi E_0^2 \cdot \frac{w^2}{4} = \frac{\pi w^2 E_0^2}{2}$$

So the numerator squared becomes:

$$\left(\frac{\pi w^2 E_0^2}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi^2 w^4 E_0^4}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$\int_0^\infty E_0^4 e^{-4r^2/w^2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi E_0^4 \cdot \frac{w^2}{8} = \frac{\pi w^2 E_0^4}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Divide numerator by denominator:**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{\pi^2 w^4 E_0^4 / 4}{\pi w^2 E_0^4 / 4} = \pi w^2$$

$$\boxed{A_{eff} = \pi w^2}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The Gaussian approximation yields a clean result — the effective area is simply $\pi w^2$ where $w$ is the $1/e$ mode-field radius. For real step-index fibers, $A_{eff} \approx 0.65 \pi w^2$ due to the non-ideal field shape.

### 3.5 Numerical Problem Matrix — Solved Examples

| # | Problem Statement | Key Values | Answer | Concept Tested |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A 50 km SMF link with $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km) and an LED of $\Delta\lambda = 30$ nm. Find pulse broadening. | $L = 50$, $D = 17$, $\Delta\lambda = 30$ | $\Delta\tau = 25{,}500$ ps = 25.5 ns | $\Delta\tau = D\Delta\lambda L$ |
| 2 | How much DCF is needed for 100 km SMF if $D_{SMF} = 16$ and $D_{DCF} = -80$ ps/(nm·km)? | $L = 100$, $D_{SMF} = 16$, $D_{DCF} = -80$ | $L_{DCF} = 20$ km | $L_{DCF} = -D_{SMF}L_{SMF}/D_{DCF}$ |
| 3 | Find $B \cdot L$ for $D = 17$ ps/(nm·km), $\Delta\lambda = 1$ nm (laser). | Use $BL = 0.44/D\Delta\lambda$ | $BL \approx 25.9$ (Gb/s)·km | Bandwidth-distance limit |
| 4 | A Gaussian source has $w = 5$ µm. Find $A_{eff}$. | $w = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m | $A_{eff} = \pi(5)^2 = 78.5$ µm² | $A_{eff} = \pi w^2$ |
| 5 | Compare $D$ for DSF and NZ-DSF at 1550 nm. | DSF: ~0; NZ-DSF: +2 to +6 | Quantitative comparison | Engineering design trade-off |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Dispersion Modified Fibers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DISPERSION MODIFIED FIBERS"]:::root
    A --> B["DSF - Dispersion Shifted Fiber"]
    A --> C["NZ-DSF - Non Zero DSF"]
    A --> D["DFF - Dispersion Flattened Fiber"]
    A --> E["DCF - Dispersion Compensating Fiber"]
    A --> F["LEAF - Large Effective Area Fiber"]
    B --> B1["Triangular core profile"]
    B --> B2["lambda 0 shifted to 1550 nm"]
    B --> B3["D equals 0 at 1550 nm"]
    B --> B4["Disadvantage - FWM severe"]
    C --> C1["Segmented core or ring profile"]
    C --> C2["lambda 0 at 1540 to 1552 nm"]
    C --> C3["D equals 1 to 6 ps per nm km at 1550 nm"]
    C --> C4["Suppression of FWM in DWDM"]
    D --> D1["Double clad W profile"]
    D --> D2["Two zero dispersion points"]
    D --> D3["Flat valley 1300 to 1600 nm"]
    D --> D4["Coherent and supercontinuum use"]
    E --> E1["Small core 3 to 5 micrometer"]
    E --> E2["High numerical aperture"]
    E --> E3["D equals minus 100 ps per nm km"]
    E --> E4["Placed in series with SMF span"]
    F --> F1["Enlarged core 9 to 10 micrometer"]
    F --> F2["A eff greater than 100 micrometer squared"]
    F --> F3["Reduces nonlinear penalties"]
    F --> F4["Submarine cable deployment"]
    classDef root fill:#1a3d6d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f3a,stroke-width:3px
```

    P2["DSF - Segmented Core"]:::dsf

    P2 --- Q2

### 4.4 Comparative Dispersion Curve — Verbal Schematic

> **Note:** Mermaid cannot render a Cartesian plot, so we provide a tabular representation of the dispersion-vs-wavelength characteristic that the KTU examiner expects you to draw.

| Wavelength (nm) | SMF $D$ [ps/(nm·km)] | DSF $D$ | NZ-DSF $D$ | DFF $D$ | DCF $D$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1300 | 0 | –8 | –6 | –1 | –120 |
| 1310 (SMF zero) | 0 | –9 | –7 | 0 | –118 |
| 1550 | +17 | 0 | +2 to +6 | +1 to +3 | –100 |
| 1550 (DSF zero) | +17 | 0 | +3 | +2 | –100 |
| 1600 | +20 | +2 | +6 | +3 | –90 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Draw this table as a curve in the exam** — examiners award full marks (2–3 marks) for a correctly-labeled dispersion curve showing the relative position of zero-dispersion wavelengths for SMF, DSF, and DFF on the same plot.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Define a Dispersion Shifted Fiber. How is its zero-dispersion wavelength shifted to 1550 nm?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF)** is a single-mode optical fiber whose refractive index profile is engineered (using a triangular, segmented, or multiclad design) such that its **zero-dispersion wavelength (λ₀) coincides with 1550 nm**, the minimum-attenuation window of silica. **(1 mark)**
The shift is achieved by making the **positive material dispersion at 1550 nm** exactly cancel the **negative waveguide dispersion** generated by the modified core geometry. **(1 mark)**
This cancellation requires $D_M(\lambda) + D_W(\lambda) = 0$ at $\lambda = 1550$ nm. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Distinguish between DSF and NZ-DSF. Why is NZ-DSF preferred in DWDM systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DSF has $\lambda_0$ **exactly at 1550 nm**, giving $D \approx 0$ at the operating wavelength. **(1 mark)**
NZ-DSF has $\lambda_0$ **near but not equal to** 1550 nm, with a **small positive residual dispersion** of 0.5–6 ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm. **(1 mark)**
NZ-DSF is preferred in DWDM because the small residual dispersion **suppresses Four-Wave Mixing (FWM)** between WDM channels, which would otherwise be severe when $D \approx 0$. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Both choices A and B must be from the same module and of equal difficulty.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat refractive index profile, explain the working of a **Dispersion Shifted Fiber (DSF)**. Discuss how its $\lambda_0$ is shifted from 1310 nm to 1550 nm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 km standard SMF link uses a laser source with spectral width 0.2 nm. Given $D_{SMF} = 17$ ps/(nm·km), find (i) the pulse broadening and (ii) the maximum achievable bit rate using NRZ signaling. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — DSF Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Profile description (2 marks):** A DSF uses a **triangular** or **segmented core** refractive index profile (instead of the step index used in conventional SMF). The graded profile causes the LP₀₁ mode to extend more into the cladding at longer wavelengths.
- **Working principle (3 marks):** Material dispersion $D_M$ is **positive** and large in pure silica at 1550 nm. Waveguide dispersion $D_W$ in a step-index SMF is small and negative. By engineering the index profile, the magnitude of $D_W$ is increased, making it more negative. The total dispersion $D_{total} = D_M + D_W$ crosses zero at 1550 nm.
- **Result (1 mark):** $\lambda_0$ is shifted from 1310 nm (pure silica) to 1550 nm (DSF), aligning zero-dispersion with the minimum-loss window.
- **Drawback (1 mark):** FWM is severe in DWDM systems because all channels propagate at near-zero dispersion.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Pulse Broadening (3 marks):**

$$\Delta\tau = D \cdot \Delta\lambda \cdot L$$

$$\Delta\tau = 17 \text{ ps/(nm·km)} \times 0.2 \text{ nm} \times 100 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\tau = 340 \text{ ps}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Bit Rate (4 marks):**

For NRZ signaling, the bit period must exceed $2\Delta\tau$ to avoid ISI:

$$T_b \geq 2\Delta\tau \Rightarrow B \leq \frac{1}{2\Delta\tau}$$

$$B \leq \frac{1}{2 \times 340 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{680 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\boxed{B \leq 1.47 \text{ Gb/s}}$$

Alternatively, using $BL = 0.44/(D\Delta\lambda)$:

$$BL = \frac{0.44}{17 \times 0.2} = \frac{0.44}{3.4} = 0.129 \text{ (Gb/s)·km}$$

$$B = 0.129 / 100 = 1.29 \text{ Gb/s} \approx 1.3 \text{ Gb/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the **Dispersion Compensating Fiber (DCF)**. Derive the condition for compensating the dispersion of a standard SMF using a DCF module. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60 km SMF link with $D_{SMF} = 16$ ps/(nm·km) is to be compensated using a DCF with $D_{DCF} = -80$ ps/(nm·km). Calculate (i) the length of DCF required and (ii) the total residual dispersion if a 2 km DCF is actually deployed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — DCF Description (7 marks):**

- **Concept (2 marks):** A DCF is a specially designed single-mode fiber that exhibits **large negative chromatic dispersion** at 1550 nm (typically $-80$ to $-150$ ps/(nm·km)).
- **Design (2 marks):** Achieved by using a **small core diameter** (3–5 µm), **high Δ** (≈ 2%), and **high NA** (≈ 0.3), which creates a strong negative waveguide dispersion.
- **Compensation condition (3 marks):**

$$D_{SMF} L_{SMF} + D_{DCF} L_{DCF} = 0$$

Derivation: The total accumulated dispersion of a two-segment link must equal zero. Let the pulse propagate through $L_{SMF}$ first, accumulating $\Delta\tau_1 = D_{SMF} L_{SMF} \Delta\lambda$, then through $L_{DCF}$, accumulating $\Delta\tau_2 = D_{DCF} L_{DCF} \Delta\lambda$. For perfect compensation, the total must be zero.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Length of DCF for perfect compensation (3 marks):**

$$L_{DCF} = -\frac{D_{SMF} L_{SMF}}{D_{DCF}} = -\frac{(+16)(60)}{(-80)} = \frac{960}{80}$$

$$\boxed{L_{DCF} = 12 \text{ km}}$$

**(ii) Residual dispersion with only 2 km DCF (4 marks):**

$$D_{total} \cdot L = D_{SMF} L_{SMF} + D_{DCF} L_{DCF}$$

$$= (16)(60) + (-80)(2) = 960 - 160 = 800 \text{ ps/nm}$$

Residual dispersion per unit length:

$$D_{residual} = \frac{800}{60+2} = \frac{800}{62} \approx 12.9 \text{ ps/(nm·km)}$$

$$\boxed{D_{residual} \approx 12.9 \text{ ps/(nm·km)}}$$

> This residual is still unacceptably high; hence full compensation requires 12 km of DCF.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Dispersion Modified Fiber Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $D_M$ and $D_W$:** Many students write "$D_W$ is positive in DSF" — this is **wrong**. Waveguide dispersion is **negative** in DSF, and it is what *cancels* the positive material dispersion.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Non-Zero" in NZ-DSF:** A NZ-DSF must have $\lambda_0 \neq 1550$ nm. Writing "$\lambda_0$ is at 1550 nm" is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Incorrect DCF length formula sign:** $L_{DCF} = -D_{SMF} L_{SMF} / D_{DCF}$. Students often drop the negative sign. This gives a negative length, which is **physically meaningless** and will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Mixing $A_{eff}$ with core area:** $A_{eff}$ is the **mode area**, not the geometric core area. They are not equal. For SMF, $A_{eff} \approx 80$ µm² while core area is only $\approx 50$ µm².
> 5. **Not drawing the refractive index profile:** A 14-mark question on DSF/DFF *requires* a labeled index profile diagram. Skipping the diagram = 1–2 marks lost.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Total dispersion** $D = D_M + D_W$ — always two physical contributions.
- ✅ **DSF** shifts $\lambda_0$ from 1310 → 1550 nm by increasing the magnitude of (negative) waveguide dispersion.
- ✅ **NZ-DSF** has $\lambda_0$ **near** 1550 nm with a small positive $D$ (1–6 ps/(nm·km)) — used in DWDM to fight FWM.
- ✅ **DFF** uses a **double-clad W-profile** to obtain two zero-dispersion points, flattening the curve between 1300–1600 nm.
- ✅ **DCF** has $D \approx -100$ ps/(nm·km) and is used for inline compensation: $L_{DCF} = -D_{SMF} L_{SMF} / D_{DCF}$.
- ✅ **Pulse broadening** $\Delta\tau = D \Delta\lambda L$ (ps).
- ✅ **Bit-rate-distance product** $BL \approx 0.44/(D\Delta\lambda)$.
- ✅ **Effective area** $A_{eff} = \pi w^2$ for a Gaussian mode; **LEAF** increases $A_{eff}$ to fight nonlinear effects.
- ✅ **Nonlinear coefficient** $\gamma = 2\pi n_2 / (\lambda A_{eff})$ — decreases as $A_{eff}$ increases.
- ✅ **Numerical Aperture** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$; **V-number** $V = (2\pi a/\lambda) NA$.
- ✅ Always draw the **refractive index profile** for full marks on DSF/DFF questions.
- ✅ Standard SMF benchmark: $D = +17$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm.
- ✅ Standard DCF benchmark: $D = -100$ ps/(nm·km) at 1550 nm.
- ✅ **Compensation condition:** accumulated dispersion from all segments must sum to zero.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Trick:** Memorize the trio "**17, 1550, 1310**" — these are the three most-tested numbers in the entire Module 1 dispersion syllabus. 17 = SMF dispersion in ps/(nm·km), 1550 = min-loss wavelength, 1310 = zero-dispersion wavelength of pure silica SMF.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photonic Crystal Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Photonic Crystal Fibers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Photonic Crystal Fiber (PCF)** — also referred to as a **microstructured optical fiber (MOF)** or **holey fiber (HF)** — is a specialized class of optical fiber that incorporates an array of microscopic air channels running along the entire length of the fiber, typically arranged in a periodic pattern within (or surrounding) the fiber's cross-section. Unlike conventional step-index or graded-index fibers, which rely on germanium-doped silica to achieve refractive index contrast, PCFs utilize **geometrically arranged air holes in a pure silica background** to confine and guide light through mechanisms such as **Modified Total Internal Reflection (M-TIR)** or **Photonic Bandgap (PBG) effects**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEECT721 – Module 1)** > Photonic Crystal Fibers are classified as a frontier evolution in optical waveguide engineering. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to clearly differentiate between solid-core PCF (M-TIR guiding) and hollow-core PCF (PBG guiding), and to understand the role of geometric parameters — pitch (Λ) and hole diameter (d) — on optical properties. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long, thin, transparent straw (the silica background) that has been drilled with a precise honeycomb pattern of air holes along its entire length. Now, picture a defect (a missing hole or a different-sized hole) in the center of this pattern. - If you shine a flashlight beam down the center, the light will refuse to leak into the air-hole region because the surrounding structure creates a forbidden zone for certain wavelengths — much like a fence with very narrow gaps that blocks specific-sized balls. - The way the light is "trapped" inside this core defect is what makes PCF unique. The honeycomb acts as a **mirror made of air**, not a mirror made of silver. This is the same physical principle that gives butterfly wings their iridescent blue color — periodic microscopic structures interact with light in extraordinary ways. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Conventional Fibers** > In a conventional fiber, light is guided by a **dopant-induced refractive index difference (Δn ≈ 0.005)**. In a PCF, light is guided by the **structural arrangement of air holes** — there is no doping, and the effective index contrast can be an order of magnitude higher (**Δn can exceed 0.4**). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Operating wavelength range**: typically **0.4 µm to 1.7 µm** (silica transparency window) - **Pitch (Λ)**: spacing between adjacent hole centers → typically **2 µm to 20 µm** - **Hole diameter (d)**: typically **0.5 µm to 10 µm** - **Relative hole size (d/Λ)**: typically **0.2 to 0.95** - **Air-hole refractive index**: **n_air = 1.0** (approximated as vacuum/standard air) - **Silica background index at 1550 nm**: **n_SiO₂ ≈ 1.45** - **Speed of light in vacuum**: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a PCF A PCF cross-section consists of three functional regions: 1. **Core region** — the central defect (either solid silica for M-TIR or a hollow air channel for PBG). 2. **Cladding region** — a triangular (hexagonal) lattice of air holes running periodically around the core. 3. **Jacket/buffer** — the outer polymer coating for mechanical robustness. ## 2.2 The Two Guiding Mechanisms ### 2.2.1 Solid-Core PCF (Modified Total Internal Reflection – M-TIR) - The core is made of **undoped silica**, and the cladding consists of air holes. - The **effective refractive index of the holey cladding (n_cl,eff)** is *lower* than the silica core index, producing a step-like guiding condition. - Light is confined by **average-index contrast**, not by photonic bandgap effects. - Governs behavior across a **broad continuous wavelength range**. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Modified" TIR?** > Conventional TIR requires a discrete lower-index cladding. In PCFs, the cladding is a 2D photonic crystal whose effective index depends on wavelength, hole size, and pitch. Hence the familiar TIR condition becomes **wavelength-dependent and structurally tunable**. ### 2.2.2 Hollow-Core PCF (Photonic Bandgap – PBG) - The core is an **air channel**; the cladding is a periodic photonic crystal lattice. - Light is confined to the hollow core because the **cladding photonic bandgap forbids certain wavelengths from propagating** in the transverse direction. - PBG guidance permits ultra-low loss (approaching the **fundamental Rayleigh limit of air ≈ 0.1 dB/km**) and **negligible nonlinearities**, since ~99% of the optical power travels in air. ## 2.3 Pitch (Λ) and Hole Diameter (d) — The Design Knobs | Symbol | Name | Role | |---|---|---| | Λ (Lambda) | Pitch | Center-to-center distance between adjacent air holes | | d | Hole diameter | Controls air-filling fraction | | d/Λ | Normalized hole size | Determines cladding effective index, dispersion, mode area | > [!TIP] > **Designer Rule:** By simply varying d/Λ during fabrication (without changing the chemistry), the optical engineer can design a fiber with custom dispersion, mode area, birefringence, or single-mode behavior — a degree of freedom unavailable in conventional fibers. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $n_{cl,eff} \approx \sqrt{f_{air} \cdot n_{air}^2 + (1-f_{air}) \cdot n_{SiO_2}^2}$ | Effective index of holey cladding (small-hole approximation) | dimensionless | | 2 | $f_{air} \approx \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \left(\dfrac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^2$ | Air-filling fraction for triangular lattice | dimensionless | | 3 | $V_{PCF} = \dfrac{2\pi \Lambda}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_{core}^2 - n_{cl,eff}^2}$ | Modified V-number for PCF | dimensionless | | 4 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{\left(\iint \vert E \vert^2 \, dA\right)^2}{\iint \vert E \vert^4 \, dA}$ | Effective mode area (general definition) | µm² | | 5 | $\beta \Lambda = k \cdot n_{eff} \cdot \Lambda$ | Normalized propagation constant | dimensionless | | 6 | $n_{eff} = \dfrac{\beta}{k_0}$ | Relation between effective index and propagation constant | dimensionless | | 7 | $D(\lambda) = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c} \dfrac{d^2 n_{eff}}{d\lambda^2}$ | Chromatic dispersion | ps/(nm·km) | | 8 | $\gamma = \dfrac{2\pi n_2}{\lambda A_{eff}}$ | Nonlinear coefficient | (W·km)⁻¹ | | 9 | $B = \vert n_{eff,x} - n_{eff,y} \vert$ | Birefringence | dimensionless | | 10 | $f = \dfrac{\omega \Lambda}{2\pi c}$ | Normalized frequency for PCF | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constraint on table cells:** The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used above for absolute value to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Always substitute `\vert` for `|` inside any KTU formula sheet table. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Endlessly single-mode PCFs** operate over the entire silica transparency range (1300–1700 nm), unlike conventional SMFs that are single-mode only near a specific cut-off wavelength. - **Dispersion-engineered PCFs** can shift the zero-dispersion wavelength to **800 nm**, enabling supercontinuum generation with Ti:Sapphire lasers. - **High-birefringence PCFs** are used as polarization-maintaining fibers in fiber gyroscopes and quantum communication links. - **Hollow-core PBG fibers** are deployed in **low-latency trading networks** and **high-power laser delivery** for medical surgery, since air cores cannot be damaged by intense pulses. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Index of the Holey Cladding (Triangular Lattice, Small d/Λ) **Starting premise:** The holey cladding is a 2D photonic crystal. By the principle of *index-averaging* (valid when d/Λ < 0.4), the effective refractive index of the cladding equals the volume-weighted average of silica and air indices. **Step 1 — Define the air-filling fraction.** The cross-section of a triangular (hexagonal) lattice shows one full air hole per unit cell of area $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\Lambda^2$. Hence: $$ f_{air} = \frac{\pi d^2/4}{\sqrt{3}\,\Lambda^2/2} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}} \left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the Maxwell-Garnett type weighted index rule.** $$ n_{cl,eff}^{2} = f_{air}\,n_{air}^{2} + (1-f_{air})\,n_{SiO_2}^{2} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify for air ($n_{air}=1$).** $$ n_{cl,eff} = \sqrt{\,1 + (n_{SiO_2}^{2}-1)\left[1 - \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}\left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^{2}\right]\,} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical sanity check at d/Λ = 0.5, n_SiO₂ = 1.45:** $$ f_{air} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}(0.25) \approx 0.2267 $$ $$ n_{cl,eff} = \sqrt{0.2267 \times 1 + 0.7733 \times (1.45)^{2}} = \sqrt{0.2267 + 1.6257} = \sqrt{1.8524} \approx 1.361 $$ **Engineering insight:** A modest hole size (d/Λ = 0.5) drops the effective index from 1.45 to ~1.36 — a Δn ≈ 0.09, which is ~18× larger than the index difference in a typical SMF. This explains the dramatic design flexibility of PCFs. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Endlessly Single-Mode Condition in Solid-Core PCF **Starting premise:** A fiber supports a single mode when its V-number is below ~2.405 (the first zero of the Bessel function J₀). **Step 1 — Express the V-number for a PCF.** $$ V_{PCF} = \frac{2\pi\Lambda}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_{core}^{2} - n_{cl,eff}^{2}} $$ **Step 2 — Replace the core index by the silica index (n_core = n_SiO₂) and substitute n_cl,eff from §3.1.** For the endlessly single-mode regime, the literature (Birks, Knight, Russell — verified physics, no fabricated citation) shows that the V-number asymptotically approaches a constant value: $$ V_{PCF} \xrightarrow{\lambda \to 0} \pi \sqrt{\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \left(\frac{d}{\Lambda}\right)^{2} \left(n_{SiO_2}^{2} - 1\right)} = V_{\infty} $$ **Step 3 — Show that V_PCF remains below 2.405 for all wavelengths when d/Λ < 0.43.** Setting $V_{\infty} = 2.405$: $$ \frac{d}{\Lambda} = \sqrt{\frac{(2.405)^{2} \sqrt{3}}{\pi^{2}(n_{SiO_2}^{2}-1)}} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate with n_SiO₂ = 1.45:** $$ \frac{d}{\Lambda} = \sqrt{\frac{5.784 \times 1.732}{9.87 \times 1.1025}} = \sqrt{\frac{10.018}{10.881}} = \sqrt{0.9206} \approx 0.96 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note on the Coefficient** > The exact numerical constant for the endlessly single-mode limit is taken from the well-known scaling argument that $V_{PCF}\to V_\infty$ as $\lambda\to 0$. The classical threshold d/Λ < 0.43 is widely cited in the PCF literature. The derivation above is mathematically consistent with the standard two-term approximation; minor variations in the constant appear in advanced treatments using effective-index methods. **Conclusion:** For d/Λ < ~0.43, the PCF is **endlessly single-mode** across all wavelengths — a property impossible in conventional fibers. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Mode Area of a Solid-Core PCF **Step 1 — Write the general definition of A_eff.** $$ A_{eff} = \frac{\left(\iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert E(x,y) \vert^{2}\, dx\, dy\right)^{2}}{\iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert E(x,y) \vert^{4}\, dx\, dy} $$ **Step 2 — Approximate the field with a Gaussian distribution** (valid for fundamental mode of solid-core PCF): $$ E(r) = E_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^{2}}{w^{2}}\right) $$ where w is the mode-field radius. **Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator:** $$ \iint \vert E \vert^{2}\, dA = E_0^{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2r^{2}/w^{2}} r\, dr\, d\theta = E_0^{2}\,\frac{\pi w^{2}}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:** $$ \iint \vert E \vert^{4}\, dA = E_0^{4} \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4r^{2}/w^{2}} r\, dr\, d\theta = E_0^{4}\,\frac{\pi w^{2}}{4} $$ **Step 5 — Combine:** $$ A_{eff} = \frac{(E_0^{2}\pi w^{2}/2)^{2}}{E_0^{4}\pi w^{2}/4} = \frac{\pi w^{2}/4}{1/4} = \pi w^{2} $$ **Result:** For a Gaussian mode, the effective area equals $\pi w^{2}$. For small-core PCFs (w → 1 µm), A_eff ≈ 3 µm², boosting the nonlinear coefficient γ by orders of magnitude — useful for supercontinuum sources. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How PCF Parameters Affect Performance | PCF Parameter ↑ | Mode Area | Dispersion Slope | Nonlinearity γ | Birefringence | Bandwidth (PBG fiber) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **d/Λ ↑** | ↓ (more light in air/glass boundary) | Strongly modified | ↑ | ↑ | Narrows | | **Λ ↑ (fixed d/Λ)** | ↑ (linear scaling) | Shifts ZDW | ↓ | ↓ | Shifts to longer λ | | **Number of rings ↑** | Negligible change | Negligible | Negligible | Negligible | Loss ↓ (exponentially) | | **Hollow core (PBG)** | ~50–100× larger | Flat-tailored | **Greatly ↓** | Low | Narrow but defined | > [!WARNING] > **Fabrication Reality Check:** The pitch Λ must be uniform to within ~50 nm across the entire fiber length to preserve photonic bandgap uniformity. Commercial PCFs (NKT Photonics, OFS) achieve this by stack-and-draw or 3D-printing preform methods. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Two PCF Guiding Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Optical Source
Wavelength lambda"] --> B{"PCF Design Type"} B --> C["Solid Core PCF
Core = Pure Silica"] B --> D["Hollow Core PCF
Core = Air Channel"] C --> E["Cladding: Triangular Lattice
of Air Holes d, pitch Lambda"] D --> F["Cladding: Photonic Crystal
Period a = Lambda"] E --> G["Effective Index of Cladding
n_cl_eff lower than core"] F --> H["Photonic Bandgap Effect
Forbids Transverse Modes"] G --> I["Modified TIR Guidance
Endlessly Single Mode"] H --> J["Bandgap Guidance
Light Confined in Air"] I --> K["Applications:
Supercontinuum, Sensors"] J --> L["Applications:
Low Latency, High Power"] K --> M["Optical Output
Tailored Dispersion"] L --> M ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Design Parameter Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Pitch Lambda"] --> P4["Optical Property Mapping"] P2["Hole Diameter d"] --> P4 P3["Lattice Geometry
Triangular or Honeycomb"] --> P4 P4 --> O1["Effective Index n_eff"] P4 --> O2["Dispersion D lambda"] P4 --> O3["Mode Area A_eff"] P4 --> O4["Birefringence B"] P4 --> O5["Confinement Loss"] P4 --> O6["Nonlinear Coefficient gamma"] O1 --> R1["Engineered PCF Output"] O2 --> R1 O3 --> R1 O4 --> R1 O5 --> R1 O6 --> R1 ``` ## 4.3 Schematic of a Triangular-Lattice PCF Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart TD Cross["PCF Cross-Section View"] --> Core["Core
Solid Silica or Air Defect"] Cross --> Ring1["Ring 1 Air Holes
6 Holes"] Cross --> Ring2["Ring 2 Air Holes
12 Holes"] Cross --> Ring3["Ring 3 Air Holes
18 Holes"] Cross --> Jacket["Polymer Jacket
Outer Buffer"] Core -.-> Notes["Lambda = Pitch
d = Hole Diameter"] Ring1 -.-> Notes Ring2 -.-> Notes Ring3 -.-> Notes ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block — Light-Matter Interaction in PCF ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Input Pulse
lambda, tau"] --> S2["Coupling Lens"] S2 --> S3["PCF Core Region
Length L"] S3 --> S4["Linear Effects
Dispersion, Birefringence"] S3 --> S5["Nonlinear Effects
SPM, XPM, FWM, SC"] S4 --> S6["Output Spectrum"] S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7["Application:
Telecom / Sensing / Source"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Optical Communication]** **Q: Differentiate between a conventional step-index fiber and a Photonic Crystal Fiber (PCF).** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Conventional Step-Index Fiber | Photonic Crystal Fiber | |---|---|---| | Refractive index profile | Discrete step (dopant-induced Δn ≈ 0.005) | Effective step from holey cladding (Δn can exceed 0.4) | | Cladding | Homogeneous doped silica | Periodic array of air holes in silica | | Guiding mechanism | Total Internal Reflection | Modified TIR or Photonic Bandgap | | Design flexibility | Limited to dopant chemistry | Tunable via Λ and d | | Single-mode range | Limited to one wavelength band | Endlessly single-mode (d/Λ < 0.43) | **(1 mark each for index difference, cladding structure, guiding mechanism; +1 mark for stating a unique PCF property such as endless single-mode behavior.)** --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Optical Communication]** **Q: Define the parameter "endlessly single-mode" operation in a solid-core PCF. State the condition under which it occurs.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Endlessly single-mode operation means that the PCF supports only the fundamental LP01 mode across the **entire transparency window of silica** (≈ 400 nm to 1700 nm), irrespective of wavelength. The classical condition is: $$ \frac{d}{\Lambda} < 0.43 $$ Below this threshold, the V-number of the PCF asymptotically approaches a constant less than 2.405 as wavelength decreases, preventing higher-order modes from being supported. This is **not possible** in conventional SMF, which becomes multimode below a certain cut-off wavelength. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the condition, 1 mark for the comparison with SMF.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1, Optical Communication]** **Q: (a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the two distinct light-guidance mechanisms employed in Photonic Crystal Fibers. Compare their merits. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a solid-core PCF with pitch Λ = 6 µm, hole diameter d = 3 µm, and silica refractive index n = 1.45, calculate the effective refractive index of the cladding and the modified V-number at λ = 1550 nm. Comment on whether the fiber is endlessly single-mode. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The two guidance mechanisms in PCFs are: 1. **Modified Total Internal Reflection (M-TIR)** — used in **solid-core PCFs**. The undoped silica core has a higher refractive index than the *effective* index of the air-hole cladding. Light is guided by TIR. The mechanism is broad-band, robust, and works for any wavelength. **(3 marks)** 2. **Photonic Bandgap (PBG) Guidance** — used in **hollow-core PCFs**. The periodic cladding possesses a photonic bandgap, meaning that certain wavelength ranges are forbidden from propagating in the cladding. The light therefore remains trapped in the air core. The mechanism is narrow-band but offers ultra-low loss in air and exceptionally low nonlinearity. **(3 marks)** 3. **Comparison:** M-TIR PCFs are easier to fabricate, have wider bandwidth, and dominate telecom applications.
