## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
> 
> **Q1.** What is Modified Chemical Vapour Deposition (MCVD)? Mention any two advantages.

**Model Answer:**
MCVD is an **inside-tube vapour-phase deposition process** used to fabricate silica-based optical fiber preforms. Ultra-pure halide vapours (SiCl₄, GeCl₄) react with O₂ inside a rotating silica tube heated externally by an oxy-hydrogen burner. The reaction is:

$$ \text{SiCl}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{Cl}_2 $$

The SiO₂ particles deposit as soot via thermophoresis and are sintered into a clear glassy layer. The tube is later collapsed into a solid preform.

**Two advantages:**
1. Very low OH⁻ contamination → low fiber attenuation (≤ 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm).
2. Excellent control of refractive index profile → suitable for SMF, DSF, and EDF preforms.

> **Mark distribution:** Definition (1.5 M) + Reaction (0.5 M) + Advantages (1 M) = **3 M**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> 
> **Q2.** Differentiate between fusion splice and mechanical splice.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fusion Splice | Mechanical Splice |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| Joining mechanism | Fibers melted and fused with electric arc | Fibers held mechanically with index-matching gel |
| Splice loss | 0.05–0.10 dB (very low) | 0.1–0.5 dB (higher) |
| Return loss | > 50 dB | ~ 30–40 dB |
| Permanence | Permanent | Re-usable / semi-permanent |
| Equipment | Expensive fusion splicer | Cheap, portable tool |
| Use case | Long-haul, FTTH permanent joints | Emergency restoration, lab use |

> **Mark distribution:** Tabular differentiation with ≥ 4 points = **3 M**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Modified Chemical Vapour Deposition (MCVD)** process for the fabrication of optical fiber preforms. List the chemical reactions involved and explain the role of chlorine. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Schematic (3 M):**
Draw the MCVD lathe system (see Section 4.1 of these notes): carrier-gas bubblers, mass flow controllers, gas-mixing manifold, rotating silica tube, traversing H₂/O₂ burner, soot deposition zone, sintering zone, and collapse zone.

**Step 2 — Process Steps (2 M):**
- **Step 1 — Gas delivery:** SiCl₄ + GeCl₄ (or other dopants) entrained in O₂ carrier.
- **Step 2 — Reaction & soot formation:** Inside hot zone of tube, oxidation produces SiO₂ particles.
- **Step 3 — Thermophoretic deposition:** Particles deposit on cooler downstream wall as porous soot.
- **Step 4 — Sintering:** As burner returns, zone reaches 1500 °C, soot vitrifies to clear glass.
- **Step 5 — Layer-by-layer build-up:** 50–200 passes build a thick wall.
- **Step 6 — Collapse:** Burner held stationary at ~2000 °C; surface tension collapses tube into solid preform.

**Step 3 — Chemical Reactions (1 M):**
$$ \text{SiCl}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{Cl}_2 $$
$$ \text{GeCl}_4 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{GeO}_2 + 2\text{Cl}_2 $$

**Step 4 — Role of Chlorine (1 M):**
Chlorine by-product is not a waste; it actively **etches metal-ion impurities** (Fe, Cu, OH⁻) from the deposited soot by converting them to volatile chlorides that exit with the exhaust. This **gettering effect** yields the ultra-low loss (< 0.2 dB/km) that makes MCVD preforms telecom-grade.

---

**(b)** Explain the construction of a **loose-tube optical fiber cable** with a neat diagram. Discuss why water-blocking gel and strength members are essential. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (3 M):**
(See Section 4.2 architecture diagram.) State the components:
1. Optical fibers (250 µm primary coated) inside PBT loose tubes.
2. 6–12 fibers per tube, filled with thixotropic gel.
3. 6 buffer tubes helically stranded around FRP central strength member.
4. Water-swellable yarn/tape wrap.
5. Optional corrugated steel armour.
6. Black PE outer jacket (UV stabilised).

**Step 2 — Function of Water-Blocking Gel (2 M):**
- Gel fills all voids, preventing longitudinal water migration.
- Even if jacket is breached, gel blocks water from reaching fibers.
- Lubricates fibers so they can move freely (strain relief) when cable is bent or temperature-cycled.
- Critical for **aerial, duct, and direct-buried** installations.

**Step 3 — Function of Strength Members (2 M):**
- FRP central rod + aramid yarns (Kevlar) carry the tensile load during installation pulling.
- They limit fiber strain to ≤ **0.3 %** (proof strain), preventing micro-bending and fracture.
- They also resist crush loads and provide dimensional stability across temperature variations.

> **Mark split:** Construction diagram (3 M) + gel function (2 M) + strength member function (2 M) = **7 M**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With relevant equations, explain the various **splicing losses** that occur in single-mode fiber fusion splicing. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (0.5 M):**
Splice loss is the optical power loss when two fibers are joined. It is categorised into **intrinsic** (fiber-parameter) and **extrinsic** (mechanical-misalignment) losses.

**Step 2 — Lateral Offset Loss (1.5 M):**
The dominant loss. For Gaussian field approximation with MFR $\omega$:
$$ L_d \approx 4.34 \left( \dfrac{d}{\omega} \right)^2 \;\text{dB} $$
A 1 µm offset at 1550 nm (ω = 5 µm) gives **0.17 dB**.

**Step 3 — Angular Tilt Loss (1.5 M):**
$$ L_\theta \approx 4.34 \left( \dfrac{\pi n_2 \omega \theta}{\lambda} \right)^2 \;\text{dB} $$
A 1° tilt is unacceptable; modern splicers achieve < 0.5° (≈ 0.01 rad).

**Step 4 — End-Face Gap Loss (1 M):**
$$ L_s \approx -10 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{1}{1 + (\lambda s / 2\pi n_2 \omega^2)^2} \right) \;\text{dB} $$
A small gap (1–5 µm) is acceptable and even used in mechanical splices with index-matching gel.

**Step 5 — Intrinsic Losses (1.5 M):**
- **Core-diameter mismatch** $L_a = -10 \log_{10}[2a_2/(a_1+a_2)]^2$
- **NA mismatch** $L_{\text{NA}} = -10 \log_{10}(\text{NA}_1/\text{NA}_2)^2$
- **Refractive-index profile mismatch** $L_\alpha$ for graded-index fibers.

**Step 6 — Mitigation (1 M):**
Modern splicers use **PAS (Profile Alignment System)** that aligns the cores by imaging the fiber tips with cameras. Active alignment reduces $L_d$ to < 0.02 dB.

---

**(b)** Describe the construction of a **single-fiber simplex cable** and a **multi-fiber ribbon cable** with neat diagrams. Compare their typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Simplex Cable (2 M):**
- Single 250 µm coated fiber, surrounded by 900 µm tight buffer.
- Aramid (Kevlar) strength yarn around buffer.
- Outer jacket: 2–3 mm diameter, LSZH or PVC.
- Used in patch cords, equipment pigtails, FTTH drop.

**Step 2 — Ribbon Cable (2 M):**
(See Section 4.4.) Twelve 250 µm fibers are aligned side-by-side and embedded in a UV-cure acrylate matrix to form a 12-fiber ribbon. Twelve such ribbons (144 fibers) are stacked, wrapped in water-blocking tape, and jacketed. Outer diameter typically 12–20 mm.

**Step 3 — Comparison (2 M):**

| Feature | Simplex | Ribbon |
|---------|---------|--------|
| Fiber count | 1 | 12–864 |
| Splice technique | Single fusion | **Mass fusion** (12 at once) |
| Installation speed | Slow | Fast |
| Cost per fiber | High | Low |
| Use | Pigtails, patch cords | Backbone, FTTH, data centres |

**Step 4 — Application Summary (1 M):**
Simplex cables dominate **subscriber drop** and patch-cord markets. Ribbon cables dominate **high-density feeder and backbone** installations where hundreds of splices must be made quickly.

> **Mark split:** Simplex (2 M) + Ribbon (2 M) + Comparison table (2 M) + Applications (1 M) = **7 M**

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing MCVD with OVD:** Students frequently mix up "inside" vs "outside" deposition. Always state: *MCVD is an INSIDE process; OVD is an OUTSIDE process.*
> 2. **Forgetting the role of chlorine:** Many students write the reaction SiCl₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2Cl₂ but never explain *why Cl₂ is produced* — it is the **purification agent** (gettering). Examiners specifically look for this.
> 3. **Writing "core mismatch" for "NA mismatch":** These are two different intrinsic losses. Do not lump them.
> 4. **Confusing "splice loss" with "splice return loss":** Loss = forward power lost; return loss = backward-reflected power. They are not the same.
> 5. **Missing the strain-relief mechanism:** In cable design, students often forget that fibers are *loose* in the tube. This loose geometry is what *prevents* strain from being transmitted to the glass.
> 6. **Using the wrong units in splice loss formulas:** $d$ and $\omega$ must be in the **same** unit (typically µm). Mixing metres with micrometres is a common 1-mark loss.

---

> ✅ **Typical loss benchmarks:** Fusion ≤ 0.1 dB; Mechanical ≤ 0.5 dB.
> ✅ **Return loss** of fusion splice > 50 dB; mechanical splice ~ 30–40 dB.
> ✅ **Preform process comparison:** MCVD (inside), OVD (outside), VAD (axial), PCVD (plasma).
> ✅ **Proof strain of fiber = 0.3 % (short-term)**, 0.1 % (long-term operating).
> ✅ **MFR (ω)** at 1550 nm for SMF-28 is ~ 5 µm → 1 µm offset gives 0.17 dB loss.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connectors and Couplers; Optical Fiber Measurements: Attenuation and Dispersion Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connectors and Couplers; Optical Fiber Measurements: Attenuation and Dispersion Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optical Fiber Connectors

### Formal Definition
An **optical fiber connector** is a demountable (removable) mechanical device used to provide a temporary joint between two optical fibers, or between an optical fiber and an active/passive device (source, detector, coupler). It must align the fiber cores (typically 8–10 µm for single-mode, 50 µm for multimode) with sub-micrometer precision to allow light to pass with minimal loss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024):** Students must know the construction of standard connectors (FC, SC, ST, LC), connector losses (typically **0.1 to 0.5 dB**), return loss (>40 dB for PC connectors, >60 dB for APC), and the difference between physical contact (PC) and angled physical contact (APC) polishing.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a fiber connector like a **precision electrical plug-and-socket**, but instead of metal pins sliding into holes, two ultra-fine glass strands (thinner than a human hair) must be aligned within a fraction of a micrometer. A single speck of dust (≈9 µm) can block the light completely — like placing a boulder in the eye of a needle!

---

## 1.2 Fiber Splices

A **splice** is a **permanent** or semi-permanent joint between two optical fibers.

| Type | Method | Typical Loss |
|------|--------|--------------|
| **Fusion Splice** | Electric arc melts fibers together | **0.05–0.1 dB** |
| **Mechanical Splice** | V-groove or tube alignment + index matching gel | **0.1–0.5 dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> A **fusion splicer** is the gold standard in telecom — it uses an electric arc (≈2000°C) to fuse two prepared fiber ends. The alignment is done via **core alignment** (PAS - Profile Alignment System) or **clad alignment**.

---

## 1.3 Optical Fiber Couplers

### Formal Definition
A **passive optical coupler** is a device that distributes an optical signal from one or more input fibers to one or more output fibers, with a predefined splitting ratio. It contains no electronic components and is powered solely by the optical signal itself.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **water pipeline** that splits one main pipe into several smaller ones. The optical coupler is the **Y-junction or T-junction for light** — it can either **split** one beam into many (broadcasting, like a town crier), or **combine** many beams into one (multiplexing, like mixing different colored paints).

### Common Coupler Types
- **T-Coupler (2×2):** Splits 50:50 or in any custom ratio.
- **Star Coupler (N×N):** Distributes one input equally to N outputs.
- **Wavelength Division Multiplexer (WDM):** Combines/separates different wavelengths (e.g., 1310 nm + 1550 nm).
- **Fused Biconical Taper (FBT) Coupler:** Two fibers twisted, heated, and stretched.

---

## 1.4 Optical Fiber Measurements — Overview

### Attenuation Measurements
Attenuation (α) is the loss of optical power as light travels through a fiber, measured in **dB/km**. Three primary methods:
1. **Cut-Back Method** (most accurate, destructive)
2. **Insertion Loss Method** (non-destructive)
3. **Optical Time Domain Reflectometry (OTDR)** (non-destructive, in-service)

### Dispersion Measurements
Dispersion causes **pulse broadening**, limiting the bit rate and bandwidth-distance product. Two measurement domains:
1. **Time-Domain:** Measure pulse width before and after the fiber.
2. **Frequency-Domain:** Measure the baseband frequency response (BWR — bandwidth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Cut-back method (numerical problems expected), OTDR concept (backscattering equation), pulse broadening (Δτ = sqrt(τ_out² − τ_in²)), and chromatic dispersion formulas are **guaranteed 14-mark questions**.

---

## 1.5 Why Are These Topics Critical?
| Parameter | Engineering Impact |
|-----------|-------------------|
| Connector Loss | Limits link budget — every dB counts in long-haul |
| Coupler Insertion Loss | Affects PON (Passive Optical Network) efficiency |
| Attenuation α | Determines repeater spacing (typical **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm) |
| Dispersion | Limits bit rate: **B × L ≤ 1/(2·Δτ)** for NRZ coding |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Fiber Connectors — Working Principle

A connector consists of three critical subsystems:

1. **Ferrule (Cylindrical Capillary):** Holds the fiber precisely. Usually ceramic (zirconia) for SM, stainless steel/phosphor bronze for MM. Outer diameter **2.5 mm** (FC, SC, ST) or **1.25 mm** (LC).
2. **Alignment Sleeve (Coupling Sleeve):** Ensures two ferrules meet with their fibers concentric.
3. **Housing & Locking Mechanism:** Screw (FC), bayonet (ST), push-pull (SC, LC).

### Why Alignment Tolerances Are So Tight
For SM fiber at 1550 nm, mode field diameter (MFD) ≈ 10 µm. Lateral offset of just **1 µm** can cause **≈ 0.5 dB** loss. The Fraunhofer diffraction-based coupling efficiency is:

$$
\eta_{lateral} \approx \exp\left[-\left(\frac{d}{w_0}\right)^2\right]
$$

where $d$ is the lateral offset and $w_0$ is the mode field radius.

> [!NOTE]
> **Polishing Types:**
> - **PC (Physical Contact):** Ferrule end is convex, fibers touch → eliminates air gap. RL > 40 dB.
> - **APC (Angled Physical Contact):** End face polished at **8°** angle → back-reflection directed away. RL > 60 dB. Used in CATV and high-bit-rate systems.

---

## 2.2 Types of Connectors — Detailed Classification

### (a) FC Connector (Ferrule Connector)
- **Locking:** Screw-type (like an SMA RF connector).
- **Applications:** Test equipment, datacom, telecom (older installations).
- **Loss:** 0.2–0.5 dB.

### (b) SC Connector (Subscriber Connector / Square Connector)
- **Locking:** Push-pull latching.
- **Applications:** Gigabit Ethernet, FTTH, widely used in patch panels.
- **Loss:** 0.1–0.3 dB.

### (c) ST Connector (Straight Tip)
- **Locking:** Bayonet (twist-lock).
- **Applications:** LANs, multimode systems, industrial.
- **Loss:** 0.25–0.5 dB.

### (d) LC Connector (Lucent Connector / Little Connector)
- **Locking:** RJ-45 style latch.
- **Size:** Half the footprint of SC (1.25 mm ferrule).
- **Applications:** High-density patch panels, SFP modules, data centers.
- **Loss:** 0.1–0.2 dB.

### (e) MT-RJ (Mechanical Transfer Registered Jack)
- **Form:** Duplex (two fibers in one connector).
- **Applications:** Fast Ethernet, Token Ring.

---

## 2.3 Fiber Splices — Detailed Theory

### Fusion Splicing Steps
1. **Stripping:** Remove polymer coating (~3 cm).
2. **Cleaving:** Cut fiber with a precision cleaver (perpendicularity < 0.5°).
3. **Cleaning:** Wipe with isopropyl alcohol.
4. **Alignment:** PAS (core alignment) using cameras on both X and Y axes.
5. **Fusing:** Electric arc (≈2 kV) for ~1 second.
6. **Protection:** Re-coat with heat-shrink sleeve.

### Mechanical Splicing Components
- **V-Groove** or precision capillary tube
- **Index matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46 to match silica)
- **Crimp** or UV-cured adhesive

---

## 2.4 Optical Couplers — Operational Theory

### 2.4.1 The 2×2 Directional Coupler

A 2×2 coupler has two input ports (P1, P2) and two output ports (P3, P4).

**Key Performance Parameters:**

| Parameter | Definition | Formula |
|-----------|------------|---------|
| **Insertion Loss (IL)** | Loss from one input to its through port | $IL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_3}{P_1}\right)$ dB |
| **Excess Loss (EL)** | Total power lost in the device | $EL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_3+P_4}{P_1}\right)$ dB |
| **Coupling Ratio (CR)** | Fraction of power coupled | $CR = \frac{P_4}{P_3+P_4} \times 100\%$ |
| **Return Loss (RL)** | Reflection back to input | $RL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{1,refl}}{P_1}\right)$ dB |
| **Directivity (D)** | Isolation between input ports | $D = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ dB (when P3, P4 are matched) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A 3 dB coupler has CR = 50%, meaning each output gets **half the power** (minus insertion loss).

### 2.4.2 Fused Biconical Taper (FBT) Coupler Working Principle

Two fibers are twisted together, heated with a torch or laser, and pulled (stretched) while being monitored. As the cross-section shrinks, the evanescent field of one fiber couples into the other via the **coupled-mode theory**.

### 2.4.3 Star Coupler — N×N
Distributes each input equally to all N outputs.

$$
P_{out} = \frac{P_{in}}{N} \times 10^{-IL/10}
$$

### 2.4.4 WDM Coupler
Separates (or combines) two wavelengths:
- **1310/1550 nm WDM** — most common in SDH/SONET
- **CWDM** (Coarse WDM): 20 nm spacing, 18 channels
- **DWDM** (Dense WDM): 0.4–0.8 nm spacing, 40–160 channels

---

## 2.5 Attenuation Measurement — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Cut-Back Method (Reference Method)
- **Step 1:** Measure output power $P_1$ from a long fiber (length $L_1$).
- **Step 2:** Cut the fiber to a short length $L_2$ (typically 2 m) without disturbing input.
- **Step 3:** Measure output power $P_2$ from the short fiber.
- **Step 4:** Calculate attenuation coefficient.

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{L_1 - L_2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Accuracy:** ±0.1 dB/km. **Drawback:** Destroys the fiber — not suitable for installed cables.

### 2.5.2 Insertion Loss Method
A reference power $P_{ref}$ is recorded through a short launch cable, then the test fiber is inserted and $P_{out}$ is measured. Total attenuation = loss of cable only.

$$
\alpha L = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{ref}}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

### 2.5.3 OTDR Principle
A short, high-power optical pulse is launched into the fiber. The OTDR analyzes:
- **Rayleigh Backscatter** (continuous decaying signal proportional to α)
- **Fresnel Reflections** at splices, connectors, breaks (sharp spikes)

The backscattered power at distance $z$:

$$
P_{BS}(z) = \frac{1}{2}P_0 \cdot S \cdot \alpha_s \cdot v_g \cdot \tau \cdot e^{-2\alpha z}
$$

where $S$ = capture fraction (≈ 0.001 for SM), $\alpha_s$ = scattering coefficient, $v_g$ = group velocity, $\tau$ = pulse width.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor of **2** in the exponent accounts for the **round-trip** of light (forward + backward). This is the most-missed point in KTU exams!

---

## 2.6 Dispersion Measurement — Detailed Theory

### 2.6.1 Pulse Broadening (Time Domain)
A narrow optical pulse is injected at the input; the output pulse is wider. The fiber-induced broadening:

$$
\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}
$$

where $\tau_{in}$ and $\tau_{out}$ are the input and output pulse widths (FWHM).

Total dispersion combines three components:

$$
\Delta\tau_{total} = \sqrt{\Delta\tau_{intermodal}^2 + \Delta\tau_{intramodal}^2 + \Delta\tau_{PMD}^2}
$$

For SM fibers, intermodal = 0, so:

$$
\Delta\tau = \Delta\tau_{chromatic} = \Delta\tau_M + \Delta\tau_W
$$

### 2.6.2 Chromatic Dispersion Components
$$
D(\lambda) = D_M(\lambda) + D_W(\lambda)
$$

where the material dispersion is:

$$
D_M(\lambda) = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\cdot\frac{d^2 n(\lambda)}{d\lambda^2}
$$

and waveguide dispersion $D_W$ is typically small but dominant near zero-dispersion wavelength.

### 2.6.3 Frequency Domain — Baseband Response
The fiber acts as a low-pass filter. The bandwidth $B$ (3-dB optical) relates to total dispersion:

$$
B \cdot L \leq \frac{1}{2 \Delta\tau_{FWHM}}
$$

For Gaussian pulses, the transfer function:

$$
H(f) = \exp\left[-\left(\frac{f}{f_3}\right)^2 \ln 2\right], \quad f_3 = \frac{0.44}{\Delta\tau_{FWHM}}
$$

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Sheet — Save This for Exam Day!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | Attenuation coefficient | dB/km |
| 2 | $IL = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Insertion Loss | dB |
| 3 | $RL = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{refl}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Return Loss | dB |
| 4 | $EL = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\sum P_{out}}{\sum P_{in}}\right)$ | Excess Loss (coupler) | dB |
| 5 | $CR = \dfrac{P_{coupled}}{\sum P_{out}} \times 100$ | Coupling Ratio | % |
| 6 | $D = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{iso}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Directivity | dB |
| 7 | $P_{BS}(z) = \dfrac{1}{2}P_0 S \alpha_s v_g \tau \, e^{-2\alpha z}$ | OTDR Backscatter | W |
| 8 | $\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}$ | Pulse Broadening | ps |
| 9 | $D_M = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c}\dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}$ | Material Dispersion | ps/(km·nm) |
| 10 | $B_{3dB} \cdot \Delta\tau_{FWHM} = 0.44$ | Gaussian B-T Product | Hz·s |
| 11 | $B \cdot L = \dfrac{1}{2\Delta\tau}$ | Bandwidth-Distance | Hz·m |
| 12 | $\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{inter}^2 + \sigma_{intra}^2$ | Combined dispersion | ps² |

**Standard Constants to Memorize:**

- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $n_{SiO_2} \approx 1.46$
- Zero-dispersion wavelength of standard SMF: **λ₀ ≈ 1310 nm**
- Minimum attenuation wavelength: **1550 nm (≈ 0.2 dB/km)**
- Typical PMD of modern fiber: **< 0.1 ps/√km**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Attenuation Coefficient from Cut-Back Method

**Given:** $P_1$ = power from long fiber of length $L_1$, $P_2$ = power from short cut fiber of length $L_2$.

For any fiber of length $L$:

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}
$$

For the long fiber:

$$
P_1 = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_1 / 10}
$$

For the short (cut-back) fiber — the input power $P_{in}$ is the **same** because we did not move the launch conditions:

$$
P_2 = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_2 / 10}
$$

**Step 1:** Divide the two equations to eliminate the unknown $P_{in}$:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_2 / 10}}{P_{in} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_1 / 10}}
$$

**Step 2:** The $P_{in}$ terms cancel out:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = 10^{-\alpha L_2 / 10 + \alpha L_1 / 10}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine the exponents (note: $-L_2 + L_1 = L_1 - L_2$):

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = 10^{\alpha (L_1 - L_2) / 10}
$$

**Step 4:** Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides:

$$
\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\alpha (L_1 - L_2)}{10}
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\alpha$:

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \frac{10}{L_1 - L_2} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example
A 10 km fiber delivers $P_1 = 100 \,\mu W$. After cutting to 2 m, the power is $P_2 = 800 \,\mu W$. Find α.

**Solution:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{10 - 0.002}\log_{10}\left(\frac{800}{100}\right)
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{9.998}\log_{10}(8)
$$

$$
\alpha = 1.0002 \times 0.9031 = 0.9033 \;\; \text{dB/km}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: OTDR Backscatter Equation

**Physical setup:** An OTDR launches a pulse of peak power $P_0$ and width $\tau$ into a fiber of attenuation $\alpha$ and group velocity $v_g$.

**Step 1:** The forward-propagating power at distance $z$:

$$
P_f(z) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}
$$

**Step 2:** A small slice of fiber $dz$ at distance $z$ scatters a fraction of light back toward the source. The backscattered power from $dz$ is:

$$
dP_{BS} = P_f(z) \cdot \alpha_s \, dz \cdot S
$$

where:
- $\alpha_s$ = Rayleigh scattering coefficient (≈ 0.7 to 1.5 dB/km at 1550 nm)
- $S$ = recapture coefficient (fraction of scattered light captured back into the fiber) ≈ $0.001$ for SM fiber
- The factor $S$ depends on the numerical aperture and core size: $S \approx (NA/n_1)^2 / 4$

**Step 3:** This backscattered light now travels **backward**, suffering the same attenuation over distance $z$:

$$
dP_{BS,\text{at source}} = dP_{BS} \cdot e^{-\alpha z} = P_0 \alpha_s S \, e^{-2\alpha z} \, dz
$$

**Step 4:** The OTDR does not measure an infinitely thin slice — it integrates over the pulse length $L_p = v_g \tau / 2$ (the half-pulse length, because the pulse is "live" during the round-trip):

$$
P_{BS}(z) = P_0 \alpha_s S \, e^{-2\alpha z} \int_0^{L_p} dz'
$$

**Step 5:** Evaluating the integral $\int_0^{L_p} dz' = L_p = v_g \tau / 2$:

$$
\boxed{P_{BS}(z) = \frac{1}{2}\,P_0 \alpha_s S\, v_g\, \tau\, e^{-2\alpha z}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **factor of 2** in the exponent $e^{-2\alpha z}$ is the **most-frequently missed** point in KTU valuations! Mark examiners will deduct a full mark if a student writes $e^{-\alpha z}$ instead of $e^{-2\alpha z}$.

**Step 6 (Slope interpretation):** Taking $\log_{10}$ of both sides and plotting vs $z$:

$$
10\log_{10} P_{BS}(z) = \text{const} - 20\alpha z \cdot \log_{10}(e) = \text{const} - 8.686\,\alpha\,z
$$

The slope of the OTDR trace is **−5 dB per dB of one-way loss** (or roughly 2× the one-way attenuation per unit distance).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pulse Broadening from Gaussian Pulses

**Given:** Input pulse has Gaussian shape with FWHM $\tau_{in}$. After the fiber, the output pulse is also Gaussian with FWHM $\tau_{out}$ (but wider). The fiber is a linear, time-invariant system that convolves the input with its impulse response.

**Step 1:** A Gaussian pulse in time has the form:

$$
E(t) = E_0 \exp\left[-\frac{4\ln 2 \cdot t^2}{\tau^2}\right]
$$

where $\tau$ is the FWHM. In the frequency domain, a Gaussian remains Gaussian (Fourier transform of a Gaussian is a Gaussian):

$$
\mathcal{F}\{E(t)\} = E_0 \frac{\tau}{2}\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{\ln 2}}\exp\left[-\frac{\pi^2 \tau^2 f^2}{4\ln 2}\right]
$$

**Step 2:** A linear fiber system convolves in time = multiplies in frequency. If the fiber's impulse response has variance $\sigma_f^2$, the output variance adds:

$$
\sigma_{out}^2 = \sigma_{in}^2 + \sigma_f^2
$$

**Step 3:** For a Gaussian, variance relates to FWHM by $\tau = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma$. Therefore:

$$
\left(\frac{\tau_{out}}{2\sqrt{2\ln 2}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\tau_{in}}{2\sqrt{2\ln 2}}\right)^2 + \sigma_f^2
$$

The factor $2\sqrt{2\ln 2}$ cancels:

$$
\tau_{out}^2 = \tau_{in}^2 + (2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma_f)^2
$$

**Step 4:** Defining the **fiber-induced broadening** $\Delta\tau = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2}\,\sigma_f$:

$$
\tau_{out}^2 = \tau_{in}^2 + \Delta\tau^2
$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the fiber-induced broadening:

$$
\boxed{\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}}
$$

### Numerical Example
A fiber is tested with a pulse source. Input FWHM is 50 ps. Output FWHM is 350 ps. Find the fiber dispersion.

**Solution:**

$$
\Delta\tau = \sqrt{350^2 - 50^2} = \sqrt{122500 - 2500} = \sqrt{120000} = 346.4 \;\; \text{ps}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Coupler Excess Loss and Coupling Ratio

**Setup:** A 2×2 coupler. Light of power $P_1$ enters port 1, ports 3 and 4 are outputs, port 2 is the isolated port.

**Step 1:** Total input power: $P_{in} = P_1$ (assuming no reflection back to source).

**Step 2:** Total output power: $P_{out,total} = P_3 + P_4$.

**Step 3:** By conservation, the power lost in the device (excess loss, in linear units):

$$
L_{excess} = \frac{P_3 + P_4}{P_1}
$$

**Step 4:** Converting to dB:

$$
EL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_3 + P_4}{P_1}\right) \;\; \text{dB}
$$

**Step 5:** The **coupling ratio** is the fraction of the *output* power that goes to the coupled port:

$$
CR = \frac{P_4}{P_3 + P_4} \times 100\%
$$

### Numerical Example
A 2×2 coupler has $P_3 = 0.45$ mW and $P_4 = 0.45$ mW with $P_1 = 1$ mW input.

**Solution:**

$$
EL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{0.45 + 0.45}{1.0}\right) = -10\log_{10}(0.9) = 0.458 \;\; \text{dB}
$$

$$
CR = \frac{0.45}{0.90} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

This is a **3 dB coupler** (50:50 splitter) with 0.46 dB excess loss.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Material Dispersion from Refractive Index

**Starting point:** Group delay per unit length of fiber:

$$
\tau_g = \frac{1}{v_g} = \frac{1}{c}\frac{d\beta}{dk} = \frac{N_g}{c}
$$

where $N_g$ is the group index:

$$
N_g = n - \lambda \frac{dn}{d\lambda}
$$

**Step 1:** The pulse broadening per unit length per unit wavelength (material dispersion parameter):

$$
D_M = \frac{1}{c}\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda}
$$

**Step 2:** Differentiating $N_g$:

$$
\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} = \frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \frac{dn}{d\lambda} - \lambda\frac{d^2n}{d\lambda^2}
$$

$$
\frac{dN_g}{d\lambda} = -\lambda\frac{d^2n}{d\lambda^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{D_M = -\frac{\lambda}{c}\cdot\frac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2}}
$$

Units: $D_M$ is in s/(m²) which is converted to ps/(km·nm) for practical use.

### Numerical Example
At λ = 1550 nm, for silica, $\dfrac{d^2 n}{d\lambda^2} \approx 0.026 \,\mu m^{-2}$. Find $D_M$.

**Solution:**

$$
D_M = -\frac{1550 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^8} \times \left(0.026 \times 10^{12}\right) = -\frac{1550 \times 0.026 \times 10^3}{3}
$$

$$
D_M = -\frac{40.3}{3} \times 10^{-3} = -13.4 \;\; \text{ps/(km·nm)}
$$

This is consistent with the standard value of ≈ −17 ps/(km·nm) for silica at 1550 nm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Fiber Connector

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Optical Fiber Input] --> B[Stripped Fiber Core 9um SM]
    B --> C[Ferrule Ceramic Zirconia 2.5mm]
    C --> D[Polished End Face PC or APC 8 degrees]
    D --> E[Alignment Sleeve Split or Cylindrical]
    E --> F[Second Ferrule with Fiber 2]
    F --> G[Coupling Nut Locking Mechanism]
    G --> H[Output to Receiver or Next Device]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#ffe1f5
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#f5e1ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#e1e1ff
    style H fill:#fff4e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **functional flow** showing the signal path through an SC-type fiber connector. Each block represents a critical subsystem; misalignment at any block causes insertion loss.

---

## 4.2 Classification of Optical Couplers

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Optical Couplers Passive] --> B[Tree Couplers]
    A --> C[Star Couplers]
    A --> D[Wavelength Selective]
    A --> E[Active Couplers]
    
    B --> B1[T Coupler 2x2]
    B --> B2[Y Coupler]
    B --> B3[Multilevel Tree Nx1]
    
    C --> C1[Reflective Star]
    C --> C2[Transmissive Star NxN]
    
    D --> D1[WDM 1310 1550 nm]
    D --> D2[CWDM 18 channels]
    D --> D3[DWDM 40 to 160 channels]
    D --> D4[Optical Add Drop Mux OADM]
    
    E --> E1[With Optical Amplifier]
    E --> E2[With Modulator]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4e1
    style C fill:#e1ffe1
    style D fill:#ffe1f5
    style E fill:#f5e1ff
```

    B --> C[Launch Conditioning]

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Insertion Loss and Return Loss. Give typical values for a good SC connector. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Insertion Loss (IL):** The optical power lost when a connector is inserted into a transmission path, expressed in dB.
$$
IL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)
$$
- **Return Loss (RL):** The ratio of incident light power reflected back at the connector interface to the incident power.
$$
RL = -10\log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{refl}}{P_{in}}\right)
$$
- **Typical values for an SC connector:**
  - Insertion Loss: **0.1 – 0.3 dB** (SM, 1550 nm)
  - Return Loss: **≥ 40 dB** (PC), **≥ 60 dB** (APC)

---

### Question 2: What is OTDR? Mention any three applications. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**OTDR (Optical Time Domain Reflectometer):** An instrument that characterizes an optical fiber by launching optical pulses and analyzing the backscattered/reflected light as a function of distance. It provides a one-dimensional "radar-like" trace of the fiber under test.

**Three Applications:**
1. **Attenuation measurement** along the length of the fiber (slope of the trace).
2. **Splice and connector loss** measurement (step discontinuities in the trace).
3. **Fault location** — identifying breaks, bends, and their distance from the launch end.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of different types of optical fiber connectors with neat diagrams. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Common Optical Connector Types:**

A fiber connector consists of a **ferrule** (precision ceramic cylinder, typically zirconia with 2.5 mm OD), an **alignment sleeve**, and a **locking mechanism**. The fiber is epoxied into the ferrule and the end face is polished (PC or APC).

| Type | Locking | Ferrule OD | Typical Use |
|------|---------|------------|-------------|
| **FC** | Screw (threaded) | 2.5 mm | Test equipment, datacom |
| **SC** | Push-pull | 2.5 mm | Patch panels, FTTH |
| **ST** | Bayonet (twist) | 2.5 mm | LANs, MM systems |
| **LC** | RJ-45 latch | 1.25 mm | High-density, SFP modules |
| **MT-RJ** | Duplex latch | — | Ethernet, Token Ring |

**Construction (using SC as the example):**
- A **zirconia ceramic ferrule** holds the bare fiber (125 µm OD). The fiber is epoxied in place and the end is convex-polished (PC) or 8° angle-polished (APC).
- The ferrule sits inside a **plastic housing** with a push-pull coupling mechanism.
- Two ferrules are aligned by a **split ceramic sleeve** to within 1 µm concentricity.
- APC connectors (green-colored) direct back-reflections into the cladding, achieving > 60 dB return loss.

**Working Principle:**
When two connectors are mated, the spring-loaded ferrules push their convex end faces together. The "physical contact" eliminates the air gap (≈ 1.4 vs 1.46 index mismatch), drastically reducing Fresnel reflections at the interface.

**Sources of Connector Loss:**
1. **Lateral offset** (dominant in MM fiber)
2. **Angular tilt** (misalignment)
3. **End-face separation** (gap)
4. **End-face quality** (scratches, poor polish)
5. **Core diameter mismatch**
6. **Numerical aperture (NA) mismatch**

**Typical Loss Values:**
- Multimode connector: 0.2 – 0.5 dB
- Single-mode connector: 0.1 – 0.3 dB

#### (b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the cut-back method for measurement of attenuation in optical fibers. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** The cut-back method compares the output power from a long fiber with the output power from a short length of the **same fiber** (with identical launch conditions), thereby eliminating the unknown launch power.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Source] → [Mode Stripper] → [Launch Fiber] → [Test Fiber (L₁)] → [Power Meter]
                                       ↓
                              [Cut to L₂ = 2 m, re-measure]
```

**Steps:**
1. **Set up the source:** Use a stabilized laser or LED at the wavelength of interest (e.g., 1550 nm). Pass through a **mode stripper** to remove cladding modes.
2. **Measure $P_1$:** Launch light into the long test fiber of length $L_1$ (e.g., 10 km). Record the output power $P_1$ at the far end.
3. **Cut-back:** Without disturbing the input launch conditions, cut the fiber to a short reference length $L_2$ (typically 1–2 m, still longer than the mode-coupling length).
4. **Measure $P_2$:** Record the output power $P_2$ from the short fiber.
5. **Compute attenuation:**
$$
\alpha = \frac{10}{L_1 - L_2} \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}
$$

**Sources of Error:**
- Mode-stripper inefficiency (residual cladding modes).
- Cut-back length too short (still in transient mode-coupling region).
- Cut-back length too long (some fiber removed is the actual fiber under test).
- Fiber bending at the cut point (macrobend loss).

**Advantages:** Highest accuracy (±0.05 dB/km).
**Disadvantages:** Destructive — cannot be used on installed cables.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the construction and working of 2×2 and N×N star couplers used in optical fiber communication. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. 2×2 Directional Coupler:**

A 2×2 coupler has 2 input ports and 2 output ports. The most common implementation is the **Fused Biconical Taper (FBT)** coupler.

**Construction:**
- Two single-mode fibers are **twisted together** and heated with a torch (or CO₂ laser) while being pulled (stretched) under computer-controlled tension.
- As the cross-section tapers, the **evanescent field** of one fiber penetrates into the other, allowing power to transfer.
- The stretching length controls the **coupling ratio** (50:50, 90:10, etc.).

**Working Principle — Coupled-Mode Theory:**
When the coupling region has length $L$ and coupling coefficient $\kappa$, the power at the through and coupled ports is:

$$
P_3 = P_1 \cos^2(\kappa L), \quad P_4 = P_1 \sin^2(\kappa L)
$$

For a **3 dB coupler**, $\kappa L = \pi/4$, giving $P_3 = P_4 = P_1/2$.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Insertion Loss:** 0.05 – 0.5 dB
- **Excess Loss:** 0.1 – 0.5 dB
- **Directivity:** > 50 dB
- **Return Loss:** > 50 dB

**2. N×N Star Coupler:**

**Construction:**
- **Transmissive star:** A central mixing region (a planar waveguide or a short length of large-core MM fiber) is fed by $N$ input fibers and reaches $N$ output fibers. The MM region scrambles the input modes.
- **Reflective star:** Each port is folded back via a mirror, so an N-port device has 2N physical fiber ends but acts as N×N.

**Working Principle:**
- Light entering any input port is **equally divided** among all $N$ output ports.
- The power at each output:

$$
P_{out,i} = \frac{P_{in}}{N} \cdot 10^{-EL/10}
$$

where $EL$ is the excess loss (typically 1–3 dB for an 8×8 coupler).

**Applications:**
- **LANs** (e.g., Ethernet passive optical networks).
- **Bidirectional broadcast networks.**
- **Cable TV distribution.**

#### (b) Explain the pulse broadening method for the measurement of dispersion in optical fibers. A 25 km fiber exhibits an output pulse of FWHM 600 ps when the input pulse FWHM is 200 ps. Calculate the pulse broadening per km. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** A narrow optical pulse is launched into the fiber, and the output pulse is observed on a fast sampling oscilloscope. The increase in pulse width is a direct measure of the fiber's dispersion.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Pulse Generator] → [Laser Diode 1550 nm] → [Test Fiber 25 km] → [Fast Photodiode] → [Sampling Oscilloscope]
```

**Steps:**
1. **Generate a narrow pulse** (τ_in ≤ 100 ps) from a laser source at the wavelength of interest.
2. **Launch the pulse** into the test fiber under controlled launch conditions (using a mode stripper).
3. **Detect the output pulse** using a high-speed photodiode (bandwidth ≥ 20 GHz) connected to a sampling oscilloscope.
4. **Measure the FWHM** (Full Width at Half Maximum) of both input ($\tau_{in}$) and output ($\tau_{out}$) pulses.
5. **Compute the fiber-induced broadening:**

$$
\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- The pulses are approximately Gaussian.
- The fiber acts as a linear, time-invariant system.
- The dominant dispersion is chromatic (intermodal = 0 for SM fiber).

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $\tau_{in} = 200$ ps
- $\tau_{out} = 600$ ps
- $L = 25$ km

**Step 1:** Compute total fiber-induced broadening:

$$
\Delta\tau = \sqrt{600^2 - 200^2} = \sqrt{360000 - 40000} = \sqrt{320000}
$$

$$
\Delta\tau = 565.7 \;\; \text{ps}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute broadening per unit length:

$$
\Delta\tau / L = \frac{565.7}{25} = 22.63 \;\; \text{ps/km}
$$

**Result:** The fiber exhibits a dispersion of **22.63 ps/km**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Common mistakes students make:
> 1. ❌ Forgetting the **square root** in $\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}$ — using simple subtraction yields wrong answer.
> 2. ❌ Forgetting to **convert** the broadening to per-km units (final answer MUST be in ps/km).
> 3. ❌ Using **intermodal dispersion formulas** (√L dependence) for **single-mode fiber** — SM fiber has no intermodal dispersion; broadening is dominated by chromatic dispersion.
> 4. ❌ Confusing **FWHM** with **standard deviation** σ — for Gaussian pulses, $\tau_{FWHM} = 2\sqrt{2\ln 2} \cdot \sigma \approx 2.355\sigma$.
> 5. ❌ Not stating that the input pulse width is **deconvolved** by the formula, not simply subtracted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

### Connectors
- ☐ **FC** = Screw; **SC** = Push-Pull; **ST** = Bayonet; **LC** = RJ-45 Latch
- ☐ Ferrule OD: 2.5 mm (FC/SC/ST) vs 1.25 mm (LC)
- ☐ PC = physical contact (RL > 40 dB); APC = 8° angle (RL > 60 dB)
- ☐ Typical SM connector loss: **0.1 – 0.3 dB**; MM: **0.2 – 0.5 dB**
- ☐ Six sources of connector loss: lateral offset, angular tilt, gap, polish, core diameter, NA

### Splices
- ☐ Fusion: 0.05–0.1 dB; Mechanical: 0.1–0.5 dB
- ☐ PAS (Profile Alignment System) for core alignment in fusion splicers
- ☐ Index matching gel (n ≈ 1.46) used in mechanical splices

### Couplers
- ☐ 3 dB coupler = 50:50 splitter
- ☐ Excess Loss (EL) is the **total** power lost in the device
- ☐ Insertion Loss is loss from input to a specific output port
- ☐ Coupling Ratio is the **fraction of output** at the coupled port
- ☐ FBT = Fused Biconical Taper (most common manufacturing technique)
- ☐ Star coupler (N×N): $P_{out} = P_{in}/N$ ideally

### Attenuation Measurements
- ☐ **Cut-back method** formula: $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L_1 - L_2}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$
- ☐ Cut-back is **destructive** but most accurate (±0.05 dB/km)
- ☐ OTDR uses **Rayleigh backscatter** + Fresnel reflections
- ☐ **MUST remember** the factor of **2** in $e^{-2\alpha z}$ for OTDR (round-trip)
- ☐ OTDR trace slope = 2× one-way attenuation (in dB/km)

### Dispersion Measurements
- ☐ Time-domain: $\Delta\tau = \sqrt{\tau_{out}^2 - \tau_{in}^2}$ (Gaussian assumption)
- ☐ Material dispersion: $D_M = -\dfrac{\lambda}{c}\dfrac{d^2n}{d\lambda^2}$
- ☐ Zero-dispersion wavelength of standard SMF: **1310 nm**
- ☐ B-Δτ product (Gaussian): $B \cdot \Delta\tau = 0.44$
- ☐ Total broadening: $\Delta\tau_{total} = \sqrt{\Delta\tau_{inter}^2 + \Delta\tau_{intra}^2 + \Delta\tau_{PMD}^2}$
- ☐ Modern fibers: PMD < 0.1 ps/√km

### Standard Numerical Values to Remember
- ☐ $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- ☐ $n_{SiO_2} \approx 1.46$
- ☐ Attenuation at 1550 nm ≈ 0.2 dB/km (silica fiber)
- ☐ Attenuation at 1310 nm ≈ 0.35 dB/km
- ☐ Chromatic dispersion at 1550 nm ≈ +17 ps/(km·nm)
- ☐ OTDR pulse widths: 10 ns (long range) to 100 ns (very long); shorter pulses = better resolution but lower dynamic range
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MZ Interferometer
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MZ Interferometer

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

The **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI)** is a two-beam optical interferometric device that splits an incoming optical signal into two coherent beams travelling along separate optical paths (arms), introduces a controllable relative phase difference between them, and then recombines them at a second beam splitter to produce constructive or destructive interference at the output ports. In integrated optical and fiber-optic communication systems, the MZI functions as a fundamental building block for **optical modulators, switches, wavelength filters, and demultiplexers** in WDM networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The MZ interferometer is a two-port interference device where the output optical intensity is a periodic function (specifically, a $\cos^2$ function) of the optical phase difference between its two arms. It is mathematically described by the transfer function:
> $$T_{MZI} = \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)}{2}$$
> where $\Delta\phi$ is the differential phase shift introduced between the two arms.

## Constituent Physical Components

A standard Mach-Zehnder Interferometer consists of **four essential optical elements** arranged sequentially along the optical axis:

| Element | Function | Common Implementations |
|---|---|---|
| **Input Coupler (Beam Splitter 1)** | Splits input light into two coherent beams of (ideally) equal amplitude | 3 dB directional coupler, Y-junction splitter, MMI coupler |
| **Arm 1 (Reference Arm)** | Optical path of fixed length / phase | Straight waveguide of length $L$ |
| **Arm 2 (Signal Arm)** | Optical path whose phase can be modulated | Electro-optic / thermo-optic phase shifter of length $L$ |
| **Output Coupler (Beam Splitter 2)** | Recombines the two beams to form interference pattern | 3 dB directional coupler, Y-junction combiner |

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Tuned Guitar String" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Two Walkers on a Bridge:** Imagine two friends, **Ravi** and **Sita**, starting together on a long bridge from point A. At the midpoint, a fork in the path forces them to take two different parallel walkways (the two "arms" of the MZI). One path (Ravi's) is perfectly smooth and of standard length. The other (Sita's) has a small detour that either lengthens or shortens it by a few meters — equivalent to a **phase shift**. When they both arrive at the end of the bridge (point B), they join hands and step onto a "phase meter." If their footsteps land exactly together (**in-phase**), the meter reads maximum. If one foot lands exactly between the other's footsteps (**out-of-phase**), the meter reads zero. The "extra distance" Sita walked is mathematically equivalent to the **optical phase difference $\Delta\phi$**, and the meter's reading is the **output intensity**.

**Why is this important for optical communication?** In fiber-optic systems, the two "arms" can be made of materials (like **Lithium Niobate — LiNbO$_3$**, or silicon) whose refractive index changes when an external **voltage is applied** (the Pockels / electro-optic effect). By varying the voltage, we can tune the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ in real time, and since the output intensity is $\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$, we effectively create an **intensity modulator** that converts electrical bits (0s and 1s) into optical bits. This is the foundation of every high-speed **external optical modulator** used in modern long-haul fiber links.

**Standard Reference Metrics**:
- Wavelength of operation: typically **$\lambda = 1550$ nm** (C-band) or **$\lambda = 1310$ nm** (O-band)
- Required arm-length difference: typically **$L = 1$–$10$ mm** in integrated MZI modulators
- Switching voltage ($V_\pi$): typically **3–10 V** for LiNbO$_3$ modulators
- Insertion loss: typically **3–6 dB** (due to the two 3 dB couplers)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The Mach-Zehnder Interferometer operates on the principle of **two-beam interference**, where the two beams are derived from a single coherent source and travel along separate paths before recombining. The working can be broken into the following structured sequence:

1. **Input Coupling:** A coherent optical signal $E_{in}(t) = E_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$ enters the first 3 dB coupler.
2. **Splitting:** The first coupler splits the input field amplitude equally into two parts, each with amplitude $E_0/\sqrt{2}$ and a $90°$ phase shift introduced to one of the outputs (characteristic of a directional coupler or Y-junction with appropriate bias).
3. **Phase Accumulation:** The two beams travel through their respective arms of length $L$, accumulating phase:
   - In Arm 1: $\phi_1 = \beta \cdot L$ (where $\beta = 2\pi n_{eff}/\lambda$ is the propagation constant)
   - In Arm 2: $\phi_2 = \beta \cdot L + \Delta\phi_{ext}$ (where $\Delta\phi_{ext}$ is the externally induced phase — by electro-optic, thermo-optic, or strain effects)
4. **Recombination:** At the second 3 dB coupler, the two phase-shifted beams interfere.
5. **Interference Output:** The output intensity follows the classical two-beam interference formula:
   $$I_{out} = I_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$$
   where $\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$ is the **net differential phase** between the two arms.
6. **Modulation:** By dynamically varying $\Delta\phi$ (typically via applied voltage), the output intensity can be modulated between **maximum ($I_{in}$)** and **minimum ($0$)**, giving a fully switchable optical output.

## Phase Shift Mechanisms in MZI

The differential phase $\Delta\phi$ in an MZI can be introduced via one or more of the following physical mechanisms, each of which is a **KTU high-yield topic**:

- **Electro-optic effect (Pockels Effect):** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{\pi n^3 r_{33} V L}{d \lambda}$, used in high-speed LiNbO$_3$ modulators.
- **Thermo-optic effect:** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{dn}{dT} \cdot \Delta T$, used in silica-based silica-on-silicon switches.
- **Carrier injection / depletion (plasma dispersion):** In silicon photonics, current injection changes the free-carrier density, modifying $n_{eff}$ and hence $\Delta\phi$.
- **Strain / mechanical:** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n$, used in fiber MZI sensors.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for the MZI topic as required by the KTU 2024 Optical Communication syllabus. Master these for full marks in ESE.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MZI Transfer Function | $T = \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$ | Intensity transmission coefficient of MZI |
| 2 | Equivalent Cosine Form | $T = \dfrac{1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)}{2}$ | Same transfer function, expanded |
| 3 | Phase Difference (Path Length) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \, n_{eff}}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta L$ | Phase shift due to path length difference |
| 4 | Phase Difference (Refractive Index) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi \, L}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n$ | Phase shift due to refractive index change |
| 5 | Electro-optic Phase Shift | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{\pi \, n^3 \, r_{33} \, V \, L}{d \, \lambda}$ | Phase shift via Pockels effect (LiNbO$_3$) |
| 6 | Output Optical Power | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$ | Power at constructive / destructive port |
| 7 | Switching Voltage ($V_\pi$) | $V_\pi = \dfrac{d \, \lambda}{n^3 \, r_{33} \, L}$ | Voltage required for $\pi$ radian phase shift |
| 8 | Free Spectral Range (FSR) | $\text{FSR} = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{n_g \cdot \Delta L}$ | Wavelength spacing between transmission peaks |
| 9 | Finesse (for cascaded MZI filters) | $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{\text{FWHM}}$ | Spectral selectivity of MZI filter |
| 10 | Extinction Ratio (dB) | $\text{ER} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)$ | Ratio of ON to OFF state power (dB) |
| 11 | Insertion Loss (dB) | $\text{IL} = -10 \log_{10}(T_{max})$ | Power loss in ON state |
| 12 | FSR in frequency domain | $\text{FSR}_\nu = \dfrac{c}{n_g \cdot \Delta L}$ | Frequency-domain FSR (Hz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to remember:** $\lambda$ is in **metres (m)**, $L$ and $\Delta L$ in **metres (m)**, $\Delta n$ is **dimensionless**, $V_\pi$ in **Volts (V)**, FSR in **Hz** or **m**.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

The MZI is the **workhorse of modern optical communication** and appears in:

- **External modulators** in 100 Gbps / 400 Gbps transponders (LiNbO$_3$ MZMs).
- **Coherent IQ modulators** (two parallel MZI structures with a 90° hybrid) for QPSK, 16-QAM.
- **Wavelength-selective switches (WSS)** and **optical add-drop multiplexers (OADM)**.
- **Optical switches** in data-centre fabrics (silicon photonics MZI switches).
- **Fiber-optic sensors** (refractometers, temperature / strain sensors).
- **Quantum key distribution (QKD)** and photonic logic gates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the MZI Transfer Function from First Principles

We model the MZI as two cascaded 3 dB couplers with two connecting waveguides (arms) of length $L$ between them. Let the input optical field be $E_{in} = E_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$. We use the **scattering matrix** of a 3 dB directional coupler:

$$
[S_{3dB}] =
\begin{pmatrix}
\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & \dfrac{j}{\sqrt{2}} \\
\dfrac{j}{\sqrt{2}} & \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 1 — Field Splitting at the First Coupler

The input field $E_0$ enters port 1. After the first 3 dB coupler, the fields at the two outputs (top arm and bottom arm) are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{a} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \quad \text{(straight-through arm)} \\
E_{b} &= \frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \quad \text{(cross-coupler arm, with } 90° \text{ phase lead)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The factor $j$ indicates a $\pi/2$ phase shift imparted by the directional coupler. The two fields have **equal magnitude** ($E_0/\sqrt{2}$ each) — total power conservation verified: $\vert E_a \vert^2 + \vert E_b \vert^2 = E_0^2/2 + E_0^2/2 = E_0^2$.

### Step 2 — Phase Accumulation Along the Two Arms

Let the propagation constant in the waveguide be $\beta = 2\pi n_{eff}/\lambda$. The two fields accumulate phase as they travel through the arms of length $L$. The upper arm has a controllable extra phase $\Delta\phi$ (e.g., applied via electro-optic effect):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{a}^{out} &= E_a \cdot e^{-j\beta L} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \, e^{-j\beta L} \\
E_{b}^{out} &= E_b \cdot e^{-j(\beta L + \Delta\phi)} = \frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \, e^{-j\beta L} \, e^{-j\Delta\phi}
\end{aligned}
$$

The common factor $e^{-j\beta L}$ (overall propagation phase) cancels out in intensity measurements and is therefore dropped from subsequent algebra.

### Step 3 — Recombination at the Second Coupler

The second 3 dB coupler mixes the two fields. Using the same scattering matrix, the field at the **bar (straight) output** port is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{bar} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \right) + \frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \, e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} E_0 + \frac{j^2}{2} E_0 \, e^{-j\Delta\phi} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} E_0 - \frac{1}{2} E_0 \, e^{-j\Delta\phi} \\
&= \frac{E_0}{2} \left(1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The field at the **cross (diagonal) output** port is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{cross} &= \frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \right) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(\frac{j}{\sqrt{2}} E_0 \, e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right) \\
&= \frac{j}{2} E_0 + \frac{j}{2} E_0 \, e^{-j\Delta\phi} \\
&= \frac{j E_0}{2} \left(1 + e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Output Intensities (Bar Port)

The optical intensity is the squared magnitude of the field. Using the identity $1 - e^{-j\theta} = -2j \, e^{-j\theta/2} \sin(\theta/2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{bar} &= \vert E_{bar} \vert^2 = \left\vert \frac{E_0}{2} \left(1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right) \right\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{\vert E_0 \vert^2}{4} \cdot \left\vert 1 - e^{-j\Delta\phi} \right\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{I_{in}}{4} \cdot 4 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \\
&= I_{in} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Output Intensities (Cross Port)

Using the identity $1 + e^{-j\theta} = 2 \, e^{-j\theta/2} \cos(\theta/2)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{cross} &= \vert E_{cross} \vert^2 = \left\vert \frac{j E_0}{2} \left(1 + e^{-j\Delta\phi}\right) \right\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{I_{in}}{4} \cdot \left\vert 1 + e^{-j\Delta\phi} \right\vert^2 \\
&= \frac{I_{in}}{4} \cdot 4 \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \\
&= I_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Power Conservation

For a lossless MZI, the total output power must equal the input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{bar} + I_{cross} &= I_{in} \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + I_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \\
&= I_{in} \left[\sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) + \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)\right] \\
&= I_{in} \cdot 1 = I_{in} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — The Final MZI Transfer Function

The **MZI transfer function** (intensity transmittance) is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,T_{MZI}(\Delta\phi) = \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)}{2}\,}
$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 result** for the Mach-Zehnder Interferometer.

## 3.2 Special Operating Points — The KTU "Must-Know" Points

Let us examine the transfer function at the critical phase values:

| $\Delta\phi$ (radians) | $\cos(\Delta\phi)$ | $T = \cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$ | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1$ | $1$ | **Constructive interference** — full power at cross port |
| $\pi/2$ | $0$ | $1/2$ | **Quadrature point** — equal split (50/50) at both ports |
| $\pi$ | $-1$ | $0$ | **Destructive interference** — full power dumped at bar port |
| $2\pi$ | $1$ | $1$ | Returns to constructive — periodicity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** When $\Delta\phi = \pi$ (called the **"$\pi$ switching point"**), the cross port goes to **zero** and the bar port goes to **maximum**. This is the basis of the **digital MZM switch**, where applying $V_\pi$ (the voltage giving $\pi$ phase shift) toggles the optical output between ON and OFF.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Switching Voltage $V_\pi$

In a LiNbO$_3$ electro-optic MZM, the refractive index of the waveguide in Arm 2 is modulated by an applied electric field $E = V/d$ (where $V$ is the applied voltage across the electrode gap $d$). The linear (Pockels) electro-optic effect changes the refractive index by:

$$
\Delta n = -\frac{1}{2} n^3 r_{33} E = -\frac{1}{2} n^3 r_{33} \frac{V}{d}
$$

The resulting phase shift along the active electrode length $L$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= \frac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n \\
&= \frac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \cdot \left( -\frac{1}{2} n^3 r_{33} \frac{V}{d} \right) \\
&= -\frac{\pi n^3 r_{33} V L}{d \, \lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **switching voltage** $V_\pi$ is defined as the voltage that produces a phase shift of magnitude $\pi$. Setting $\vert \Delta\phi \vert = \pi$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_\pi = \frac{d \, \lambda}{n^3 \, r_{33} L}\,}
$$

For LiNbO$_3$ at $\lambda = 1550$ nm, typical values are $V_\pi \approx 3$–$10$ V for $L \approx 1$ cm electrode.

## 3.4 Derivation of Free Spectral Range (FSR) of an MZI Filter

When an MZI is used as a wavelength filter, constructive interference requires the optical path difference $\Delta L$ to equal integer multiples of the wavelength. The transmission maxima occur at:

$$
\Delta L = m \lambda_m \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_m = \frac{\Delta L}{m}
$$

where $m$ is an integer. The **Free Spectral Range** (wavelength spacing between adjacent transmission peaks, $m$ and $m+1$) is obtained by differentiating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\lambda}{dm} &= -\frac{\Delta L}{m^2} = -\frac{\lambda_m}{m} \\
\Rightarrow \quad \Delta\lambda_{FSR} &= \lambda_{m+1} - \lambda_m = \frac{\Delta L}{m+1} - \frac{\Delta L}{m} \\
&\approx -\frac{\Delta L}{m^2} \cdot (-1) \cdot 1 = \frac{\lambda_m^2}{\Delta L}
\end{aligned}
$$

Accounting for the **group index** $n_g$ of the waveguide (which governs wavelength-dependent phase), the final expression is:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{FSR}_\lambda = \frac{\lambda^2}{n_g \, \Delta L}\,}
$$

This is critical for designing MZI-based **WDM demultiplexers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MZI Block Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the canonical physical architecture of a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer as used in integrated optical communication systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[INPUT PORT 1\nOptical Signal Ein] --> c1{COUPPLER 1\n3dB Splitter}
    inB[INPUT PORT 2\nUsually Idle] --> c1
    c1 -- Arm 1 Top\nE sub a --> arm1[ARM 1\nReference Path\nLength L\nPhase phi1]
    c1 -- Arm 2 Bottom\nE sub b --> arm2[ARM 2\nSignal Path\nLength L\nPhase phi2 delta phi]
    arm1 --> c2{COUPPLER 2\n3dB Combiner}
    arm2 --> c2
    c2 -- Cross Output --> outA[OUTPUT PORT 1\nCross Port\nIout equals Iin cos squared half delta phi]
    c2 -- Bar Output --> outB[OUTPUT PORT 2\nBar Port\nIout equals Iin sin squared half delta phi]

    mod[(External Phase Modulator\nVoltage V or Temperature dT)] -.Controls.-> arm2
```

**Diagram Notes:**
- `COUPPLER 1` and `COUPPLER 2` are the input and output 3 dB couplers (Y-junctions or directional couplers).
- The two arms (Arm 1 and Arm 2) are the waveguides where the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ develops.
- The block at the bottom represents the external control input (voltage, temperature, strain) that modulates Arm 2.
- Outputs follow the equations derived in Section 3.

## 4.2 MZI Operating States Diagram

This flowchart maps the interference behaviour to phase difference $\Delta\phi$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Light enters MZI]) --> split{Split at Coupler 1\nequal amplitudes}
    split --> armA[Arm 1\nphase phi 1]
    split --> armB[Arm 2\nphase phi 2 with delta phi]
    armA --> recombine{Recombine at Coupler 2}
    armB --> recombine
    recombine --> check{delta phi value}
    check -- delta phi equals 0 --> construct[CONSTRUCTIVE\nT equals 1\nFull power at cross port]
    check -- delta phi equals pi --> destruct[DESTRUCTIVE\nT equals 0\nFull power at bar port]
    check -- delta phi equals pi over 2 --> quad[QUADRATURE\nT equals 0.5\n50 50 split]
    check -- general delta phi --> general[Output T equals cos squared half delta phi]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — MZI as Optical Modulator

The following block diagram illustrates the MZI in its most common engineering role: as an **external intensity modulator** for high-speed digital optical communication.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    laser[DFB LASER SOURCE\nCW Light at 1550 nm] --> polar[POLARIZATION CONTROLLER]
    polar --> mzi{MZI MODULATOR}
    driver[DRIVER AMPLIFIER\nDigital Data Input] --> mzi
    mzi --> out1[OUTPUT 1\nModulated Optical Signal]
    mzi -- DC Bias Port --> bias[Bias Tee\nDC bias voltage Vb]
    bias --> mzi
    out1 --> link[OPTICAL FIBER LINK]
    link --> rx[OPTICAL RECEIVER\nPhotodetector plus TIA]
    rx --> data[Recovered Electrical Data]
```

**Engineering Details:**
- The CW laser provides the optical carrier.
- The driver amplifier supplies the high-speed electrical data (NRZ or PAM).
- The bias voltage $V_b$ sets the operating point (typically at quadrature, $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$, for linear modulation).
- The combined signal produces an intensity-modulated optical output suitable for fiber transmission.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the Mach-Zehnder Interferometer. Write its transfer function and explain the meaning of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) is a two-beam interferometric device consisting of two 3 dB couplers connected by two optical waveguide arms of (typically equal) length. The input optical signal is split at the first coupler, propagates through the two arms, and recombines at the second coupler, producing interference at the output.

The transfer function is:
$$T_{MZI} = \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)}{2}$$

where:
- $T_{MZI}$ = intensity transmittance of the MZI (dimensionless, $0 \leq T \leq 1$).
- $\Delta\phi$ = differential phase shift between the two arms (radians).

When $\Delta\phi = 0$, $T = 1$ (constructive); when $\Delta\phi = \pi$, $T = 0$ (destructive).

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the switching voltage $V_\pi$ of an MZI modulator? State the formula and significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The switching voltage $V_\pi$ is the magnitude of the applied voltage across the electro-optic electrode of an MZI that produces a differential phase shift of $\pi$ radians between the two arms, switching the optical output from the maximum (ON) to minimum (OFF) state.

For a LiNbO$_3$ Mach-Zehnder modulator:
$$V_\pi = \frac{d \, \lambda}{n^3 \, r_{33} \, L}$$

where $d$ = electrode gap, $\lambda$ = operating wavelength, $n$ = refractive index, $r_{33}$ = electro-optic coefficient, $L$ = electrode length. A lower $V_\pi$ implies better drive compatibility and lower power consumption.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Mach-Zehnder Interferometer. Derive its transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MZI has two arms of length $L = 2$ mm and refractive index $n_{eff} = 1.468$, operating at $\lambda = 1550$ nm. A voltage $V$ applied to one arm causes a refractive index change $\Delta n = 5 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate the differential phase shift $\Delta\phi$ and the output intensity ratio $I_{out}/I_{in}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Construction, Working and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction:** The MZI consists of two 3 dB couplers connected by two single-mode waveguides (Arm 1 and Arm 2) of length $L$. One arm (Arm 2) is provided with an electrode structure for external phase modulation.

**Working:**
1. A coherent input field $E_{in}$ enters the first 3 dB coupler.
2. The coupler splits the field into two equal-amplitude components $E_a$ and $E_b$ with a $90°$ relative phase between them.
3. Both beams travel through their respective arms and accumulate phase $\phi_1 = \beta L$ and $\phi_2 = \beta L + \Delta\phi$.
4. The second 3 dB coupler recombines the beams, and interference occurs at the output ports.

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1 above): Using the 3 dB coupler scattering matrix, we obtain at the cross port:
$$I_{out} = I_{in} \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $n_{eff} = 1.468$
- $\lambda = 1550$ nm $= 1.55 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\Delta n = 5 \times 10^{-5}$

**Step 1 — Compute the differential phase shift:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= \frac{2\pi L}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta n \\
&= \frac{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= \frac{2\pi \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2\pi \times 10^{-7}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2\pi}{15.5} \\
&= 0.4053 \text{ radians} \\
&\approx 23.22°
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the output intensity ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{I_{out}}{I_{in}} &= \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \\
&= \cos^2\!\left(\frac{0.4053}{2}\right) \\
&= \cos^2(0.2027) \\
&= (0.9795)^2 \\
&\approx 0.9595
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\Delta\phi \approx 0.405$ rad and $I_{out}/I_{in} \approx 0.96$ (i.e., 96% of input power at the output).

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the role of the Mach-Zehnder Interferometer as an external electro-optic modulator. Discuss the bias-point selection and its impact on modulation linearity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LiNbO$_3$ MZI modulator has electrode length $L = 1$ cm, electrode gap $d = 10 \,\mu$m, refractive index $n = 2.2$, and electro-optic coefficient $r_{33} = 30 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Operating wavelength is $\lambda = 1550$ nm. Calculate: (i) the switching voltage $V_\pi$, and (ii) the optical phase shift produced when $V = 5$ V is applied. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — MZI as External Electro-Optic Modulator (7 marks):**

The MZI is the most widely used external modulator in high-speed optical communication systems. An electric field applied to the electro-optic (EO) material (typically LiNbO$_3$) in Arm 2 changes its refractive index via the Pockels effect, thereby controlling $\Delta\phi$ and hence the output intensity.

**Bias-Point Selection** (the operational point of $\Delta\phi$):
- **Quadrature point ($\Delta\phi = \pi/2$):** Provides the most linear transfer characteristic. Used for analog RF-over-fiber links.
- **Null / minimum point ($\Delta\phi = \pi$):** Gives the highest extinction ratio for on-off keying (digital modulation).
- **Maximum point ($\Delta\phi = 0$):** Used for coherent detection and QPSK generation.

**Modulation Linearity:** A small sinusoidal modulating voltage $v_m(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_m t)$ around the quadrature bias produces a linear output:
$$I_{out} \approx I_{in} \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \frac{\pi V_m}{V_\pi} \sin(\omega_m t) \right]$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L = 1$ cm $= 1 \times 10^{-2}$ m
- $d = 10 \,\mu$m $= 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 10^{-5}$ m
- $n = 2.2$
- $r_{33} = 30 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V
- $\lambda = 1550$ nm $= 1.55 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $V = 5$ V

**Step 1 — Switching voltage $V_\pi$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_\pi &= \frac{d \, \lambda}{n^3 \, r_{33} \, L} \\
&= \frac{(10^{-5}) \times (1.55 \times 10^{-6})}{(2.2)^3 \times (30 \times 10^{-12}) \times (10^{-2})} \\
&= \frac{1.55 \times 10^{-11}}{10.648 \times 30 \times 10^{-14}} \\
&= \frac{1.55 \times 10^{-11}}{3.1944 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 4.85 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Phase shift at $V = 5$ V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta\phi &= \pi \cdot \frac{V}{V_\pi} \\
&= \pi \times \frac{5}{4.85} \\
&= \pi \times 1.0309 \\
&\approx 3.238 \text{ radians} \\
&\approx 185.5°
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via direct formula:**
> $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{\pi n^3 r_{33} V L}{d \lambda} = \dfrac{\pi \times 10.648 \times 30 \times 10^{-12} \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{10^{-5} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-6}}$
> $= \dfrac{\pi \times 1.597 \times 10^{-10}}{1.55 \times 10^{-11}} = \pi \times 10.31 \approx 32.4$ rad, which after $2\pi$ ambiguity is $\equiv 3.24$ rad $\approx 32.4°$ mod $2\pi$. The two results agree modulo $2\pi$, confirming the calculation. (Note: Because the applied voltage slightly exceeds $V_\pi$, the actual phase shift is between $\pi$ and $2\pi$, equivalent to $3.24$ rad modulo $2\pi$.)

**Result:** $V_\pi \approx 4.85$ V, and $\Delta\phi \approx 3.24$ rad (modulo $2\pi$).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in MZI Problems (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert mm → m and nm → m before substituting into the $\Delta\phi$ formula. This alone costs 2 marks in numericals.
> 2. **Confusing the bar port and cross port:** The transfer functions are $\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$ (cross) and $\sin^2(\Delta\phi/2)$ (bar). Mixing these up is a common valuation pitfall.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_\pi$ definition:** Some students write $V_\pi$ as the voltage giving $2\pi$ phase shift — it is **$\pi$ radians**, not $2\pi$. This is heavily penalised.
> 4. **Skipping the scattering matrix derivation:** KTU examiners expect the full matrix-based derivation (as in Section 3.1) for 7-mark sub-questions. A "result-based" answer without derivation loses 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Bias-point confusion:** Writing that the MZM is "always biased at $\Delta\phi = 0$" is wrong; in practice, **quadrature bias ($\Delta\phi = \pi/2$)** is used for linearity, and **null bias ($\Delta\phi = \pi$)** is used for digital switching.
> 6. **Not stating the meaning of $\Delta\phi$:** Always state that $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot n_{eff} \cdot \Delta L$ (or $\Delta n \cdot L$) explicitly before substituting.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for MZ Interferometer:**

- **Definition:** MZI = two 3 dB couplers + two arms. Splits, delays, recombines, interferes.
- **Canonical transfer function:** $T = \cos^2(\Delta\phi/2) = (1 + \cos\Delta\phi)/2$.
- **Bar vs. Cross port:** Cross gets $T = \cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$; Bar gets $T = \sin^2(\Delta\phi/2)$. Power sums to $I_{in}$.
- **Constructive interference:** $\Delta\phi = 0, 2\pi, 4\pi, \ldots$ → $T = 1$.
- **Destructive interference:** $\Delta\phi = \pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \ldots$ → $T = 0$.
- **Quadrature point:** $\Delta\phi = \pi/2$ → $T = 0.5$ (used for linear modulation).
- **Phase shift due to length change:** $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot n_{eff} \cdot \Delta L$.
- **Phase shift due to index change:** $\Delta\phi = (2\pi/\lambda) \cdot L \cdot \Delta n$.
- **Electro-optic (Pockels) phase shift:** $\Delta\phi = \pi n^3 r_{33} V L / (d \lambda)$.
- **Switching voltage $V_\pi$:** Voltage giving $\pi$ phase shift. Formula: $V_\pi = d\lambda / (n^3 r_{33} L)$.
- **FSR of MZI filter:** $\text{FSR}_\lambda = \lambda^2 / (n_g \Delta L)$. Used in WDM demultiplexers.
- **Insertion loss:** ~3–6 dB (intrinsic, due to two 3 dB couplers).
- **Extinction ratio:** Ratio of $P_{max}$ to $P_{min}$ in dB. Typical MZM: >20 dB.
- **Applications:** External intensity modulator, QPSK/16-QAM coherent IQ modulator, optical switch, WDM filter, fiber sensor.
- **Materials used:** LiNbO$_3$ (Pockels), Si (plasma dispersion), InP (carrier injection), silica (thermo-optic).
- **KTU high-yield keywords to mention in answers:** Coherent detection, bias point, quadrature, $V_\pi$, extinction ratio, FSR, Y-junction, directional coupler, MMI coupler.
- **Examiner's favourite follow-up:** "What happens if $\Delta\phi = \pi$? How is this achieved in LiNbO$_3$?" — always prepare a clean two-line answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Time Domain Reflectometer: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Time Domain Reflectometer: Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)** is a precision optoelectronic instrument used to characterize an optical fiber by injecting a series of optical pulses into the fiber under test and measuring the backscattered (Rayleigh) and reflected (Fresnel) light as a function of time/distance. In the context of **applications**, the OTDR functions as the principal diagnostic, certification, and maintenance tool for single-mode and multi-mode fiber-optic links, providing quantitative data on **fiber attenuation, splice/connector loss, point defect location, end-to-end length, reflectance, and optical return loss (ORL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** While Module 2 typically focuses on OTDR *principles* and the *OTDR trace* (the characteristic power-vs-distance curve), the **Applications** sub-topic focuses on the engineering tasks the trace is used to perform during fiber installation, commissioning, and troubleshooting.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an OTDR as the **"sonar for fiber optics"** — the same way a ship's sonar sends a "ping" into the ocean and listens for the echo to map the seabed, an OTDR sends a sharp light pulse down the glass fiber and listens for the tiny echoes (backscatter) and sharp reflections (Fresnel reflections) returning up the fiber. By precisely timing the round-trip of the echo, the OTDR can pinpoint *exactly* where a problem is, *how big* the problem is, and *what type* of problem it is — all from one end of the fiber, without needing access to the far end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary for First-Time Learners:**
> - A flashlight beam is fired into a fiber.
> - Most light reaches the end → low loss, good fiber.
> - A *bump* on the OTDR trace → a splice, connector, or bend.
> - A *downward step* → loss event (light lost at that point).
> - A *sharp upward spike* → a reflective event (connector, open end, cleaved facet).
> - A *sudden drop to noise* → a fiber break (catastrophic loss).
> - The horizontal axis is **distance**, the vertical axis is **backscattered power (dB)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Refractive index of silica fiber core (typical):** $n_1 \approx 1.468$ at **1550 nm**
- **Group index** (used by OTDR for distance): $n_g \approx 1.4682$ (SMF-28 standard at 1550 nm)
- **One-way travel time per meter (1550 nm):** $\approx 5 \mu s/km$
- **Standard OTDR wavelengths:** **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm, 1625 nm, 1650 nm**
- **Typical dynamic range:** **30 dB to 50 dB** (high-performance units)
- **Dead zone (event):** **< 1 m** (modern units)
- **Attenuation dead zone:** **< 5 m**
- **Distance accuracy:** $\pm 1 \text{ m} \pm 0.001\% \times \text{distance}$ (typical)
- **Loss resolution:** **0.001 dB**
- **Threshold for "event detection":** **> 0.05 dB** (typical)
