# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why an OTDR is Needed — The Engineering Rationale

Traditional **cutback method** and **insertion loss method** with a power meter and source (per **IEC 61315 / FOTP-171**) require access to **both ends** of the fiber and *interrupt* the link. They cannot localize a fault. The OTDR was developed specifically to:

1. **Localize** events (splices, bends, breaks, connectors) along the fiber length.
2. **Quantify** the loss contribution of each individual event.
3. **Measure** the cumulative attenuation, ORL, and length in a **single-ended, non-destructive** test.
4. **Certify** installed fiber plants against design loss budgets (TIA-568 / ISO/IEC 11801 standards).
5. **Troubleshoot** live or dark networks, often with in-service testing on 1625/1650 nm while traffic runs on 1310/1550 nm.

## 2.2 Trace Interpretation — The Anatomy of an OTDR Trace

A typical OTDR trace (in log-power vs. linear-distance) consists of five distinct zones:

| Zone | Visual Appearance on Trace | Physical Origin | Engineering Use |
|------|---------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Launch zone** | Initial high-power pulse | Front-panel reflection (coupler) | Calibrates reference power level |
| **Linear decreasing region** | Smooth straight-line falloff (slope) | Rayleigh backscatter from the fiber bulk | Measures **fiber attenuation coefficient** $\alpha$ |
| **Step-down events** | Sudden drop in power | **Loss events** — splices, macrobends, fiber sections | Measures **splice loss** & **bend loss** |
| **Step-up (reflective) events** | Sharp vertical spike above the backscatter line | **Fresnel reflections** — connectors, open ends, cleaved facets, mechanical splices | Measures **reflectance** & **ORL** |
| **Noise floor / End-of-fiber** | Erratic noise, no signal | Detector noise floor or fiber break | Defines the **maximum measurable distance** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The two measurement algorithms the trace enables are **(a) the Two-Point Method** and **(b) the Least-Squares Method (LSM)**. Both are examinable for the 14-mark KTU question on OTDR applications.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Distance from time-of-flight** | $L = \dfrac{v_g \cdot t}{2} = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2 n_g}$ | $L$ = length, $v_g$ = group velocity, $t$ = round-trip time, $n_g$ = group index | m | Factor of 2 for round trip |
| 2 | **One-way attenuation coefficient** (Two-Point) | $\alpha = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{L}$ | $P_1, P_2$ = backscatter power at two points (dB) | dB/km | Use trace cursors at $z_1$ and $z_2$ |
| 3 | **Attenuation coefficient** (Least-Squares) | $\alpha_{LSM} = \dfrac{\sum (z_i - \bar z)(P_i - \bar P)}{\sum (z_i - \bar z)^2}$ | mean positions and powers from a sliding window | dB/km | More accurate, used in modern OTDRs |
| 4 | **Splice / Event Loss (Step Loss)** | $L_{event} = P_{before}(z) - P_{after}(z)$ | Backscatter levels immediately before/after the event | dB | Read at event cursor |
| 5 | **Reflectance of a single event** | $R = 10 \log_{10}\!\!\left(\dfrac{P_{reflected}}{P_{incident}}\right)$ | $P_{reflected}$ = peak reflected power, $P_{incident}$ = power at that point | dB | Always negative (e.g., $-40$ dB) |
| 6 | **Optical Return Loss (ORL) of a link** | $\text{ORL} = 10 \log_{10}\!\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{back}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_i 10^{R_i/10} + \alpha_{RBS} L \right)$ | $P_{in}$ = launched power, $P_{back}$ = total back-reflected power, $R_i$ = individual reflectances, $\alpha_{RBS}$ = Rayleigh backscatter coefficient | dB | Typical link ORL: $\ge 30$ dB for analog video, $\ge 27$ dB for digital |
| 7 | **Insertion Loss (end-to-end)** | $IL = P_{launch} - P_{receive}$ | Powers at the two ends | dB | Compared with OTDR sum for cross-check |
| 8 | **Power-budget margin verification** | $P_{TX} - \alpha L - \sum L_i - M \ge P_{RX(min)}$ | $M$ = safety margin (typically 3 dB) | dB | The fundamental design check |
| 9 | **Rayleigh backscatter coefficient** | $\alpha_{RBS} \approx \dfrac{3}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{NA}{n_1}\right)^{2}\!\cdot \dfrac{1}{kT} \cdot \alpha$ | $NA$ = numerical aperture, $k$ = Boltzmann, $T$ = effective scattering temperature | 1/m | Determines backscatter level; sets dynamic range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Trick (for 14-mark answers):** When asked to *calculate* the attenuation coefficient from a given OTDR trace, the examiner expects you to:
> (i) read $P_1$ at $z_1$ and $P_2$ at $z_2$ from the trace,
> (ii) apply $\alpha = (P_1 - P_2)/(z_2 - z_1)$,
> (iii) state units (dB/km) and the implicit *one-way* nature of the result.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of OTDR Applications

- **Telecom backbone networks** (long-haul, metro): Commissioning 100+ km spans, certifying per ITU-T G.65x.
- **FTTH / FTTx access networks** (PON, GPON, XGS-PON): Verifying drop-fiber quality, splitter loss, and detecting fiber cuts from the OLT (central office).
- **Data centers (DCI)**: Certifying short single-mode runs, finding macrobends in patch panels.
- **Submarine cable systems**: Initial shipboard OTDR characterization plus continuous **coherent OTDR (C-OTDR)** for vibration / intrusion sensing.
- **Industrial / Smart-grid fiber**: Distributed temperature (DTS) and strain (DSS) sensing systems use OTDR-derived backscatter principles on specialty fibers.
- **Defense / Aerospace**: Testing tactical fiber cables; finding breaks in deployable reels.
- **CATV / HFC networks**: Return-path monitoring at 1625 nm to find ingress points.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Distance from Round-Trip Time (OTDR Range Equation)

The OTDR launches a pulse at $t = 0$. The pulse travels down the fiber at the **group velocity** $v_g = c / n_g$, scatters, and the backscatter returns to the detector. The detector records the returning power at time $t$.

**Step 1 — One-way distance traveled by light in time $t$ is:**

$$L_{one-way} = v_g \cdot t = \frac{c}{n_g} \cdot t$$

**Step 2 — Because the *same* pulse had to travel the distance $L$ to the scattering point *and* return, the round-trip distance is $2L$:**

$$2L = v_g \cdot t \quad \Longrightarrow \quad L = \frac{v_g \cdot t}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $v_g = c/n_g$:**

$$L = \frac{c \cdot t}{2 \, n_g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** We divide by 2 because the *time* measured by the OTDR is for the **round trip**, but the *distance* we want is the **one-way** distance to the event. Group index $n_g$ (not phase index $n$) is used because OTDR measures a *pulse*, which is a wave packet — its energy travels at $v_g$, not at the phase velocity $c/n$.

**Step 4 — Numerical example (typical KTU sub-part):**

A fault echo is detected at $t = 100 \ \mu s$. Fiber: SMF-28 at 1550 nm with $n_g = 1.4682$.

$$L = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}) (100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2 \times 1.4682} = \frac{3 \times 10^{4}}{2.9364} \approx 10{,}217 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 10.22 \text{ km}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fiber Attenuation Coefficient (Two-Point Method)

The backscattered power from a position $z$ in a fiber of attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ (in linear units, $\text{m}^{-1}$) and launched power $P_0$ follows:

$$P_{BS}(z) = K \cdot P_0 \cdot e^{-2\alpha z}$$

The factor of **2** appears because the backscatter suffers loss on the *forward* trip *and* on the *return* trip.

**Step 1 — Take the logarithm (base 10) to convert to dB:**

$$10 \log_{10} P_{BS}(z) = 10 \log_{10}(K P_0) - 20 \alpha z \log_{10}(e)$$

**Step 2 — Define the per-unit-length backscatter slope constant in dB/km as $\alpha_{dB}$:**

$$\alpha_{dB} = 20 \alpha \log_{10}(e) \approx 8.686 \cdot \alpha \quad \text{[dB/km]}$$

**Step 3 — Hence the backscatter level at any distance $z$ (in dB) is:**

$$P_{BS}^{dB}(z) = P_0^{dB} + 10\log_{10} K - \alpha_{dB} \cdot z$$

**Step 4 — Read two points $(z_1, P_1)$ and $(z_2, P_2)$ from the trace and eliminate the unknown $P_0^{dB} + 10\log_{10} K$:**

$$\alpha_{dB} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{z_2 - z_1} \quad \text{[dB/km]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the factor 2:** *Each meter* of fiber attenuates the *forward* pulse by $\alpha$ dB *and* the *returning* scatter by $\alpha$ dB, so the backscatter trace falls *twice* as fast as the actual fiber attenuation. OTDRs automatically account for this, so the displayed slope equals the true one-way $\alpha_{dB}$.

**Step 5 — Numerical example:**

From a trace, at $z_1 = 5$ km, $P_1 = -32.0$ dBm; at $z_2 = 15$ km, $P_2 = -34.4$ dBm.

$$\alpha = \frac{-32.0 - (-34.4)}{15 - 5} = \frac{2.4}{10} = 0.24 \text{ dB/km}$$

This is consistent with the 1550 nm attenuation of a standard silica SMF (~0.20–0.25 dB/km).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Splice / Event Loss from a Step on the Trace

At a splice at position $z_s$, the backscatter power just *before* the splice is $P_{BS}(z_s^-)$ and just *after* is $P_{BS}(z_s^+)$. The splice loss $L_s$ in dB is simply the **vertical step**:

$$L_s \text{ [dB]} = P_{BS}^{dB}(z_s^-) - P_{BS}^{dB}(z_s^+)$$

**Proof sketch:** The backscatter level is proportional to the *local optical power* in the fiber at that point. Right before the splice, the local power is $P(z_s^-)$. The splice removes a fraction $L_s$ dB. Right after the splice, the power is $P(z_s^-) \cdot 10^{-L_s/10}$. The backscatter level is linear in the local power, so:

$$P_{BS}^{dB}(z_s^+) = P_{BS}^{dB}(z_s^-) - L_s$$

Rearranging gives the formula. **This is the standard KTU 7-mark sub-part answer.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU 2024):** For a *reflective* event (e.g., a connector or mechanical splice with an air gap), you **cannot** read the step directly at the spike — the spike itself is *not* the loss. The step is measured from the *backscatter baseline before* the spike to the *backscatter baseline after* the spike, ignoring the spike. Failing to do this costs ~2 marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reflectance and ORL of a Single Event

A reflective event (e.g., a connector's glass-air interface) reflects a fraction $R_F$ of the incident power.

**Step 1 — Define reflectance in dB:**

$$R_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} R_F = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{reflected}}{P_{incident}}\right)$$

For a clean physical-contact (PC) connector: $R_{dB} \approx -40$ to $-50$ dB.
For an angled physical-contact (APC) connector: $R_{dB} \approx -65$ to $-70$ dB.
For a cleaved facet / open end: $R_{dB} \approx -14$ dB (Fresnel at $n \approx 1.468$).

**Step 2 — Fresnel reflectance at a single glass-air interface:**

$$R_F = \left(\frac{n_1 - n_0}{n_1 + n_0}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.468 - 1.000}{1.468 + 1.000}\right)^{2} = (0.1895)^{2} \approx 0.0359$$

$$R_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.0359) \approx -14.45 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Optical Return Loss of the full link** (in linear units, the sum of all back-reflected powers):

$$\text{ORL}_{linear} = R_{RBS} + \sum_{i} R_{F,i}$$

where $R_{RBS}$ is the distributed Rayleigh-backscatter contribution and $R_{F,i}$ are the discrete Fresnel reflectances.

**Step 4 — In dB, the link ORL is the *sum* (in linear) of contributions:**

$$\text{ORL} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{-R_{RBS,dB}/10} + \sum_i 10^{R_{F,i,dB}/10}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Because powers add *linearly*, you must convert *each* dB contribution back to a linear ratio, sum them, then convert to dB. **This is the most common KTU trap** — students add dB values directly, which is dimensionally wrong.

---

## 3.5 Application Flow — How an OTDR is Used in Practice

1. **Pre-installation check** — Use a *launch reference cable* (typically 100 m to 1 km of known fiber) to overcome the OTDR's dead zone and verify the connector on the OTDR port itself.
2. **Baseline trace capture** — Connect the OTDR to the link and capture traces at all required wavelengths (typically 1310 + 1550 nm, plus 1625 nm for in-service).
3. **Set pass/fail thresholds** — Per project spec, e.g., splice loss $\le 0.1$ dB, connector reflectance $\le -40$ dB, attenuation $\le 0.35$ dB/km at 1310 nm.
4. **Event-by-event analysis** — Cursor through every detected event; record splice loss, connector loss, reflectance, distance.
5. **End-of-fiber check** — Verify the expected end-of-fiber event appears at approximately the documented cable length (within $\pm 1\%$).
6. **ORL measurement** — Use OTDR's ORL function or compute from total backscatter.
7. **Compare with power-meter measurement** — Cross-validate: sum of OTDR splice + connector losses should match the insertion-loss measured end-to-end within $\pm 0.5$ dB.
8. **Archive trace** — Save the trace (`.sor` file per **Telcordia SR-4731** standard) for warranty and future comparison.
9. **Periodic re-testing** — Compare current trace with the baseline to identify *new* events (degrading splices, water ingress, animal damage).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an OTDR

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pulse Generator] --> B[Laser Source 1310 1550 1625 nm]
    B --> C[Optical Coupler 1x2]
    C --> D[Directional Coupler 3 dB]
    D --> E[Fiber Under Test FUT]
    E --> F[Backscattered Light Rayleigh]
    E --> G[Reflected Light Fresnel]
    F --> D
    G --> D
    D --> H[APD Avalanche Photodiode]
    H --> I[Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    I --> J[Signal Averager Boxcar or Cumulative]
    J --> K[ADC Analog to Digital]
    K --> L[Microprocessor DSP]
    L --> M[Display Backscatter Power vs Distance]
    L --> N[Event Detection Algorithm]
    L --> O[ORL Reflectance Calculator]
    M --> P[Operator or SOR File]
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The OTDR is essentially a *radar* in which the laser fires, the directional coupler separates the outgoing pulse from the weak returning signal, the APD amplifies the small backscatter, the averager improves SNR (signal-to-noise ratio) by synchronous integration over thousands of pulses, and the microprocessor converts the time axis into distance.

## 4.2 Application Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Visual Inspection] --> S2[Step 2 Clean Connectors]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Connect Launch Cable]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Set Wavelength and Pulse Width]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Capture Reference Trace]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Identify Events Splice Connector Bend]
    S6 --> S7{Step 7 Pass or Fail}
    S7 -- Pass --> S8[Step 8 Archive SOR File]
    S7 -- Fail --> S9[Step 9 Localize and Repair]
    S9 --> S4
    S8 --> S10[Step 10 Compare with Power Meter]
```

## 4.3 OTDR Trace Anatomy — Annotated Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OTDR Port Reflection] --> B[Launch Cable Linear Slope]
    B --> C[Splice 1 Step Down]
    C --> D[Connector Reflective Spike]
    D --> E[Macrobend Step Down]
    E --> F[Fiber Break Sudden Drop]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway from the trace anatomy:**
> - A **step-down** without a spike = *loss event* (splice, bend, fiber section change).
> - A **spike** = *reflective event* (connector, open end, cleaved facet, mechanical splice).
> - A **step-down** that *gradually recovers* over many meters = *macrobend* (wavelength-dependent — worse at 1625 nm than 1550 nm).
> - A **sudden drop into the noise floor** = *fiber break* or *end with no terminator*.

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which OTDR Measurement When?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What do you want to measure?}
    Q1 -- Fiber attenuation --> R1[Use Two Point or LSM on linear region of trace]
    Q1 -- Splice loss --> R2[Use step height between cursors]
    Q1 -- Connector reflectance --> R3[Use spike amplitude vs backscatter line]
    Q1 -- Link ORL --> R4[Use built in ORL function or integrate total backscatter]
    Q1 -- Length to a fault --> R5[Use cursor on the last event before noise floor]
    Q1 -- Macrobend diagnosis --> R6[Compare 1310 nm vs 1550 nm vs 1625 nm traces]
    R6 -- Excess loss at 1625 nm --> R7[Macrobend confirmed]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Set A]`
**List any six applications of an OTDR in optical fiber networks.**

**Model Answer (concise points — 3 marks):**

1. **Measurement of fiber attenuation coefficient** (dB/km) using the backscatter slope.
2. **Localization and loss measurement of splices** along the fiber link.
3. **Reflectance and loss measurement of connectors** and other reflective events.
4. **Fault localization** — pinpointing the distance to a fiber break or severe bend.
5. **Cable length verification** — end-to-end distance measurement.
6. **Optical Return Loss (ORL) measurement** of the link.
7. (Bonus) **In-service network monitoring** at 1625/1650 nm while traffic runs on 1310/1550 nm.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Explain the difference between a "loss event" and a "reflective event" on an OTDR trace.**

**Model Answer (concise — 3 marks):**

- **Loss event:** Appears as a **step-down (drop)** in the backscatter level with no spike. Physically represents a splice, macrobend, or section of higher-loss fiber. The event loss is read as the **vertical step height** in dB.
- **Reflective event:** Appears as a **sharp upward spike** above the backscatter line, indicating a Fresnel reflection from a glass–air interface (connector, open end, cleaved facet). Its **spike amplitude** is related to the event's **reflectance** in dB, while the *baseline step* (ignoring the spike) gives the **event loss**.

> [!NOTE]
> For 3 marks, the examiner awards: 1 mark for loss-event description, 1 mark for reflective-event description, 1 mark for the correct trace-appearance distinction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an OTDR. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the major **applications of an OTDR** in fiber-optic communication systems, with reference to the typical trace features used for each application. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Working Principle (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
An OTDR injects narrow, high-peak-power optical pulses from a laser (typically at 1310 nm, 1550 nm, or 1625 nm) into the fiber under test. As the pulse propagates, three phenomena send light back to the OTDR:
- **Rayleigh backscatter** — caused by microscopic refractive-index fluctuations in the silica.
- **Fresnel reflections** — at glass–air interfaces (connectors, breaks, cleaved ends).
- **Non-linear effects** — Raman/Brillouin scatter (negligible for short links).

The OTDR measures the returned power as a function of time, which it converts to distance, producing the characteristic **backscatter trace** of power (dB) versus distance (km).

**2. Block Diagram (3 marks) — see SECTION 4.1.**
The student should describe:
- **Laser + pulse generator** for the test pulse.
- **3-dB directional coupler (or circulator)** to separate outgoing pulse from returning signal.
- **APD detector** with high sensitivity (to detect the very weak backscatter, often –60 to –80 dBm).
- **Averager / Boxcar integrator** to build SNR by averaging $N$ repeated traces; SNR improves as $\sqrt{N}$.
- **Microprocessor** that performs: (i) time-to-distance conversion using $L = ct/(2n_g)$, (ii) trace display, (iii) event detection, (iv) loss calculation.

**3. Distance equation and trace axes (2 marks):**
- Distance axis: $L = ct/(2n_g)$, group index $n_g \approx 1.4682$ at 1550 nm for SMF-28.
- Power axis: backscatter level in dB.
- Slope of the linear region = fiber attenuation coefficient (dB/km).
- Events: step-downs (loss), spikes (reflection), and end-of-fiber drop.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Applications of OTDR (7 marks)

The applications, each tied to a trace feature, are:

1. **Fiber attenuation coefficient measurement (2 marks):**
   - Read two backscatter points $P_1$ at $z_1$ and $P_2$ at $z_2$ on the linear portion of the trace.
   - Apply $\alpha = (P_1 - P_2)/(z_2 - z_1)$ in **dB/km**.
   - The two-point method is quick; the **Least-Squares Method (LSM)** is more accurate over a sliding window.

2. **Splice and connector loss measurement (2 marks):**
   - A *step-down* on the trace indicates a loss event.
   - The step height = splice/connector loss in dB.
   - For a *reflective* connector, ignore the spike; read the baseline step only.

3. **Fault localization (1.5 marks):**
   - The horizontal distance from the launch point to a sudden drop in the noise floor = distance to the **fiber break** or severe bend.
   - Use $L = ct/(2n_g)$ to convert echo time to distance.

4. **Reflectance and ORL measurement (1.5 marks):**
   - Reflectance $R = 10\log_{10}(P_{reflected}/P_{incident})$ read from the spike amplitude.
   - ORL integrates all back-reflections, both Fresnel and Rayleigh, and reports a single figure of merit in dB.

> [!NOTE]
> For 7 marks, present the application, name the trace feature, state the formula (if any), and state the engineering use.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, Internal Choice]`
**(a)** Describe the principle of operation of an OTDR. Derive the expression for the distance to a fault in terms of the round-trip time, the speed of light, and the group index. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the following OTDR applications with trace interpretation: (i) Macrobend detection, (ii) Splice loss measurement, (iii) Optical Return Loss measurement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Principle and Distance Derivation (7 marks)

1. **Working principle (3 marks):** Same as Question A part (a) above.
2. **Derivation (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Light pulse travels at group velocity $v_g = c/n_g$ in the fiber.

**Step 2:** Round-trip distance $= v_g \cdot t$ (because the same pulse goes to the event and back).

**Step 3:** One-way distance to the event:

$$L = \frac{v_g \, t}{2} = \frac{c \, t}{2 \, n_g}$$

**Step 4:** Discussion of group index vs phase index — group index is used because the OTDR pulse is a wave packet, and its energy (and information) travels at the group velocity.

**Step 5 (numerical example for completeness, 1 mark):**
For $n_g = 1.4682$, $t = 100 \ \mu s$, $L = 10.22$ km (as derived in Section 3.1).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Three Applications (7 marks — 2.33 marks each)

**(i) Macrobend detection (≈ 2.3 marks):**
- A macrobend appears as a *gradual step-down* in the trace, often with partial recovery further along.
- It is **wavelength-dependent**: macrobend loss is much higher at **1625 nm** than at **1310 nm** or **1550 nm**.
- **Diagnostic method:** Take traces at 1310 nm, 1550 nm, and 1625 nm. If the 1625 nm trace shows extra loss that is not present at 1310 nm, a macrobend is present.
- This is a routine commissioning test for indoor cable installations and patch-panel routing.

**(ii) Splice loss measurement (≈ 2.3 marks):**
- A fusion splice produces a **step-down** (non-reflective loss event) on the trace.
- Splice loss = backscatter level immediately *before* the splice minus backscatter level immediately *after* the splice, in dB.
- Typical acceptance: $\le 0.1$ dB for single-mode fusion splices on G.652 fiber at 1550 nm.
- A *mechanical splice* often shows a small *reflective spike* in addition to the step — read the baseline step only, ignoring the spike.

**(iii) Optical Return Loss (ORL) measurement (≈ 2.3 marks):**
- ORL is the ratio (in dB) of total launched power to total back-reflected power.
- Computed by the OTDR's built-in ORL function, which integrates the full backscatter trace and sums all Fresnel reflections.
- Formula (linear): $\text{ORL}_{lin} = \sum_i 10^{R_{F,i}/10} + \alpha_{RBS} L$.
- Formula (dB): $\text{ORL}_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_i 10^{R_{F,i}/10} + \alpha_{RBS} L \right)$.
- Typical spec: link ORL $\ge 27$ dB (digital) or $\ge 30$ dB (analog video). High ORL prevents laser instability in the transmitter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on OTDR-application questions:**
>
> 1. **Adding dB values directly** when computing ORL or total link loss. Always convert to linear, sum, then convert back to dB. *(−2 marks)*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the OTDR distance equation. The recorded time is round-trip. *(−1 mark)*
> 3. **Using phase index $n$** instead of group index $n_g$ in distance calculations. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Reading the spike amplitude as splice loss** for a reflective connector. Always read the *baseline step*, not the spike. *(−2 marks)*
> 5. **Skipping the averaging explanation** in the block diagram. The averager is essential — without it, the trace is buried in noise. *(−1 mark)*
> 6. **Confusing the two-point method with the LSM**. Two-point method = two cursors; LSM = linear regression over a window. *(−1 mark)*
> 7. **Not stating units** in the final answer for attenuation. Always **dB/km**. *(−0.5 mark)*
> 8. **Failing to mention that macrobend loss is wavelength-dependent** when discussing bend-detection applications. *(−1 mark)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — OTDR Applications (Module 2)**

- **OTDR** = single-ended, non-destructive fiber diagnostic tool using Rayleigh backscatter and Fresnel reflections.
- **Trace axes:** horizontal = distance (km) via $L = ct/(2n_g)$; vertical = backscatter power (dB).
- **Distance formula:** $L = \dfrac{c \, t}{2 \, n_g}$ — remember the **factor of 2** (round trip) and use **group index** $n_g \approx 1.4682$ at 1550 nm for SMF-28.
- **Fiber attenuation** (Two-Point Method): $\alpha = \dfrac{P_1 - P_2}{z_2 - z_1}$ in **dB/km**.
- **Fiber attenuation** (LSM): linear regression of backscatter level over a sliding window; more accurate.
- **Splice / event loss** = step height in dB on the trace.
- **Connector loss** = baseline step (ignore the spike).
- **Reflectance** = spike amplitude vs backscatter level; $R = 10\log_{10}(P_{ref}/P_{inc})$ in dB.
- **ORL** = $-10\log_{10}$ of the linear sum of all back-reflections; high ORL ($\ge 27$ to $30$ dB) is required for stable laser operation.
- **Macrobend** = wavelength-dependent loss; worst at **1625 nm**, used as a diagnostic wavelength.
- **Fresnel reflectance** of an open cleaved fiber $\approx -14.4$ dB; APC connector $\approx -65$ dB; PC connector $\approx -40$ to $-50$ dB.
- **Applications of OTDR:** (1) attenuation measurement, (2) splice/connector loss, (3) fault localization, (4) length verification, (5) reflectance, (6) ORL, (7) macrobend detection, (8) in-service monitoring (1625/1650 nm), (9) link certification per TIA-568 / ISO 11801, (10) submarine cable commissioning.
- **Averaging** is essential — SNR improves as $\sqrt{N}$ for $N$ accumulated traces.
- **Launch reference cable** is needed to overcome the OTDR's **dead zone** at the front panel.
- **Trace storage** uses the standardized **`.sor` (Telcordia SR-4731)** file format — important for warranty and trend comparison.
- **Pulse-width trade-off:** short pulse → better spatial resolution but lower dynamic range; long pulse → higher dynamic range but worse resolution.
- **Standard test wavelengths:** 850 / 1300 nm (multimode); 1310 / 1550 / 1625 / 1650 nm (single-mode).
- **KTU 2024 Module 2 must-know keywords:** Rayleigh backscatter, Fresnel reflection, dead zone, dynamic range, two-point method, least-squares method, ORL, reflectance, macrobend, launch cable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LEDs and LDs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** LEDs and LDs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Light Emitting Diodes (LEDs)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a semiconductor p–n junction device, usually fabricated as a **Double Hetrostructure (DH)** or **Quantum Well (QW)** device, that converts injected electrical current into incoherent light through the mechanism of **spontaneous emission** of photons during electron–hole recombination across the direct bandgap of the active region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LEDs used in optical communication are **NOT** indicator LEDs. They are high-radiance, high-speed, double-heterostructure devices designed for efficient coupling into 50/125 µm or 62.5/125 µm multimode fibers at the 850 nm, 1300 nm, or 1550 nm windows.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Rainstorm of Photons":**
Imagine a crowded room where everyone has a small ball (an electron in the conduction band) and there is a low platform (the valence band). When the lights are turned on (forward bias), people drop their balls off the platform *independently and randomly*. Each drop emits a tiny flash of light. No two flashes happen at the same instant, in the same direction, or with the same color. This is **spontaneous emission** — the heart of LED operation. The light waves are *incoherent* (random phase), *broadband* (mixed wavelengths), and travel in *all directions* (large divergence).

### 1.2 Laser Diodes (LDs)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A **Laser Diode (LD)** or **Semiconductor Laser** is a forward-biased p–n heterojunction device that produces **coherent, monochromatic, and highly directional** optical radiation by the process of **stimulated emission** under conditions of **population inversion** inside a resonant **Fabry–Perot (FP) optical cavity**, often refined into **DFB, DBR, or VCSEL** geometries.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Military March of Photons":**
Now imagine the same room, but with a strict drill sergeant. When one person drops a ball and shouts "FALL IN!", every nearby person drops their ball at the *exact same moment, in the exact same direction, shouting the exact same word*. This cascade — one photon triggering the emission of an identical photon — is **stimulated emission**. The photons are *coherent* (in phase), *monochromatic* (one wavelength), and travel as a *narrow, directional beam*. The drill sergeant is the optical resonator, and the rhythm is the cavity resonance.

### 1.3 Photometric vs. Radiometric Units Used in KTU Problems

> [!NOTE]
> KTU questions frequently mix these units. Remember: **radiometric** units deal with raw power (Watts), while **photometric** units deal with the eye's response (lumens).

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Used For |
|----------|--------|------|----------|
| Radiant Flux (Power) | Φ or P | **Watt (W)** | LEDs, LDs, optical power |
| Luminous Flux | F | **Lumen (lm)** | Visible display LEDs |
| Luminous Efficacy | ηᵥ | **lm/W** | Efficiency comparison |
| Radiant Intensity | I | **W/sr** | Output per solid angle |
| Luminance | L | **cd/m²** | Surface brightness |

**Standard Constants to Memorize (KTU Board Favourite):**
- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- Boltzmann's constant: **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Electron charge: **q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J
- Wavelength–frequency relation: **λ = c/ν**
- Photon energy: **E = hν = hc/λ**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Einstein Processes — Foundation of All Light Sources

Every semiconductor optical source is governed by the interaction of photons with a two-level atomic system. Einstein identified **three** fundamental processes:

**Step 1 — Absorption:**
An electron in the lower energy state E₁ absorbs a photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ and is excited to the upper state E₂. The rate of upward transitions is proportional to the photon density ρ(ν) and is governed by the **Einstein B coefficient B₁₂**.

**Step 2 — Spontaneous Emission (LED Mechanism):**
The excited electron in E₂ falls *randomly* to E₁, releasing a photon. This happens *without* any external trigger. Each photon has a random phase, direction, and polarization. The rate is governed by the **A coefficient A₂₁** and is independent of the radiation field.

**Step 3 — Stimulated Emission (LD Mechanism):**
An incoming photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ "triggers" an excited electron in E₂ to drop to E₁, emitting a *second photon* that is **identical in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization** to the triggering photon. The rate is governed by **B₂₁** and is proportional to ρ(ν).

**Einstein's Crucial Relations (Derivable from Thermodynamic Equilibrium):**
- B₁₂ = B₂₁
- A₂₁ / B₂₁ = (8πhν³) / c³

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ratio A₂₁/B₂₁ ∝ ν³ tells us why **higher-frequency (shorter wavelength) lasers are harder to build** — spontaneous emission dominates, and population inversion becomes much more demanding.

### 2.2 LED — Detailed Physics

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Forward Biasing:** The p–n junction is forward biased, injecting electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side into the active (depletion) region.
2. **Carrier Confinement:** In a **Double Heterojunction (DH) LED**, the active layer has a *smaller bandgap* sandwiched between two *larger bandgap* cladding layers. This creates both **electrical confinement** (potential well for carriers) and **optical confinement** (higher refractive index core, like a fiber).
3. **Recombination:** Electrons and holes recombine across the direct bandgap. Because GaAs, AlGaAs, InGaAsP are **direct bandgap** materials, recombination is *radiative* (emits light) rather than non-radiative (heat).
4. **Photon Generation:** Each recombination event emits a photon of energy **E = hc/λ ≈ E_g** (bandgap energy), with λ(µm) = 1.24 / E_g(eV).
5. **Photon Escape:** The emitted photons travel to the surface. Only those within the **escape cone** (defined by total internal reflection at the semiconductor–air interface, with critical angle ≈ 17°) can leave. This is why LEDs are fabricated with **domed lenses** or **etched wells** (Burrus-type).

**Two LED Geometries (KTU Standard):**

| Feature | Surface Emitter (Burrus) | Edge Emitter (ELED) |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Emission Direction | Perpendicular to junction | Parallel to junction |
| Active Area | Small (~50 µm dia) | Stripe |
| Beam Divergence | **Lambertian (~120°)** | Narrower (~30° × 120°) |
| Coupling Efficiency into MMF | Moderate | Higher |
| Spectral Width | 30–60 nm | 30–60 nm |
| Modulation Bandwidth | Lower (~50–100 MHz) | Higher (~100–200 MHz) |
| Output Power | Lower | Higher |

### 2.3 LD — Detailed Physics

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. **Heavy Forward Bias:** Current density J > J_th (threshold) — typically 1–5 kA/cm².
2. **Population Inversion:** The number of electrons in the conduction band exceeds the number in the valence band in the active region. This is the *necessary* condition for optical amplification.
3. **Optical Resonator:** Two parallel, perfectly cleaved (or coated) facets act as mirrors. The cavity length L typically = 250–500 µm, with facet reflectivity R ≈ 0.3 (cleaved) to 0.95 (HR coated).
4. **Optical Feedback:** Photons traveling along the cavity axis bounce back and forth, stimulating more emissions, building up an intense, coherent standing wave.
5. **Threshold Condition:** Optical gain equals total losses (mirror losses + internal absorption + free carrier loss). Above threshold, output power rises **linearly** with current.

**LD Threshold Condition (the most important equation):**

$$g_{th} = \alpha_i + \frac{1}{2L} \ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Where:
- $g_{th}$ = threshold gain per unit length (cm⁻¹)
- $\alpha_i$ = internal loss coefficient (cm⁻¹)
- $L$ = cavity length (cm)
- $R_1, R_2$ = facet reflectivities
- $(1/2L) \ln(1/R_1R_2)$ = mirror loss

**Population Inversion Condition:**
Stimulated emission rate must exceed absorption rate. This requires:

$$E_{Fc} - E_{Fv} > E_g = h\nu$$

i.e., the **quasi-Fermi level separation** inside the active region must exceed the bandgap.

**Three LD Geometries (KTU Standard):**

| Geometry | Cavity Type | Spectral Width | Application |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| Fabry–Perot (FP) | Cleaved facets | 1–5 nm (multi-mode) | Older systems, LAN |
| **DFB (Distributed Feedback)** | Bragg grating inside cavity | **< 0.1 nm** | Long-haul, DWDM |
| **DBR (Distributed Bragg Reflector)** | Bragg grating as mirrors | < 0.1 nm | Tunable lasers |
| **VCSEL (Vertical Cavity)** | Bragg mirrors top & bottom | < 0.5 nm | Gigabit Ethernet, MMF |

> [!NOTE]
> **DFB vs DBR:** In a **DFB**, the Bragg grating runs *alongside* the entire active region (distributed feedback throughout). In a **DBR**, the gratings are at the *ends*, acting as frequency-selective mirrors. DFB is the workhorse of telecom.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_g(eV) = \dfrac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)}$ | Bandgap–wavelength relation | eV, µm |
| 2 | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules |
| 3 | $P_{out} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \dfrac{h\nu}{q}(I - I_{th})$ | LD output power above threshold | W |
| 4 | $g_{th} = \alpha_i + \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$ | LD threshold gain | cm⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\eta_D = \dfrac{q}{h\nu}\dfrac{dP}{dI}$ | External differential quantum efficiency | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\eta_i \cdot \eta_D = \eta_{ext}$ | Internal × differential = external | dimensionless |
| 7 | $I_{th} = I_0 \exp(T_0/T)$ | Threshold current vs temperature | A |
| 8 | $\Delta\nu_{LED} \approx \dfrac{kT \cdot c}{\lambda^2}$ | LED spectral width (full FWHM) | Hz |
| 9 | $\Delta\nu_{LD} = \dfrac{h\nu \cdot \Delta\nu_{cavity}}{P_{out}}$ | Schawlow–Townes linewidth | Hz |
| 10 | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | LED modulation bandwidth (carrier lifetime limited) | Hz |
| 11 | $P_{coupled} = P_{source} \cdot \eta_{coupling}$ | Power coupled into fiber | W |
| 12 | $\eta_{coupling} \approx (NA)^2$ | For LED to MMF (Lambertian source) | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical pipe `|` symbol is mathematically forbidden in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. This is enforced by the KTU e-content standard for clean rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEDs** dominate **short-reach, low-cost** links: Ethernet (100 Mbps–1 Gbps), PON (Passive Optical Networks) for FTTH, in-car networks, and industrial sensors where temperature stability and longevity matter more than bandwidth.
- **LDs (DFB/VCSEL)** are the heart of **long-haul DWDM systems**, **data center interconnects**, **5G fronthaul**, and **LiDAR** — anywhere you need narrow linewidth, high modulation speed (>10 GHz), and high coupling into single-mode fiber.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: The LED Wavelength from Bandgap Energy

**Problem Setup (KTU-typical):** A GaAs LED has a bandgap energy of E_g = 1.42 eV. Find the peak emission wavelength λ in nanometres.

**Step 1 — Recognize the physics:** The peak emission occurs when a conduction band electron recombines with a valence band hole, releasing a photon of energy equal to the bandgap:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g$$

**Step 2 — Isolate λ:**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values, but use a convenient form:** Recall that 1 eV = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. It is far easier in optical communication to use the engineering shortcut:

$$\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(eV)}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1.24}{1.42} = 0.8732 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Convert to nm and round:**

$$\lambda = 873.2 \, nm \approx 873 \, nm$$

This is the famous **first transmission window (850 nm)** of optical fiber. **Answer: λ ≈ 870 nm.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: LD Threshold Condition

**Problem Setup:** A GaAs laser diode has cavity length L = 300 µm, internal loss α_i = 10 cm⁻¹, and both facets are uncoated cleaved (R₁ = R₂ = 0.32). Calculate the threshold gain g_th.

**Step 1 — Recall the threshold condition:** At threshold, the round-trip optical gain exactly equals the round-trip optical loss. The standard form is:

$$g_{th} = \alpha_i + \alpha_{mirrors}$$

**Step 2 — Express mirror loss.** For a Fabry–Perot cavity, after one round trip, the surviving fraction is $R_1 R_2 \cdot e^{-2\alpha_i L}$. At threshold, the gain must compensate this:

$$e^{2g_{th} L} \cdot R_1 R_2 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural log of both sides:**

$$2 g_{th} L + \ln(R_1 R_2) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for g_th:**

$$g_{th} = -\frac{\ln(R_1 R_2)}{2L} = \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

Combining with internal loss gives the master equation:

$$g_{th} = \alpha_i + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert L to cm:** L = 300 µm = 300 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 3 × 10⁻² cm.

**Step 6 — Compute mirror loss:**

$$\alpha_{mirrors} = \frac{1}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-2}} \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.32 \times 0.32}\right)$$

$$\alpha_{mirrors} = \frac{1}{0.06} \ln\left(\frac{1}{0.1024}\right) = \frac{1}{0.06} \times 2.279$$

$$\alpha_{mirrors} = 37.98 \, cm^{-1}$$

**Step 7 — Add internal loss:**

$$g_{th} = 10 + 37.98 = 47.98 \, cm^{-1} \approx 48 \, cm^{-1}$$

**Answer: g_th ≈ 48 cm⁻¹.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Output Power Above Threshold

**Problem Setup:** A 1550 nm InGaAsP laser has threshold current I_th = 20 mA, external differential quantum efficiency η_D = 0.4, and is driven at I = 60 mA. Find the optical output power from one facet.

**Step 1 — Recall the linear P–I equation above threshold:**

$$P_{out} = \eta_D \cdot \frac{h\nu}{q}(I - I_{th})$$

**Step 2 — Compute hν at 1550 nm:**

$$h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$h\nu = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.282 \times 10^{-19} \, J$$

Alternatively, use the KTU engineering shortcut:

$$\frac{h\nu}{q}(V) = \frac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)} = \frac{1.24}{1.55} = 0.8 \, V$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the power equation:**

$$P_{out} = 0.4 \times 0.8 \times (60 - 20) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 40 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{out} = 12.8 \times 10^{-3} \, W = 12.8 \, mW$$

**Step 4 — Convert to dBm (KTU loves dBm):**

$$P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{12.8 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(12.8)$$

$$P_{dBm} = 10 \times 1.107 = 11.07 \, dBm$$

**Answer: P_out ≈ 12.8 mW ≈ 11.07 dBm from one facet.**

---

### 3.4 Derivation: LED Coupling Efficiency into Multimode Fiber

**Problem Setup:** An LED with Lambertian emission pattern is butt-coupled to a step-index MMF of core diameter d = 50 µm and NA = 0.2. The LED emitting area diameter D = 50 µm. Find the coupling efficiency.

**Step 1 — Lambertian source coupling formula:**

$$\eta_{coupling} = (NA)^2 \quad \text{(when LED area} \le \text{fiber core area)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\eta_{coupling} = (0.2)^2 = 0.04 = 4\%$$

**Step 3 — In dB:**

$$\eta_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.04) = -13.98 \, dB \approx -14 \, dB$$

**Engineering Insight:** This is *exactly* why LDs are preferred for long-distance links — a laser diode with NA ≈ 0.1 and matched spot size can achieve > 50% coupling (–3 dB), giving a **~10 dB advantage** over an LED at the launch alone. The rest of the link budget is dominated by fiber attenuation and dispersion.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LED vs LD

| Parameter | LED | LD (DFB / VCSEL) |
|-----------|-----|------------------|
| Emission Mechanism | Spontaneous | Stimulated |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Coherent |
| Spectral Width (Δλ) | 30–60 nm | < 0.1 nm (DFB), ~0.5 nm (VCSEL) |
| Output Power (into fiber) | –20 to –10 dBm | 0 to +13 dBm |
| Modulation Bandwidth | 50–200 MHz | > 10 GHz |
| Threshold Current | None | 5–50 mA |
| Temperature Sensitivity | Low | High (needs TEC/cooler) |
| Linearity | Good (analog) | Less linear (kinks) |
| Lifetime | > 10⁶ hours | > 10⁵ hours |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Drive Circuit | Simple | Complex (with cooler) |
| Fiber Type | Multimode (MMF) | Single-mode (SMF) or MMF (VCSEL) |
| Eye Safety | Safer | Class 3R/3B caution |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: LED Cross-Section & Carrier Confinement (Double Heterojunction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["P Contact Metal Layer"] --> nodeB["P Cladding Layer Large Bandgap AlGaAs"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Active Region Small Bandgap GaAs Light Emits Here"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["N Cladding Layer Large Bandgap AlGaAs"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["N Contact Metal Layer"]
    nodeF["Injected Electrons Confinement"] --> nodeC
    nodeG["Injected Holes Confinement"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH["Photon Emission 870 nm Spontaneous"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Epoxy Dome Lens Escape Cone"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Multimode Fiber Core 50 um"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading:** The AlGaAs cladding layers have a *larger bandgap* than the GaAs active region, creating a potential well that traps both electrons and holes. They also have a *lower refractive index*, creating an optical waveguide that guides emitted photons toward the fiber. This is the **double confinement** that makes DH-LEDs vastly superior to homojunction LEDs.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: LD Fabry-Perot Cavity & Threshold Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Forward Bias Current I"] --> nodeB["Active Region InGaAsP Population Inversion"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Spontaneous Photons Initial Seed"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Optical Cavity Length L 300 um"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Left Facet Mirror R1 0.32 Cleaved"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Right Facet Mirror R2 0.32 Cleaved"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Reflected Photon Stimulates More Emission"]
    nodeG --> nodeB
    nodeF --> nodeH["Output Coherent Beam 1550 nm"]
    nodeI["Below Threshold I less than Ith LED like emission"] --> nodeJ["Output Power mW"]
    nodeK["Above Threshold I greater than Ith Laser Action"] --> nodeJ
```

### 4.3 P-I Characteristics (Light vs Current Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Drive Current I mA"] --> nodeB{"Compare to Threshold Ith"}
    nodeB -- "I less than Ith" --> nodeC["Region 1 Spontaneous Emission Broad LED like"]
    nodeB -- "I greater than Ith" --> nodeD["Region 2 Stimulated Emission Coherent LD Mode"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Output Power Low and Linear with I small slope"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Output Power Steep Linear Slope eta D h nu by q"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Kink Region Possible Nonlinearity"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Thermal Rolloff Output Saturates"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The P-I curve's slope above threshold is $\eta_D \cdot (h\nu/q)$. Drawing this curve with proper labels (I_th, slope, kink) is worth 2–3 marks in ESE.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of Three Einstein Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Photon in Energy hnu"] --> nodeB["Atom in Ground State E1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Absorption Atom Jumps to E2"]
    nodeA2["Excited Atom at E2"] --> nodeD["Spontaneous Emission Random Photon Out LED"]
    nodeA3["Incoming Photon hnu"] --> nodeE["Excited Atom at E2"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Stimulated Emission Identical Photon Out Twice as Intense LD"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Amplification Chain Stimulated Emission Cascades"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Coherent Laser Beam"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between spontaneous emission and stimulated emission. Which mechanism is responsible for light emission in an LED and an LD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Spontaneous Emission | Stimulated Emission |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Trigger | None — random | Required incoming photon |
| Phase | Random | Identical to trigger |
| Direction | Random | Same as trigger |
| Wavelength | Broad (spectral width) | Single (monochromatic) |
| Mechanism | LED | LD (Laser Diode) |

Spontaneous emission is the **random** decay of an excited electron with no external trigger — the emitted photon has random phase, polarization, and direction. This is the **mechanism in LEDs**, producing incoherent, broadband light.

Stimulated emission occurs when an **incoming photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁** forces an excited electron to drop, emitting a second photon **identical** in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization. Under **population inversion** and optical feedback, this is the **mechanism in LDs**, producing coherent, narrow-linewidth laser light.

*(Award 1 mark for tabulated difference, 1 mark for LED mechanism, 1 mark for LD mechanism.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define threshold current in a laser diode. What happens to the output power below and above threshold?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Threshold current (I_th)** is the minimum forward-bias current at which the optical gain in the active region equals the total optical loss (internal + mirror loss), establishing **population inversion** and sustained lasing action.

- **Below threshold (I < I_th):** Output is dominated by **spontaneous emission**. Power is very low (~µW), broadband, and behaves much like an LED. The P–I slope is small.
- **Above threshold (I > I_th):** Output is dominated by **stimulated emission**. Power rises **linearly and steeply** with current. The light becomes coherent, monochromatic, and highly directional.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark below threshold, 1 mark above threshold.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Question Bank (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the working of a **Double Heterojunction (DH) LED**. Discuss the role of bandgap and refractive index differences in carrier and optical confinement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **threshold condition** for a Fabry–Perot semiconductor laser. An InGaAsP laser has cavity length L = 250 µm, internal loss α_i = 8 cm⁻¹, and facet reflectivities R₁ = 0.95 (HR coated) and R₂ = 0.32 (cleaved). Calculate the **threshold gain** g_th. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks:**

**Energy Band Diagram (textual representation, 1 mark):**

```
   P-AlGaAs      GaAs (Active)    N-AlGaAs
   E_g = 1.8 eV   E_g = 1.42 eV    E_g = 1.8 eV
   n ≈ 3.4        n ≈ 3.6          n ≈ 3.4
   ▲▲▲▲▲▲         ▼▼▼▼▼▼           ▲▲▲▲▲▲
   |              |   ◄— e⁻ + h⁺ recombine
   | E_c          |                 | E_c
   |              |  ◄— Active      |
   |              |    Region        |
   | E_v          |                 | E_v
```

**Working — 4 marks:**
1. The DH-LED consists of a **p-AlGaAs / GaAs / n-AlGaAs** sandwich. The middle GaAs layer has both a **smaller bandgap** (1.42 eV) and a **higher refractive index** (3.6) than the surrounding AlGaAs (bandgap 1.8 eV, n = 3.4).
2. **Carrier Confinement:** Under forward bias, electrons from the n-AlGaAs and holes from the p-AlGaAs fall into the GaAs potential well (because the larger bandgap of the claddings acts as energy barriers). This is **electrical confinement** — carriers are trapped in the active region where they recombine radiatively. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Optical Confinement:** Because the active layer has a higher refractive index than the claddings, emitted photons undergo total internal reflection at the heterojunctions and are guided along the active region toward the emitting facet. This is **optical confinement**. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Light Extraction:** Only photons within the escape cone (critical angle ~17° at the semiconductor–air interface) can exit. Surface-emitter (Burrus) LEDs use an etched well and domed epoxy lens to maximize this. *(1 mark)*

**Advantages over homojunction LED:** thinner active region → higher carrier density → higher radiative efficiency; better heat dissipation; faster modulation. *(1 mark)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a Fabry–Perot cavity of length L with facet reflectivities R₁ and R₂. A photon of intensity I₀ starts at one facet, travels distance L, reflects at the far facet with reflectivity R₂, travels back L, reflects at the first facet with R₁, and so on. The internal loss α_i causes the intensity to decay as $e^{-\alpha_i x}$.

**Round-trip condition** for sustained oscillation (gain compensates loss):

$$R_1 R_2 \cdot e^{-2\alpha_i L} \cdot e^{2 g L} = 1$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$2 g L + \ln(R_1 R_2) - 2 \alpha_i L = 0$$

Solving for the threshold gain g_th:

$$g_{th} = \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2L}\ln(R_1 R_2) = \alpha_i + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

The first term $\alpha_i$ is the **internal loss coefficient**. The second term is the **mirror loss** $\alpha_{mirrors}$.

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: L = 250 µm = 250 × 10⁻⁴ cm = 2.5 × 10⁻² cm; α_i = 8 cm⁻¹; R₁ = 0.95; R₂ = 0.32.

**Step 1 — Compute R₁ × R₂:**

$$R_1 R_2 = 0.95 \times 0.32 = 0.304$$

**Step 2 — Compute 1/(R₁R₂):**

$$\frac{1}{R_1 R_2} = \frac{1}{0.304} = 3.289$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural log:**

$$\ln(3.289) = 1.190$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mirror loss term:**

$$\frac{1}{2L}\ln\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) = \frac{1.190}{2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{1.190}{0.05} = 23.8 \, cm^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Add internal loss:**

$$g_{th} = 8 + 23.8 = 31.8 \, cm^{-1}$$

**Answer: g_th ≈ 31.8 cm⁻¹.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake:** Forgetting to convert L from µm to cm. 250 µm = 2.5 × 10⁻² cm. Losing 2 marks for this is the #1 reason.
> 2. **Sign Error:** Some students write $g_{th} = \alpha_i - (1/2L)\ln(R_1R_2)$. The minus sign is wrong; the master equation uses $+\frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1R_2)$.
> 3. **Forgetting I_th:** The threshold condition *assumes* population inversion. A complete answer should mention that the bias must exceed I_th for the derivation to hold.
> 4. **DFB vs FP Confusion:** This derivation is for a Fabry–Perot LD. For a DFB, the Bragg grating provides wavelength-selective feedback, and the threshold condition is modified.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a semiconductor laser diode (LD)** with a neat diagram. Compare its performance with an LED. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs laser diode emits at λ = 870 nm. The threshold current is I_th = 30 mA, and the external differential quantum efficiency is η_D = 0.3. Calculate: (i) the output power at I = 100 mA, and (ii) the corresponding photon flux (photons per second) emitted from the laser. **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (a) — 7 marks:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A semiconductor laser diode is a heavily doped p–n heterojunction (typically **DH AlGaAs/GaAs/AlGaAs** for 870 nm, or **InP/InGaAsP/InP** for 1300/1550 nm). The two end facets are cleaved along crystal planes to act as partially reflecting mirrors (R ≈ 0.3 for uncoated facets, or R ≈ 0.95 for HR-coated). The cavity length L is typically 250–500 µm. The active region is a thin (0.1–0.2 µm) layer of smaller bandgap material.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Heavy forward bias (current density J > J_th ≈ 1–5 kA/cm²) is applied. This injects a high density of electrons and holes into the active region.
2. **Population inversion** is achieved when the electron density in the conduction band exceeds the hole density in the valence band within the active region.
3. Initial **spontaneous** photons travel along the cavity axis and trigger **stimulated emission** from excited carriers.
4. The cleaved facets reflect these photons back into the cavity, producing a chain reaction of stimulated emission. The light builds up coherently as a standing wave.
5. Above threshold, a fraction of the light is emitted through the partially transmitting output facet as a **coherent, narrow, highly directional laser beam**.

**LED vs LD Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | LED | LD |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Emission | Spontaneous | Stimulated |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Coherent |
| Spectral Width | 30–60 nm | < 1 nm (DFB < 0.1 nm) |
| Output Power | Low (~mW) | Higher (~10–100 mW) |
| Threshold | None | I_th exists |
| Speed | ~100 MHz | > 10 GHz |
| Linewidth | Broad | Very narrow |
| Drive Circuit | Simple | Complex |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

---

**MODEL SOLUTION (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** λ = 870 nm = 0.87 µm, I_th = 30 mA, η_D = 0.3, I = 100 mA.

**(i) Output Power (4 marks):**

The P–I equation above threshold:

$$P_{out} = \eta_D \cdot \frac{h\nu}{q}(I - I_{th})$$

**Step 1 — Compute hν/q at 870 nm:**

$$\frac{h\nu}{q} = \frac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)} = \frac{1.24}{0.87} = 1.425 \, V$$

**Step 2 — Compute (I − I_th):**

$$I - I_{th} = 100 - 30 = 70 \, mA = 70 \times 10^{-3} \, A$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$P_{out} = 0.3 \times 1.425 \times 70 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.3 \times 0.09975 = 0.0299 \, W \approx 29.9 \, mW$$

**Answer: P_out ≈ 30 mW** (from one facet, assuming both facets are identical).

**(ii) Photon Flux (3 marks):**

Photon flux Φ_p is the number of photons emitted per second:

$$\Phi_p = \frac{P_{out}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{out} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 1 — Use the engineering form:**

$$\Phi_p = \frac{P_{out} \cdot \lambda(\mu m)}{1.989 \times 10^{-25}} \quad \text{(with P in W, λ in µm)}$$

Or directly:

$$\Phi_p = \frac{P_{out}}{(h\nu/q) \cdot q}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$h\nu = (h\nu/q) \cdot q = 1.425 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 2.28 \times 10^{-19} \, J$$

**Step 3 — Compute flux:**

$$\Phi_p = \frac{29.9 \times 10^{-3}}{2.28 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.31 \times 10^{17} \, \text{photons/second}$$

**Answer: Φ_p ≈ 1.31 × 10¹⁷ photons/s.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** hν/q in volts, but multiplying by (I − I_th) in mA gives milliwatts, not watts. Be careful: (V) × (A) = (W). So 1.425 × 70 mA = 99.75 mW before η_D.
> 2. **Forgetting η_D:** Always multiply by the external differential quantum efficiency. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing hν/q with hν:** The shortcut hν/q = 1.24/λ(µm) gives a value in *volts* — students often write it in joules by mistake.
> 4. **Photon flux units:** Always express in photons/s, never photons/s/cm² (the latter is a flux density, not a total flux).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Read 5 minutes before the KTU exam:**

- **LED = Spontaneous Emission.** No threshold, no coherence, broad spectrum (30–60 nm), safer, cheaper, used in MMF LAN/PON.
- **LD = Stimulated Emission.** Requires threshold current, population inversion, optical resonator (Fabry–Perot / DFB / VCSEL), produces coherent narrow-linewidth light.
- **Einstein relations:** $B_{12} = B_{21}$ and $A_{21}/B_{21} = 8\pi h\nu^3/c^3$. Higher ν → harder to lase.
- **Bandgap–Wavelength shortcut:** $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(eV)$. Memorize this — used in 80% of KTU numericals.
- **DH structure:** Two key roles — (1) **electrical confinement** via bandgap difference, (2) **optical confinement** via refractive index difference.
- **Threshold condition:** $g_{th} = \alpha_i + (1/2L)\ln(1/R_1R_2)$. Always convert L to **cm** before substituting.
- **P–I equation above threshold:** $P_{out} = \eta_D \cdot (h\nu/q)(I - I_{th})$. Output is **linear** above I_th.
- **DFB vs DBR vs VCSEL:** DFB has grating *inside* active region (distributed feedback); DBR has grating at *ends* (acts as mirror); VCSEL emits *vertically* with Bragg mirrors top and bottom.
- **Coupling efficiency:** LED → MMF: $\eta \approx (NA)^2 \approx 4\%$ (for NA = 0.2). LD → SMF: > 50% achievable.
- **Modulation bandwidth LED:** Limited by carrier recombination lifetime τ. $f_{3dB} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- **Temperature:** I_th doubles every 10–30°C rise; LDs need Thermo-Electric Coolers (TEC). LEDs are more stable.
- **Materials:** GaAlAs/GaAs for 850 nm; InGaAsP/InP for 1300 nm and 1550 nm.
- **Eye safety:** LEDs inherently safer; LDs are Class 3R/3B — never look into a live fiber connector.

> **Best of luck for your KTU examination — focus on diagrams, formula derivations, and numericals in Part B!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** General Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Optical Fiber — General Structure**
> An **optical fiber** is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide composed of a central **core** (radius $a$, refractive index $n_1$) surrounded by a **cladding** (radius $b$, refractive index $n_2 < n_1$), with optional protective polymer **buffer/jacket** layers. The *general structure* of an optical fiber refers to the geometric and refractive-index configuration that determines how optical modes propagate, the numerical aperture, and the bandwidth-distance product.

The **general structure** of an optical fiber can be classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

1. **Refractive-Index Profile (RIP)** — how $n(r)$ varies with radial distance $r$ from the fiber axis.
   - **Step-Index Fiber (SIF)**: $n(r) = n_1$ for $r < a$, and $n(r) = n_2$ for $a \leq r \leq b$ (abrupt transition).
   - **Graded-Index Fiber (GIF)**: $n(r)$ varies continuously (typically parabolically) with $r$.

2. **Mode Count (V-Parameter Regime)** — the number of guided modes supported.
   - **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)**: $V > 2.405$, supports hundreds of modes.
   - **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)**: $V < 2.405$, supports only the fundamental LP₀₁ mode.

The combined classification yields **four canonical structures** studied in KTU Module 3:

| # | Structure | Abbreviation | Typical Core Diameter |
|---|-----------|--------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber | SI-MMF | 50 / 62.5 / 100 µm |
| 2 | Step-Index Single-Mode Fiber | SI-SMF | 8–10 µm |
| 3 | Graded-Index Multi-Mode Fiber | GI-MMF | 50 / 62.5 µm |
| 4 | (Plastic / Special) — context dependent | — | 980 µm (POF) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Highway & Toll-Booth" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Think of the optical fiber as a **multi-lane highway tunnel through a mountain**.
> - The **core** is the *tunnel itself* (high-index glass, like clear air inside).
> - The **cladding** is the *mountain rock* (lower-index glass).
> - The **jacket** is the *concrete reinforcement* protecting the tunnel from weather and impact.
> - A **light ray** entering at a steep angle (large angle with respect to the axis) is like a car entering a sharp curve — it crashes into the cladding wall and is *reflected back* (Total Internal Reflection) — keeping it trapped inside the core.
> - A **multi-mode** fiber is a *wide tunnel* allowing many cars (rays) to travel simultaneously but at different speeds → leads to **traffic jams** (= modal dispersion).
> - A **single-mode** fiber is a *narrow, single-lane tunnel* where only one "car" can pass at a time → no traffic jams, but the entrance is restricted (small core, precise alignment).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following are *standard* parameters universally used in KTU Optical Communication problems:
> - **Free-space wavelength** $\lambda_0 = 1550 \text{ nm}$ (C-band) or **1310 nm** (O-band) — both **standard telecom windows**.
> - **Speed of light in vacuum** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
> - **First zero of Bessel function** $J_0$ (cutoff condition) = **2.405**.
> - **Refractive index of pure silica** $n_{\text{SiO}_2} \approx 1.45$ at 1550 nm.
> - **V-parameter cutoff** for single-mode operation: $V_c = 2.405$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Structural Breakdown

A real optical fiber is a **concentric cylindrical structure** with up to **six distinct layers**:

1. **Core** (radius $a$, index $n_1$): Carries the optical signal. Doped silica (e.g., with GeO₂) raises $n_1$.
2. **Inner Cladding** (radius $b$, index $n_2$): Provides the lower-index boundary that confines light via TIR. Typically pure silica.
3. **Outer Cladding / Coating** (radius $c$, index $n_3$): Optional second protective glass layer.
4. **Primary Coating** (acrylic, soft): Buffers micro-bending losses.
5. **Secondary Coating** (hard polymer): Provides mechanical strength.
6. **Jacket / Buffer** (color-coded PVC or LSZH): Final environmental protection; **color-code standard**: Yellow = SMF, Orange = MMF 50 µm, Aqua = MMF 50 µm laser-optimized (OM3/OM4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **relative index difference** $\Delta$ is a key dimensionless design parameter:
> $$\Delta = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} \approx \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \quad (\text{for small } \Delta)$$

For typical telecom fibers, $\Delta \approx 0.003$ to $0.01$ (i.e., 0.3% to 1%).

## 2.2 The Four Canonical General Structures — Detailed Logic

### (a) Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (SI-MMF)
- **How it works**: Sharp index step at $r = a$. Light rays travel in **zig-zag meridional paths** with discrete bounce angles.
- **Why it matters**: Simple to manufacture; cheap; used in LANs, data centers, short-haul links.
- **Drawback**: Severe **intermodal dispersion** (≈ 15–30 ns/km) limits bandwidth-distance product to ≈ 10–100 MHz·km.

### (b) Step-Index Single-Mode Fiber (SI-SMF)
- **How it works**: Very small core ($a \approx 4$–5 µm) and low $\Delta$ ensures $V < 2.405$. Only the **fundamental LP₀₁ mode** propagates; no zig-zag rays.
- **Why it matters**: Eliminates intermodal dispersion; used for long-haul (100s of km), high-bit-rate (>100 Gbps) links.
- **Drawback**: Precise splicing/connector alignment required; more expensive.

### (c) Graded-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (GI-MMF)
- **How it works**: $n(r)$ decreases *continuously* (parabolically) from $n_1$ at $r = 0$ to $n_2$ at $r = a$. Rays follow **sinusoidal/helical** paths. Higher-angle rays travel *faster* in the lower-index outer regions, compensating path-length differences.
- **Why it matters**: Reduces intermodal dispersion by ≈ 100× compared to SI-MMF; bandwidth-distance product ≈ 0.5–10 GHz·km. Standard for 1 Gbps Ethernet (OM1/OM2/OM3/OM4).

### (d) Specialty / Plastic Optical Fiber (POF)
- **How it works**: Large core (≈ 980 µm), $n_1 \approx 1.49$ (PMMA core), $n_2 \approx 1.41$ (fluorinated cladding). Step-index, multi-mode.
- **Why it matters**: Cheap, robust, easy to connectorize; used in automotive, home networks, industrial control.

## 2.3 Logical Step-Trace of the Refractive-Index Profile Equations

**Step-Index Profile (SIF):**
$$n(r) = \begin{cases} n_1, & 0 \le r \le a \\ n_2, & a < r \le b \\ n_3, & r > b \end{cases}$$

**Graded-Index Profile (power-law, $\alpha$-profile):**
$$n(r) = \begin{cases} n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^\alpha}, & 0 \le r \le a \\ n_2, & r > a \end{cases}$$

- $\alpha = \infty$ → step-index.
- $\alpha = 2$ → **parabolic** (optimum for minimum intermodal dispersion in MMF).
- $\alpha = 1$ → triangular profile.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for "General Structures" — Module 3**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Acceptance angle (in air) | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | radians / degrees |
| 3 | V-parameter (normalized frequency) | $V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} \, NA$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Single-mode cutoff condition | $V < 2.405$ | $V_c = J_0$ first zero |
| 5 | Number of guided modes (MMF, large $V$) | $M \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ (step-index) | Parabolic: $M \approx V^2/4$ |
| 6 | Fractional index difference | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ (approx.) | Small-$\Delta$ approx. |
| 7 | Step-index profile | $n(r) = n_1,\ r<a;\ n_2,\ r \ge a$ | Constant inside core |
| 8 | Power-law profile | $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1-2\Delta(r/a)^\alpha}$ | $\alpha=2$ parabolic |
| 9 | Critical angle (TIR) | $\sin \phi_c = n_2 / n_1$ | At core-cladding boundary |
| 10 | Core diameter (multi-mode) | $d = 2a = 50,\,62.5,\,100$ µm | Standard sizes |

> **Engineering utility:** The **V-parameter** is the single most important design number — it tells a network engineer whether the fiber will support one mode or many, and it directly drives bandwidth, splicing tolerance, and cost. For a KTU problem, *always* compute $V$ first, then check $V \lessgtr 2.405$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Numerical Aperture (NA) from Snell's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ — the most-tested KTU formula for fiber structures.

**Step 1 — Geometry of acceptance.** A ray from outside (medium $n_0 \approx 1$, air) strikes the fiber end-face at angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis. By Snell's law at the air-core interface:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

**Step 2 — Refraction inside the core.** The ray then propagates inside the core and strikes the core-cladding boundary. The angle of incidence (with respect to the *normal* of the cylindrical wall) is $\phi$. From ray geometry:

$$\theta_1 + \phi = 90^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 90^\circ - \theta_1$$

**Step 3 — TIR condition.** For guidance, the ray must undergo total internal reflection at the core-cladding interface:

$$\sin \phi \ge \sin \phi_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the geometry.** $\sin \phi = \cos \theta_1 = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1}$. Therefore:

$$\sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_1} \ge \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$1 - \sin^2 \theta_1 \ge \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\sin^2 \theta_1 \le 1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2} = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}$$

**Step 5 — Combine with Snell's law at the entrance.** $\sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$, so:

$$\sin^2 \theta_0 = n_1^2 \sin^2 \theta_1 \le n_1^2 \cdot \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2} = n_1^2 - n_2^2$$

**Step 6 — Definition of NA.** Define:

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

and the **maximum acceptance angle** is:

$$\boxed{\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the V-Parameter

**Step 1 — Start from Maxwell's wave equation** in a cylindrical dielectric. Solutions for guided modes exist only when the transverse wavenumber in the core, $u$, and the cladding decay constant, $w$, satisfy the **modal eigenvalue equation** (Bessel-function characteristic equation):

$$\frac{J_{l+1}(u a)}{u a \, J_l(u a)} = \frac{K_{l+1}(w a)}{w a \, K_l(w a)}$$

**Step 2 — Define the V-parameter** as the combination $V^2 = u^2 a^2 + w^2 a^2$, where $u$ and $w$ are related to $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda_0$ by:

$$u^2 = n_1^2 k_0^2 - \beta^2, \quad w^2 = \beta^2 - n_2^2 k_0^2$$

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$V^2 = (n_1^2 k_0^2 - \beta^2) a^2 + (\beta^2 - n_2^2 k_0^2) a^2 = (n_1^2 - n_2^2) k_0^2 a^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $k_0 = 2\pi/\lambda_0$ and $NA^2 = n_1^2 - n_2^2$:

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} NA$$

**Step 5 — Single-mode cutoff.** The fundamental LP₀₁ mode is the *only* mode when the next-higher mode (LP₁₁) cannot exist. The LP₁₁ mode is *cut off* when $V = 2.405$ (first zero of $J_0$). Therefore:

$$\boxed{V < 2.405 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Single-mode operation}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four General Structures

| Property | SI-MMF | SI-SMF | GI-MMF | POF |
|----------|--------|--------|--------|-----|
| Core diameter | 50–100 µm | 8–10 µm | 50–62.5 µm | ~980 µm |
| Cladding diameter | 125 µm | 125 µm | 125 µm | 1000 µm |
| $n(r)$ profile | Step (constant) | Step (constant) | Parabolic ($\alpha = 2$) | Step |
| Typical $NA$ | 0.20–0.37 | 0.10–0.14 | 0.20–0.30 | ~0.50 |
| $V$ at 1310 nm | > 25 | ≈ 2.0–2.4 | > 25 | > 100 |
| Modal dispersion | High (~30 ns/km) | None (only one mode) | Low (~1 ns/km) | Very high |
| Bandwidth-distance | 10–100 MHz·km | > 500 GHz·km (limited by chromatic) | 0.5–10 GHz·km | < 10 MHz·km |
| Typical use | LAN, short links | Long-haul, metro, submarine | 1G/10G LAN, data center | Auto, home, industrial |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Moderate | Lowest (per meter) |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A step-index fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, core radius $a = 25$ µm. Find (i) NA, (ii) acceptance angle, (iii) V-parameter at $\lambda_0 = 1300$ nm, and (iv) state the operating regime.

**Solution:**

**(i) NA:**
$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} = 0.2425$$

**(ii) Acceptance angle:**
$$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.2425) = 14.03^\circ$$

**(iii) V-parameter:**
$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} \cdot NA = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{1300 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.2425$$
$$V = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-4}}{1.3 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2425 = 120.83 \times 0.2425 = 29.30$$

**(iv) Operating regime:** Since $V = 29.30 \gg 2.405$, the fiber supports **many modes** — this is a **Step-Index Multi-Mode Fiber (SI-MMF)**.

> **Approximate number of modes:** $M \approx V^2/2 = (29.30)^2/2 \approx 429$ modes.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Anatomy of a General Optical Fiber (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["AXIAL CORE - n1 - radius a - carries optical signal"] --> B["INNER CLADDING - n2 less than n1 - provides TIR confinement - radius b"]
    B --> C["OUTER CLADDING OR COATING - n3 - optional - extra protection"]
    C --> D["PRIMARY BUFFER - soft acrylic - absorbs microbending"]
    D --> E["SECONDARY BUFFER - hard polymer - mechanical strength"]
    E --> F["OUTER JACKET - color coded PVC or LSZH - Yellow SMF Orange MMF Aqua OM3"]
```

## 4.2 Classification Tree of General Fiber Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["OPTICAL FIBER GENERAL STRUCTURES"] --> P1["BY REFRACTIVE INDEX PROFILE"]
    ROOT --> P2["BY MODE COUNT"]
    P1 --> S1["STEP INDEX n1 constant in core"]
    P1 --> G1["GRADED INDEX n1 varies with r parabolic alpha 2"]
    P2 --> MMF["MULTI MODE V greater than 2.405"]
    P2 --> SMF["SINGLE MODE V less than 2.405"]
    S1 --> SIMMF["SI MMF core 50 100 um"]
    S1 --> SISM["SI SMF core 8 10 um"]
    G1 --> GIMMF["GI MMF core 50 62.5 um parabolic"]
    MMF --> SIMMF
    MMF --> GIMMF
    SMF --> SISM
```

## 4.3 Ray-Propagation Schematic (SI vs GI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S["STEP INDEX FIBER - constant n1 inside core"]
        R1["Input ray at angle theta"] --> Z1["Zig zag meridional path - equal time per bounce only if equal angle"]
        Z1 --> O1["Output ray - all rays exit but high angle rays arrive later"]
    end
    subgraph G["GRADED INDEX FIBER - parabolic n1 r"]
        R2["Input ray at large angle"] --> H1["Helical sinusoidal path - higher angle travels in lower index outer region FASTER"]
        H1 --> H2["Compensates longer path length - all rays arrive at SAME TIME"]
        H2 --> O2["Output ray - all rays in phase at exit"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Design Flow for a General Fiber

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1["STEP 1 - Define operating wavelength lambda 0 - 850 1310 1550 nm"] --> ST2["STEP 2 - Choose desired bandwidth distance - MHz km or GHz km"]
    ST2 --> ST3["STEP 3 - Compute NA required from link budget and source divergence"]
    ST3 --> ST4["STEP 4 - Choose core radius a to satisfy V less than 2.405 for SMF or V greater than 2.405 for MMF"]
    ST4 --> ST5["STEP 5 - Select index profile step or graded alpha"]
    ST5 --> ST6["STEP 6 - Verify mechanical tolerances and connector compatibility"]
    ST6 --> OUT["FINAL FIBER SPECIFICATION - core cladding NA V profile type"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~80 words):**
> *Numerical Aperture (NA) is a dimensionless measure of the light-collecting ability of an optical fiber, defined as* $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$. *It determines the maximum acceptance angle* $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ *for coupling light into the fiber, and is a key design parameter governing V-parameter, mode count, and bend sensitivity. A higher NA allows easier coupling from incoherent sources but increases modal dispersion.*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Step-Index and Graded-Index optical fibers.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~80 words):**
> *In a **Step-Index Fiber (SIF)**, the core has a constant refractive index* $n_1$ *with an abrupt transition to* $n_2$ *at* $r = a$*; light propagates as zig-zag meridional rays and suffers high intermodal dispersion. In a **Graded-Index Fiber (GIF)**, the index decreases continuously (typically parabolically) from* $n_1$ *at the axis to* $n_2$ *at the boundary; rays follow helical paths with self-focusing, reducing intermodal dispersion by ~100× and increasing bandwidth-distance product.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the general structure of an optical fiber. Label the core, cladding, and protective layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for Numerical Aperture (NA) of a step-index fiber in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. A SI fiber has $n_1 = 1.50$ and $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate (i) NA, (ii) acceptance angle, and (iii) the fractional index difference $\Delta$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) General Structure of an Optical Fiber (7 marks):**

> An optical fiber is a cylindrical dielectric waveguide consisting of concentric layers:

| Layer | Radius | Refractive Index | Function |
|-------|--------|------------------|----------|
| **Core** | $a$ (4–100 µm) | $n_1$ | Carries the optical signal; doped silica |
| **Inner Cladding** | $b$ (≈ 62.5 µm) | $n_2 < n_1$ | Confines light via TIR; pure silica |
| **Outer Cladding** | — | $n_3$ | Optional second cladding for double-clad fibers |
| **Primary Coating** | — | Polymer (soft) | Buffers micro-bending losses |
| **Secondary Coating** | — | Polymer (hard) | Mechanical strength |
| **Jacket** | 250 µm OD | PVC / LSZH | Environmental protection; color-coded |

*Key points for full marks:* mention $n_1 > n_2$ for TIR, list core/cladding/jacket, mention typical dimensions, and explain that the core-cladding boundary is where total internal reflection occurs.

**(b) Derivation of NA (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Snell's law at air-core interface: $n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$.

**Step 2:** Geometry at core-cladding wall: $\phi = 90^\circ - \theta_1$.

**Step 3:** TIR condition: $\sin \phi \ge n_2 / n_1$.

**Step 4:** Substituting: $\cos \theta_1 \ge n_2/n_1 \Rightarrow \sin^2 \theta_1 \le 1 - n_2^2/n_1^2$.

**Step 5:** Using Snell's law: $\sin^2 \theta_0 = n_1^2 \sin^2 \theta_1 \le n_1^2 - n_2^2$.
