**Step 6:** Defining $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ and $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$.

**Numerical Computation (with $n_1 = 1.50$, $n_2 = 1.45$):**

(i) $NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2} = \sqrt{2.25 - 2.1025} = \sqrt{0.1475} = 0.3841$

(ii) $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.3841) = 22.59^\circ$

(iii) $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{1.50 - 1.45}{1.50} = \dfrac{0.05}{1.50} = 0.0333$ (i.e., 3.33%)

> **[Examiner's Pitfall Warning]** *Students often forget the small-Δ approximation and write $\Delta = (n_1^2 - n_2^2)/(2n_1^2)$ instead of $(n_1 - n_2)/n_1$. For Δ > 5%, the approximation introduces > 10% error — use the exact form. Also, the acceptance angle is with respect to the **fiber axis**, not the normal to the end-face.*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the four general structures of optical fibers based on (i) refractive index profile and (ii) mode count. Compare their bandwidth-distance products. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A step-index fiber has core diameter $2a = 50$ µm, $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$. Operating wavelength $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm. Calculate (i) V-parameter, (ii) number of guided modes $M$, (iii) cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ for single-mode operation, and (iv) state whether it is single-mode or multi-mode at 1310 nm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

**(a) Four General Structures (7 marks):**

> **(i) Step-Index Multi-Mode (SI-MMF):** Constant $n_1$ in core; supports hundreds of modes via zig-zag rays. Bandwidth-distance ≈ 10–100 MHz·km.
> **(ii) Step-Index Single-Mode (SI-SMF):** Tiny core ($a$ ≈ 4–5 µm), $V < 2.405$, only LP₀₁ mode. Bandwidth-distance > 500 GHz·km (limited by chromatic dispersion).
> **(iii) Graded-Index Multi-Mode (GI-MMF):** Parabolic $n(r)$, helical ray paths, modal dispersion minimized. Bandwidth-distance ≈ 0.5–10 GHz·km. Used in Ethernet (OM1–OM4).
> **(iv) Plastic Optical Fiber (POF):** Large PMMA core, step-index multi-mode. Bandwidth-distance < 10 MHz·km; used in automotive/home.

*Comparison Summary:* $B \cdot L$ increases as: POF < SI-MMF < GI-MMF ≪ SI-SMF.

**(b) Numerical Computation (with $2a = 50$ µm, $n_1 = 1.48$, $n_2 = 1.46$, $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm):**

(i) **V-parameter:**
$$NA = \sqrt{(1.48)^2 - (1.46)^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} = 0.2425$$
$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} \cdot NA = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{1310 \times 10^{-9}} \times 0.2425$$
$$V = \frac{1.5708 \times 10^{-4}}{1.31 \times 10^{-6}} \times 0.2425 = 119.91 \times 0.2425 \approx 29.08$$

(ii) **Number of modes** (large $V$, step-index approximation):
$$M \approx \frac{V^2}{2} = \frac{(29.08)^2}{2} = \frac{845.6}{2} \approx 423 \text{ modes}$$

(iii) **Cutoff wavelength for single-mode** ($V = 2.405$):
$$\lambda_c = \frac{2\pi a \cdot NA}{V_c} = \frac{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2425}{2.405}$$
$$\lambda_c = \frac{3.809 \times 10^{-5}}{2.405} = 1.584 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 1584 \text{ nm}$$

(iv) **Operating regime:** Since operating wavelength $\lambda_0 = 1310$ nm $< \lambda_c = 1584$ nm, the fiber is in the **multi-mode regime** at 1310 nm (it would be single-mode only at $\lambda > 1584$ nm).

> **[Examiner's Pitfall Warning]** *Common error: students report "single-mode" simply because the core is "small." Always compute V first, then compare to 2.405. Also, for step-index multi-mode, the formula is $M \approx V^2/2$, **not** $V^2/4$ (the latter applies to parabolic graded-index).* Mixing these is a guaranteed **2-mark loss** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "General Structures" (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Core, Cladding, Jacket** — three-layer concentric cylinder; $n_1 > n_2$ for TIR.
- ☐ **Two classification axes:** (1) index profile (Step vs Graded) and (2) mode count (Multi vs Single).
- ☐ **NA = $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$** — most-tested formula; dimensionless.
- ☐ **Acceptance angle** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ — measured with respect to the fiber **axis**.
- ☐ **V-parameter** $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda_0} NA$ — the *master design number*.
- ☐ **Single-mode cutoff:** $V < 2.405$ (equivalently $\lambda > \lambda_c$).
- ☐ **Number of modes (SI):** $M \approx V^2/2$; **(parabolic GI):** $M \approx V^2/4$.
- ☐ **Power-law profile:** $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta(r/a)^\alpha}$; $\alpha = 2$ for parabolic.
- ☐ **Color codes:** Yellow = SMF, Orange/Aqua = MMF.
- ☐ **Standard core diameters:** 8–10 µm (SMF), 50/62.5/100 µm (MMF), ~980 µm (POF).
- ☐ **Graded-index advantage:** self-focusing helical rays → reduces intermodal dispersion by ~100× vs SI-MMF.
- ☐ **Bandwidth-distance ordering:** POF < SI-MMF < GI-MMF ≪ SI-SMF.
- ☐ **First step in any KTU numerical problem:** compute $V$, then check $V \lessgtr 2.405$.

> **Final tip:** In KTU valuation, *always* state the operating regime (single-mode vs multi-mode) explicitly and justify with a numerical $V$ comparison. Examiners reward clarity of thought over rote formulas.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Optical Source Characteristics

In optical fiber communication systems, the term **"Characteristics"** refers to the set of performance-defining parameters and transfer functions that quantitatively describe how an optical source (semiconductor **LED** or **Laser Diode**) converts an electrical drive current into a modulated optical output. These characteristics govern the **fidelity**, **bandwidth**, **spectral purity**, **linearity**, and **temperature stability** of the transmitted optical signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> *Optical source characteristics are the measurable electrical, optical, and spectral performance parameters of a light-emitting device (LED/LD) that determine its suitability for use in a fiber-optic communication link, typically evaluated as a function of drive current, temperature, and modulation frequency.*

## 1.2 Primary Characteristics Classified

The characteristics of optical sources are broadly classified into **four functional domains**:

| Domain | Parameter Group | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Optical Output** | Output Power ($P$), Luminous Intensity | Link budget & receiver sensitivity |
| **Electrical Input** | Drive Current ($I$), Threshold Current ($I_{th}$), Forward Voltage ($V_f$) | Driver circuit design |
| **Spectral** | Peak Wavelength ($\lambda_p$), Spectral Width ($\Delta\lambda$), Side-Mode Suppression | Chromatic dispersion control |
| **Dynamic** | Modulation Bandwidth ($f_{3dB}$), Rise/Fall Time ($\tau_r,\tau_f$), Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$) | Data rate & switching speed |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Water Tap and Pressure Gauge":**
> Think of an LED as a **standard household water tap** — when you turn the knob slightly (small forward current), water (light) dribbles out gently. The flow is broad, splashes in many directions (wide spectrum), and the tap never "snaps" on. 
>
> Now imagine a **Laser Diode as a high-pressure fire hose nozzle** — the water (light) doesn't flow until you cross a critical valve pressure (**threshold current** $I_{th}$). After crossing that point, the water shoots as a thin, fast, coherent, single-color jet (narrow $\Delta\lambda$, high directionality). 
>
> The **P-I curve** is the *pressure-vs-flow* chart, the **spectral width** is the *color purity*, and the **modulation bandwidth** is *how fast you can wiggle the valve* before the hose can't keep up.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard constants and metrics are universally used in the KTU syllabus:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Electron charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Operating wavelength windows:** **850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm**
- **Threshold current (typical LD):** $I_{th} = 5$–$100$ mA
- **Spectral width (LED):** $\Delta\lambda = 30$–$150$ nm
- **Spectral width (LD, multimode):** $\Delta\lambda = 1$–$5$ nm
- **Spectral width (LD, single-mode / DFB):** $\Delta\lambda \approx 0.01$–$1$ nm

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle Recap

**LED (Light Emitting Diode):** Operates on **spontaneous emission** in a forward-biased **p-n junction (or heterojunction)**. Recombination of injected electrons and holes in the active region produces photons. No optical feedback cavity exists; the output is **incoherent** and **broadband**.

**Laser Diode (LD):** Operates on **stimulated emission** within an **optical resonant cavity** formed by cleaved semiconductor facets. Above the **threshold current** $I_{th}$, population inversion is achieved, leading to **coherent**, **narrow-linewidth** output.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of Key Characteristics

### A. Optical Power–Current (P–I) Characteristic
1. Below $I_{th}$: LED emits spontaneous, low-power, superlinear light; LD emits negligible stimulated light.
2. At $I = I_{th}$: LD undergoes "**kink**" — sharp slope change as lasing begins.
3. Above $I_{th}$: LD output rises **linearly** with a steep differential quantum efficiency $\eta_d$.
4. Thermal rollover: At high current, junction heating reduces output, causing a **negative slope** (saturation/rollover).

### B. Spectral Characteristics
1. LED: Broad Gaussian emission (FWHM $\Delta\lambda \sim 30$–$150$ nm) due to carrier recombination energy spread.
2. Fabry–Perot (FP) LD: Multiple longitudinal modes, $\Delta\lambda \sim 1$–$5$ nm.
3. DFB / DBR LD: Single longitudinal mode, $\Delta\lambda \sim 0.01$–$1$ nm (side-mode suppression ratio > 30 dB).
4. Peak wavelength shifts with temperature: $\frac{d\lambda_p}{dT} \approx 0.5$ nm/°C (LD) and $\approx 0.3$ nm/°C (LED).

### C. Modulation Bandwidth
1. Limited by **carrier recombination lifetime** $\tau_r$ in the active region.
2. Optical power modulation transfer function:
$$H(f) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \tau_r)^2}}$$
3. **3-dB bandwidth:** $f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_r}$.
4. **Injection-limited bandwidth** (practical LD): up to **tens of GHz**.

### D. Quantum Efficiency Definitions
1. **Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_i$):** Ratio of photons generated internally to electrons injected.
2. **External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$):** Ratio of photons escaping the device to electrons injected — accounts for internal absorption and Fresnel reflection.
3. **Differential Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_d$):** Slope of P–I curve above threshold (LD only).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the formulas below verbatim. The $\eta_d$ and $f_{3dB}$ equations are **guaranteed 7-markers** in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_i = \frac{\text{photons generated internally/sec}}{I/q}$ | Internal QE — bulk efficiency | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{ext} = \frac{P_{opt}/h\nu}{I/q}$ | External QE — useful emitted light | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\eta_d = \frac{q}{h\nu}\cdot \frac{dP}{dI}\bigg\vert_{I>I_{th}}$ | Differential QE (LD slope) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\eta_d = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)}{(I_2 - I_1)} \cdot \frac{q}{h\nu}$ | Numerical form using two P–I points | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_r}$ | Modulation bandwidth | Hz |
| 6 | $H(f) = \left[1 + (f/f_{3dB})^2\right]^{-1/2}$ | Frequency response (normalized) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta\lambda = \frac{\lambda_p^2}{nL}$ | Longitudinal mode spacing (FP cavity) | nm |
| 8 | $E_g = \frac{1.24}{\lambda_p}\,(\mu m)$ | Bandgap–wavelength relation | eV, μm |
| 9 | $I_{th}(T) = I_0 \exp(T/T_0)$ | Threshold current vs temperature | mA |
| 10 | $\text{Rise time}, t_r \approx 0.35 / f_{3dB}$ | Rise time–bandwidth product | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The P–I curve slope is the **first measurement** performed in any optical transceiver qualification (Telcordia GR-468). Spectral width directly governs the **chromatic dispersion limit** of long-haul links, and $f_{3dB}$ defines the **maximum NRZ bit-rate** achievable by the source.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of External Quantum Efficiency $\eta_{ext}$

**Statement:** External quantum efficiency is the ratio of the number of photons emitted usefully into free space (or into the fiber) per second to the number of electrons injected into the device per second.

**Step 1 — Photon Energy:**
Each emitted photon has energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_p}$.

**Step 2 — Number of Photons Emitted per Second:**
The optical output power $P_{opt}$ (in watts) divided by the energy per photon gives the photon emission rate:

$$
N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda_p}{hc}
$$

**Step 3 — Number of Electrons Injected per Second:**
For a forward drive current $I$ amperes:

$$
N_{e} = \frac{I}{q}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the Ratio:**
By definition:

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{N_{ph}}{N_{e}} = \frac{P_{opt}/(h\nu)}{I/q} = \frac{P_{opt}\,q}{I\,h\nu}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\nu = c/\lambda_p$:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{ext} = \frac{P_{opt}\,q\,\lambda_p}{I\,h\,c}\;}
$$

**Physical Insight:** For an LED, $\eta_{ext} \ll 1$ (typically 1–10%) because most photons are trapped by total internal reflection. For a laser diode above threshold, $\eta_{ext}$ approaches 30–60% because stimulated emission is directional and cavity-favoured.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Differential Quantum Efficiency $\eta_d$ (Slope Method)

**Statement:** The differential quantum efficiency of a laser diode is the slope of the linear portion of the P–I curve above threshold, normalized by photon energy.

**Step 1 — Slope of P–I curve above $I_{th}$:**

Take two points $(I_1, P_1)$ and $(I_2, P_2)$ on the linear lasing region ($I_1, I_2 > I_{th}$):

$$
\frac{dP}{dI} \approx \frac{P_2 - P_1}{I_2 - I_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Optical Slope to Photon Rate:**
Multiply by $\dfrac{\lambda_p}{hc}$ to convert watts → photons/sec:

$$
\frac{dN_{ph}}{dI} = \left(\frac{P_2 - P_1}{I_2 - I_1}\right)\cdot \frac{\lambda_p}{hc}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert Current Slope to Electron Rate:**
Each ampere corresponds to $1/q$ electrons per second.

**Step 4 — Form the Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_d = \frac{(dN_{ph}/dI)}{(1/q)} = \frac{q\lambda_p}{hc}\cdot \frac{(P_2 - P_1)}{(I_2 - I_1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form:**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_d = \frac{q\,\lambda_p}{h\,c}\cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta I}\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of 3-dB Modulation Bandwidth

**Statement:** Derive the relationship between carrier recombination lifetime $\tau_r$ and the -3 dB electrical bandwidth of the optical source.

**Step 1 — Rate Equation for Carrier Density $N$:**
The rate of change of carrier density in the active region:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{I}{qV_a} - \frac{N}{\tau_r} - G(N)\cdot S
$$

For **small-signal** modulation, stimulated term $G(N)\cdot S$ is approximated as a constant and dropped, leaving:

$$
\frac{dN}{dt} + \frac{N}{\tau_r} = \frac{I}{qV_a}
$$

**Step 2 — Small-Signal AC Solution:**
Let $N = N_0 + n(t)$ and $I = I_0 + i(t)$. The steady-state terms cancel, yielding:

$$
\frac{dn}{dt} + \frac{n}{\tau_r} = \frac{i}{qV_a}
$$

**Step 3 — Phasor/Frequency Substitution:**
Assume $n(t) = \tilde{n}\,e^{j\omega t}$. Then $\dfrac{dn}{dt} = j\omega \tilde{n}\,e^{j\omega t}$.

$$
j\omega \tilde{n} + \frac{\tilde{n}}{\tau_r} = \frac{\tilde{i}}{qV_a}
$$

$$
\tilde{n}\left(j\omega + \frac{1}{\tau_r}\right) = \frac{\tilde{i}}{qV_a}
$$

$$
\tilde{n} = \frac{\tilde{i}/(qV_a)}{\frac{1}{\tau_r} + j\omega}
$$

**Step 4 — Output Optical Power Proportional to $n$ (small-signal):**
Since $\Delta P_{opt} \propto n$:

$$
H(\omega) = \frac{\Delta P_{opt}}{\Delta P_{opt}(\omega=0)} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega \tau_r}
$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude / 3-dB Point:**

$$
\vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (2\pi f \tau_r)^2}
$$

Setting $\vert H(f) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2}$ (which is the -3 dB condition):

$$
1 + (2\pi f_{3dB} \tau_r)^2 = 2
$$

$$
2\pi f_{3dB} \tau_r = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{\;f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_r}\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A GaAs laser diode emits at $\lambda_p = 850$ nm. When driven at $I_1 = 30$ mA the output is $P_1 = 1$ mW; at $I_2 = 50$ mA the output is $P_2 = 5$ mW. Find (a) the differential quantum efficiency, and (b) the threshold current.

**Given:**
- $\lambda_p = 850 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $\Delta P = 5 - 1 = 4$ mW = $4 \times 10^{-3}$ W
- $\Delta I = 50 - 30 = 20$ mA = $20 \times 10^{-3}$ A

**Solution (a) — Differential QE:**

$$
\eta_d = \frac{q\,\lambda_p}{h\,c}\cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta I}
$$

Compute the photon-current factor:

$$
\frac{q\,\lambda_p}{h\,c} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(850 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}
$$

$$
= \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.6843
$$

Now:

$$
\eta_d = 0.6843 \times \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.6843 \times 0.2 = 0.1369
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_d \approx 13.7\%}
$$

**Solution (b) — Threshold Current:**
Extrapolating the linear P–I curve back to $P = 0$:

$$
I_{th} = I_1 - \frac{P_1}{\eta_d} \cdot \frac{q\lambda_p}{hc} = I_1 - \frac{P_1}{\Delta P/\Delta I}
$$

$$
I_{th} = 30\text{ mA} - \frac{1\text{ mW}}{(4/20)\text{ mW/mA}} = 30 - 5 = 25\text{ mA}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{th} = 25 \text{ mA}}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 P–I Characteristic Curves of LED and LD

The following Mermaid block renders the functional flow comparison between LED and Laser Diode P–I behaviour, output spectrum, and modulation response.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input: Forward Current I"] --> B{"Device Type"}
    B -- "LED" --> C["Spontaneous Emission Region"]
    B -- "Laser Diode" --> D{"I vs I_th ?"}
    D -- "I less than I_th" --> E["Weak Spontaneous LED-like Output"]
    D -- "I greater than I_th" --> F["Stimulated Emission Linear Lasing Region"]
    C --> G["Output: Broad Spectrum 30 to 150 nm"]
    E --> H["Output: Weak + Broad Spectrum"]
    F --> I["Output: Narrow Spectrum 0.01 to 5 nm"]
    G --> J["Modulation: Low f_3dB few MHz to 100 MHz"]
    I --> K["Modulation: High f_3dB GHz range"]
    H --> L["Coherence: Incoherent"]
    I --> M["Coherence: Coherent"]
    J --> N["Final Performance Summary"]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N["KTU Board Result: Trade-off between Power, BW, Coherence"]
```

```mermaid
    subgraph Driver_Circuit
        D1["DC Bias Supply"] --> D2["Temperature Controller TEC"]
        D2 --> D3["Modulation Current Source"]
    end
    D3 --> DEV{"Optical Source Chip"}
    DEV -- "Spontaneous" --> E1["LED Active Region"]
    DEV -- "Stimulated" --> E2["LD with Fabry-Perot Cavity"]
    E1 --> OUT1["Multi-mode Broad Output"]
    E2 --> OUT2["Single-mode Coherent Output"]
    OUT1 --> F1["Coupling into Multimode Fiber"]
    OUT2 --> F2["Coupling into Single-mode Fiber"]

    F2 --> M

```

## 4.3 Modulation Bandwidth Transfer Function (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["f = 0 Hz"] --> B["Normalized Gain = 1"]
    C["f = f_3dB"] --> D["Normalized Gain = 0.707 minus 3 dB point"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define the term "differential quantum efficiency" of a laser diode. How is it obtained from the P–I curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Differential quantum efficiency $\eta_d$ is defined as the **slope of the linear stimulated-emission region of the P–I characteristic**, normalized by photon energy. It is the ratio of the increase in optical output power (in photon flux) to the corresponding increase in injected electrons (in coulombs) **above threshold** $I_{th}$.

$$
\eta_d = \frac{q}{h\nu}\cdot \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta I}
$$

It is obtained by drawing a tangent to the linear portion of the P–I curve above $I_{th}$ and computing $\Delta P / \Delta I$. **Numerical value:** typically 30–60% for commercial LDs. *(1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark numerical significance.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]**
**Distinguish between the spectral width of an LED and a single-mode laser diode. State typical values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | LED | Single-Mode Laser (DFB) |
|---|---|---|
| Emission mechanism | Spontaneous | Stimulated |
| Spectral width (FWHM) | **30–150 nm** | **0.01–1 nm** |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Highly coherent |
| Spectral purity (SMSR) | N/A | > 30 dB |

LEDs emit a broad Gaussian spectrum due to the **statistical spread of carrier recombination energies**, while single-mode laser diodes use a **distributed feedback grating** to suppress all but one longitudinal mode. *(1 mark mechanism + 1 mark values + 1 mark justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> (1) When asked for $\eta_d$, students often forget to **multiply by $q\lambda_p/(hc)$** — they give only $\Delta P / \Delta I$ and lose 4 marks. (2) Units of $I_{th}$ must be in **mA**, not A, when plugging into the slope — examiners deduct 2 marks for unit confusion. (3) Always **state the assumption** that $I > I_{th}$ when using $\eta_d$.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **external quantum efficiency** of an LED in terms of output optical power, drive current, and emission wavelength. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A GaAs LED emits at 870 nm with $\eta_{ext} = 3\%$. Find the output optical power when the drive current is 50 mA. Compare the result with a laser diode operating at the same wavelength and current, having $\eta_{ext} = 25\%$. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $\eta_{ext}$ — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Photon energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = hc/\lambda_p$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Photon emission rate $N_{ph} = P_{opt}/(h\nu) = P_{opt}\lambda_p/(hc)$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** Electron injection rate $N_e = I/q$. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4:** Definition:

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{N_{ph}}{N_e} = \frac{P_{opt}\,q}{I\,h\nu}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $\nu = c/\lambda_p$:

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{P_{opt}\,q\,\lambda_p}{I\,h\,c}
$$

*(1 mark — final boxed formula)*

**Step 6:** Physical meaning: $\eta_{ext}$ is **always < $\eta_i$** because of internal absorption, Fresnel reflection loss, and total internal reflection at the semiconductor–air interface. Typical value for LED: 1–10%. *(1 mark)*

**Step 7:** Real-world note: Antireflection coating and dome-shaped encapsulation are used to increase $\eta_{ext}$. *(1 mark)*

---

**(b) Numerical comparison — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\lambda_p = 870$ nm, $I = 50$ mA, $\eta_{ext,LED} = 0.03$, $\eta_{ext,LD} = 0.25$.

**Step 1:** Rearrange the $\eta_{ext}$ formula:

$$
P_{opt} = \frac{\eta_{ext} \cdot I \cdot h \cdot c}{q \cdot \lambda_p}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2:** Compute the constant $hc/q$:

$$
\frac{hc}{q} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.242 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V·m}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3:** LED output:

$$
P_{LED} = 0.03 \times \frac{50 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.242 \times 10^{-6}}{870 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
= 0.03 \times \frac{6.21 \times 10^{-8}}{8.7 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.03 \times 0.0714 = 2.14 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{LED} \approx 2.14 \text{ mW}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 4:** LD output:

$$
P_{LD} = 0.25 \times 0.0714 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W}/\text{mA} \cdot 50\text{ mA}
$$

$$
= 0.25 \times 3.57 \times 10^{-3} = 8.93 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{W (per unit scaling)}
$$

Recomputing directly:

$$
P_{LD} = 0.25 \times 0.0714 \times 50\,\text{mA (scaled)} = 17.85 \text{ mW}
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 5:** Comparison:
- $P_{LD} / P_{LED} = 17.85 / 2.14 \approx 8.34$
- A laser diode produces **~8.3× more optical power** than an LED at the same current. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **P–I and spectral characteristics** of a semiconductor laser diode. Sketch and label the curves. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A laser diode has $I_{th} = 20$ mA at 25 °C. At $I_1 = 30$ mA, $P_1 = 2$ mW; at $I_2 = 40$ mA, $P_2 = 6$ mW. Calculate (i) the differential quantum efficiency, (ii) the threshold current, and (iii) the output power at $I = 50$ mA. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) P–I and Spectral Characteristics — 7 marks:**

**P–I Curve (3 marks):**
- Below $I_{th}$: weak spontaneous emission, low power, superlinear slope.
- At $I = I_{th}$: sharp "kink" — stimulated emission begins.
- Above $I_{th}$: linear steep increase in output power.
- At very high current: **thermal rollover** reduces output (negative slope).
- Slope above threshold = $\eta_d$ × $h\nu/q$.

**Spectral Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Fabry–Perot LD: multiple longitudinal modes spaced by $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_p^2/(2nL)$.
- DFB LD: single longitudinal mode (enforced by Bragg grating).
- Peak wavelength $\lambda_p$ shifts with temperature: $d\lambda_p/dT \approx 0.5$ nm/°C.

**Sketch (2 marks):** (Drawn as standard P–I curve with kink at $I_{th}$ and inset showing multi-mode vs single-mode spectrum.)

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $I_1 = 30$ mA, $P_1 = 2$ mW, $I_2 = 40$ mA, $P_2 = 6$ mW, $\lambda_p = 1550$ nm (assumed standard).

**Step 1 — Slope of P–I curve:**

$$
\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta I} = \frac{6 - 2}{40 - 30} = \frac{4\text{ mW}}{10\text{ mA}} = 0.4\text{ mW/mA}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Differential QE:**

$$
\frac{q\lambda_p}{hc} = \frac{(1.6 \times 10^{-19})(1550 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)} \approx 1.247
$$

$$
\eta_d = 1.247 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3} = 0.499 \times 10^{-3} = 0.000499 ?
$$

**Correction:** units check — slope is $0.4 \text{ mW/mA} = 0.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} / 10^{-3}\,\text{A} = 0.4$ W/A.

$$
\eta_d = 1.247 \times 0.4 = 0.499 \approx 50\%
$$

*(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — Threshold current check (linear extrapolation):**

$$
I_{th} = I_1 - \frac{P_1}{\eta_d}\cdot \frac{q\lambda_p}{hc} = 30 - \frac{2\text{ mW}}{0.4\text{ mW/mA}} = 30 - 5 = 25 \text{ mA}
$$

The given $I_{th} = 20$ mA; the slight discrepancy is due to non-ideal sub-threshold spontaneous contribution. Using the given value $I_{th} = 20$ mA is **acceptable** in KTU valuation. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4 — Output power at $I = 50$ mA:**

$$
P(50\text{ mA}) = \eta_d \cdot \frac{hc}{q\lambda_p}\cdot (I - I_{th})
$$

$$
= 0.5 \times \frac{1}{1.247}\cdot (50 - 20)\text{ mA}
$$

$$
= 0.401 \times 30 = 12.03 \text{ mW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 12 \text{ mW}}
$$

*(2 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Characteristics of Optical Sources"**

- ✅ **P–I Curve:** LED has superlinear, monotonic rise; LD has a clear **kink at $I_{th}$** followed by a steep linear region.
- ✅ **Spectral Width:** LED = 30–150 nm (incoherent); FP-LD = 1–5 nm; **DFB-LD = 0.01–1 nm (single mode)**.
- ✅ **Peak Wavelength:** Determined by bandgap $E_g = 1.24/\lambda_p$ (μm).
- ✅ **Temperature Sensitivity:** $I_{th}$ rises **exponentially** with temperature; $\lambda_p$ shifts with $d\lambda_p/dT \approx 0.5$ nm/°C.
- ✅ **Quantum Efficiencies:** $\eta_i > \eta_{ext}$ always; $\eta_d$ is defined **only above $I_{th}$** (LD).
- ✅ **Modulation Bandwidth:** $f_{3dB} = 1/(2\pi \tau_r)$; rise time $t_r \approx 0.35 / f_{3dB}$.
- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $\eta_{ext} = P_{opt} q \lambda_p / (I h c)$
  - $\eta_d = q \lambda_p \Delta P / (h c \Delta I)$
  - $f_{3dB} = 1/(2\pi \tau_r)$
  - $E_g (\text{eV}) = 1.24 / \lambda_p (\mu m)$
- ✅ **Common Pitfalls:** (1) Confusing $I_{th}$ with operating current; (2) Using $\eta_{ext}$ in place of $\eta_d$ above threshold; (3) Forgetting units of $h$, $c$, $q$ — examiners deduct for unit mismatch.
- ✅ **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** LED for **short-reach, low-cost** links (< 1 Gbps, < 5 km); LD for **long-haul, high-speed** links (> 1 Gbps, > 10 km).
- ✅ **KTU Board Pattern:** A 14-mark question typically splits as **7 marks theory (with sketch) + 7 marks numerical** — always carry drawing instruments to the exam hall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulators Using LEDs and LDs; Coupling with Fibres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modulators Using LEDs and LDs; Coupling with Fibres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Modulators Using LEDs and LDs — Definition

In optical communication transmitters, the **modulator** is the electronic interface that converts the incoming electrical information signal (analog voice, digital data, or RF) into a proportional **variation in the optical output** (light intensity/power) emitted by the **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** or **LD (Laser Diode)**. Because the source itself is a current-driven semiconductor diode, modulation is achieved by **modulating the drive current** through the device — this is known as **direct modulation** (the dominant scheme for low-to-moderate bit rates ≤ 10 Gbps in KTU syllabus context).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An *optical source modulator/driver circuit* is an electronic circuit that superimposes the information signal on the bias current of the optical source such that the emitted optical power P(t) follows the message waveform m(t), while maintaining the source within its safe operating region (above threshold for LDs, below thermal saturation for LEDs).

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Faucet of Light"**
> Think of an LED or LD as a **water tap** controlled by a knob. The **driver circuit** is the plumber's hand that opens/closes the tap in perfect sync with your voice. When you speak softly (low signal amplitude), only a small steady stream flows; when you shout, the tap opens wider, sending a stronger jet. For an LED, the tap can be turned slowly (analog) or flipped on/off (digital). For a laser diode, the tap has a critical "stiction point" — a threshold current below which the laser does not lase coherently. The driver must *never* let the current dip below this threshold, or the laser's coherence and output power collapse. Hence LD drivers are far more sophisticated than LED drivers.

## 1.2 Coupling with Fibres — Definition

**Source-to-Fiber Coupling** is the mechanism by which the **maximum possible fraction of the optical power** emitted by the LED/LD is efficiently launched into the core of the optical fiber. The efficiency of this power transfer depends on:
- **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the fiber
- **Emission pattern** of the source (Lambertian for LED, narrow beam for LD)
- **Geometric area mismatch** between source emitting area and fiber core
- **Mechanical alignment** (lateral, angular, longitudinal tolerances)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** *Coupling Efficiency (η)* is defined as the ratio of **optical power accepted by the fiber (P_fiber)** to the **total optical power emitted by the source (P_source)**, expressed as a percentage or in dB. For an LED emitting a perfect Lambertian pattern into a step-index fiber, η = (NA)² — a formula that KTU examiners love.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "Funneling Rain into a Bucket"**
> Imagine an LED as a **showerhead spraying water in all directions** (wide Lambertian pattern) and the optical fiber core as a **small bucket held underneath**. Most water misses the bucket — that's the **coupling loss**. To collect more, you use a **funnel (lens)** or hold the bucket closer (butt coupling) or align it perfectly (alignment). A laser diode, by contrast, is like a **focused jet of water** that can be aimed directly into the bucket with much less waste. The bucket's mouth size = fiber core diameter, and the funnel's mouth-to-throat ratio relates to the fiber's **NA**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Threshold Current (I_th)** of LD: typically **5 mA to 100 mA** (GaAs/GaInAsP devices)
- **LED Modulation Bandwidth**: 10 MHz – 200 MHz
- **LD Modulation Bandwidth**: 1 GHz – 10+ GHz
- **Standard Fiber NA**: **0.10 to 0.40** (multimode); **~0.10 to 0.14** (single-mode)
- **Coupling Loss Target**: < 1 dB (butt), 3–6 dB (microlens), 10+ dB (unlensed LED-to-MM fiber)
- **Speed of light in vacuum (c)** = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Planck's constant (h)** = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LED Modulation — Operating Principle

The LED is a **spontaneous emission** device. Its optical output power P follows a near-linear relation with the forward current I above a small cut-in voltage:

$$P \approx \eta_{\text{ext}} \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} \cdot (I - I_0)$$

where **η_ext** is the **external quantum efficiency** and **I_0** is the small threshold/cut-in current. This linearity makes LED modulation circuits relatively simple.

### LED Driver Circuit Requirements
1. Provide a **constant DC bias current** (I_bias) to set the operating point (Class A region for analog, mid-point for digital).
2. Superimpose the **AC modulating signal** on the bias via coupling capacitor or summing node.
3. Maintain drive current within LED's **maximum ratings** to prevent thermal runaway and Catastrophic Optical Damage (COD).
4. Provide **linearity correction** (pre-emphasis) when used with analog modulation (e.g., AM-VSB video).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DC bias the LED?** To avoid the non-linear turn-on knee and to allow **bidirectional analog modulation** (positive and negative signal excursions). The bias point is typically chosen at the **midpoint of the linear P–I characteristic** to maximize undistorted signal swing.

### LED Modulation — Two Schemes

| Scheme | Application | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Analog Modulation** | Video / Audio / CATV | Continuous linear variation of P(t) with m(t) |
| **Digital Modulation** | Data (OOK / NRZ) | LED switched ON (1) / OFF (0) — simpler |

## 2.2 Laser Diode (LD) Modulation — Operating Principle

Unlike the LED, the LD has a **threshold current I_th** below which it emits **incoherent spontaneous light** (like an LED) and above which it emits **coherent stimulated emission** (laser action). Above threshold, the P–I curve becomes **very steep and reasonably linear**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept — The Threshold Problem:** If the LD drive current is allowed to drop below I_th during the OFF state of digital modulation, two harmful effects occur:
> (i) **Chirp** — relaxation oscillations produce wavelength/frequency fluctuations on the rising edge of the pulse, causing dispersion penalty in the fiber.
> (ii) **Turn-on delay** — the carrier population must build up before lasing, slowing the modulation speed.
> **Solution:** Bias the LD at a point **I_b ≥ I_th** (called **pre-bias or above-threshold biasing**), so the OFF state is just below I_th (quasi-bias, "I_b ≈ 0.9 I_th") or exactly at I_th.

### LD Driver Circuit Block Diagram (Conceptual)

The LD driver is typically a **current-summing node**:
- DC bias current source (sets operating point at or above I_th)
- Modulation current source (delivers the AC message signal)
- Both sum into the LD anode; cathode returns to ground via a sense resistor for feedback
- **Automatic Power Control (APC)** loop with monitor photodiode (PD)
- **Automatic Temperature Control (ATC)** using a thermo-electric cooler (TEC) and thermistor

### LD Pre-emphasis and Pre-bias Schemes

| Bias Scheme | OFF-State Current | ON-State Current | Chirp | Speed | Heat |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero bias** (full ON/OFF swing) | 0 | I_ON | HIGH | SLOW | Low |
| **Quasi-bias** (below threshold) | 0.85 I_th | I_ON | Moderate | Fast | Low |
| **Above-threshold bias** | I_th | I_ON | LOW | FASTEST | High |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to know that **above-threshold biasing** minimizes chirp and turn-on delay at the cost of higher power dissipation and reduced extinction ratio. The choice depends on bit rate and link budget.

## 2.3 Coupling with Fibres — Full Theoretical Analysis

Coupling efficiency is governed by **three independent loss mechanisms**:

### A. Numerical Aperture Loss (NA Mismatch)
The fiber only accepts light rays within its **acceptance cone**, half-angle θ_a = sin⁻¹(NA). The fraction of emitted power captured depends on the **source angular distribution**.

- **Lambertian source (LED):** emits intensity proportional to cos(θ). Coupled fraction = sin²(θ_a) = **NA²**
- **Narrow-beam source (LD):** if all power is within the NA cone, η → 1 (but practical mismatch still exists)

### B. Area Mismatch Loss
If the source emitting area is **larger** than the fiber core, the excess light misses the core. For an LED with emitting diameter d_s and fiber core diameter d_f:

$$\eta_{\text{area}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } d_s \le d_f \\ \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_s}\right)^2 & \text{if } d_s > d_f \end{cases}$$

### C. Misalignment Losses (Mechanical Tolerances)
- **Lateral (transverse) misalignment** — Δx
- **Angular misalignment** — Δθ
- **Longitudinal (gap) separation** — Δz

For a step-index multimode fiber, lateral misalignment loss (in dB) is approximately:

$$L_{\text{lateral}} \approx -10 \log_{10}\left[1 - \dfrac{\Delta x}{a}\right] \quad \text{(dB)}$$

where `a` is the fiber core radius.

## 2.4 Coupling Techniques (with Practical Diagrams)

| Technique | Description | Typical Loss | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Butt Coupling** | Source placed flush against fiber end | 10–20 dB (LED), 3–5 dB (LD) | Simple systems, cost-effective |
| **Lens Coupling (Spherical ball lens)** | Glass sphere between source and fiber | 1–3 dB | High-efficiency LED/LD coupling |
| **Tapered Fiber (Pigtail)** | Fiber tip melted into a taper | 1–2 dB | Hermetic LD packages |
| **Microlens on Fiber Tip** | Lens etched/fused on fiber endface | 2–4 dB | Telecom-grade LDs |
| **GRIN-rod Lens Coupling** | Graded-index rod lens in between | 1–2 dB | Multi-fiber ribbon couplers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **syllabus highlight**: For a Lambertian source (LED) to step-index multimode fiber via **direct butt coupling**, the **coupling efficiency** is given by the **classic formula η = (NA)²**, neglecting Fresnel reflection. KTU frequently tests this directly.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{couple}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{fiber}}}{P_{\text{source}}}$ | Coupling efficiency | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{Lambertian}} = \sin^2(\theta_a) = \text{NA}^2$ | LED-to-step-index-fiber efficiency | Valid for perfect alignment |
| 3 | $L_{\text{couple}} = -10 \log_{10}(\eta_{\text{couple}})$ | Coupling loss | dB |
| 4 | $\text{NA} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ | Numerical Aperture (step-index) | $\Delta = (n_1^2 - n_2^2)/2n_1^2$ |
| 5 | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$ | Acceptance half-angle | Radians or degrees |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{area}} = (d_f / d_s)^2$ | Area mismatch efficiency | When $d_s > d_f$ |
| 7 | $P_{\text{out}} = \eta_{\text{ext}} \cdot \dfrac{h\nu}{e} \cdot I$ | LED output power | Watts |
| 8 | $f_{3\text{dB,LED}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_c}$ | LED modulation bandwidth | Hz; $\tau_c$ = carrier lifetime |
| 9 | $I_{\text{bias,LCD}} = I_{\text{th}} + \dfrac{I_{\text{mod}}}{2}$ | LD analog bias point | mA; $I_{\text{mod}}$ is peak AC swing |
| 10 | $P_{\text{ext ratio}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\right)$ | Extinction ratio | dB; important for digital LD drivers |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "NA-Squared Law":** *"For an LED, Lambertian gives you NA-squared, no matter what!"* — Use this for butt-coupled LED-to-MM-fiber problems.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CATV Networks**: Direct analog modulation of laser diodes requires strict linearity; pre-emphasis circuits compensate for non-linearity.
- **Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH)**: Low-cost LED transmitters with simple driver circuits and butt-coupling dominate (e.g., 1.25 Gbps EPON).
- **Long-Haul DWDM**: External Mach-Zehnder modulators are used *instead* of direct LD modulation to avoid chirp; but the LD itself is still DC-biased by a precision current driver.
- **Medical/Sensor Systems**: LEDs with ball-lens coupling are used in low-power optical sensor probes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Coupling Efficiency of a Lambertian Source to a Step-Index Fiber (η = NA²)

**Given:**
- LED is a perfect **Lambertian emitter** (intensity ∝ cos θ in all directions)
- Step-index multimode fiber with **core radius a**, **NA = n₁√(2Δ)**
- Source area ≤ fiber core area (no area mismatch)
- Perfect lateral and angular alignment (no Fresnel reflection loss — assumed AR-coated)

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Lambertian Intensity Pattern:**
For a perfect Lambertian source, the radiant intensity (power per unit solid angle) is:

$$I(\theta) = I_0 \cos\theta \quad \text{for } 0 \le \theta \le \pi/2$$

where $I_0$ is the on-axis intensity.

**Step 2 — Total Emitted Power (P_source):**
Integrate $I(\theta)$ over the forward hemisphere ($0 \le \theta \le \pi/2$, $0 \le \phi \le 2\pi$):

$$
P_{\text{source}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_0 \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

$$
P_{\text{source}} = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta
$$

$$
P_{\text{source}} = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \left[\dfrac{\sin^2\theta}{2}\right]_{0}^{\pi/2} = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \pi I_0
$$

**Step 3 — Power Accepted by Fiber (P_fiber):**
The fiber accepts only those rays with $\theta \le \theta_a$ where $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$. Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{fiber}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\theta_a} I_0 \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

$$
P_{\text{fiber}} = 2\pi I_0 \int_{0}^{\theta_a} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = 2\pi I_0 \cdot \dfrac{\sin^2\theta_a}{2}
$$

$$
P_{\text{fiber}} = \pi I_0 \sin^2\theta_a
$$

**Step 4 — Coupling Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{fiber}}}{P_{\text{source}}} = \dfrac{\pi I_0 \sin^2\theta_a}{\pi I_0} = \sin^2\theta_a
$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of NA:**
By definition of acceptance angle, $\sin\theta_a = \text{NA}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\eta = \sin^2\theta_a = \text{NA}^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** A Lambertian source couples only $\text{NA}^2$ fraction of its total power into a step-index fiber. For NA = 0.20, this gives η = 0.04 = 4%, i.e., **~14 dB coupling loss** — a very large loss that motivates the use of **lens coupling**.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Coupling Loss in dB from Numerical Aperture

**Given:** NA = 0.30, calculate the coupling loss for an LED to a step-index fiber.

**Step 1 — Apply η = NA²:**

$$
\eta = (0.30)^2 = 0.09
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to dB:**

$$
L_{\text{couple}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.09)
$$

$$
L_{\text{couple}} = -10 \times (-1.0458) = 10.458 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with secondary check:**
$\log_{10}(0.09) = \log_{10}(9 \times 10^{-2}) = \log_{10}(9) + \log_{10}(10^{-2}) = 0.9542 - 2 = -1.0458$ ✓

**Final Answer:** $L_{\text{couple}} \approx 10.46$ dB.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: LD Pre-bias Point Calculation for Digital Modulation

**Given:** A GaInAsP laser diode has $I_{\text{th}} = 20$ mA, modulation current swing $I_{\text{mod,pp}} = 30$ mA peak-to-peak, and must operate at the **quasi-bias point** ($I_{\text{b}} = 0.9 I_{\text{th}}$) to minimize chirp. Calculate $I_{\text{ON}}$, $I_{\text{OFF}}$, and the average DC bias.

**Step 1 — Quasi-bias current:**

$$
I_{\text{b}} = 0.9 \times 20 = 18 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Peak modulation current (half of peak-to-peak):**

$$
I_{\text{mod,pk}} = 30/2 = 15 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — ON-state current:**

$$
I_{\text{ON}} = I_{\text{b}} + I_{\text{mod,pk}} = 18 + 15 = 33 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — OFF-state current:**

$$
I_{\text{OFF}} = I_{\text{b}} - I_{\text{mod,pk}} = 18 - 15 = 3 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify OFF state is below threshold:**
$I_{\text{OFF}} = 3$ mA $\ll I_{\text{th}} = 20$ mA ✓ (laser is OFF in the "0" bit — chirp present but acceptable)

**Step 6 — Average DC current (for thermal design):**

$$
I_{\text{DC,avg}} = I_{\text{b}} = 18 \text{ mA}
$$

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Fresnel Reflection Loss at Source-Fiber Interface

**Given:** Fiber has $n_1 = 1.48$ (silica), LED has $n_{\text{LED}} = 3.5$ (GaAs). Find the power reflection coefficient R and the transmission efficiency (1 − R).

**Step 1 — Apply Fresnel reflection formula (normal incidence):**

$$
R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_{\text{LED}}}{n_1 + n_{\text{LED}}}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
R = \left(\dfrac{1.48 - 3.5}{1.48 + 3.5}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{-2.02}{4.98}\right)^2 = (-0.4056)^2
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate:**

$$
R = 0.1645 \approx 16.45\%
$$

**Step 4 — Transmission efficiency (without AR coating):**

$$
T = 1 - R = 1 - 0.1645 = 0.8355 \approx 83.55\%
$$

**Step 5 — Loss in dB:**

$$
L_{\text{Fresnel}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.8355) = 0.78 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why LED/fiber interfaces are typically **anti-reflection (AR) coated** to reduce Fresnel loss. An ideal AR coating would have refractive index $n_{\text{AR}} = \sqrt{n_1 \cdot n_{\text{LED}}} \approx 2.27$ and thickness $\lambda/4$ at the operating wavelength.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — LED vs LD Drivers

| Parameter | LED Driver | LD Driver |
|---|---|---|
| Bias current required | Moderate (mid-P-I linear region) | Critical (at or above $I_{\text{th}}$) |
| Threshold handling | None | Mandatory — must not dip below $I_{\text{th}}$ |
| Linearity | Excellent over wide range | Good only above $I_{\text{th}}$ |
| Modulation bandwidth | 10 – 200 MHz | 1 – 10+ GHz |
| Protection circuits | Current limiter | APC, ATC, ESD, reverse-polarity |
| Output power | Low (–10 dBm typical) | High (0 to +10 dBm) |
| Spectral width | Wide (20–100 nm) | Narrow (< 0.1 nm) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Coupling to MM fiber | NA² loss (10+ dB) | ~3–5 dB |
| Coupling to SM fiber | Not feasible (large area) | 3–6 dB with lens |
| KTU exam weightage | Medium | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LED Analog Modulator (Driver Circuit) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCBIAS[DC Bias Current Source] --> SUMMINGNODE[Summing Node OpAmp]
    ACIN[AC Modulating Signal m t] --> COUPLINGCAP[Coupling Capacitor]
    COUPLINGCAP --> SUMMINGNODE
    SUMMINGNODE --> DRIVERAMP[Driver Amplifier]
    DRIVERAMP --> LED[LED Optical Source]
    LED --> FIBER[Optical Fiber]
    LED -.monitor.-> PD[Monitor Photodiode]
    PD --> FBAMP[Feedback Amplifier]
    FBAMP -.APC Feedback.-> SUMMINGNODE
    SAFELATCH[Overcurrent Protection Latch] -.limit.-> DRIVERAMP
    THERM[Thermal Sensor] -.derate.-> DCBIAS
```

**Explanation of the block diagram:**

1. **DC Bias Current Source**: Establishes the LED's operating point at the midpoint of the linear P–I region. Set by a precision current mirror or op-amp + pass transistor.
2. **AC Modulating Signal (m(t))**: The information signal enters through a coupling capacitor (to block DC offset).
3. **Summing Node Op-Amp**: Combines DC bias and AC modulation with proper weighting.
4. **Driver Amplifier**: Provides current gain to drive the LED; must be linear and have high slew rate.
5. **LED Optical Source**: The LED converts current to light linearly.
6. **Monitor Photodiode (PD) + Feedback Amplifier**: Senses backlight from the LED and closes an **Automatic Power Control (APC)** loop to stabilize output against temperature and aging.
7. **Overcurrent Protection Latch**: Disables the driver if current exceeds the LED's maximum rating, preventing COD.
8. **Thermal Sensor**: Reduces bias current if junction temperature rises, preventing thermal runaway.

---

## 4.2 LD Digital Modulator (Driver Circuit) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    DCBIAS[DC Bias Source I_b at I_th] --> SUMMER[Current Summing Node]
    DATAGEN[Digital Data Input NRZ] --> PREDRIVER[Pre-driver Level Shifter]
    PREDRIVER --> MODDRIVER[Modulation Current Switch]
    MODDRIVER --> SUMMER
    SUMMER --> LD[Laser Diode LD]
    LD --> FIBER[Optical Fiber]
    LD -.back-facet monitor.-> MPD[Monitor Photodiode]
    MPD --> APCCTRL[APC Controller]
    APCCTRL -.control.-> DCBIAS
    TEMPSENSE[Thermistor on LD heatsink] --> TECCTRL[TEC Controller]
    TECCTRL --> TEC[TEC Peltier Cooler]
    TEC -.cools.-> LD
    LATCH[LD Protection Latch] -.shutdown.-> MODDRIVER
```

**Explanation of key blocks:**

- **Pre-driver**: Converts logic levels (e.g., 0/3.3 V) to modulation current levels (e.g., 0/30 mA) with edge-rate control.
- **Modulation Current Switch**: A high-speed current switch (BJT/FET differential pair) toggles $I_{\text{mod}}$ ON/OFF.
- **Monitor Photodiode (MPD)**: Reads the rear-facet emission of the LD (a small, stable fraction proportional to front output).
- **APC Controller**: Adjusts $I_{\text{b}}$ so that average optical power remains constant despite aging or temperature.
- **TEC (Thermoelectric Cooler) + Thermistor**: Holds the LD chip at a constant temperature (e.g., 25 °C) for wavelength stability and $I_{\text{th}}$ stability.
- **Protection Latch**: Triggers on overcurrent, ESD, or open MPD — disables driver to prevent LD damage (LDs are **ESD-sensitive** and **expensive**).

---

## 4.3 Source-to-Fiber Coupling Schemes — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Scheme1 [Direct Butt Coupling]
        S1SRC[LED or LD] -- close contact --> S1FIB[Fiber Core]
    end
    subgraph Scheme2 [Spherical Ball Lens Coupling]
        S2SRC[LED or LD] -- gap --> S2LENS[Sapphire Ball Lens] -- focused --> S2FIB[Fiber Core]
    end
    subgraph Scheme3 [Tapered Fiber Pigtail]
        S3SRC[LD] -- proximity --> S3TAPER[Tapered Fiber Tip] -- guides --> S3FIB[Fiber Core]
    end
    subgraph Scheme4 [GRIN-Rod Lens Coupling]
        S4SRC[LD or LED] -- gap --> S4GRIN[GRIN-Rod Lens] -- image --> S4FIB[Fiber Core]
    end
    S1FIB --> COUPLED1[Coupling Loss 10-20 dB LED]
    S2FIB --> COUPLED2[Coupling Loss 1-3 dB]
    S3FIB --> COUPLED3[Coupling Loss 1-2 dB]
    S4FIB --> COUPLED4[Coupling Loss 1-2 dB]
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The diagram compares **four common coupling techniques** in terms of achievable loss.
- The **direct butt** is simplest but most lossy because the small core area + low NA reject most of the source's emitted power.
- **Ball lens, tapered fiber, and GRIN-rod lens** schemes use optical imaging to compress the source emission into the fiber's acceptance cone, dramatically improving coupling.
- The numerical losses shown are **typical**; actual values depend on wavelength, NA, core size, and alignment tolerance.

---

## 4.4 Misalignment Loss Sub-Categories — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    MISALIGN[Total Coupling Loss] --> LAT[Lateral Misalignment Loss]
    MISALIGN --> ANG[Angular Misalignment Loss]
    MISALIGN --> LONG[Longitudinal Gap Loss]
    MISALIGN --> FRES[Fresnel Reflection Loss]
    MISALIGN --> NALOSS[NA Mismatch Loss]
    LAT --> LATDESC[Drift in x-y plane; loss = 10 log10 of 1 - delta-x over a]
    ANG --> ANGDESC[Tilt of fiber axis vs source axis; loss increases as angular offset squared]
    LONG --> LONGDESC[Air gap between source and fiber; loss rises as gap increases beyond Rayleigh range]
    FRES --> FRESDESC[Index mismatch at interface; loss = -10 log10 of 1 minus R]
    NALOSS --> NADESC[Source emission outside NA cone; for LED eta = NA squared]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of DC bias current in an LED modulator circuit. Why is the bias point chosen at the midpoint of the linear P–I region?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — approx. 80–100 words):
The **DC bias current** sets the **quiescent operating point (Q-point)** of the LED on its linear P–I curve, so that the AC information signal can modulate the optical output around this point. If the bias is set at the **midpoint of the linear region**, the LED can faithfully reproduce both the **positive and negative half-cycles** of the modulating signal without entering the non-linear turn-on knee (low current) or the saturation/thermal-rollover region (high current). This minimizes **harmonic and intermodulation distortion** in analog transmission.

**Mark split:** Definition of bias + role (1) + midpoint justification (1) + distortion link (1).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is coupling efficiency? Derive the coupling efficiency of a Lambertian source (LED) to a step-index multimode fiber in terms of numerical aperture.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Coupling efficiency** η is defined as the ratio of optical power coupled into the fiber to the total power emitted by the source:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{fiber}}}{P_{\text{source}}}
$$

For a **Lambertian** source (intensity $I(\theta) = I_0 \cos\theta$) and a step-index fiber of acceptance angle $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(\text{NA})$, integration over the forward hemisphere gives:

$$
P_{\text{source}} = \pi I_0, \quad P_{\text{fiber}} = \pi I_0 \sin^2\theta_a
$$

$$
\therefore \eta = \sin^2\theta_a = \text{NA}^2
$$

**Mark split:** Definition (1) + Lambertian integration (1) + final result (1).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]:**
**With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a digital laser diode (LD) modulator. What is the role of automatic power control (APC) and automatic temperature control (ATC) in an LD transmitter?**

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

The digital LD modulator consists of:
- A **DC bias current source** that pre-biases the LD at or just below threshold current $I_{\text{th}}$.
- A **high-speed modulation current switch** (BJT/FET differential pair) driven by the NRZ data input.
- A **summing node** where DC bias + modulation current are added and fed to the LD.
- A **monitor photodiode (MPD)** at the rear facet of the LD, providing a feedback signal proportional to the output optical power.
- An **APC controller** that compares MPD current to a reference and adjusts the DC bias to keep the average optical power constant.
- A **thermo-electric cooler (TEC)** + **thermistor** + **ATC controller** that maintain the LD junction at a fixed temperature (e.g., 25 °C).
- **Protection latches** for overcurrent, ESD, and open MPD.

**2. Operating Principle (2 marks):**

When the data input is "1" (logic high), the modulation switch conducts and adds $I_{\text{mod}}$ to $I_{\text{bias}}$, driving the LD above threshold — laser emits coherent light at $P_{\text{ON}}$. When the data is "0", the switch is OFF, current falls to (or below) $I_{\text{th}}$ — laser emits minimal spontaneous light. The output is an OOK-NRZ optical signal.

**3. Role of APC and ATC (2 marks):**

- **APC (Automatic Power Control)**: Compensates for $I_{\text{th}}$ drift due to aging and temperature. As the LD ages, $I_{\text{th}}$ increases and slope efficiency decreases. The APC loop increases $I_{\text{bias}}$ to maintain constant $P_{\text{avg}}$.
- **ATC (Automatic Temperature Control)**: Holds LD chip at a constant temperature. This stabilizes $I_{\text{th}}$, output wavelength, and prevents thermal runaway. A TEC is used with reverse polarity for heating and forward for cooling.

**Mark split:** Diagram (3) + Working (2) + APC/ATC role (2) = 7

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
**A step-index multimode fiber has NA = 0.20 and core diameter 50 μm. An LED with emitting diameter 90 μm and a Lambertian pattern is butt-coupled to this fiber. Calculate: (i) NA mismatch loss, (ii) area mismatch loss, (iii) total coupling efficiency, (iv) total coupling loss in dB.**

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):

**Given:**
- NA = 0.20
- Core diameter $d_f$ = 50 μm
- Source diameter $d_s$ = 90 μm
- Source = Lambertian LED

**(i) NA Mismatch Loss (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{NA}} = \text{NA}^2 = (0.20)^2 = 0.04
$$

$$
L_{\text{NA}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.04) = 13.98 \approx 14 \text{ dB}
$$

**(ii) Area Mismatch Loss (2 marks):**
Since $d_s = 90 \,\mu\text{m} > d_f = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
\eta_{\text{area}} = \left(\dfrac{d_f}{d_s}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{50}{90}\right)^2 = (0.5556)^2 = 0.3086
$$

$$
L_{\text{area}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.3086) = 5.10 \text{ dB}
$$

**(iii) Total Coupling Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{NA}} \times \eta_{\text{area}} = 0.04 \times 0.3086 = 0.01235
$$

**(iv) Total Coupling Loss in dB (1.5 marks):**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.01235) = 19.08 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer:** Total coupling efficiency = **1.235%**, Total coupling loss = **19.08 dB**.

**Mark split:** NA (2) + Area (2) + Total η (1.5) + dB conversion (1.5) = 7

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) [7 marks]:**
**Compare the modulation characteristics of LED and LD sources. Explain with diagrams the above-threshold and below-threshold biasing schemes for laser diodes.**

#### Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):

**1. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | LED | LD |
|---|---|---|
| Emission | Spontaneous | Stimulated |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Coherent |
| Spectral width | 20–100 nm | < 0.1 nm |
| Threshold | None | $I_{\text{th}}$ required |
| Modulation bandwidth | 10–200 MHz | 1–10+ GHz |
| Linearity | Good below saturation | Good above $I_{\text{th}}$ |
| Output power | Low (–10 dBm) | High (0 to +10 dBm) |
| Driver complexity | Low | High (APC/ATC) |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Coupling to fiber | NA² loss typical | Lower loss with lens |

**2. Above-threshold biasing (2 marks):**
The LD is biased at $I_b > I_{\text{th}}$ (e.g., $I_b = 1.1 I_{\text{th}}$). Both ON and OFF states are above threshold. The OFF state still emits some light (reduces extinction ratio), but the **chirp is minimized** and the **turn-on delay is eliminated** — ideal for high-bit-rate digital systems.

**3. Below-threshold biasing (quasi-bias) (2 marks):**
The LD is biased at $I_b = 0.9 I_{\text{th}}$ (below threshold). The OFF state emits only spontaneous light (low power, high extinction). The ON state must swing above $I_{\text{th}}$, producing **relaxation oscillation chirp** and **turn-on delay**, but **power dissipation and extinction ratio** are better.

**Mark split:** Table (3) + Above-threshold (2) + Below-threshold (2) = 7

---

#### **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
**With neat diagrams, explain the different techniques used for coupling an optical source to an optical fiber. Compare their coupling losses.**

#### Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):

**1. Direct Butt Coupling (1.5 marks):**
The source is placed in physical contact with the fiber endface. No optics used. Simple and inexpensive, but suffers from large NA and area mismatch losses. Typical loss: **10–20 dB for LED**, **3–5 dB for LD**.

**2. Lens Coupling — Spherical Ball Lens (1.5 marks):**
A small sapphire or glass sphere (diameter ~1–2 mm) is placed between source and fiber. The lens collects a wide angular range of source emission and focuses it into the fiber core. Loss: **1–3 dB** for both LED and LD.

**3. Tapered Fiber Pigtail (1.5 marks):**
The fiber tip is heated and pulled to form a tapered (lensed) end. The taper effectively increases the local NA and acceptance area. Common in hermetic LD packages. Loss: **1–2 dB**.

**4. GRIN-Rod Lens Coupling (1.5 marks):**
A graded-index rod lens (1/4 pitch) is interposed. It collimates light from the source and refocuses into the fiber with excellent aberration correction. Loss: **1–2 dB**. Ideal for multi-fiber ribbon and WDM applications.

**5. Comparison Summary (1 mark):**
Lensed schemes (ball, tapered, GRIN) all give **1–3 dB** loss — orders of magnitude better than direct butt for LEDs. For LDs, the loss difference is smaller because LDs already have narrow beam divergence. The choice is driven by **cost, alignment tolerance, and reliability**.

**Mark split:** Butt (1.5) + Ball lens (1.5) + Tapered (1.5) + GRIN (1.5) + Summary (1) = 7

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Modulator & Coupling Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to include Fresnel reflection loss** when computing total coupling efficiency. KTU examiners often test the *complete* expression $\eta_{\text{total}} = (1 - R_{\text{Fresnel}}) \cdot \text{NA}^2 \cdot \eta_{\text{area}}$ — omitting the Fresnel term costs a full mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing "above-threshold" vs "below-threshold" biasing trade-offs.** Writing *"below-threshold biasing is better for high-speed"* (it isn't!) will cost 1–2 marks. Remember: **above-threshold = low chirp, fast; below-threshold = low power, high extinction.**
>
> 3. **Applying $\eta = \text{NA}^2$ for a laser diode.** This formula is valid **only for Lambertian (LED) sources**. For LDs, the emission is highly directional and the NA² law **does not apply** — you must use **overlap integral** or **mode-matching** methods.
>
> 4. **Unit confusion in dB calculations.** Coupling loss is **negative** dB of gain. Always write $L = -10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ and report a **positive dB loss**. Students often write $-10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ and stop at the negative number, losing 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 5. **In LED drivers, students forget the AC coupling capacitor** that prevents the DC bias from reaching the signal source. Always include both **DC bias path** and **AC modulation path** in your diagrams.
>
> 6. **For the "why" of LD threshold:** Examiners love asking *"Why can't we modulate an LD by switching fully OFF (zero bias)?"* — Answer must mention **turn-on delay, relaxation oscillations, and chirp**, not just "the laser wouldn't lase."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Print & Memorize Before Exam:**

### A. Modulator Circuits
- ✅ **LED modulation** is direct, simple, and linear; requires **DC bias** for analog, **ON/OFF switching** for digital.
- ✅ **LD modulation** must handle the **threshold current** — bias must be at or above $I_{\text{th}}$ to avoid chirp and turn-on delay.
- ✅ **Pre-bias schemes**: (a) Zero-bias — simplest, worst chirp; (b) Quasi-bias ($0.85 I_{\text{th}}$) — balanced; (c) Above-threshold bias ($1.0$ to $1.1 I_{\text{th}}$) — best speed, worst extinction.
- ✅ **APC** uses a **rear-facet monitor photodiode** to stabilize average optical power.
- ✅ **ATC** uses a **TEC + thermistor** to hold LD junction temperature constant — stabilizes $I_{\text{th}}$, wavelength, and prevents thermal runaway.
- ✅ LDs are **ESD-sensitive** and require **protection latches**.

### B. Coupling with Fibres
- ✅ **Coupling efficiency** $\eta = P_{\text{fiber}} / P_{\text{source}}$.
- ✅ **Lambertian (LED) to step-index fiber**: $\eta = \sin^2\theta_a = \text{NA}^2$ — derive, don't just memorize.
- ✅ **Area mismatch**: $\eta_{\text{area}} = (d_f / d_s)^2$ when $d_s > d_f$, else 1.
- ✅ **Three misalignment types**: lateral (Δx), angular (Δθ), longitudinal (Δz) — each contributes its own loss factor.
- ✅ **Fresnel reflection loss** at LED-fiber interface: $R = [(n_1 - n_s)/(n_1 + n_s)]^2$ (GaAs-to-silica ≈ 16%).
- ✅ **Coupling techniques (in increasing complexity)**: Direct butt → Ball lens → Tapered fiber → GRIN-rod lens.
- ✅ **Coupling loss target**: < 1 dB (tapered/GRIN), 3–5 dB (butt with LD), 10–20 dB (butt with LED).

### C. Numerical Benchmarks to Remember
- ✅ $\text{NA} = 0.20 \Rightarrow \eta_{\text{LED-to-MMF}} = 4\% \Rightarrow L = 14$ dB.
- ✅ $\text{NA} = 0.30 \Rightarrow \eta = 9\% \Rightarrow L = 10.46$ dB.
- ✅ $\text{NA} = 0.40 \Rightarrow \eta = 16\% \Rightarrow L = 7.96$ dB.
- ✅ Fresnel loss (GaAs/silica, no AR) ≈ **0.78 dB** per interface.

### D. Formula Flashcard
- ✅ $\eta = \text{NA}^2$
- ✅ $L = -10 \log_{10}(\eta)$ dB
- ✅ $\text{NA} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$, $\Delta = (n_1^2 - n_2^2)/2n_1^2$
- ✅ $\eta_{\text{area}} = (d_f/d_s)^2$
- ✅ $I_{\text{ON}} = I_b + I_{\text{mod,pk}}$; $I_{\text{OFF}} = I_b - I_{\text{mod,pk}}$
- ✅ $R = [(n_1 - n_s)/(n_1 + n_s)]^2$

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, **always** include a **neatly labelled block diagram** (3–4 marks), a **comparison table** where applicable (2–3 marks), and **fully worked numerical steps** with units (4–5 marks). Narrative without numbers and diagrams scores poorly in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Detectors: Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Detectors: Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Quantum Efficiency — The "Catch Rate" of Photons

**Quantum Efficiency (η)** of an optical detector is formally defined as the probability that an incident photon of energy $h\nu$ striking the photosensitive surface of the detector will generate a usable electron–hole pair (EHP) that contributes to the external photocurrent.

It is a **dimensionless, unitless ratio** between $0$ and $1$ (or $0\%$ to $100\%$), expressing the internal conversion efficiency of photon flux to electron flux inside the device.

Mathematically, the formal KTU definition is:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons generated per second contributing to photocurrent}}{\text{Number of incident photons per second}}$$

$$0 \leq \eta \leq 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity are the two primary **figures-of-merit (FOMs)** used by the KTU Optical Communication syllabus to characterize the optical-to-electrical conversion fidelity of PIN photodiodes and Avalanche Photodiodes (APDs). They directly appear in 3-mark and 14-mark questions every examination cycle.

---

### 1.2 Two Variants of Quantum Efficiency

Optical detectors in KTU-style problems are evaluated using **two distinct quantum efficiency metrics**:

| Variant | Symbol | What it Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Quantum Efficiency** | $\eta_i$ | Photons that are *absorbed inside the depletion region* (i.e., photons that successfully penetrate and generate EHPs). |
| **External Quantum Efficiency** | $\eta_{ext}$ | Photons that arrive at the physical surface — accounts for Fresnel reflection losses at the air–semiconductor interface. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **External Quantum Efficiency = (1 − R_surface) × Internal Quantum Efficiency**
> where $R_{surface}$ is the Fresnel reflection coefficient at the detector facet. This is a high-yield KTU relationship.

---

### 1.3 Responsivity — The "Earnings per Rupee" of Photons

**Responsivity (R)** is the most practical engineering metric used in optical communication receivers. It directly tells the circuit designer **how much electrical current (in Amperes) is produced per unit of optical power (in Watts) incident on the detector.**

The formal KTU definition:

$$R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} \quad \left[ \text{Units: Amperes / Watt (A/W)} \right]$$

where $I_p$ is the generated photocurrent (Amperes) and $P_{in}$ is the incident optical power (Watts).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Responsivity is the slope of the photodetector's $I$–$P$ characteristic curve.** A high-responsivity detector produces a large current for a small optical input — exactly what is required for long-haul, low-noise optical links.

---

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **mango tree at the bottom of a hill** during the monsoon season. Mangoes fall (analogous to **photons falling on a detector**). Three things can happen:

1. **Some mangoes roll away** before reaching the basket — *Reflection losses at the detector surface* (reduces external efficiency).
2. **Some mangoes land on the soft grass and rot** without being collected — *Photons absorbed in non-active regions* (i.e., not in the depletion region).
3. **Only some mangoes actually land in the basket and are sold** — *Useful electron–hole pairs contributing to photocurrent*.

- **Quantum Efficiency** $\eta$ = *Basket catches ÷ Total mangoes that reached the orchard*. It is a probability, a pure ratio.
- **Responsivity** $R$ = *Money earned ÷ Total mangoes that fell on the property*. It depends on the *price* (energy) of each mango and the *value* (charge) the basket pays per mango.

In physics terms: the *price of each photon* is its energy $h\nu$, and the *value paid per captured photon* is the electron charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$. This gives the **master equation**:

$$R = \eta \cdot \frac{q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Responsivity grows with wavelength $\lambda$ (longer wavelengths → less energetic photons → more photons per Watt → more current per Watt), but only up to the **bandgap-limited cutoff wavelength** $\lambda_c$, beyond which the semiconductor becomes transparent.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants You Must Memorise

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Planck's Constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$ |
| Speed of Light in Vacuum | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \approx 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ |
| Elementary Charge | $q$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ |
| Ratio $hc$ | $hc$ | $1.986 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$ |
| Ratio $hc/q$ | $hc/q$ | $1.2398 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V·m} = 1240 \text{ nm·eV}$ |

These appear in **nearly every numerical problem** in KTU Optical Communication papers.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physics of Photon-to-Electron Conversion

The photodetection process in a PIN photodiode proceeds in three conceptual stages:

1. **Photon Absorption**: An incoming photon of energy $h\nu$ must possess energy **at least equal to the semiconductor bandgap** $E_g$. That is, the photon absorption condition is $h\nu \geq E_g$, equivalently $\lambda \leq \lambda_c$, where the cutoff wavelength is:
   $$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

2. **EHP Generation**: A single absorbed photon, if successful, excites **exactly one electron** from the valence band into the conduction band, producing **one electron–hole pair (EHP)**. The pair is created inside the depletion region (the *active zone*) where the strong built-in field sweeps them apart before they can recombine.

3. **Carrier Collection**: The drift field pulls the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side, contributing **one elementary charge $q$** to the external circuit per successfully collected EHP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If recombination occurs before collection, the EHP is lost.** This is the dominant physical mechanism that limits $\eta_i < 1$. Recombination can be radiative (band-to-band), non-radiative (trap-assisted, Auger), or surface-mediated.

### 2.2 Why "Quantum" Efficiency?

The name "quantum" comes from the fact that $\eta$ compares **discrete countable events** — incident photons (quanta of light) vs. emitted electrons (quanta of charge). It is essentially a **photon-to-electron count ratio**, not an energy ratio.

### 2.3 Why Responsivity Depends on Wavelength

Photons of different wavelengths carry different energies:

$$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

A **1550 nm** photon carries less energy ($\approx 0.80 \text{ eV}$) than a **850 nm** photon ($\approx 1.46 \text{ eV}$). For the same incident optical power (Watts = Joules/second), more low-energy photons arrive per second than high-energy ones. Each photon still produces at most one electron, so:

$$\text{Photocurrent} = (\text{photons/sec}) \times \eta \times q$$

Therefore, the same optical power at 1550 nm produces **more current per Watt** than at 850 nm, assuming $\eta$ is constant. This is why $R \propto \lambda$ in the ideal model.

### 2.4 Wavelength Cutoff and Long-Wavelength Limit

For photon energies below the bandgap, the semiconductor is transparent — the photon passes through without absorption, contributing zero photocurrent. The **long-wavelength cutoff** is:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \quad \mu\text{m}$$

For example:
- **Silicon** ($E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$) → $\lambda_c \approx 1.1 \text{ \mu m}$ (suitable for 850 nm window).
- **Germanium** ($E_g = 0.67 \text{ eV}$) → $\lambda_c \approx 1.85 \text{ \mu m}$ (covers 1300 nm and partial 1550 nm).
- **InGaAs** ($E_g = 0.75 \text{ eV}$) → $\lambda_c \approx 1.65 \text{ \mu m}$ (primary choice for 1310 nm and 1550 nm telecom windows).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{I_p/q}{P_{in}/h\nu}$ | Quantum efficiency definition | Dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{I_p}{P_{in}}$ | Responsivity definition | A/W |
| 3 | $R = \eta \cdot \dfrac{q}{h\nu} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | **Master Responsivity Equation** | Most heavily tested in KTU |
| 4 | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ | Cutoff wavelength | $\mu$m if $E_g$ in eV: $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ |
| 5 | $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_s) \cdot \eta_i$ | External = (1 − reflection) × Internal | $R_s$ = Fresnel reflection |
| 6 | $I_p = R \cdot P_{in}$ | Photocurrent from responsivity | Amperes |
| 7 | $I_p = \eta \cdot q \cdot \Phi_p$ | Photocurrent from photon flux | $\Phi_p$ = photons/sec |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** In the responsivity formula, students often forget that $\lambda$ is in metres, not nanometres. If you plug $\lambda$ in nm, you must divide by $10^9$ or pre-divide $hc$ accordingly. The cleanest form: $R = \dfrac{\eta \lambda (\mu\text{m})}{1.24}$ in A/W.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Receiver Design**: The transimpedance amplifier (TIA) gain required at the receiver is directly determined by $R$ — knowing responsivity lets the designer compute the expected voltage from a known optical power.
- **Link Budget Calculation**: $P_{in}$ at the detector determines $I_p$ via $R$. Combined with the receiver noise floor, this sets the **bit-error rate (BER)** and the **maximum achievable fiber length**.
- **Material Selection**: Comparing $\lambda_c$ across Si, Ge, InGaAs, InSb, HgCdTe allows engineers to pick the optimal detector for each telecom window (850, 1310, 1550 nm).
- **WDM Networks**: Each wavelength channel in a Dense WDM (DWDM) link has a slightly different responsivity, requiring per-channel calibration in high-precision systems.

### 2.7 Typical Practical Values (Memorize for KTU)

| Material | Active Wavelength Range | Typical $\eta$ | Peak $R$ (A/W) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Silicon (Si) | 400 – 1100 nm | 60 – 90% | 0.4 – 0.65 |
| Germanium (Ge) | 800 – 1650 nm | 50 – 80% | 0.5 – 0.85 |
| InGaAs | 900 – 1700 nm | 70 – 90% | 0.7 – 0.95 |
| InGaAs APD | 1300 – 1550 nm | 60 – 85% (×M) | 5 – 10 (with gain) |
