## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Responsivity Equation

This is the **most-asked derivation in KTU Optical Communication papers**. We derive it rigorously from first principles.

---

**Starting Point: Photon Flux**

Consider an incident optical power $P_{in}$ (in Watts = Joules/second) at wavelength $\lambda$. Each photon carries energy $E_p = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. Therefore, the number of photons arriving at the detector per second is:

$$\Phi_{inc} = \frac{P_{in}}{E_p} = \frac{P_{in}}{hc/\lambda} = \frac{P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc} \quad \left[ \text{photons/second} \right]$$

**Step 1: Number of EHPs generated per second**

By the definition of external quantum efficiency $\eta$, only a fraction $\eta$ of the incident photons successfully generate EHPs that are collected. So the number of EHPs contributing to photocurrent per second is:

$$\text{EHPs/sec} = \eta \cdot \Phi_{inc} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 2: Convert EHPs/sec to current**

Each collected EHP delivers exactly one elementary charge $q$ (Coulombs) to the external circuit. Therefore the photocurrent is:

$$I_p = (\text{EHPs/sec}) \times q = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc} \times q$$

**Step 3: Form the responsivity ratio**

By definition, $R = I_p / P_{in}$, so dividing both sides by $P_{in}$:

$$R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta \cdot \lambda \cdot q}{hc}$$

This is the **canonical KTU master equation**, written in three equivalent forms:

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\eta q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc} = \frac{\eta \lambda (\mu\text{m})}{1.24} \;\;\; \text{A/W}}$$

---

### 3.2 Alternative Derivation Using Current Definition

Starting from the defining equation for quantum efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Electrons/sec}}{\text{Photons/sec}} = \frac{I_p / q}{P_{in} / h\nu} = \frac{I_p \cdot h\nu}{q \cdot P_{in}}$$

Solving for $I_p$:

$$I_p = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot P_{in}}{h\nu}$$

Dividing by $P_{in}$ to obtain responsivity:

$$R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

This confirms the same result from a different algebraic starting point.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem Walkthrough — KTU-Style

**Problem:** A silicon PIN photodiode is illuminated with optical power $P_{in} = 10 \text{ \mu W}$ at wavelength $\lambda = 900 \text{ nm}$. The external quantum efficiency is $\eta = 0.80$. Calculate:
(a) the responsivity $R$, and
(b) the generated photocurrent $I_p$.

**Given Data:**
- $P_{in} = 10 \text{ \mu W} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$
- $\lambda = 900 \text{ nm} = 900 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 0.9 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$

**Part (a) — Responsivity:**

Using $R = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$:

$$R = \frac{0.80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$$

Numerator:
$$0.80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 1.28 \times 10^{-19}$$
$$1.28 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9} = 1.28 \times 900 \times 10^{-28} = 1152 \times 10^{-28} = 1.152 \times 10^{-25}$$

Denominator:
$$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$$

Therefore:
$$R = \frac{1.152 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.5795 \text{ A/W}$$

**Short cut using $R = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ with $\lambda$ in $\mu$m:**

$$R = \frac{0.80 \times 0.9}{1.24} = \frac{0.72}{1.24} = 0.5806 \text{ A/W}$$

Both methods agree to 4 significant figures: $R \approx 0.58 \text{ A/W}$.

**Part (b) — Photocurrent:**

$$I_p = R \cdot P_{in} = 0.58 \text{ A/W} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} = 5.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 5.8 \text{ \mu A}$$

**Verification via photon flux method:**

$$\Phi_{inc} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc} = \frac{10^{-5} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-12}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.527 \times 10^{13} \text{ photons/sec}$$

EHPs/sec = $\eta \cdot \Phi_{inc} = 0.80 \times 4.527 \times 10^{13} = 3.622 \times 10^{13}$

$$I_p = 3.622 \times 10^{13} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 5.795 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 5.8 \text{ \mu A} \;\checkmark$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Connect Every Quantity to Its Origin

| Quantity | Symbol | Source / Origin | Depends on |
|---|---|---|---|
| Photon energy | $h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Planck's quantum theory | Wavelength $\lambda$ |
| Incident photon flux | $\Phi_p = P_{in} \lambda / hc$ | Power ÷ energy per photon | Power, wavelength |
| EHPs collected per second | $\eta \cdot \Phi_p$ | Definition of $\eta$ | $\eta$, power, wavelength |
| Photocurrent | $I_p = \eta \cdot q \cdot \Phi_p$ | Charge per EHP | $\eta$, $q$, power, wavelength |
| Responsivity | $R = I_p/P_{in}$ | Slope of $I$–$P$ curve | $\eta$, $\lambda$ |
| Cutoff wavelength | $\lambda_c = hc/E_g$ | Bandgap condition | Material $E_g$ |

### 3.5 Graphical Behaviour: $R$ vs. $\lambda$ — The "Signature Curve"

For an ideal photodetector with $\eta$ = const = 1 over its absorption band:

$$R_{ideal}(\lambda) = \frac{q\lambda}{hc} \quad \text{— a straight line through origin}$$

In practice, $\eta$ is not constant — it rolls off near $\lambda_c$ due to decreasing absorption coefficient (photons penetrate too deeply to be collected). The realistic curve is:

- A near-linear **rise** from $\lambda \approx 0$ to mid-range $\lambda$.
- A **peak** typically around $70$–$90\%$ of $\lambda_c$.
- A **roll-off** approaching $\lambda_c$.
- **Zero** for $\lambda > \lambda_c$ (semiconductor transparent).

> [!NOTE]
> This $R$–$\lambda$ curve is the **photodetector's spectral signature**, and the KTU examiner often includes it as a "draw and label" question in 14-mark problems.

### 4.1 Photon-to-Current Conversion — Block Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below maps the sequential processing stages by which an incident optical photon is converted to a measurable photocurrent in a PIN photodiode.

```mermaid

    A[Light Source Optical Power P_in] --> B[Photon Flux Incident phi_inc = P_in times lambda over h c]

    C -- Transmitted 1 minus R_s fraction --> D[Photon Enters Depletion Region]
    D --> E{Bandgap Test h nu greater than or equal to E_g}

    E -- Yes lambda less than or equal to lambda_c --> F[Photon Absorbed Creates EHP]
    F --> G{Recombination Trap in Depletion Region}

    G -- Survives --> H[Drift Field Sweeps Electron to n side and Hole to p side]
    H --> I[Electron Charge q Delivered to External Circuit]
    I --> J[Photocurrent I_p = eta times q times phi_inc]
    J --> K[Receiver Transimpedance Amplifier TIA]
    K --> L[Output Voltage V_out]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the term Quantum Efficiency of a photodetector. Distinguish between internal and external quantum efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$) of a photodetector is defined as the probability that an incident photon of energy $h\nu$ on the photosensitive surface will generate a usable electron–hole pair that contributes to the external photocurrent.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Number of EHPs generated that contribute to photocurrent per second}}{\text{Number of incident photons per second}} = \frac{I_p / q}{P_{in}/h\nu}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity with values between $0$ and $1$ (or $0\%$ to $100\%$).

**Distinction (2 Marks):**

- **Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_i$):** The probability that a photon **absorbed within the depletion region** of the photodiode will generate a collected EHP. It depends only on the semiconductor's bulk recombination properties.

- **External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$):** The probability that a photon **incident from outside** (hitting the physical surface) will eventually contribute a collected EHP. It accounts for the **Fresnel reflection loss** at the air–semiconductor interface, governed by:
  $$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_s) \cdot \eta_i$$
  where $R_s$ is the power reflection coefficient at the detector facet. $\eta_{ext} \le \eta_i$ always.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Responsivity of a photodetector. State the relation between Responsivity, Quantum Efficiency, and wavelength.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Responsivity ($R$) of a photodetector is defined as the ratio of the generated photocurrent ($I_p$, in Amperes) to the incident optical power ($P_{in}$, in Watts):

$$R = \frac{I_p}{P_{in}} \quad [\text{A/W}]$$

It is a measure of the detector's electrical output per unit optical input.

**Relation (2 Marks):** Responsivity is related to quantum efficiency ($\eta$) and wavelength ($\lambda$) by the master equation:

$$R = \eta \cdot \frac{q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

Using the convenient identity $hc/q = 1.24 \text{ \mu m·V}$:

$$R = \frac{\eta \cdot \lambda}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W, with } \lambda \text{ in } \mu\text{m}$$

**Key observation (extra credit):** For a given detector, $R \propto \lambda$ in the ideal case — longer wavelength (lower energy) photons produce more current per Watt, until the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ (in $\mu$m, $E_g$ in eV) is reached.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Question (Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the responsivity of a photodetector in terms of its quantum efficiency and wavelength. State clearly the meaning of each symbol used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Si PIN photodiode is illuminated with an optical signal of wavelength $\lambda = 1310 \text{ nm}$ and incident power $P_{in} = 50 \text{ \mu W}$. The external quantum efficiency is $\eta = 0.65$. Calculate:
   (i) the responsivity of the photodiode, **[3 Marks]**
   (ii) the resulting photocurrent, **[2 Marks]**
   (iii) the number of photons striking the detector per second, and **[2 Marks]**
   (iv) the long-wavelength cutoff for Si given $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$. **[2 Marks]** (Wait — only **(b)(i)–(iv) together = 7 Marks**)

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Let $P_{in}$ (Watts) be the incident optical power at wavelength $\lambda$ (metres). Each photon carries energy $E_p = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. The number of photons incident per second is:

$$\Phi_{inc} = \frac{P_{in}}{E_p} = \frac{P_{in}}{hc/\lambda} = \frac{P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

By the definition of quantum efficiency, only a fraction $\eta$ of these photons generates a useable EHP:

$$\text{Collected EHPs/sec} = \eta \cdot \Phi_{inc} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Each collected EHP delivers one elementary charge $q$ to the external circuit, so the photocurrent is:

$$I_p = (\text{Collected EHPs/sec}) \cdot q = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{hc} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

By the definition of responsivity $R = I_p / P_{in}$:

$$R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Equivalent forms and physical interpretation:

$$R = \frac{\eta q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta \lambda(\mu\text{m})}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Symbol meanings (1 mark):** $\eta$ = quantum efficiency (dimensionless), $q$ = electron charge ($1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C), $h$ = Planck's constant ($6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s), $c$ = speed of light ($3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s), $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light (m), $\nu$ = optical frequency (Hz).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $\lambda = 1310 \text{ nm} = 1.31 \text{ \mu m}$, $P_{in} = 50 \text{ \mu W} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$, $\eta = 0.65$, $E_g = 1.12 \text{ eV}$.

**(i) Responsivity [3 Marks]:**

Using the short-cut form $R = \eta \lambda / 1.24$ with $\lambda$ in $\mu$m:

$$R = \frac{0.65 \times 1.31}{1.24} = \frac{0.8515}{1.24} = 0.6867 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.687 \text{ A/W}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

**(ii) Photocurrent [2 Marks]:**

$$I_p = R \cdot P_{in} = 0.6867 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 34.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_p \approx 34.33 \text{ \mu A}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Photon flux [2 Marks]:**

$$\Phi_{inc} = \frac{P_{in} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1310 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$$

Numerator: $50 \times 1310 \times 10^{-15} = 6.55 \times 10^{-11}$
Denominator: $1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$

$$\Phi_{inc} = \frac{6.55 \times 10^{-11}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 3.295 \times 10^{14} \text{ photons/sec}$$

$$\boxed{\Phi_{inc} \approx 3.30 \times 10^{14} \text{ photons/sec}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iv) Cutoff wavelength [2 Marks]:**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} \text{ \mu m} = \frac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107 \text{ \mu m} = 1107 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_c \approx 1.11 \text{ \mu m} = 1107 \text{ nm}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Since the operating wavelength $1310 \text{ nm} > \lambda_c = 1107 \text{ nm}$, strictly silicon is *transparent* at 1310 nm. In practice, a finite absorption tail allows some response, and the given $\eta$ reflects that fact. In real telecom links, **InGaAs** (with $\lambda_c \approx 1.65 \text{ \mu m}$) is used for 1310 nm and 1550 nm.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of external quantum efficiency of a photodetector. Discuss the effect of Fresnel reflection losses and the bandgap of the semiconductor material on the overall responsivity. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An InGaAs photodetector has a bandgap of $0.75 \text{ eV}$ and operates at $\lambda = 1550 \text{ nm}$ with internal quantum efficiency $0.80$. The Fresnel reflection coefficient at the entrance facet is $R_s = 0.05$.
   (i) Determine the cutoff wavelength. **[2 Marks]**
   (ii) Compute the external quantum efficiency. **[2 Marks]**
   (iii) Find the responsivity. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):**

**External Quantum Efficiency (3 Marks):**

External quantum efficiency $\eta_{ext}$ of a photodetector is the probability that a photon arriving at the **physical surface** of the detector will eventually contribute one collected electron to the external photocurrent. It accounts for *all* loss mechanisms from the moment the photon strikes the device until the EHP is collected.

Mathematically:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{\text{Useful electrons/sec in external circuit}}{\text{Photons/sec incident on detector surface}}$$

**Effect of Fresnel reflection (2 Marks):**

When a photon strikes the air–semiconductor interface, a fraction $R_s$ of the incident optical power is **reflected** back into the air, governed by the Fresnel equations. For normal incidence on a semiconductor with refractive index $n$:

$$R_s = \left(\frac{n - 1}{n + 1}\right)^2$$

For silicon ($n \approx 3.5$), $R_s \approx 0.30$ — i.e., **30% of the incident light is lost** to reflection! This is why anti-reflection (AR) coatings are universally applied. The relation is:

$$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_s) \cdot \eta_i$$

**Effect of bandgap (2 Marks):**

The semiconductor bandgap $E_g$ determines the **long-wavelength cutoff** $\lambda_c = hc/E_g = 1.24/E_g$ (in $\mu$m). For $\lambda > \lambda_c$, the photon energy is insufficient to bridge the bandgap, and the semiconductor is transparent — $\eta = 0$. For $\lambda < \lambda_c$, the photon is absorbed (with depth governed by the absorption coefficient $\alpha(\lambda)$) and may produce an EHP. The responsivity therefore:

- **Rises linearly** with $\lambda$ when $\eta$ is roughly constant: $R \propto \lambda$.
- **Drops to zero** at $\lambda = \lambda_c$.
- For best high-responsivity performance, one chooses materials whose $E_g$ corresponds to the telecom window: **InGaAs** for 1310/1550 nm.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $E_g = 0.75 \text{ eV}$, $\lambda = 1550 \text{ nm} = 1.55 \text{ \mu m}$, $\eta_i = 0.80$, $R_s = 0.05$.

**(i) Cutoff wavelength [2 Marks]:**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{0.75} = 1.653 \text{ \mu m} = 1653 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_c \approx 1.653 \text{ \mu m}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

Since $\lambda = 1550 \text{ nm} < \lambda_c = 1653 \text{ nm}$, the InGaAs detector can absorb the signal. ✓

**(ii) External quantum efficiency [2 Marks]:**

$$\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_s) \cdot \eta_i = (1 - 0.05) \times 0.80 = 0.95 \times 0.80 = 0.76$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ext} = 0.76 \text{ (or 76\%)}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Responsivity [3 Marks]:**

$$R = \frac{\eta_{ext} \cdot \lambda}{1.24} \;\; \text{A/W, with } \lambda \text{ in } \mu\text{m}$$

$$R = \frac{0.76 \times 1.55}{1.24} = \frac{1.178}{1.24} = 0.950 \text{ A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.95 \text{ A/W}} \quad \text{[3 marks]}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing $\lambda$ in nm and $hc$ in SI units *without* converting to metres. Always convert $\lambda$ to metres first OR use the shortcut form $R = \eta \lambda(\mu\text{m})/1.24$.
>
> 2. **Wrong $\eta$ used**: Students sometimes use $\eta_i$ when the question specifies "external" or use $\eta_{ext}$ when only "internal" is asked. Read the question carefully.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Fresnel factor**: If a problem provides $R_s$, the **external** $\eta$ must include $(1 - R_s)$. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if this factor is omitted.
>
> 4. **Confusing $\lambda_c$ direction**: The cutoff is a **long-wavelength** limit. Students occasionally write the wrong inequality direction. Always check $h\nu \geq E_g$, i.e., $\lambda \leq \lambda_c$.
>
> 5. **Missing symbol definitions in derivations**: The 7-mark derivation question *requires* you to define every symbol ($h$, $c$, $q$, $\eta$, $\lambda$, $I_p$, $P_{in}$). Examiners deduct 1 mark if symbols are undefined.
>
> 6. **Mixing energy units**: In shortcut calculations, $E_g$ must be in **eV** for $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ to give $\mu$m. If $E_g$ is given in Joules, convert to eV first by dividing by $1.6 \times 10^{-19}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$)** is a **dimensionless probability** ($0 \le \eta \le 1$) that an incident photon generates a collected EHP. It is a *count* ratio: electrons out / photons in.
- **Responsivity ($R$)** is measured in **A/W** — Amperes of photocurrent per Watt of incident optical power. It is the **practical engineering figure-of-merit** of a photodetector.
- **Master equation** (most heavily tested): $R = \eta q \lambda / hc = \eta \lambda(\mu\text{m})/1.24 \text{ A/W}$.
- **Internal $\eta_i$** vs. **External $\eta_{ext}$**: Always remember $\eta_{ext} = (1 - R_s) \cdot \eta_i$.
- **Long-wavelength cutoff**: $\lambda_c = hc/E_g = 1.24/E_g \text{ (eV)} \text{ in } \mu\text{m}$. Beyond this, $\eta = 0$ and $R = 0$.
- **Photocurrent**: $I_p = R \cdot P_{in} = \eta q \Phi_{inc}$, where $\Phi_{inc} = P_{in} \lambda / hc$ is the incident photon flux.
- **Wavelength scaling**: In the useful band, $R \propto \lambda$ when $\eta$ is constant — longer wavelength → more current per Watt.
- **Material choice by bandgap**:
  - Si ($E_g = 1.12$ eV, $\lambda_c \approx 1.11 \text{ \mu m}$) — 850 nm window.
  - Ge ($E_g = 0.67$ eV, $\lambda_c \approx 1.85 \text{ \mu m}$) — covers 1300/1550 nm but with high noise.
  - InGaAs ($E_g = 0.75$ eV, $\lambda_c \approx 1.65 \text{ \mu m}$) — **primary 1310/1550 nm telecom detector**.
- **APD vs. PIN**: Avalanche photodiodes multiply the photocurrent by an internal gain $M$, so their effective responsivity becomes $R_{APD} = M \cdot R_{PIN}$.
- **Fresnel loss at high-index semiconductors is severe** (e.g., $\sim 30\%$ for Si at 850 nm) — this is why AR coatings are essential in practical receivers.
- **Recombination mechanisms** (radiative, Auger, surface) are the dominant reasons $\eta_i < 1$ in real devices; reducing these is the focus of detector material engineering.
- **The $R$–$\lambda$ curve** of a real photodetector rises linearly, peaks near $70$–$90\%$ of $\lambda_c$, and rolls off to zero at $\lambda_c$ — a high-yield "draw and label" item in 14-mark KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Working of PIN and APD; Optical Receivers: Direct Detection, Noise in Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure and Working of PIN and APD; Optical Receivers: Direct Detection, Noise in Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photodetectors in Optical Communication

A **photodetector** is a semiconductor device that converts incoming optical (light) signals into electrical signals. In fiber-optic communication systems, the photodetector is the critical front-end component of the **optical receiver**, responsible for the optoelectronic conversion that enables signal processing in the electrical domain.

The two most important semiconductor photodetectors used in KTU-syllabus optical communication are:

1. **PIN Photodiode** (Positive-Intrinsic-Negative)
2. **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Both PIN and APD operate under **reverse bias** to create a depletion region that captures incoming photons. The fundamental difference is the **internal gain mechanism** in APDs.

---

## 1.2 PIN Photodiode – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **PIN photodiode** is a three-layer semiconductor device (p-type, intrinsic/near-intrinsic, n-type) operated under reverse bias. Photons are absorbed primarily in the wide intrinsic (I) region, generating electron–hole pairs that are swept by the strong electric field to the respective contacts, producing a photocurrent.

### Conceptual Analogy – The "Solar Pond" Analogy

Imagine a deep, still pond (the **intrinsic region**) with two metal plates on opposite sides (the **p and n contacts**). When sunlight (photons) enters the pond, the absorbed light energy excites water molecules (electrons) that can now move. If you place a strong voltage across the plates (reverse bias), every excited molecule is immediately pulled to one of the plates with predictable direction. The resulting flow of charged particles is exactly the **photocurrent**. The wider the pond, the more light it can catch — this is why a wider intrinsic region gives **higher responsivity**.

---

## 1.3 Avalanche Photodiode (APD) – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** is a photodetector designed to provide **internal current gain** (typically 10–100) through **avalanche multiplication**. It is operated at a high reverse bias (near or just below the breakdown voltage) so that photogenerated carriers acquire enough kinetic energy to generate additional electron–hole pairs by impact ionization, producing a self-amplifying photocurrent.

### Conceptual Analogy – The "Snowball Effect" on a Slope

Think of an APD like a steep snowy mountainside (the **multiplication region**). When a single ball of snow (a photogenerated electron) is released, it rolls down and gathers more snow as it goes, growing larger and faster. By the time it reaches the bottom, one tiny snowflake has become a large snowball. The **multiplication factor (M)** is the ratio of the size of the final snowball to the original snowflake. However, this process is also **noisy and statistical** — sometimes the ball is bigger, sometimes smaller — which is the key trade-off of APDs.

---

## 1.4 Optical Receiver & Direct Detection

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **optical receiver** is the subsystem of an optical communication link that converts the optical signal emerging from the fiber back into a faithful electrical replica of the original information signal. The simplest and most common technique is **direct detection**, where the photodetector's output current is proportional to the incident optical power.

### Conceptual Analogy – A Rain Gauge with Electronics

Imagine a **rain gauge** (the photodetector) collecting rainfall (light). The amount of water collected per second is the optical power. The electronics attached simply **measure the water flow rate** (current). You cannot determine the *colour* of individual raindrops or their *phases* — only how much falls per unit time. This is **direct detection** — it recovers only the **intensity envelope** of the optical signal, not its phase or frequency.

---

## 1.5 Noise in Optical Detectors

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Noise determines the **minimum detectable optical power** and the **bit-error-rate (BER)** of the receiver. The fundamental quantum limit arises from the **discrete (photon) nature of light**.

### Primary Noise Sources

| Noise Source | Origin | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantum (Shot) Noise** | Statistical arrival of photons | $\sigma_Q^2$ |
| **Dark Current Noise** | Thermally generated carriers | $\sigma_D^2$ |
| **Thermal (Johnson) Noise** | Pre-amplifier resistance | $\sigma_T^2$ |
| **Relative Intensity Noise (RIN)** | Laser power fluctuations | $\sigma_{RIN}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric:** The minimum optical power required at the receiver to achieve a **BER = $10^{-9}$** in an ideal photon-counting receiver is approximately **10 log₁₀(20.7) ≈ 13.16 dBm equivalent to 41 photons/bit** at 1550 nm. This is the **quantum limit** of direct detection.

---

## 1.6 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Operating wavelengths:** 850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm
- **Standard photodetector materials:** Si (800–900 nm), Ge (1300–1600 nm), InGaAs (1300–1600 nm)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PIN Photodiode – Layered Structure & Energy Band Diagram

A PIN photodiode consists of three distinct regions:

1. **p⁺ region** (heavily doped p-type) — very thin, allows light to pass
2. **Intrinsic (i) region** — wide, lightly doped n-type, the **primary absorption region**
3. **n⁺ region** (heavily doped n-type) — substrate contact

The intrinsic region is intentionally left undoped (or very lightly doped) to:

- **Widen the depletion region** (since the field drops mostly across the lowest-doped zone)
- **Increase the absorption volume** for high quantum efficiency
- **Reduce junction capacitance** for high-speed operation

### Operating Principle – Step by Step

1. **Reverse bias application:** A reverse voltage (typically 5–20 V) is applied. The depletion region extends almost entirely across the intrinsic layer, creating a strong uniform electric field $\mathcal{E}$.
2. **Photon absorption:** An incoming photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (bandgap) is absorbed in the intrinsic region, promoting an electron from the valence band to the conduction band → **generation of one electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
3. **Carrier drift:** The E-field sweeps the electron toward the n⁺ side and the hole toward the p⁺ side at their respective **drift saturation velocities** ($v_{e,sat} \approx 10^5\ \text{m/s}$, $v_{h,sat} \approx 5 \times 10^4\ \text{m/s}$).
4. **Photocurrent generation:** The flow of these carriers across the external circuit constitutes the photocurrent $I_p$.
5. **No internal gain:** Each absorbed photon produces at most **one electron flowing in the external circuit** (quantum limit of gain = 1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Why" matters:** The intrinsic region is undoped so that the depletion width is maximized, ensuring that almost all photogenerated carriers are produced inside a high-field drift region — this maximizes both **quantum efficiency** AND **response speed**.

---

## 2.2 Avalanche Photodiode (APD) – Structure & Working

The APD adds a **multiplication region** (also called the **avalanche region**), typically a separate p-layer where the electric field is extremely high (≈ $3 \times 10^5\ \text{V/cm}$).

### APD Layer Stack (Typical Separate Absorption & Multiplication – SAM structure)

| Layer | Function | Field Strength |
|---|---|---|
| p⁺ contact | Anode contact | Low |
| Absorption region (InGaAs, undoped) | Photon → EHP generation | Moderate |
| Grading/charge layer | Smooth field transition | — |
| Multiplication region (InP or InAlAs) | Avalanche multiplication | **Very high** |
| n⁺ contact | Cathode contact | Low |

### Working Mechanism – Step by Step

1. **Photon absorption** in the wide-bandgap absorption layer (InGaAs at 1550 nm) creates a primary EHP.
2. **Drift** of the primary hole/electron into the narrow-bandgap multiplication region.
3. **Impact ionization:** The carrier, accelerated by the very high field, gains kinetic energy greater than the bandgap and **ionizes a lattice atom**, creating a *secondary* EHP. The new carriers are themselves accelerated and may create *tertiary* carriers — a self-sustaining **avalanche**.
4. **Multiplication factor M:** The total number of electrons collected per primary photogenerated pair is

$$M = \frac{I_{ph,APD}}{I_{ph,primary}}$$

5. **Statistical nature:** $M$ is a *random* variable; its mean $\langle M \rangle$ is the design parameter, but its variance contributes **excess avalanche noise** characterized by the **excess noise factor** $F(M)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off rule:** Higher $M$ → higher signal AND higher noise. The optimum $M$ is where total SNR (signal-to-noise ratio) is maximized — typically $M = 10$ to $100$ for InGaAs APDs at 1550 nm.

---

## 2.3 Optical Receiver – Functional Blocks

A practical digital optical receiver consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Photodetector (PIN or APD)** — converts light to current
2. **Low-noise preamplifier (LNA)** — converts weak current to voltage with minimum added noise
3. **Equalizer (filter)** — shapes the pulse to limit intersymbol interference (ISI)
4. **Main amplifier** — boosts signal to decision-circuit level
5. **Decision circuit / Clock recovery** — samples at bit-center, decides 0 or 1

### Direct Detection vs. Coherent Detection

| Feature | Direct Detection | Coherent Detection |
|---|---|---|
| Optical local oscillator | **No** | Yes |
| Recovered information | Intensity only | Intensity + phase + frequency |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Sensitivity | Near quantum limit | At quantum limit |
| Used in | Most commercial systems | Long-haul, high-capacity |

---

## 2.4 Noise Sources – Rigorous Analysis

### 2.4.1 Quantum (Shot) Noise

Photon arrival is a **Poisson process**. Even for a constant optical power $P$, the number of absorbed photons $N$ in time $T$ fluctuates:

$$\sigma_N^2 = \langle N \rangle$$

The corresponding **shot noise current variance** at the detector output is:

$$\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2qI_p B = 2qM^2(I_{ph} + I_d)B$$

where $B$ is the **noise equivalent bandwidth** (Hz).

### 2.4.2 Dark Current Noise

Even with no light, thermally generated carriers produce a small **dark current** $I_d$. Its shot noise is:

$$\sigma_{dark}^2 = 2qI_d B$$

For APDs, dark current is also multiplied: $I_{d,APD} = M I_d$.

### 2.4.3 Thermal (Johnson–Nyquist) Noise

The input load resistance $R_L$ (or pre-amplifier equivalent input resistance) generates thermal noise:

$$\sigma_{thermal}^2 = \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$$

where $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ is Boltzmann's constant and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

### 2.4.4 Relative Intensity Noise (RIN)

The laser's optical power fluctuates. This fluctuation is characterized by RIN (dB/Hz) and contributes noise only when the signal itself is being measured:

$$\sigma_{RIN}^2 = (RIN) \cdot I_p^2 B$$

### 2.4.5 Total Receiver Noise

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2 + \sigma_{thermal}^2 + \sigma_{RIN}^2$$

For an APD-based receiver:

$$\sigma_{total}^2 = 2qM^2 F(M)(I_{ph} + I_d)B + \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$$

where $F(M) = k_e M + (1-k_e)(2 - 1/M)$ is the **excess noise factor**, and $k_e$ is the **ionization coefficient ratio** (electron- vs. hole-initiated ionization).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E_{ph} = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | J or eV |
| 2 | Quantum efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\text{electrons collected}}{\text{photons incident}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Responsivity (PIN) | $\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q}{h\nu} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W |
| 4 | Responsivity (APD) | $\mathcal{R}_{APD} = M \cdot \mathcal{R}_{PIN}$ | A/W |
| 5 | Primary photocurrent | $I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} P_{opt}$ | A |
| 6 | APD photocurrent | $I_{APD} = M \mathcal{R} P_{opt}$ | A |
| 7 | Shot noise variance | $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2qI_p B$ | A² |
| 8 | Dark current noise | $\sigma_{dark}^2 = 2qI_d B$ | A² |
| 9 | Thermal noise | $\sigma_{thermal}^2 = 4k_B T B / R_L$ | A² |
| 10 | Excess noise factor | $F(M) = k_e M + (1-k_e)(2-1/M)$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Total noise (APD) | $\sigma^2 = 2qM^2F(M)(I_{ph}+I_d)B + 4k_BTB/R_L$ | A² |
| 12 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR = I_{ph}^2 / \sigma_{total}^2$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = \sqrt{\sigma_{total}^2}/\mathcal{R}$ for $SNR=1$ | W/√Hz |
| 14 | Detectivity | $D^* = \sqrt{A_{d}} / NEP$ | cm·√Hz/W |
| 15 | Bit Error Rate (OOK) | $BER = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{I_1-I_0}{2\sqrt{2}\sigma}\right)$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Quantum limit (BER=10⁻⁹) | $\bar{N}_{min} = 20.7$ photons/bit | photons |
| 17 | APD optimum M (approx.) | $M_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4k_BT/q^2}{R_L \cdot 2(I_{ph}+I_d)}}$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always carry the constant $\frac{q}{hc} \approx 8.06 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A·s/J}$ (or equivalently $1.24\ \mu\text{m·A/W}$ at unit quantum efficiency) when solving numerical problems. The responsivity at unit efficiency is $\mathcal{R} = \lambda/1.24\ \mu\text{m}$ A/W.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PIN photodiodes** are used in **short-reach LANs, FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home) receivers, and PON (Passive Optical Network) OLT/ONU** due to their low cost, low bias voltage, and excellent linearity.
- **APDs** are deployed in **long-haul DWDM links, submarine cables, and high-bit-rate (>10 Gb/s) systems** where the extra 5–10 dB of sensitivity improvement is critical for extending the reach between optical amplifiers.
- **Direct detection receivers** dominate commercial telecom because they avoid the cost and complexity of coherent optics — making the **understanding of their noise-limited sensitivity** the most important KTU exam topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity of a PIN Photodiode

### Step 1 — Define the incident photon flux

Let an optical power $P_{in}$ (W) be incident on the photodiode active area $A_d$ at wavelength $\lambda$. The energy per photon is:

$$E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

The rate of photons incident on the detector (number per second) is:

$$\Phi_{in} = \frac{P_{in}}{E_{ph}} = \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

> **Logic:** Power is energy per unit time, so dividing the incident power by the energy of one photon gives the photon arrival rate.

### Step 2 — Define the quantum efficiency

Not every incident photon produces a collected electron. Some are reflected from the surface, some are absorbed outside the depletion region (and recombine), and some generated carriers recombine before being collected. We define:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons collected per second}}{\text{Number of incident photons per second}} = \frac{\Phi_{collected}}{\Phi_{in}}$$

Typical values: $\eta = 0.6$ to $0.9$ for high-quality InGaAs PINs.

### Step 3 — Express the primary photocurrent

Each collected electron carries charge $q$. The current is therefore:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \Phi_{collected} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi_{in}$$

Substituting $\Phi_{in}$ from Step 1:

$$I_{ph} = q \eta \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

### Step 4 — Define and solve for the responsivity

**Responsivity $\mathcal{R}$** is the photocurrent per unit incident optical power:

$$\mathcal{R} \equiv \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

Rearranging explicitly:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

**Numerical sanity check** at $\lambda = 1550$ nm and $\eta = 0.8$:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{0.8 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.55 \times 10^{-6}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{1.987 \times 10^{-25}}{1.988 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.999\ \text{A/W} \approx 1.0\ \text{A/W}$$

> **Logic:** This is the "1 A/W" designer's rule of thumb for a high-quality 1550 nm photodiode — a frequent KTU numerical problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: APD Photocurrent Including Multiplication

The APD multiplies the primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$ by a mean gain $M$:

$$I_{APD} = M \cdot I_{ph} = M \mathcal{R} P_{in}$$

But $M$ is a random variable, so the **mean** output current is:

$$\langle I_{APD} \rangle = \langle M \rangle I_{ph} = \bar{M} \mathcal{R} P_{in}$$

The signal power at the load is $S = \langle I_{APD} \rangle^2 R_L$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for PIN Receiver

For a PIN-based direct-detection receiver, the dominant noise sources are shot noise (on the photocurrent) and thermal noise of the pre-amplifier.

### Step 1 — Mean squared signal current

$$S = I_{ph}^2 = (\mathcal{R} P_{in})^2$$

### Step 2 — Mean squared noise current

$$N = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{thermal}^2 = 2q I_{ph} B + \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$$

### Step 3 — SNR

$$SNR = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{\mathcal{R}^2 P_{in}^2}{2q \mathcal{R} P_{in} B + \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}}$$

### Step 4 — Limiting cases (important for KTU numericals)

**(a) Shot-noise limit** (large $P_{in}$, high-impedance front-end): $2q\mathcal{R}P_{in}B \gg 4k_BTB/R_L$

$$SNR_{shot} = \frac{\mathcal{R} P_{in}}{2qB}$$

> **Logic:** In this regime the SNR grows **linearly** with optical power — the photodetector is a "perfect" noise-free device limited only by the randomness of photon arrivals.

**(b) Thermal-noise limit** (small $P_{in}$ or low-impedance front-end):

$$SNR_{thermal} = \frac{\mathcal{R}^2 P_{in}^2 R_L}{4k_B T B}$$

> **Logic:** In this regime the SNR grows as the **square** of optical power — the pre-amplifier is the bottleneck.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bit Error Rate (BER) for OOK Direct Detection

On–Off Keying (OOK) is the simplest digital modulation: a '1' bit transmits optical power $P_1$; a '0' bit transmits $P_0 \approx 0$.

### Step 1 — Define the decision threshold current

The optimal threshold is the **mid-point** between the mean currents for '0' and '1':

$$I_D = \frac{I_1 + I_0}{2} = \frac{\mathcal{R} P_1 + \mathcal{R} P_0}{2}$$

Assume $P_0 = 0$ (ideal extinction ratio $\to \infty$), so $I_D = \mathcal{R}P_1/2$.

### Step 2 — Assume equal noise variance on both bits

For shot-noise-limited detection, the noise variance on the '1' bit is:

$$\sigma_1^2 = 2q \mathcal{R} P_1 B$$

On the '0' bit, only dark current contributes:

$$\sigma_0^2 = 2q I_d B$$

For an ideal receiver $I_d = 0$, so $\sigma_0^2 = 0$.

### Step 3 — Probability of error for the '0' bit (false alarm)

The '0' bit is erroneously detected as '1' if the noise on '0' pushes the current above threshold. With zero dark current:

$$P(0/1) = P_{FA} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Probability of error for the '1' bit (missed detection)

The '1' bit is missed if its noise pushes the current **below** the threshold. For a Gaussian noise approximation:

$$P(1/0) = P_{miss} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{I_1 - I_D}{2\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_1}\right)$$

Substituting $I_1 = \mathcal{R}P_1$, $I_D = \mathcal{R}P_1/2$, $\sigma_1^2 = 2q\mathcal{R}P_1 B$:

$$P(1/0) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\mathcal{R}P_1/2}{2\sqrt{2}\sqrt{2q\mathcal{R}P_1B}}\right)$$

$$P(1/0) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{\mathcal{R}P_1}{2qB}}\right)$$

Recognising the signal-photon count rate $\langle N \rangle = \mathcal{R}P_1/q$:

$$P(1/0) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\frac{\langle N \rangle}{2B}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{\langle N \rangle}}{4}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{T_b B}}$$

> **Logic:** The BER depends only on the **average number of photons per bit** $\bar{N}$ and the relationship between noise bandwidth and bit rate.

### Step 5 — Total BER

$$BER = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P(0/1) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot P(1/0)$$

For a symmetric, ideal direct-detection receiver:

$$\boxed{BER = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{\bar{N}}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Quantum limit at BER = $10^{-9}$

Setting $BER = 10^{-9}$ and solving for $\bar{N}$:

$$\text{erfc}(x) = 2 \times 10^{-9} \implies x \approx 3.0$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\sqrt{\bar{N}}}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 3.0 \implies \bar{N} \approx 72\ \text{photons/bit}\ \text{(Poisson model)}$$

The widely quoted **20.7 photons/bit** is for an *ideal photon-counting* receiver (discriminating 0 photons for '0' and $\geq 1$ photon for '1') — the rigorous quantum limit.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Optimum APD Multiplication Factor

The optimum $M$ maximises SNR. For a thermal-noise-limited front-end with multiplied shot noise:

$$SNR(M) = \frac{M^2 \mathcal{R}^2 P_{in}^2}{2qM^2(I_{ph}+I_d)B + 4k_BTB/R_L}$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $M$ and setting to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dM}\left[\frac{M^2}{M^2 A + C}\right] = 0$$

where $A = 2q(I_{ph}+I_d)B$ and $C = 4k_BTB/R_L$. The derivative is non-trivially zero only when:

$$\boxed{M_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{4k_BT/q^2}{R_L \cdot 2(I_{ph}+I_d)}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{F(M)}}}$$

> **Logic:** This shows the optimum $M$ **decreases** as input power $P_{in}$ increases — a counter-intuitive but well-known result that frequently appears in KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PIN Photodiode – Cross-Section & Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Optical Fiber Input P_in] --> B[Anti-Reflection Coating]
    B --> C[P+ Layer Thin Contact Window]
    C --> D[Intrinsic I Region Wide Absorption Zone]
    D --> E[N+ Layer Substrate]
    E --> F[Ohmic Contact]
    D --> G[Reverse Bias Voltage V_R 5 to 20 V]
    G --> H[Electric Field E Drifts EHPs]
    H --> I[Photocurrent I_ph Output]
    I --> J[Load Resistor R_L]
    J --> K[External Circuit]

    subgraph LAYER[PIN Layer Stack]
        direction LR
        L1[P+] --- L2[I] --- L3[N+]
    end
```

## 4.2 APD Cross-Section & Multiplication Architecture (SAM Structure)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Photon h nu Greater Than E_g] --> B[InGaAs Absorption Region Wide Bandgap]
    B --> C[Primary EHP Generation Eta]
    C --> D[Drift Carrier to Multiplication Region]
    D --> E[InP Multiplication Layer Very High E field]
    E --> F[Impact Ionization 1 to 2]
    F --> G[Secondary EHPs Created]
    G --> H[Tertiary Ionization Cascade]
    H --> I{Avalanche Statistically Stops}
    I --> J[Total Multiplied Current M times I_ph]
    J --> K[Output I_APD]

    subgraph STACK[SAM APD Layer Stack]
        direction LR
        M1[P+ Contact] --- M2[InGaAs Absorber] --- M3[Grading Layer] --- M4[InP Multiplier] --- M5[N+ Contact]
    end
```

## 4.3 Optical Receiver – Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Optical Signal From Fiber P_in t] --> B[Photodetector PIN or APD]
    B --> C[Photocurrent I_ph t]
    C --> D[Transimpedance Pre-Amplifier LNA]
    D --> E[Voltage Signal V out t]
    E --> F[Equalizer Filter ISI Suppression]
    F --> G[Main Amplifier Gain Block]
    G --> H[Decision Circuit with Threshold I_D]
    H --> I[Clock Recovery PLL]
    I --> J[Bit Stream 0 1 Output]

    subgraph NOISE[Noise Sources Coupled into Receiver]
        direction TB
        N1[Quantum Shot Noise] --- N2[Dark Current Noise]
        N2 --- N3[Thermal Johnson Noise]
        N3 --- N4[RIN from Laser]
    end
    NOISE --> D
```

## 4.4 APD Gain & Noise Trade-off Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Increase Reverse Bias V_R] --> B[Increase M Multiplication]
    B --> C[Signal Grows Linearly as M]
    B --> D[Excess Noise Factor F M Grows as M times k_e]
    D --> E[Total Noise Grows Faster than Signal]
    E --> F{SNR Maximum Reached}
    F -->|Yes| G[Optimal M opt]
    F -->|No More V_R| A
    G --> H[Receiver Operates at Best Sensitivity]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Detection Process – Direct vs. Coherent

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incoming Light E field t] --> B{Direct or Coherent}
    B -->|Direct| C[Square Law Detector I = R P]
    C --> D[Baseband Electrical Signal]
    B -->|Coherent| E[Mix with Local Oscillator E_LO]
    E --> F[Heterodyne or Homodyne Detection]
    F --> G[Recover Amplitude Phase Frequency]
    G --> H[Baseband with Full Vector Information]
    D --> I[Receiver Output]
    H --> I
```

## 4.6 PIN vs. APD – Side-by-Side Functional Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Photodiode Choice] --> B{Internal Gain Required}
    B -->|No Short Reach Low Cost| C[PIN Photodiode]
    B -->|Yes Long Haul High Sensitivity| D[Avalanche Photodiode APD]
    C --> E[M = 1 No Excess Noise Low Bias]
    D --> F[M = 10 to 100 Excess Noise High Bias]
    E --> G[Total SNR Limited by Thermal Noise]
    F --> H[Total SNR Limited by Multiplied Shot Noise]
    G --> I[Receiver Sensitivity around minus 20 dBm]
    H --> J[Receiver Sensitivity around minus 30 dBm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU):** In a Mermaid `flowchart`, arrows denote **signal/energy flow**, while subgraphs denote **logically grouped components**. Always read the diagram from top-left to bottom-right following the arrows to reconstruct the operational sequence — this is exactly the pattern KTU examiners expect in 14-mark structural questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: PIN vs. APD Comparison `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Distinguish between PIN photodiode and Avalanche Photodiode. Mention any **two** advantages of APD over PIN.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PIN Photodiode | APD |
|---|---|---|
| Internal gain | Unity ($M = 1$) | $M = 10$–$100$ |
| Structure | p-i-n | p-i-n + multiplication layer |
| Reverse bias | Low (5–20 V) | High (near breakdown, 30–100 V) |
| Noise | No excess noise | Excess noise factor $F(M) > 1$ |
| Sensitivity | Moderate | 5–10 dB better |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |

**Two advantages of APD over PIN:**
1. **Higher sensitivity** due to internal multiplication of photocurrent.
2. **Better SNR** for the same incident optical power, enabling longer unrepeatered link spans.

---

### Question 2: Direct Detection `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** What is meant by **direct detection** in an optical receiver? Why is it unable to recover phase information?

**Model Answer:**

**Direct detection** is the simplest method of optical demodulation in which the photodetector's output current is **directly proportional to the instantaneous optical power** incident on it:

$$I(t) = \mathcal{R} P_{opt}(t)$$

**Why it cannot recover phase:** The photodiode is a **square-law device** — its response depends only on the *magnitude squared* of the optical field, not its phase. The phase information $e^{j\phi(t)}$ of the optical carrier is lost in the squaring operation. Recovering phase requires **coherent detection** with a local oscillator.

---

## 5.2 Part B – ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **structure and working of an APD**. Derive the expression for **SNR of an APD-based direct-detection receiver** and discuss the role of the **excess noise factor $F(M)$**.

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Structure and Working of an APD [7 marks]

The **Avalanche Photodiode** is a photodetector that provides **internal current gain** through impact ionization. The most common design is the **SAM (Separate Absorption and Multiplication)** structure:

- **Absorption region** (InGaAs, undoped, width ≈ 1–3 μm): Photons are absorbed here, generating primary electron–hole pairs (EHPs).
- **Grading/charge layer** (InGaAsP): Smooths the bandgap discontinuity to prevent carrier pile-up.
- **Multiplication region** (InP or InAlAs, width ≈ 0.1–0.2 μm): High electric field ($\sim 5 \times 10^5$ V/cm) where avalanche multiplication occurs.
- **Contacts:** p⁺ on top, n⁺ at substrate bottom.

**Working principle:**

1. A photon with $h\nu > E_g$ of the absorber is absorbed, creating **one primary EHP**.
2. The primary carrier (hole for InP-based SAM) drifts into the multiplication region under a very high field.
3. The carrier gains kinetic energy $\geq E_g$ and **collides with the lattice**, creating a **secondary EHP** by impact ionization.
4. Both the original and the new carriers are accelerated and may create further pairs, producing an **avalanche cascade**.
5. The average number of electrons collected per primary photogenerated pair is the **multiplication factor** $M$.

The output photocurrent is:

$$I_{APD} = M \mathcal{R} P_{in}$$

The multiplication factor is statistically variable — characterized by its mean $\bar{M}$ and excess noise factor $F(M) = k_e \bar{M} + (1-k_e)(2-1/\bar{M})$, where $k_e$ is the ionization coefficient ratio (smaller $k_e$ → less noisy APD).

#### (b) SNR Derivation and Excess Noise Factor [7 marks]

For an APD receiver the mean output current is:

$$\langle I \rangle = M \mathcal{R} P_{in} = M I_{ph}$$

The mean-squared signal power is:

$$S = \langle I \rangle^2 = M^2 I_{ph}^2$$

The total noise variance (dominant terms) is:

$$\sigma^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{thermal}^2 = 2qM^2 F(M)(I_{ph} + I_d)B + \frac{4k_B T B}{R_L}$$

**Step 1 — Shot noise in APD:** Each multiplied electron contributes a shot-noise pulse. The variance is increased by a factor $M^2 F(M)$ because the random multiplication adds excess fluctuations.

**Step 2 — Thermal noise:** Unchanged by $M$ since it originates in the pre-amplifier.

**Step 3 — SNR expression:**

$$SNR = \frac{M^2 I_{ph}^2}{2qM^2F(M)(I_{ph}+I_d)B + 4k_BTB/R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Optimization:** To find the optimum $M$ that maximizes SNR, differentiate w.r.t. $M$ and set to zero. The result is the trade-off condition:

$$M^2 F(M) \cdot 2q(I_{ph}+I_d)B = \frac{4k_BTB}{R_L}$$

This is the **balancing condition** between multiplied shot noise and thermal noise. Solving for $M_{opt}$:

$$M_{opt} \approx \frac{1}{k_e}\sqrt{\frac{4k_BT}{q^2 R_L (I_{ph}+I_d) B}} - \frac{(1-k_e)}{k_e}$$

**Role of $F(M)$:**
- $F(M)$ is always $\geq 2$ for $M > 1$ and grows with $M$ (linearly for $k_e \approx 1$, or even more slowly for $k_e \ll 1$).
- Materials with **low $k_e$** (e.g., InAlAs, Si) produce less excess noise — preferred for high-sensitivity APDs.
- $F(M)$ is what limits the practical maximum $M$ — without it, arbitrarily large $M$ would give arbitrarily good SNR, but with $F(M)$ growing with $M$, the SNR peaks at a finite optimum.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the **structure and working of a PIN photodiode**. Derive the expressions for **(i) quantum efficiency** and **(ii) responsivity**, and discuss the **various noise sources** present in an optical receiver.

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Structure and Working of PIN Photodiode [7 marks]

A **PIN photodiode** is a three-layer semiconductor junction consisting of a heavily doped p⁺ layer, a wide intrinsic (i) layer (lightly n-type), and a heavily doped n⁺ layer, all sandwiched between metallic contacts. An **anti-reflection (AR) coating** is deposited on the optical input face to minimize Fresnel reflection.

**Working principle:**

1. **Reverse bias application:** A reverse voltage (5–20 V) is applied, completely depleting the intrinsic region and creating a strong, uniform electric field $\mathcal{E}$ across it.
2. **Photon absorption:** Incoming photons with $h\nu \geq E_g$ penetrate through the thin p⁺ layer and are absorbed in the **wide intrinsic region**, where the bandgap $E_g$ is engineered to match the operating wavelength.
3. **EHP generation:** Each absorbed photon creates one electron–hole pair (EHP).
4. **Carrier drift:** The electric field immediately separates the pair — the electron drifts toward the n⁺ contact, the hole toward the p⁺ contact — at their saturation velocities.
5. **Photocurrent generation:** The flow of these charges through the external load produces the photocurrent $I_{ph} = \mathcal{R} P_{in}$.
6. **No internal gain:** Each absorbed photon contributes at most **one electron** to the external current, so $M = 1$.

**Why this structure?** The intrinsic region serves three purposes:
- Increases the absorption volume → higher quantum efficiency.
- Reduces the junction capacitance → faster response.
- Ensures that almost all carriers are generated within a high-field drift region → minimal diffusion-induced slow response.

#### (b) Quantum Efficiency and Responsivity [4 marks]

**Quantum efficiency** $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of electrons collected to photons incident:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Electrons collected per second}}{\text{Photons incident per second}} = \frac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{in}/(h\nu)}$$

Solving for $I_{ph}$:

$$I_{ph} = \eta q \frac{P_{in}}{h\nu} = \eta q \frac{P_{in} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Responsivity** is photocurrent per unit optical power:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{in}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$

At unit quantum efficiency ($\eta = 1$) the responsivity reduces to $\mathcal{R} = \lambda/1.24\ \mu\text{m}$ A/W.

#### (b) Noise Sources in an Optical Receiver [3 marks]

| Noise Source | Origin | Variance |
|---|---|---|
| Quantum/Shot noise | Random photon arrival (Poisson) | $2qI_pB$ |
| Dark current noise | Thermally generated EHPs | $2qI_dB$ |
| Thermal (Johnson) noise | Pre-amp resistance | $4k_BTB/R_L$ |
| RIN | Laser power fluctuations | $RIN \cdot I_p^2 B$ |

**Total noise:** $\sigma_{total}^2 = \sigma_{shot}^2 + \sigma_{dark}^2 + \sigma_{thermal}^2 + \sigma_{RIN}^2$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to multiply shot noise by $M^2 F(M)$** in APD-based SNR derivations. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss. Always state explicitly: *"Because of avalanche multiplication, the shot-noise variance is enhanced by $M^2 F(M)$."* Also, do not confuse the **quantum limit (20.7 photons/bit)** with the **practical receiver sensitivity (≈ 100–1000 photons/bit)** — KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PIN photodiode:** Three-layer p–i–n structure, reverse biased, **no internal gain** ($M = 1$), primary absorption in the intrinsic region.
- [x] **APD:** Same as PIN + a **multiplication region** where **impact ionization** produces **avalanche gain** $M$.
- [x] **SAM-APD structure:** Separate Absorption (InGaAs) and Multiplication (InP/InAlAs) regions — used to avoid excess tunneling dark current.
- [x] **Responsivity formula:** $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$. For APD: $\mathcal{R}_{APD} = M\mathcal{R}_{PIN}$.
- [x] **Rule of thumb:** $\mathcal{R}(\eta=1) = \lambda/1.24\ \mu\text{m}$ A/W. So at 1550 nm, $\mathcal{R} \approx 1.25$ A/W.
- [x] **Noise sources in order of importance for high $P_{in}$:** Shot > Thermal > Dark > RIN.
- [x] **Excess noise factor:** $F(M) = k_e M + (1-k_e)(2 - 1/M)$. Materials with **low $k_e$** (Si, InAlAs) give less noisy APDs.
- [x] **APD SNR trade-off:** SNR is non-monotonic in $M$ — it peaks at an **optimum $M$** that balances multiplied shot noise and thermal noise.
- [x] **Direct detection:** Square-law detection — recovers only intensity, not phase. Requires $\geq 10$ photons/bit minimum (Poisson model) or $\geq 20.7$ photons/bit (quantum limit, BER=$10^{-9}$).
- [x] **BER for OOK:** $BER = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{\bar{N}}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)$ for shot-noise-limited detection.
- [x] **Receiver block diagram order:** Photodetector → Pre-amp (LNA) → Equalizer → Main amplifier → Decision & Clock recovery.
- [x] **Why PIN vs. APD choice?** PIN: short reach, low cost, low bias. APD: long haul, high sensitivity, high bias. (Common 7-mark question.)
- [x] **$F(M)$ behaviour:** Always $F(M) \geq 2$ for $M > 1$, monotonically increasing with $M$. A common KTU trap is to assume $F(M) = M$.
- [x] **NEP** (Noise Equivalent Power) is the optical power required for $SNR = 1$ in 1 Hz bandwidth — lower NEP = better detector.
- [x] **Detectivity $D^*$** is inversely proportional to NEP and normalized to detector area — the standard figure of merit for photodetectors.
- [x] **Direct detection vs. coherent detection:** Direct = intensity only, simple; Coherent = full vector (amplitude + phase), complex but better sensitivity and spectral efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** BER Analysis; Coherent Detection Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bit Error Rate (BER) Analysis

### Formal Academic Definition
**Bit Error Rate (BER)** is the fundamental performance metric of any digital optical communication link, defined as the *ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted* over a sufficiently long observation interval. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, BER is evaluated under probabilistic models of noise (thermal, shot, and relative intensity noise) at the receiver decision circuit.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P_e = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{N_{error}}{N_{total}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The "BER Analysis" sub-topic demands a quantitative treatment of error probability using the **Q-function (Marcum Q-function)**, the **complementary error function erfc(·)**, and the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** at the decision instant. Students must also link BER to the optical **receiver sensitivity** (in dBm) and the **Q-factor** of the eye diagram.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Imagine a postal worker delivering 1,000 letters in heavy rain. Some letters get smudged and the addresses become illegible. If 2 out of 1,000 letters arrive damaged, the "error rate" is 2/1000 = 0.002 (or 0.2 %). In an optical link, the "rain" is composed of photons of **shot noise**, **thermal noise**, and **amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) noise**, while the "letters" are the bits (0s and 1s) of information. BER is the receiver's way of saying: *"Out of every million bits I tried to read, this many got smudged beyond recovery."*

A typical telecom-grade requirement is **BER $\le 10^{-9}$** (one error in a billion bits), and a forward-error-correction (FEC) threshold is often **BER $\le 10^{-3}$**.

---

## 1.2 Coherent Detection Principles

### Formal Academic Definition
**Coherent Detection** is an optical reception technique in which the incoming modulated optical signal is linearly mixed (heterodyned or homodyned) with a strong **Local Oscillator (LO)** laser at the receiver, before photodetection. Because the photodetector responds to the *square of the total field* (E_signal + E_LO), the mixing action translates the optical phase/frequency information of the signal down to the electrical domain, enabling the recovery of advanced modulation formats such as **ASK, PSK, FSK** and their quadrature variants (QAM).

There are two principal architectures:

| Architecture | LO Frequency | Intermediate Frequency (IF) | Demodulation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homodyne** | $f_{LO} = f_{signal}$ | 0 (baseband) | Direct baseband |
| **Heterodyne** | $f_{LO} \neq f_{signal}$ | $f_{IF} = \vert f_{signal} - f_{LO} \vert$ | Electrical IF stage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coherent vs. Direct (IM/DD) Detection:** In *Intensity Modulation / Direct Detection* (IM/DD), the receiver only measures optical *power*. In *Coherent Detection*, the receiver measures both **amplitude AND phase** of the optical field, which is why coherent systems achieve a **3 dB (or higher) sensitivity advantage** and can natively support spectrally efficient multi-level formats.

### Intuitive Real-World Analogy
Think of a stethoscope used by a doctor. The doctor's ears cannot directly hear a patient's heartbeat inside a noisy crowded room. The stethoscope channels the heart's weak sound and combines it with the doctor's own steady listening focus — the heartbeat now stands out clearly. In coherent detection, the **weak incoming optical signal** is the heartbeat, the **strong Local Oscillator laser** is the doctor's amplified listening ability, and the **photodiode (mixer)** is the stethoscope. The combination dramatically improves detection fidelity, just as a stethoscope does for a faint heartbeat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module**
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
> - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s
> - Photon energy: $E_{ph} = hf = hc/\lambda$
> - Boltzmann's constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
> - Electron charge: $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C

---

## 1.3 The Q-Factor — The Bridge Between Eye Diagram and BER

The **Q-factor** is an experimental figure-of-merit extracted from the **eye diagram** of the received signal. For a binary waveform with two Gaussian noise distributions on the '1' and '0' rails:

$$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$$

where $\mu_1, \mu_0$ are the mean signal levels of marks and spaces, and $\sigma_1, \sigma_0$ are their respective standard deviations of noise. The Q-factor is the **single number** an experimentalist reports when characterizing a link, and it has a one-to-one mapping to the BER under the Gaussian noise assumption:

$$BER = P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-Q^2/2} \quad \text{for large } Q$$

For example, **Q = 6** corresponds to **BER $\approx 10^{-9}$** — the canonical telecom benchmark.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Noise Sources at the Coherent Receiver

The total noise corrupting a coherent optical receiver is the sum of three dominant, statistically independent contributions:

1. **Shot Noise** $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2q(I_{sig} + I_{LO})B$ — arises from the discrete (quantum) nature of photodetection. Dominant when the LO power is moderate-to-high.
2. **Thermal Noise** $\sigma_{th}^2 = (4k_B T / R_L) F_n B$ — generated in the front-end transimpedance amplifier (TIA). Independent of optical power.
3. **Relative Intensity Noise (RIN)** $\sigma_{RIN}^2 = (\text{RIN}) \cdot I_{LO}^2 B$ — LO laser intensity fluctuations; usually negligible for high-quality external-cavity LO lasers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shot-Noise-Limited Regime:** When the LO power $P_{LO}$ is made sufficiently large, $\sigma_{shot}^2 \gg \sigma_{th}^2$, and the receiver enters the *quantum-limited shot-noise-limited* regime. This is the operational regime assumed in most KTU derivations.

---

## 2.2 Coherent Detection — Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Field Superposition:** The incoming optical field $E_s(t)$ and the LO field $E_{LO}(t)$ impinge simultaneously on the photodetector. The total instantaneous field is:

$$E_{total}(t) = E_s(t) + E_{LO}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Square-Law Detection:** The photodiode responds to the optical *intensity* (proportional to the field squared):

$$I_{PD}(t) = R \cdot \langle \vert E_{total}(t) \vert^2 \rangle = R \left[ P_s + P_{LO} + 2\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\,\cos(\Delta \omega t + \phi_s - \phi_{LO}) \right]$$

where $R$ is the photodiode responsivity (A/W), and $\Delta \omega = \omega_s - \omega_{LO}$.

**Step 3 — Why this matters:** The cross-term $2\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\cos(\Delta\omega t + \phi_s - \phi_{LO})$ is the *coherent gain* term. It is proportional to $\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$ — meaning a strong LO linearly amplifies the signal's contribution to the photocurrent, beating down the relative impact of noise.

**Step 4 — Heterodyne vs. Homodyne:** If $f_{LO} = f_s$ (homodyne), $\Delta\omega = 0$ and the cosine becomes a DC term $\cos(\phi_s - \phi_{LO})$ — phase information is preserved as a baseband voltage, requiring an *optical phase-locked loop (OPLL)*. If $f_{LO} \neq f_s$ (heterodyne), the result is a bandpass signal at $f_{IF}$ that is then electrically demodulated — easier to implement, at the cost of 3 dB SNR.

**Step 5 — Demodulation:** The detected photocurrent is low-pass filtered, sampled at the bit-center, and compared against a threshold to produce the binary decision $\hat{b}_n$.

---

## 2.3 BER for Common Coherent Modulation Formats

Assuming **shot-noise-limited**, **synchronous** detection with optimal thresholding, the symbol error probability for the major formats is:

| Modulation Format | BER / Symbol Error Rate Expression | Quantum Limit (Photons/bit) |
|---|---|---|
| **OOK (ASK)** | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | $\approx 10$ |
| **Homodyne BPSK** | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s}\right)$ | $\mathbf{9}$ |
| **Heterodyne BPSK / FSK** | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s/2}\right)$ | $\mathbf{18}$ |
| **Homodyne QPSK** | $P_e = \text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s/2}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left[\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s/2}\right)\right]^2$ | $\mathbf{18}$ |
| **Heterodyne DPSK** | $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,e^{-n_s}$ | $\mathbf{20}$ |

where $n_s$ is the **average number of photons per bit** at the receiver input.

---

## 2.4 The Quantum Limit — Why 9 Photons?

The **quantum limit of detection** is the absolute minimum photon count required to achieve a target BER with an ideal receiver. For homodyne BPSK, the derivation hinges on the fact that the *minimum mean-square error* in distinguishing two coherent states $\vert\alpha\rangle$ and $\vert -\alpha\rangle$ scales as $\exp(-2\vert\alpha\vert^2)$. Setting this to the BER = $10^{-9}$ threshold yields:

$$n_s = \frac{\bar{N}_1 + \bar{N}_0}{2} = 9 \text{ photons/bit (at } P_e = 10^{-9}\text{)}$$

This is the famous **Personick / Helstrom quantum limit**, and it represents a hard physical bound below which *no* detector, classical or quantum, can recover the bit without errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "9" and not "1"?** The answer lies in the overlap of the two coherent-state wavefunctions in phase space. Even with perfect detectors, the states cannot be perfectly distinguished. The 9-photon figure is the energy budget needed to push the *Helstrom bound* down to $10^{-9}$.

---

| # | Formula / Identity | Physical Meaning | Units |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Submarine Long-Haul Systems:** Coherent detection combined with QPSK or 16-QAM is the *de facto* standard for trans-oceanic cables (e.g., 100 Gbps, 400 Gbps per wavelength), because the 3-dB sensitivity gain translates directly into **doubling the unrepeatered reach** (~80 km → 160 km per dB).
- **Data-Center Interconnects (DCI):** Coherent pluggable modules (QSFP-DD, OSFP) at 400G/800G/1.6T use coherent detection to achieve high spectral efficiency over standard single-mode fiber.
- **5G/6G Fronthaul:** Coherent optics are penetrating metro and access networks as cost-effective DSP-ASIC technology matures.
- **LIDAR & Quantum Key Distribution (QKD):** Coherent homodyne receivers are also the workhorses of continuous-variable (CV) QKD and coherent LIDAR.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Q-Factor to BER Relationship

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\tfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ assuming Gaussian noise on both rails.

**Step 1.** Let the conditional probability density functions (PDFs) of the received signal voltage $v$ at the sampling instant be Gaussian:

$$p(v \mid 1) = \frac{1}{\sigma_1 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(v - \mu_1)^2}{2\sigma_1^2}\right]$$

$$p(v \mid 0) = \frac{1}{\sigma_0 \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(v - \mu_0)^2}{2\sigma_0^2}\right]$$

**Step 2.** The decision threshold $v_{th}$ is chosen to minimize the *total* error probability. Assuming *a priori* equal probability of 0 and 1, the optimal threshold satisfies $p(v_{th} \mid 1) = p(v_{th} \mid 0)$. For symmetric noise ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_0 = \sigma$), this gives:

$$v_{th} = \frac{\mu_0 + \mu_1}{2}$$

**Step 3.** The probability of error is the sum of two tail integrals:

$$P_e = \tfrac{1}{2} P(0 \mid 1) + \tfrac{1}{2} P(1 \mid 0)$$

**Step 4.** Each tail integral is the area of a Gaussian tail beyond $v_{th}$:

$$P(0 \mid 1) = \int_{-\infty}^{v_{th}} p(v \mid 1)\,dv = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\mu_1 - v_{th}}{\sigma_1 \sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

where in the last equality we substituted $v_{th} = (\mu_0+\mu_1)/2$ and used $\sigma_1 = \sigma_0$.

**Step 5.** By symmetry, $P(1 \mid 0) = P(0 \mid 1)$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}$$

**Step 6.** Asymptotic approximation: For $Q \gg 1$, $\text{erfc}(x) \approx \tfrac{e^{-x^2}}{x\sqrt{\pi}}$, giving the **logarithmic form** often used in system design:

$$P_e \approx \frac{1}{Q\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{Q^2}{2}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — BER for Homodyne BPSK

**Goal:** Derive $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s})$ for ideal homodyne binary phase-shift keying.

**Step 1.** The signal field is $E_s(t) = \sqrt{P_s}\,e^{j(\omega t + \phi_s)}$, and the LO field is $E_{LO}(t) = \sqrt{P_{LO}}\,e^{j(\omega t + \phi_{LO})}$. In BPSK, the data is encoded in $\phi_s \in \{0, \pi\}$.

**Step 2.** The total field at the photodiode: $E_{tot} = E_s + E_{LO}$. The instantaneous photocurrent after square-law detection is:

$$I_{PD} = R \vert E_{tot} \vert^2 = R\left[ P_s + P_{LO} + 2\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\cos(\phi_s - \phi_{LO}) \right]$$

**Step 3.** For homodyne detection, $f_{LO} = f_s$ and an OPLL locks $\phi_{LO} = 0$. The DC term $R(P_s + P_{LO})$ is filtered out, leaving the signal:

$$i(t) = 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\,\cos(\phi_s) = \pm 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$$

since $\cos(0) = +1$ and $\cos(\pi) = -1$. The two signal levels are thus $\pm A$ where $A = 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$.

**Step 4.** The shot-noise variance in this strong-LO regime is dominated by the LO shot noise:

$$\sigma^2 = 2q\,I_{LO}\,B = 2q\,R\,P_{LO}\,B$$

**Step 5.** The Q-factor of the resulting binary eye is:

$$Q = \frac{2A}{2\sigma} = \frac{2 \cdot 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}}{2\sqrt{2qRP_{LO}B}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 R^2 P_s}{qRB}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 R P_s}{qB}}$$

**Step 6.** Expressing $P_s$ in photons-per-bit. The number of signal photons per bit is:

$$n_s = \frac{P_s}{hf R_b}$$

and the electrical noise-equivalent bandwidth is $B \approx R_b/2$ (raised-cosine Nyquist filtering), giving:

$$\frac{R P_s}{qB} = \frac{R \cdot n_s hf R_b}{q \cdot R_b/2} = \frac{2 n_s hf R}{q}$$

**Step 7.** Substituting $R = \eta q/(hf)$, we get $R/q = \eta/(hf)$, hence:

$$Q^2 = \frac{2 \cdot 2 n_s hf \cdot \eta/(hf)}{1} = 4 \eta n_s$$

For an ideal quantum-efficient detector ($\eta = 1$): $Q^2 = 4 n_s$, so $Q = 2\sqrt{n_s}$.

**Step 8.** Plug into the BER formula:

$$\boxed{P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s}\right)}$$

**Step 9.** **Quantum limit evaluation:** Set $P_e = 10^{-9}$ and solve for $n_s$ using the asymptotic erfc:

$$\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s}) = 2 \times 10^{-9} \implies \sqrt{n_s} \approx 5.9 \implies n_s \approx 36 \text{ (asymptotic)??}$$

**Important correction:** The asymptotic form $P_e \approx \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-n_s}$ would give $e^{-n_s} = 2 \times 10^{-9} \implies n_s \approx 20$. The exact value $n_s = 9$ for the *ideal homodyne BPSK quantum limit* is obtained using the **Helstrom bound** (minimum error discrimination of two coherent states $\vert\alpha\rangle$ and $\vert-\alpha\rangle$), not the classical shot-noise result. KTU examiners accept the classical expression and the standard tabulated $n_s = 9$ figure as the canonical quantum limit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Heterodyne Detection SNR Penalty

**Goal:** Show that heterodyne detection incurs a 3 dB SNR penalty relative to homodyne.

**Step 1.** In a heterodyne receiver, the LO is offset by $f_{IF}$ from the signal, so the photodetected current contains a beat term at $f_{IF}$:

$$i_{IF}(t) = 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\,\cos(2\pi f_{IF} t + \phi_s - \phi_{LO})$$

**Step 2.** This is a bandpass signal centered at $f_{IF}$. After down-conversion and low-pass filtering, the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components are extracted, each spanning a noise bandwidth of $B$.

**Step 3.** The signal power (after demodulation) is:

$$S = 2 R^2 P_s P_{LO} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = R^2 P_s P_{LO}$$

where the factor $1/2$ comes from $\langle \cos^2 \rangle$.

**Step 4.** The shot noise spectral density (one-sided) is $N_0 = q I_{LO} = q R P_{LO}$ (A²/Hz). After demodulation, the I and Q components each have noise variance $\sigma^2 = N_0 B = q R P_{LO} B$, but only one quadrature carries the signal.

**Step 5.** Thus, the electrical SNR (signal in I-channel) is:

$$\text{SNR}_{het} = \frac{R^2 P_s P_{LO}}{2 \cdot q R P_{LO} B} = \frac{R P_s}{2 q B}$$

**Step 6.** Compare to homodyne: from Derivation #2 Step 5, $Q^2_{homo} = 2 R P_s / (qB)$, so $\text{SNR}_{homo} = R P_s / (qB)$.

**Step 7.** The penalty is the ratio:

$$\frac{\text{SNR}_{het}}{\text{SNR}_{homo}} = \frac{R P_s / (2 qB)}{R P_s / (qB)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Heterodyne penalty} = 10\log_{10}(0.5) = -3 \text{ dB}}$$

This is why **homodyne is theoretically superior** in sensitivity, but **heterodyne is more practical** because it does not require an optical phase-locked loop (OPLL).

---

## 3.4 Derivation #4 — Receiver Sensitivity in dBm

**Goal:** Convert a photon-count requirement $n_s$ into the engineering-friendly dBm receiver sensitivity.

**Step 1.** Average received optical power required per bit:

$$P_{avg} = n_s \cdot E_{ph} \cdot R_b = n_s \cdot hf \cdot R_b$$

**Step 2.** Convert to milliwatts and then to dBm. Using $1 \text{ W} = 10^3$ mW:

$$P_{avg}(\text{mW}) = n_s \cdot hf \cdot R_b \cdot 10^{3}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the dBm definition: $P(\text{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}\!\left[P(\text{mW})\right]$:

$$\boxed{P_{min}(\text{dBm}) = 10\log_{10}(n_s) + 10\log_{10}(hf \cdot R_b) + 30}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $\lambda = 1550$ nm, $R_b = 10$ Gbps, BPSK homodyne ($n_s = 36$ for shot-noise limit at BER $= 10^{-9}$):

$$hf = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \cdot 3 \times 10^8}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.282 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

$$P_{avg} = 36 \cdot 1.282 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 10^{10} = 4.62 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W} = 46.2 \text{ nW}$$

$$P_{min} = 10\log_{10}(46.2 \times 10^{-6}) = 10\log_{10}(4.62 \times 10^{-5}) \approx -43.4 \text{ dBm}$$

This is in excellent agreement with the **$-43$ dBm** sensitivity benchmark quoted in textbooks for an ideal 10-Gbps BPSK homodyne receiver at the $10^{-9}$ threshold.

## 4.1 High-Level Coherent Detection Architecture

The block-level functional architecture of a generic coherent optical receiver:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TxSig["Tx Signal\nE_s at f_s"]:::sig
    Fiber["Optical Fiber\nChannel"]:::fiber
    RxIn["Receiver\nInput"]:::node1
    LO["Local\nOscillator\nE_LO at f_LO"]:::lo
    Coupler["50:50\nOptical\nCoupler"]:::node2
    BPD["Balanced\nPhotodetector\n(BPD)"]:::node3
    TIA["Transimpedance\nAmplifier"]:::node4
    ADC["ADC + DSP\nCarrier & Data\nRecovery"]:::node5
    Dec["Decoded\nBits"]:::node6

    TxSig --> Fiber --> RxIn --> Coupler
    LO --> Coupler
    Coupler --> BPD --> TIA --> ADC --> Dec

    classDef lo fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** The incoming signal (blue) and the local oscillator (yellow) are combined in a 50:50 coupler. The mixed optical signal strikes a **balanced photodetector** (cyan), which cancels DC LO noise and produces a differential current. The TIA (orange) converts this to a voltage, and the ADC + DSP chain (purple) handles carrier-phase estimation, symbol synchronization, and final bit recovery.
