# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Bit Error Rate (BER). What is the typical BER threshold used in optical communication systems, and how is it mathematically related to the Q-factor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** BER is defined as the ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted over a sufficiently long observation interval. Mathematically, $BER = N_{error}/N_{total}$.
- **Threshold (1 mark):** The standard telecom-grade BER threshold is $10^{-9}$ (one error per billion bits), while FEC-enabled systems often operate at the pre-FEC threshold of $10^{-3}$.
- **Q-factor relationship (1 mark):** Assuming Gaussian noise on both rails, $BER = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$, where $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$. For $Q = 6$, BER $\approx 10^{-9}$.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between homodyne and heterodyne coherent detection. Which one offers a better sensitivity, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Homodyne (1 mark):** The local oscillator frequency $f_{LO}$ exactly matches the signal carrier $f_s$, so the down-converted signal is at baseband (IF = 0). An optical phase-locked loop (OPLL) is required to track the phase.
- **Heterodyne (1 mark):** $f_{LO} \neq f_s$, producing an intermediate frequency $f_{IF} = \vert f_s - f_{LO} \vert$. The IF signal is electrically demodulated; no OPLL is required.
- **Sensitivity (1 mark):** Homodyne offers 3 dB better sensitivity because the entire signal power lies in a single baseband quadrature, whereas heterodyne splits power between I and Q and the signal occupies only one quadrature — the SNR penalty is $10\log_{10}(0.5) = -3$ dB.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks and provides an *internal choice* between two question alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts (a) and (b) worth 7 marks each. KTU valuation expects a labeled diagram wherever the question says "explain with a block diagram" (1–2 marks reserved for the figure).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of coherent detection in optical communication systems. Compare its performance with direct detection (IM/DD). **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the architecture in Section 4.1. The receiver consists of: incoming signal $\rightarrow$ 50:50 optical coupler $\rightarrow$ combined with LO laser $\rightarrow$ balanced photodetector (BPD) $\rightarrow$ TIA $\rightarrow$ ADC + DSP $\rightarrow$ decoded bits.

**Principle of Operation (3 marks):**
The incoming modulated optical field $E_s(t)$ is linearly combined with a strong coherent LO field $E_{LO}(t)$ at a 50:50 coupler. The summed field strikes a photodetector, which — by the square-law nature of photodetection — produces a current proportional to $\vert E_s + E_{LO}\vert^2 = \vert E_s\vert^2 + \vert E_{LO}\vert^2 + 2\,\text{Re}\{E_s E_{LO}^*\}$. The third (cross) term is the *coherent gain* term: it is proportional to $\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$ and contains the **amplitude and phase** information of the signal modulated onto a strong LO carrier. A balanced photodetector suppresses the DC LO common-mode noise and amplifies the differential signal.

**Comparison with IM/DD (2 marks):**

| Parameter | IM/DD | Coherent |
|---|---|---|
| Information recovered | Intensity only | Amplitude + Phase |
| Sensitivity | Pin-receiver: $-20$ dBm typical | Homodyne BPSK: $-43$ dBm (10 Gbps) |
| Spectral efficiency | Limited to OOK/NRZ | Supports QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM |
| Local oscillator | Not required | Required (narrow-linewidth) |
| DSP complexity | Low | High (carrier recovery, equalization) |

**A. (b)** Derive the expression for the BER of a homodyne BPSK receiver in the shot-noise-limited regime. Show that the quantum limit corresponds to 9 photons per bit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Setting up the photocurrent (2 marks):**
For BPSK, $\phi_s \in \{0, \pi\}$. The received photocurrent (after OPLL locking $\phi_{LO} = 0$) is:

$$i(t) = 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\,\cos(\phi_s) = \pm 2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$$

**Computing the Q-factor (2 marks):**
The signal swing is $\Delta I = 4R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}$. With shot-noise-dominated variance $\sigma^2 = 2q R P_{LO} B$:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta I}{2\sigma} = \frac{4R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}}{2\sqrt{2qRP_{LO}B}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 R P_s}{qB}}$$

**BER expression (1 mark):**

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{R P_s}{qB}}\right)$$

**Quantum limit (2 marks):**
With $B = R_b/2$ and $R = \eta q/(hf)$, we get $Q^2 = 4\eta n_s$. For $\eta = 1$:

$$P_e = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{n_s}\right)$$

The *Helstrom bound* — the absolute minimum error in distinguishing two coherent states $\vert\alpha\rangle$ and $\vert -\alpha\rangle$ — is $P_e^{min} = \tfrac{1}{2}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - e^{-4n_s}}\right]$. Setting this to $10^{-9}$ and solving yields:

$$n_s = 9 \text{ photons/bit}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B. (a)** Explain the Q-factor method of BER estimation. An optical link has the following measured eye-diagram parameters: $\mu_1 = 1.0$ mV, $\mu_0 = 0.1$ mV, $\sigma_1 = 0.08$ mV, $\sigma_0 = 0.07$ mV. Calculate the Q-factor and the corresponding BER. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Q-factor Method (3 marks):**
The Q-factor method is an *experimental* technique for estimating BER from the eye diagram of a received signal. The eye is sampled at the optimal decision instant, producing two histograms — one for '1' symbols, one for '0' symbols. Each histogram is approximated as Gaussian with mean $\mu_{1,0}$ and standard deviation $\sigma_{1,0}$. The Q-factor is:

$$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$$

This single number is then mapped to BER via $BER = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$. The Q-method is widely used in lab characterization because it requires only a sampling oscilloscope and avoids the need to count actual bit errors (which would require $10^{12}$ bits for $10^{-9}$ BER).

**Calculation (4 marks):**

**Step 1 — Q-factor (2 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{1.0 - 0.1}{0.08 + 0.07} = \frac{0.9}{0.15} = 6.0$$

**Step 2 — BER (1 mark):**

$$BER = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{6.0}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(4.243)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation (1 mark):** Using the asymptotic Gaussian-tail approximation $\text{erfc}(x) \approx \tfrac{e^{-x^2}}{x\sqrt{\pi}}$:

$$\text{erfc}(4.243) \approx \frac{e^{-18}}{4.243 \sqrt{\pi}} \approx \frac{1.522 \times 10^{-8}}{7.51} \approx 2.03 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$BER \approx 1.01 \times 10^{-9}$$

This is the canonical telecom-grade performance threshold of $10^{-9}$.

---

**B. (b)** Compare the BER performance and receiver sensitivity of OOK, BPSK, and DPSK modulation formats under coherent detection. Tabulate the quantum limits and indicate the engineering trade-offs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Modulation Format Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | OOK / ASK | Homodyne BPSK | Heterodyne DPSK |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bit encoding | Intensity (on/off) | Optical phase (0/$\pi$) | Differential phase |
| BER expression | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$ | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s})$ | $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}e^{-n_s}$ |
| Quantum limit (BER $= 10^{-9}$) | $\approx 10$ photons/bit | $\mathbf{9}$ photons/bit | $\mathbf{20}$ photons/bit |
| LO requirement | Not required (for IM/DD) | Required (OPLL) | Required (no OPLL) |
| Sensitivity at 10 Gbps | $\sim -36$ dBm (coherent) | $\sim -43$ dBm | $\sim -41$ dBm |
| DSP complexity | Low | High | Moderate |

**Engineering Trade-offs (3 marks):**

1. **OOK/ASK** is the simplest format, requires no LO in IM/DD form, but is the *least* sensitive in coherent form. Best for cost-sensitive short-reach links.
2. **BPSK (homodyne)** achieves the *best* sensitivity (9 photons/bit) at the cost of requiring a narrow-linewidth LO and an OPLL — challenging at high symbol rates.
3. **DPSK** sacrifices ~3 dB in sensitivity (20 vs. 9 photons/bit) but avoids the OPLL by using a 1-bit-delay interferometer at the receiver — a popular compromise for 10/40 Gbps systems.
4. The trend in modern systems is toward **QPSK, 16-QAM, and probabilistically shaped constellations** in coherent DSP, trading sensitivity for higher spectral efficiency (bits/s/Hz).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Conflating OOK and BPSK quantum limits:** Students frequently write "the quantum limit is 9 photons/bit for OOK." It is **NOT**. The 9-photon figure is the **Helstrom bound for homodyne BPSK**. OOK requires ~10 photons/bit even with ideal detection.
> - **Forgetting the 3-dB heterodyne penalty:** When asked for sensitivity comparison, students often quote the same number for both. Always state the $-3$ dB penalty explicitly.
> - **Unit confusion in dBm conversion:** $P_{avg}$ in Watts must first be converted to **milliwatts** before applying $10\log_{10}$ to obtain dBm. Forgetting the factor of $10^3$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> - **Confusing the *asymptotic* and *exact* quantum limits:** The classical formula $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s})$ at $n_s = 9$ gives a BER *much lower* than $10^{-9}$. The 9-photon figure comes from the **Helstrom quantum bound**, not from inverting the classical formula. KTU accepts both approaches as long as the student states which one is being used.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: BER Analysis & Coherent Detection**

- **BER Definition:** $P_e = N_{error}/N_{total}$; target $10^{-9}$ for telecom, $10^{-3}$ for FEC-threshold.
- **Q-Factor Definition:** $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$; $Q = 6 \Leftrightarrow BER \approx 10^{-9}$.
- **Q-to-BER mapping:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$.
- **Coherent Detection Principle:** Mix signal with strong LO; square-law detection recovers amplitude and phase information.
- **Homodyne vs. Heterodyne:** Homodyne is baseband (no IF, requires OPLL); Heterodyne has IF (no OPLL, easier to implement).
- **3-dB Heterodyne Penalty:** $10\log_{10}(0.5) = -3$ dB SNR penalty relative to homodyne.
- **Shot-Noise Variance:** $\sigma_{shot}^2 = 2q(I_{sig}+I_{LO})B$.
- **Thermal Noise Variance:** $\sigma_{th}^2 = 4k_B T F_n B / R_L$.
- **Photodiode Responsivity:** $R = \eta q \lambda / (hc)$.
- **Photons per bit:** $n_s = P_{avg}/(hf R_b)$.
- **Quantum Limits (BER = $10^{-9}$):** OOK ≈ 10, BPSK homodyne = **9**, QPSK homodyne = **18**, DPSK heterodyne = **20** photons/bit.
- **BPSK Homodyne BER:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s})$.
- **QPSK Homodyne BER:** $P_e = \text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s/2}) - \tfrac{1}{2}[\text{erfc}(\sqrt{n_s/2})]^2$.
- **DPSK Heterodyne BER:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-n_s)$.
- **Coherent gain term:** Photocurrent has a cross-term $2R\sqrt{P_s P_{LO}}\cos(\phi_s - \phi_{LO})$ — the *amplification* mechanism.
- **Balanced Photodetector (BPD):** Suppresses common-mode LO noise and doubles the signal swing.
- **Receiver Sensitivity in dBm:** $P_{min} = 10\log_{10}(n_s) + 10\log_{10}(hf R_b) + 30$.
- **Engineering applications:** Long-haul submarine cables, 5G/6G fronthaul, hyperscale DCI, coherent LIDAR, CV-QKD.
- **Modern trend:** Coherent DSP + multi-level QAM is the dominant architecture for 100G/400G/800G/1.6T per wavelength systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Amplifiers: EDFA - Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optical Amplifiers: EDFA - Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 | Optical Amplifiers: EDFA - Principle

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA)** is an optical amplifier consisting of a short length (typically 10–30 m) of silica fiber whose core is doped with trivalent Erbium ions (Er³⁺), optically pumped at **980 nm** or **1480 nm** to achieve population inversion in the ⁴I₁₃/₂ metastable state, enabling direct in-line amplification of optical signals in the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)** through stimulated emission — without converting the signal back to the electrical domain.

In the KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the EDFA is classified as a **doped-fiber amplifier** (a category distinct from semiconductor optical amplifiers and Raman amplifiers) and forms the cornerstone of wavelength-division-multiplexed (WDM) long-haul optical networks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Relay Booster"

Imagine a long-distance relay race where runners (light pulses) grow tired over a long track (the optical fiber) and slow down due to attenuation. Instead of converting them into electricity, restitching their shoes, and sending them on, the coach places a **hydration station** (the erbium-doped fiber) at strategic points. Here, an external helper (the **pump laser**) hands out energy to the resting runners from a reserve tank. When the next runner arrives carrying a weak signal, the helper's stored energy is **transferred onto the signal pulse itself**, making it strong again — and the runner continues the race. The helper must continuously refill the tank, which is exactly what the pump laser does.

This is precisely what an EDFA does:
- The **erbium ions** are the resting helpers.
- The **pump laser** is the energy source.
- The **input signal** is the weak runner.
- The **amplified output signal** is the boosted runner.

### 1.3 Operating Window and Spectral Bands

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Amplification Window of EDFA:** **C-band (Conventional band), 1530–1565 nm**, with peak gain near **1532 nm** and a flattened gain region between **1540–1560 nm** (using gain-flattening filters). The L-band (**1570–1610 nm**) amplification is also achievable by manipulating the inversion level.

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Active Ion | Er³⁺ (trivalent Erbium) |
| Host Material | Silica (SiO₂) Fiber |
| Pump Wavelengths | 980 nm and 1480 nm |
| Signal Wavelength Range | 1530 – 1565 nm (C-band) |
| Metastable State Lifetime | ~ 10 ms |
| Typical Small-Signal Gain | 30 – 50 dB |
| Saturation Output Power | 10 – 20 dBm |

### 1.4 Why EDFA is Revolutionary

Before EDFA (pre-1990s), the only way to amplify an optical signal was **OEO (Optical-Electrical-Optical) regeneration**, requiring photodetectors, electrical amplifiers, and laser transmitters at every ~80 km repeater station. EDFA enabled:
- **All-optical amplification** (no OEO conversion).
- **Bit-rate transparency** (works for 1 Gb/s, 10 Gb/s, 100 Gb/s, and beyond).
- **Wavelength transparency** (amplifies many WDM channels simultaneously).
- **Long unrepeatered spans** (>100 km at 2.5 Gb/s, >80 km at 10 Gb/s).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Structure of an EDFA

A practical EDFA consists of the following key components:

1. **Pump Laser Source** — typically a high-power **980 nm** or **1480 nm** semiconductor laser (output power: 50–500 mW).
2. **Wavelength-Division Multiplexing (WDM) Coupler** — combines the pump and signal wavelengths into the erbium-doped fiber.
3. **Erbium-Doped Fiber (EDF)** — 10–30 m of single-mode fiber doped with ~100–1000 ppm of Er³⁺ ions in the core.
4. **Optical Isolators** — placed at input and output to prevent backward-propagating Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE) from causing laser oscillations and to suppress reflections.
5. **Gain-Flattening Filter (optional)** — used in WDM systems to equalize gain across all channels.
6. **Tap Couplers and Monitor Photodiodes** — for automatic gain control (AGC) and pump power regulation.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Three-Level / Quasi-Three-Level System

The Er³⁺ ion exhibits a **quasi-three-level laser system** for amplification at 1550 nm. The relevant energy levels are:

| Level | Symbol | Energy | Lifetime | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ground State | ⁴I₁₅/₂ | 0 eV (reference) | Stable | Initial resting state |
| Pump Level (980 nm) | ⁴I₁₁/₂ | ~ 1.27 eV | ~ 1 μs | Excited by 980 nm pump |
| Pump Level (1480 nm) | ⁴I₁₃/₂ upper sub-band | ~ 0.84 eV | ~ 10 ms | Excited by 1480 nm pump |
| Metastable State | ⁴I₁₃/₂ lower sub-band | ~ 0.80 eV | ~ 10 ms | Provides optical gain |
| (Non-radiative decay) | — | — | — | Bridges 980 nm pumping to metastable state |

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Cycle

**Step 1 — Optical Pumping:**
A high-power pump photon (980 nm or 1480 nm) is absorbed by an Er³⁺ ion, exciting it from the ground state ⁴I₁₅/₂ to a higher pump level. This is the **absorption** process.

**Step 2 — Rapid Non-Radiative Decay:**
For 980 nm pumping, the ion quickly (within microseconds) decays non-radiatively from ⁴I₁₁/₂ to the metastable ⁴I₁₃/₂ level, releasing energy as phonons (heat) to the silica lattice. For 1480 nm pumping, the ion is excited directly into the upper sub-band of ⁴I₁₃/₂.

**Step 3 — Population Inversion:**
If the pump power is sufficient, the rate of ions arriving at the metastable state exceeds the rate of spontaneous decay, so the population of the metastable level **N₂** becomes greater than that of the ground level **N₁**. This is the condition of **population inversion**, which is essential for optical amplification.

**Step 4 — Stimulated Emission (Signal Amplification):**
An incoming signal photon (1550 nm) traveling through the EDF stimulates an excited Er³⁺ ion to drop to the ground state, emitting a second photon that is **identical** to the stimulating photon in phase, frequency, polarization, and direction. The signal is thus amplified by **stimulated emission**.

**Step 5 — Spontaneous Emission (Noise):**
A fraction of excited ions decay **spontaneously**, emitting photons in random directions and frequencies. This is the source of **Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE)**, which represents the dominant noise in an EDFA.

## 2.3 Two Pumping Schemes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two primary pumping configurations are **co-propagating (forward pumping)** and **counter-propagating (backward pumping)**, with **bidirectional pumping** (both ends) used for high-gain, high-power applications.

| Feature | 980 nm Pumping | 1480 nm Pumping |
|---|---|---|
| Quantum Efficiency | ~ 90 % | ~ 70 % |
| Noise Figure | ~ 4–5 dB (excellent) | ~ 5–7 dB |
| Pump Power Available | Up to ~250 mW | Up to ~500 mW |
| Pump Absorption | High (efficient) | Moderate |
| Best Use | Low-noise pre-amplifier | High-power booster amplifier |
| Excites To | ⁴I₁₁/₂ (then non-radiative to ⁴I₁₃/₂) | Upper sub-band of ⁴I₁₃/₂ directly |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to compare 980 nm and 1480 nm pumps, students often forget to mention that **980 nm gives better noise figure** but **1480 nm provides higher output power**, which is a high-yield point.

## 2.4 Gain Characteristics of EDFA

The small-signal gain of an EDFA follows the relation:

$$G = \exp\left(\int_0^L \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \left[N_2(z) - N_1(z)\right] \, dz\right)$$

where:
- $L$ = length of erbium-doped fiber
- $\Gamma_s$ = overlap factor (mode-confinement factor)
- $\sigma_s$ = signal stimulated-emission cross-section
- $N_2$, $N_1$ = population densities of metastable and ground states

The **gain saturation behaviour** is a critical concept for KTU problems:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat}}}}$$

where:
- $G_0$ = unsaturated (small-signal) gain
- $P_{\text{in}}$ = input signal power
- $P_{\text{sat}}$ = saturation input power (typically 1–10 mW)

Equivalently, in terms of output power:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}}$$

where $P_{\text{sat,out}}$ is the saturation output power.

## 2.5 Noise Figure and ASE

The dominant noise mechanism in an EDFA is **Amplified Spontaneous Emission (ASE)**. The optical noise figure is defined as:

$$F = \frac{(\text{SNR})_{\text{in}}}{(\text{SNR})_{\text{out}}}$$

For an EDFA in the high-gain limit, the noise figure approaches the **quantum limit**:

$$F_{\min} = 2 \, n_{\text{sp}}$$

where the spontaneous emission factor (or **population inversion factor**) is:

$$n_{\text{sp}} = \frac{N_2}{N_2 - N_1}$$

For **complete population inversion** ($N_1 \rightarrow 0$), $n_{\text{sp}} = 1$, so $F_{\min} = 2$, corresponding to:

$$F_{\min} \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3 \text{ dB}$$

This is the **theoretical minimum noise figure** of any linear optical amplifier, often called the **"3 dB quantum limit."**

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every formula a student must memorize** for KTU 2024 ESE questions on EDFA. All `|...|` symbols are written as `\vert ... \vert` to prevent markdown-table corruption.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}}$ | Optical power gain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Gain in decibels | dB |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat}}}}$ | Gain saturation with input power | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G = \dfrac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}}$ | Gain saturation with output power | dimensionless |
| 5 | $n_{\text{sp}} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_2 - N_1}$ | Population inversion factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $F_{\min} = 2 \, n_{\text{sp}}$ | Minimum noise figure | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | Noise figure in dB | dB |
| 8 | $P_{\text{ASE}} = 2 \, n_{\text{sp}} \, h \, \nu \, (G - 1) \, \Delta \nu$ | ASE power per polarization | W |
| 9 | $G = \exp\!\left(\Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N \, L\right)$ | Simplified small-signal gain (uniform inversion) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta N = N_2 - N_1$ | Population inversion density | ions/m³ |
| 11 | $P_{\text{sat}} = \dfrac{h \nu \, A_{\text{eff}}}{\sigma_s \, \tau}$ | Saturation power (intrinsic) | W |
| 12 | $\eta_Q = \dfrac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_s}$ | Quantum limit of pump efficiency | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

EDFA is the workhorse of:
- **Submarine long-haul trans-oceanic cables** (e.g., trans-Pacific, trans-Atlantic links using cascaded EDFAs every 80–120 km).
- **Terrestrial long-haul DWDM trunks** (e.g., 80-channel × 100 GHz WDM systems).
- **CATV distribution networks** as in-line boosters.
- **Optical pre-amplifiers** in receivers to improve sensitivity by ~10 dB.
- **Booster amplifiers** at transmitter ends to launch high power into long fibers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the EDFA Gain Equation (Simplified Two-Level Model)

We model the Er³⁺ ion as a **two-level system** between the ground state ⁴I₁₅/₂ (level 1) and the metastable state ⁴I₁₃/₂ (level 2). The signal is at frequency $\nu_s$ (around 1550 nm) and the pump is at $\nu_p$ (980 nm or 1480 nm).

### Step 1 — Define the population densities

Let $N_1$ and $N_2$ be the population densities (ions/m³) of levels 1 and 2 respectively. The total erbium concentration is:

$$N_t = N_1 + N_2$$

### Step 2 — Write the rate equation for level 2

The rate of change of $N_2$ is governed by (i) absorption of pump photons, (ii) stimulated emission by pump, (iii) stimulated absorption/emission by signal, and (iv) spontaneous decay.

$$\frac{dN_2}{dt} = \frac{\sigma_{p,a} \, P_p}{h \nu_p \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_1 - \frac{\sigma_{p,e} \, P_p}{h \nu_p \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_2 + \frac{\sigma_{s,a} \, P_s}{h \nu_s \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_1 - \frac{\sigma_{s,e} \, P_s}{h \nu_s \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_2 - \frac{N_2}{\tau}$$

where:
- $\sigma_{p,a}$, $\sigma_{p,e}$ = pump absorption and emission cross-sections
- $\sigma_{s,a}$, $\sigma_{s,e}$ = signal absorption and emission cross-sections
- $P_p$, $P_s$ = pump and signal powers
- $A_{\text{eff}}$ = effective mode area
- $\tau$ = spontaneous lifetime of metastable state (~10 ms)

### Step 3 — Apply steady-state condition $dN_2/dt = 0$

Under steady-state (CW operation), set $dN_2/dt = 0$ and solve for $N_2$:

$$N_2 = \frac{\dfrac{\sigma_{p,a} \, P_p}{h \nu_p \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_1 + \dfrac{\sigma_{s,a} \, P_s}{h \nu_s \, A_{\text{eff}}} N_1}{\dfrac{\sigma_{p,e} \, P_p}{h \nu_p \, A_{\text{eff}}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{s,e} \, P_s}{h \nu_s \, A_{\text{eff}}} + \dfrac{1}{\tau}}$$

### Step 4 — Define the population inversion

$$\Delta N = N_2 - N_1 = 2 N_2 - N_t$$

### Step 5 — Derive the small-signal gain

The signal power grows along the fiber length as:

$$\frac{dP_s}{dz} = \Gamma_s \left[ \sigma_{s,e} N_2 - \sigma_{s,a} N_1 \right] P_s$$

In the idealized case where $\sigma_{s,e} = \sigma_{s,a} = \sigma_s$ (which is approximately true for EDFA in the C-band):

$$\frac{dP_s}{dz} = \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N \, P_s$$

Integrating over the fiber length $L$ (assuming uniform $\Delta N$ along the fiber for simplicity):

$$P_s(L) = P_s(0) \, \exp\!\left( \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N \, L \right)$$

Hence the **small-signal (unsaturated) gain** is:

$$G_0 = \exp\!\left( \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N \, L \right)$$

In decibels:

$$G_{0,\text{dB}} = 4.343 \, \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N \, L$$

where the conversion factor $10 \log_{10}(e) \approx 4.343$ is used.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gain-Saturation Equation

### Step 1 — Start with the saturated gain

When the signal power is large enough to deplete the population inversion, $\Delta N$ itself becomes a function of $P_s$. Using the **homogeneous saturation model** of an optical amplifier:

$$\Delta N = \frac{\Delta N_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_s}{P_{\text{sat}}}}$$

where $\Delta N_0$ is the unsaturated inversion and $P_{\text{sat}}$ is the intrinsic saturation power.

### Step 2 — Substitute into the gain equation

$$G = \exp\!\left( \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N_0 \, \frac{L}{1 + P_s/P_{\text{sat}}} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Define the unsaturated gain

$$G_0 = \exp\!\left( \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N_0 \, L \right)$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\ln G_0 = \Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N_0 \, L$$

So $\Gamma_s \, \sigma_s \, \Delta N_0 = \ln G_0 / L$, and the saturated gain becomes:

$$G = \exp\!\left( \frac{\ln G_0}{1 + P_s/P_{\text{sat}}} \right) = G_0^{1/(1 + P_s/P_{\text{sat}})}$$

### Step 4 — Approximate for $P_s \ll P_{\text{sat}}$ (small-signal limit)

When $P_s \ll P_{\text{sat}}$:
$$G \approx G_0 \quad \text{(no saturation)}$$

### Step 5 — Approximate for moderate saturation

For the case when the exponent $1/(1+P_s/P_{\text{sat}})$ is small (deep saturation), the **empirical two-level saturation law** commonly used in textbooks and KTU solutions is:

$$\boxed{\,G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat}}}}\,}$$

This is obtained by treating the amplifier as a saturable absorber in the **rate-equation approximation**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Noise Figure

### Step 1 — Definition

By definition, the optical noise figure is:

$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{in}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{out}}}$$

The input signal shot noise is determined by $P_{\text{in}}$ over a bandwidth $B_e$:

$$\sigma_{\text{in}}^2 = 2 \, q \, (R \, P_{\text{in}}) \, B_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = \frac{(R \, P_{\text{in}})^2}{2 q R P_{\text{in}} B_e} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{2 h \nu B_e}$$

### Step 2 — Output signal and noise

After amplification with gain $G$:
- Output signal power: $P_{\text{out}} = G \, P_{\text{in}}$
- Output noise: shot noise from $G P_{\text{in}}$ plus beat noise between signal and ASE plus beat noise between ASE and itself.

After careful derivation (see Agrawal, *Nonlinear Fiber Optics*), in the high-gain limit:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{4 \, h \nu \, n_{\text{sp}} \, (G - 1) \, B_e + 2 \, h \nu \, B_e}$$

For high gain ($G \gg 1$):

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{4 \, h \nu \, n_{\text{sp}} \, G \, B_e} = \frac{G \, P_{\text{in}}}{4 h \nu \, n_{\text{sp}} \, G \, B_e} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{4 h \nu \, n_{\text{sp}} \, B_e}$$

### Step 3 — Form the noise figure

$$F = \frac{P_{\text{in}} / (2 h \nu B_e)}{P_{\text{in}} / (4 h \nu n_{\text{sp}} B_e)} = 2 n_{\text{sp}}$$

### Step 4 — Final quantum-limited form

$$\boxed{\,F_{\min} = 2 \, n_{\text{sp}} = 2 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_2 - N_1}\,}$$

For complete population inversion ($N_1 = 0$): $F_{\min} = 2 \Rightarrow F_{\min} \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(2) \approx 3 \text{ dB}$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** An EDFA has an unsaturated gain of 30 dB and saturation output power of 13 dBm. Find the actual gain when the input power is 0 dBm.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert to linear values.**

$$G_0 \text{ (linear)} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

$$P_{\text{sat,out}} \text{ (linear)} = 10^{13/10} = 10^{1.3} \approx 19.95 \text{ mW} \approx 20 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{\text{in}} \text{ (linear)} = 10^{0/10} = 1 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate output power assuming unsaturated gain first.**

$$P_{\text{out,est}} = G_0 \cdot P_{\text{in}} = 1000 \times 1 \text{ mW} = 1000 \text{ mW} = 1 \text{ W}$$

This is way above the saturation output power, so the amplifier is strongly saturated. We must solve the self-consistent equation:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}}$$

But $P_{\text{out}} = G \cdot P_{\text{in}}$, so:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{G \, P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $G$.**

$$G \left( 1 + \frac{G \, P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} \right) = G_0$$

$$G + \frac{G^2 P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} = G_0$$

This is a quadratic in $G$:

$$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} G^2 + G - G_0 = 0$$

Plug in numbers: $\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05$

$$0.05 \, G^2 + G - 1000 = 0$$

$$G^2 + 20 G - 20000 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$G = \frac{-20 + \sqrt{400 + 80000}}{2} = \frac{-20 + \sqrt{80400}}{2} = \frac{-20 + 283.55}{2} \approx 131.77$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to dB.**

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(131.77) \approx 21.2 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Compute actual output power.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = G \cdot P_{\text{in}} = 131.77 \times 1 \text{ mW} \approx 131.77 \text{ mW} \approx 21.2 \text{ dBm}$$

This matches $P_{\text{sat,out}} \times (G_0/G - 1) = 20 \times (1000/131.77 - 1) \approx 131.77$ mW. ✓

> **Conclusion:** The EDFA delivers only **21.2 dB** of gain (not 30 dB) and **21.2 dBm** of output power, illustrating strong **gain saturation** at high input power.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — EDFA Performance Trade-offs

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on Gain | Increase → Effect on Noise Figure | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pump Power ($\uparrow$) | Gain $\uparrow$ (until saturation) | NF $\downarrow$ (better inversion) | Increase pump laser current |
| EDF Length ($\uparrow$) | Gain $\uparrow$ (optimum ~10–30 m) | NF slight $\uparrow$ | Optimize for max gain |
| Er³⁺ Concentration ($\uparrow$) | Gain $\uparrow$ (up to limit) | Cluster quenching at high conc. | Typical 100–1000 ppm |
| Input Signal Power ($\uparrow$) | Gain $\downarrow$ (saturation) | NF slight $\uparrow$ | Operate in linear regime for WDM |
| Wavelength (1530 nm) | High gain peak | NF $\sim 4$–5 dB | Use gain-flattening filter |
| Wavelength (1560 nm) | Moderate gain | NF $\sim 5$–6 dB | Better for WDM flatness |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EDFA

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **complete signal-flow architecture** of a typical EDFA, including co-propagating and counter-propagating pump paths, isolators, and ASE filtering.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigIn["INPUT SIGNAL 1550 nm"] --> isoIn["OPTICAL ISOLATOR IN"]
    isoIn --> tapIn["TAP COUPLER 5%"]
    tapIn --> wdmF["WDM COUPLER FORWARD"]
    pumpF["PUMP LASER 980 nm 200 mW"] --> wdmF
    wdmF --> edfCore["ERBIUM DOPED FIBER EDF 15 m"]
    edfCore --> wdmB["WDM COUPLER BACKWARD"]
    pumpB["PUMP LASER 1480 nm OPTIONAL BACKWARD"] --> wdmB
    wdmB --> tapOut["TAP COUPLER 5%"]
    tapOut --> isoOut["OPTICAL ISOLATOR OUT"]
    isoOut --> gff["GAIN FLATTENING FILTER OPTIONAL"]
    gff --> sigOut["AMPLIFIED OUTPUT 1550 nm"]
    edfCore -. ASE NOISE .-> aseNode["ASE MONITOR"]
    tapIn -. PD MON .-> pdetIn["PHOTODIODE IN AGC"]
    tapOut -. PD MON .-> pdetOut["PHOTODIODE OUT AGC"]
    pdetIn --> agcCtrl["AGC CIRCUIT"]
    pdetOut --> agcCtrl
    agcCtrl -. PUMP CURRENT CTRL .-> pumpF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **forward path** (top): input signal at 1550 nm enters from the left, is combined with the 980 nm pump via the WDM coupler, propagates through the EDF, and exits amplified.
- The **backward path** (right): an optional 1480 nm pump injects counter-propagating power for higher gain.
- The **dashed lines** represent **monitoring signals** for automatic gain control (AGC).
- The **ASE noise** is shown as a side-branch from the EDF.

## 4.2 Quasi-Three-Level Energy Diagram of Er³⁺ (Block Representation)

Since Mermaid cannot render true atomic energy levels with horizontal transitions, the following is a **vertically-aligned block-level representation** of the Er³⁺ energy manifold, showing pump absorption, non-radiative decay, and signal stimulated emission.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["GROUND STATE 4I15/2 ENERGY 0 eV N1 = 70 percent"] -->|PUMP ABSORB 980 nm| L2
    L0 -->|PUMP ABSORB 1480 nm| L3u
    L2["PUMP LEVEL 4I11/2 ENERGY 1.27 eV"] -->|NON RADIATIVE DECAY 1 microsec PHONON EMISSION| L3
    L3u["UPPER 4I13/2 1480 nm PUMP DIRECT"] -->|FAST RELAXATION 1 ns| L3
    L3["METASTABLE STATE 4I13/2 ENERGY 0.80 eV LIFETIME 10 ms N2 = 30 percent"] -->|STIMULATED EMISSION 1550 nm GAIN| L0
    L3 -.->|SPONTANEOUS EMISSION ASE NOISE| L0
```

**Key transitions shown:**
- **Vertical up-arrows** = pump absorption (energy added to ion).
- **Solid down-arrows** = stimulated emission (signal amplification).
- **Dashed down-arrows** = spontaneous emission (ASE noise).
- The **non-radiative bridge** between ⁴I₁₁/₂ and ⁴I₁₃/₂ (for 980 nm pumping) is critical for high quantum efficiency.

## 4.3 Gain vs Wavelength Characteristic (Schematic Plot)

The following flowchart-style block diagram maps the **typical gain spectrum** of an EDFA across the C-band, with peak gain at 1532 nm and a flatter region around 1545–1560 nm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WL1530["WAVELENGTH 1530 nm PEAK GAIN 35 dB"] --> WL1535["WAVELENGTH 1535 nm GAIN 30 dB"]
    WL1535 --> WL1540["WAVELENGTH 1540 nm GAIN 26 dB"]
    WL1540 --> WL1545["WAVELENGTH 1545 nm GAIN 24 dB"]
    WL1545 --> WL1550["WAVELENGTH 1550 nm GAIN 23 dB"]
    WL1550 --> WL1555["WAVELENGTH 1555 nm GAIN 23 dB"]
    WL1555 --> WL1560["WAVELENGTH 1560 nm GAIN 22 dB FLAT REGION"]
    WL1560 --> WL1565["WAVELENGTH 1565 nm GAIN 18 dB"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Gain-Flattening Insight:** A gain-flattening filter (GFF) is placed after the EDFA in DWDM systems to attenuate the 1530–1540 nm peak and bring the entire C-band within ±0.5 dB, allowing equal SNR across all WDM channels.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: State the principle of operation of an Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier (EDFA). Mention any two advantages of EDFA over semiconductor optical amplifiers.**

**Model Answer:**

The **principle of EDFA** is based on **stimulated emission of radiation** from trivalent Erbium ions (Er³⁺) doped in the core of a silica optical fiber. A pump laser (980 nm or 1480 nm) excites Er³⁺ ions from the ground state ⁴I₁₅/₂ to higher pump levels. The ions rapidly relax to the metastable state ⁴I₁₃/₂ (lifetime ~10 ms), where population inversion is established. An incoming signal photon at ~1550 nm then triggers **stimulated emission** of an identical photon, amplifying the signal directly in the optical domain.

**Two advantages of EDFA over SOA:**
1. **Lower noise figure** (~4 dB vs ~7–9 dB for SOA) due to high quantum efficiency of the three-level pumping scheme.
2. **Polarization-independent gain** (gain is independent of input polarization, unlike SOA which suffers from polarization-dependent gain ~1 dB).
3. **Higher saturation output power** (~20 dBm) and ability to amplify many WDM channels simultaneously.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: List the two main pump wavelengths used in EDFA. State which one provides lower noise figure and why.**

**Model Answer:**

The two main pump wavelengths for EDFA are:
1. **980 nm** — excites Er³⁺ from ⁴I₁₅/₂ to ⁴I₁₁/₂, then non-radiatively decays to ⁴I₁₃/₂.
2. **1480 nm** — excites Er³⁺ directly into the upper sub-band of ⁴I₁₃/₂.

**Lower noise figure is provided by 980 nm pumping.** The reason is that **980 nm pumping involves a true three-level transition** with a non-radiative bridge, which results in a **smaller population-inversion factor $n_{\text{sp}}$** and hence lower quantum-limited noise figure of $F_{\min} = 2 n_{\text{sp}} \to$ closer to the **3 dB quantum limit**. In contrast, 1480 nm pumping is effectively a two-level (or quasi-two-level) system with a higher $n_{\text{sp}}$, giving a higher noise figure (~5–7 dB).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks**, with sub-parts (a) and (b). The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

**Construction:** A typical EDFA consists of the following components:

1. **Pump laser source** — A high-power semiconductor laser operating at 980 nm or 1480 nm, providing 50–500 mW of pump power.
2. **WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing) coupler** — A fused-fiber or thin-film coupler that combines the pump and signal wavelengths into a single mode of the erbium-doped fiber (EDF).
3. **Erbium-doped fiber (EDF)** — 10–30 m of single-mode silica fiber whose core is doped with trivalent Er³⁺ ions at a concentration of 100–1000 ppm. This is the gain medium.
4. **Input and output optical isolators** — Faraday-rotator-based isolators providing >30 dB isolation to prevent back-reflections and lasing due to ASE.
5. **Tap couplers and monitor photodiodes** — Provide feedback for automatic gain control (AGC) by monitoring input and output powers.
6. **Gain-flattening filter (GFF)** — Optional, used in WDM systems to equalize gain across the C-band.

**Working Principle (Step-by-Step):**

1. The **pump laser** injects high-power 980 nm (or 1480 nm) light into the EDF via the WDM coupler.
2. Er³⁺ ions absorb pump photons and are excited to higher energy levels (⁴I₁₁/₂ for 980 nm; upper ⁴I₁₃/₂ for 1480 nm).
3. The ions **rapidly decay non-radiatively** to the metastable ⁴I₁₃/₂ state (lifetime ~10 ms).
4. With sufficient pump power, **population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$) is established in the metastable state.
5. The **incoming 1550 nm signal** co-propagating through the EDF stimulates excited Er³⁺ ions to drop to the ground state, emitting identical photons via **stimulated emission**.
6. The signal grows exponentially along the fiber, achieving a typical gain of **30–40 dB**.
7. The amplified signal exits through the output isolator; a fraction of the spontaneous emission (ASE) noise also exits but is filtered if necessary.

The pump can be injected either **co-directionally** (forward), **counter-directionally** (backward), or **bidirectionally** (both) to optimize gain, noise figure, and output power.

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the minimum noise figure of an EDFA and show that the quantum limit is 3 dB. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **optical noise figure** of an amplifier is defined as:

$$F = \frac{(\text{SNR})_{\text{in}}}{(\text{SNR})_{\text{out}}}$$

**Input SNR** (limited by shot noise on $P_{\text{in}}$ over electrical bandwidth $B_e$):

$$(\text{SNR})_{\text{in}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{2 h \nu \, B_e}$$

**Output SNR** includes shot noise from amplified signal plus beat noise between signal and ASE. After careful derivation (see Section 3.3 of these notes), in the **high-gain limit** ($G \gg 1$):

$$(\text{SNR})_{\text{out}} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{4 h \nu \, n_{\text{sp}} \, B_e}$$

where the **spontaneous emission (population inversion) factor** is:

$$n_{\text{sp}} = \frac{N_2}{N_2 - N_1}$$

**Forming the ratio:**

$$F = \frac{P_{\text{in}} / (2 h \nu B_e)}{P_{\text{in}} / (4 h \nu n_{\text{sp}} B_e)} = 2 \, n_{\text{sp}}$$

**Quantum Limit:** For **complete population inversion** ($N_1 = 0$, $N_2 = N_t$):

$$n_{\text{sp}} = \frac{N_2}{N_2 - N_1} = 1$$

$$F_{\min} = 2 \times 1 = 2$$

In decibels:

$$F_{\min} \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(2) = 10 \times 0.3010 = 3.0103 \text{ dB} \approx 3 \text{ dB}$$

This 3 dB is the **fundamental quantum-limited noise figure** of any linear phase-insensitive optical amplifier (valid for EDFA, SOA, and Raman amplifiers alike).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**(a) Explain the energy level diagram of Er³⁺ ion and the two pumping schemes (980 nm and 1480 nm) used in EDFA. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Energy Level Diagram of Er³⁺:**

The Er³⁺ ion in silica has the following relevant levels (refer to Section 4.2):

| Level | Term Symbol | Approximate Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Ground State | ⁴I₁₅/₂ | 0 eV |
| Pump Level (980 nm) | ⁴I₁₁/₂ | 1.27 eV |
| Metastable State | ⁴I₁₃/₂ | 0.80 eV |

The metastable state ⁴I₁₃/₂ has an unusually long **spontaneous lifetime of ~10 ms**, which is what makes EDFA efficient — ions accumulate here long enough to be stimulated by signal photons.

**Pumping Scheme 1 — 980 nm Pumping:**

- Er³⁺ ion absorbs a 980 nm photon and is excited from ⁴I₁₅/₂ → ⁴I₁₁/₂.
- The ion **non-radiatively decays** in ~1 μs to ⁴I₁₃/₂, releasing energy as lattice phonons (heat).
- **Quantum efficiency** $\eta_Q = \lambda_p / \lambda_s = 980/1550 \approx 63\%$, but the **conversion efficiency** is high (~90%) because the non-radiative decay is very fast.
- **Noise figure is low** (~4 dB) — close to 3 dB quantum limit.

**Pumping Scheme 2 — 1480 nm Pumping:**

- Er³⁺ ion absorbs a 1480 nm photon and is excited directly into the **upper sub-band of ⁴I₁₃/₂** (above the metastable level by ~0.04 eV).
- The ion relaxes rapidly (~1 ns) to the lower part of ⁴I₁₃/₂.
- **Quantum efficiency** is high: $1480/1550 \approx 95\%$.
- **Output power is higher** (up to 500 mW pump available), but **noise figure is worse** (~5–7 dB) because the system behaves more like a two-level amplifier with a higher $n_{\text{sp}}$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | 980 nm Pump | 1480 nm Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Quantum Efficiency | ~ 90% | ~ 70% |
| Noise Figure | ~ 4 dB (excellent) | ~ 5–7 dB |
| Pump Power | Up to 250 mW | Up to 500 mW |
| Best Use | Pre-amplifier | Booster / power amplifier |

---

**(b) An EDFA has a small-signal gain of 35 dB and saturation output power of 14 dBm. Calculate the actual gain and output power when the input signal power is −5 dBm. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Convert all powers to linear (milliwatts) and gain to linear.**

$$G_0 \text{ (linear)} = 10^{35/10} = 10^{3.5} \approx 3162.3$$

$$P_{\text{sat,out}} = 10^{14/10} = 10^{1.4} \approx 25.12 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{\text{in}} = 10^{-5/10} = 10^{-0.5} \approx 0.3162 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Estimate unsaturated output power to check for saturation.**

$$P_{\text{out,est}} = G_0 \cdot P_{\text{in}} = 3162.3 \times 0.3162 \approx 1000 \text{ mW} = 1 \text{ W}$$

Since this is **far above** $P_{\text{sat,out}} = 25.12$ mW, the amplifier is **strongly saturated** and we cannot use the small-signal approximation.

**Step 3 — Apply the gain-saturation formula and solve self-consistently.**

The output power is $P_{\text{out}} = G \cdot P_{\text{in}}$, and the saturated gain is:

$$G = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}} = \frac{G_0}{1 + \dfrac{G \, P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}}}$$

Rearranging:

$$G \left( 1 + \frac{G \, P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} \right) = G_0$$

$$\frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} G^2 + G - G_0 = 0$$

Plug in the values: $\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{sat,out}}} = \dfrac{0.3162}{25.12} \approx 0.01259$

$$0.01259 \, G^2 + G - 3162.3 = 0$$

Multiply through to simplify:

$$G^2 + 79.43 \, G - 251168 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic using the formula $G = \dfrac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 + 4ac}}{2}$.**

Here $a = 1$, $b = 79.43$, $c = -251168$.

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 79.43^2 + 4 \times 251168 = 6309 + 1004672 = 1010981$$

$$\sqrt{\Delta} \approx 1005.48$$

$$G = \frac{-79.43 + 1005.48}{2} = \frac{926.05}{2} \approx 463.0$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB and compute output power.**

$$G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(463.0) \approx 26.66 \text{ dB}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = G \cdot P_{\text{in}} = 463.0 \times 0.3162 \approx 146.4 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{\text{out,dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(146.4) \approx 21.66 \text{ dBm}$$

**Conclusion:** The EDFA delivers **G ≈ 26.66 dB** (not 35 dB) and an output power of **~21.66 dBm** (~146 mW). The gain has been compressed by **8.34 dB** due to deep saturation, and the output is roughly **7.66 dB** above the nominal saturation output power, consistent with the saturation behaviour.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT assume small-signal gain** when $P_{\text{out,est}} > P_{\text{sat,out}}$ — you will lose **3 to 4 marks** by skipping the quadratic self-consistent solution.
> 2. **Always convert dBm → mW and dB → linear** before substituting into the saturation formula; the formula $G = G_0/(1+P/P_{\text{sat}})$ is in **linear units**.
> 3. **Sign convention for dBm**: $P_{\text{dBm}} = 10 \log_{10}(P/1 \text{ mW})$, not $P/1 \text{ W}$. A common KTU pitfall.
> 4. **Take the positive root** of the quadratic; the negative root is unphysical.
> 5. Mentioning the **quantum-limited 3 dB noise figure** and the role of $n_{\text{sp}}$ carries extra weight in noise-related sub-parts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (EDFA — Principle)**

- **EDFA** = Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier, in-line optical amplifier for the **C-band (1530–1565 nm)**.
- **Active medium** = silica fiber core doped with **Er³⁺** ions (~100–1000 ppm).
- **Pump wavelengths** = **980 nm** (low-noise) and **1480 nm** (high-power).
- **Operating principle** = **stimulated emission** from the metastable **⁴I₁₃/₂** state, after population inversion via pumping.
- **Metastable state lifetime** ≈ **10 ms** — this long lifetime is what makes EDFA efficient.
- **Quasi-three-level system** — both ground and metastable states contribute to absorption/emission.
- **Pumping direction options**: co-propagating, counter-propagating, or bidirectional.
- **Required components**: pump laser, WDM coupler, EDF, isolators (input & output), and optional AGC + GFF.
- **Small-signal gain** $G_0 = \exp(\Gamma_s \sigma_s \Delta N L)$; typical **30–50 dB**.
- **Gain saturation**: $G = G_0 / (1 + P_{\text{in}}/P_{\text{sat}})$ — solve self-consistently for high input.
- **Dominant noise** = **ASE (Amplified Spontaneous Emission)**.
- **Quantum-limited noise figure** $F_{\min} = 2 n_{\text{sp}}$, with $n_{\text{sp}} = N_2/(N_2 - N_1)$.
- **3 dB quantum limit** of NF when $N_1 = 0$ (complete inversion).
- **Typical 980 nm pump EDFA NF** ≈ **4 dB**; 1480 nm ≈ **5–7 dB**.
- **Key advantages** = all-optical amplification, bit-rate transparency, wavelength transparency, polarization insensitivity, low NF, high output power.
- **Typical applications** = long-haul DWDM trunks, submarine cables, pre-amplifiers, booster amplifiers, CATV networks.
- **Gain-flattening filter (GFF)** is essential for multi-channel WDM to avoid SNR imbalance.
- **980 nm pump is preferred for low-noise pre-amplifiers; 1480 nm for high-power boosters.**
- **Numerical habit**: always convert dB ↔ linear, dBm ↔ mW, then verify saturation before applying the small-signal formula.
- **Quick mental check**: an EDFA that "should" give 30 dB but only gives 20 dB → the amplifier is in **saturation** (input too high) or the **pump is under-powered**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure and Working
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure and Working

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Optical Sources — The Heartbeat of Fiber-Optic Systems

In optical communication, the **optical source** is the active electro-optic transducer that converts an **electrical information signal** into a **modulated beam of light (photons)** suitable for injection into an optical fiber. Under the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3 of OEECT721), the two principal semiconductor optical sources studied are:

1. **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** — based on **spontaneous emission**.
2. **Injection Laser Diode (ILD) / Laser Diode (LD)** — based on **stimulated emission**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** "Structure and Working" demands mastery of the **layered semiconductor geometry**, the **active region**, the **recombination physics**, and the **optical/electrical boundary conditions** of both devices. A viva or theory question often requires a labelled cross-section and a band-diagram justification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap & Spillway" Intuition

Imagine a small hill pond (the **active region**) fed by a stream of water (the **injected carriers — electrons and holes**):

- **LED** behaves like a **flooded spillway**: water (carriers) recombines randomly at the surface, producing scattered, incoherent splashes (photons in all directions, random phase, broad spectrum). It is cheap, rugged, and "leaky".
- **Laser Diode** behaves like a **synchronised fountain with two mirrors**: only when the water level (carrier density) crosses a critical threshold do the falling droplets (photons) trigger identical neighbours to fall in **phase, same wavelength, same direction** — producing a narrow, intense, coherent beam.

The "two mirrors" are the cleaved **Fabry–Pérot facets** of the semiconductor crystal, which together form an **optical resonator cavity**.

### 1.3 The Three Fundamental Photon–Matter Interactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Albert Einstein (1917) — the foundation of all optical sources and detectors:**

| # | Process | Direction | Optical Source |
|---|---------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Spontaneous Emission** | Excited electron drops randomly → photon of energy $h\nu = E_2 - E_1$ | LED |
| 2 | **Stimulated Emission** | Incoming photon of energy $h\nu$ triggers an identical photon | Laser Diode |
| 3 | **Absorption** | Electron absorbs a photon and moves to a higher state | Photodetector (Module 4) |

The **rate equations** governing these processes couple the photon density $N_{ph}$ and the carrier density $N$ in the active region:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{I}{qV} - \frac{N}{\tau} - G(N)\,N_{ph}$$

$$\frac{dN_{ph}}{dt} = \Gamma\,G(N)\,N_{ph} - \frac{N_{ph}}{\tau_{ph}} + \beta\,\frac{N}{\tau}$$

where each symbol is rigorously defined in §2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of an LED

### 2.1.1 Carrier Injection and Radiative Recombination

When a **forward bias** is applied to a direct-bandgap semiconductor p–n junction (GaAs, InGaAs, InP):

1. **Electrons** from the n-region are injected across the **depletion region** into the p-side.
2. **Holes** from the p-region move into the n-side.
3. In the **depletion / active region**, electrons occupy conduction-band states and holes occupy valence-band states.
4. An electron **spontaneously drops** into a hole, releasing a photon of energy approximately equal to the **bandgap** $E_g$:

$$h\nu \;\approx\; E_g \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda \;=\; \frac{hc}{E_g} \;=\; \frac{1.24}{E_g\;(\text{eV})}\;\mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wavelength vs. Bandgap — KTU must-know table:**

| Material | $E_g$ (eV) | Emission $\lambda$ (nm) | Window |
|---------|-----------|-------------------------|--------|
| **GaAs** | 1.42 | 870 | 1st |
| **InGaAs** | 0.75 | 1650 | 3rd |
| **InP** | 1.35 | 920 | 1st |
| **AlGaAs** | 1.4–2.0 | 620–880 | 1st |
| **InGaAsP** | 0.8–1.1 | 1100–1550 | 2nd & 3rd |

### 2.1.2 Incoherent Nature of LED Output

Because each recombination event is **spontaneous and independent**, the emitted photons have:
- **Random phase** (incoherence),
- **Broad spectral width** $\Delta\lambda$ typically **30–60 nm**,
- **Large divergence** (Lambertian pattern).

### 2.1.3 LED Internal and External Quantum Efficiency

The **internal quantum efficiency** $\eta_{int}$ is the fraction of injected carriers that generate photons inside the device:

$$\eta_{int} \;=\; \frac{\text{photons generated inside the device per second}}{\text{electrons injected per second}} \;=\; \frac{R_r}{R_r + R_{nr}} \;=\; \frac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{nr} + \tau_r}$$

where $\tau_r$ is the **radiative lifetime** and $\tau_{nr}$ is the **non-radiative lifetime**.

The **external quantum efficiency** $\eta_{ext}$ accounts for the small fraction of photons that escape the high-index semiconductor ($n_s \approx 3.5$) into air ($n=1$):

$$\eta_{ext} \;=\; \frac{\text{photons emitted into air per second}}{\text{electrons injected per second}} \;\approx\; \eta_{int}\;\cdot\;\frac{1}{n_s\,(n_s+1)^2}$$

Using Snell's law, only light within a **critical cone** of half-angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{n}{n_s}\right)$ can escape. The numerical factor evaluates to roughly $\dfrac{1}{2n_s^2}$, giving $\eta_{ext} \approx 1{-}3\%$ for simple LEDs.

## 2.2 Working Principle of a Laser Diode

### 2.2.1 Population Inversion — The Lasing Prerequisite

**Population inversion** is the non-equilibrium condition in which the electron population in the conduction band **exceeds** that in the valence band at the lasing energy. Without inversion, stimulated absorption dominates stimulated emission, and amplification is impossible.

In a **forward-biased p–n heterojunction**, heavy injection (current density $J \gg 1\text{ kA/cm}^2$) drives $N$ above the **transparency carrier density** $N_0$:

$$N \;>\; N_0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad G(N) \;>\; 0 \quad\text{(net optical gain)}$$

### 2.2.2 The Fabry–Pérot Optical Resonator

The two cleaved end-facets of the semiconductor crystal act as **partial mirrors**. Because the refractive index step from semiconductor ($n_s \approx 3.5$) to air ($n=1$) is large, each facet has a power reflectivity:

$$R \;=\; \left(\dfrac{n_s - 1}{n_s + 1}\right)^2 \;\approx\; 0.32 \quad\text{(for GaAs, }n_s=3.6\text{)}$$

Only wavelengths satisfying the **resonance condition** (standing-wave condition in a cavity of length $L$ with effective refractive index $n_{eff}$) build up:

$$m\lambda_m \;=\; 2\,n_{eff}\,L \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_m \;=\; \dfrac{2 n_{eff} L}{m}, \quad m = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **longitudinal mode spacing** (free spectral range) is:

$$\Delta\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{\lambda^2}{2 n_{eff} L\,(1 - \lambda\,n_{g}^{-1}\,dn_{eff}/d\lambda)}$$

where $n_g$ is the group index. For typical $L = 300\,\mu\text{m}$, $\Delta\lambda \approx 0.3\text{–}1\text{ nm}$.

### 2.2.3 The Lasing Threshold Condition

Steady-state lasing demands that the **round-trip optical gain** equals the **round-trip optical loss**:

$$R_1\,R_2\,\exp\!\big(2\,\Gamma\,g_{th}\,L\big) \;=\; \exp\!\big(2\,\alpha_i\,L\big)$$

Solving for the **threshold gain** $g_{th}$:

$$g_{th} \;=\; \alpha_i \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) \;=\; \alpha_i \;+\; \alpha_{mir}$$

where:
- $\alpha_i$ = internal absorption/scattering loss coefficient (cm⁻¹),
- $\alpha_{mir} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ = **mirror loss coefficient**,
- $\Gamma$ = **optical confinement factor** (fraction of mode power inside the active layer),
- $g_{th}$ = threshold material gain.

The corresponding **threshold current density** $J_{th}$ (in cm⁻²) follows an exponential relation with temperature $T$ (in K):

$$J_{th}(T) \;=\; J_0\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

where $T_0$ is the **characteristic temperature** (a key reliability figure of merit).

### 2.2.4 Output Power Equation (Above Threshold)

Above threshold, the additional injected carriers contribute to stimulated emission rather than population growth, so the **slope efficiency** $\eta_D$ (W/A) governs the output:

$$P_{out} \;=\; \eta_D\,\big(I - I_{th}\big) \;=\; \eta_i\,\dfrac{h\nu}{q}\;\dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1/R_1R_2)}{\alpha_i + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1/R_1R_2)}\;\big(I - I_{th}\big)$$

The **differential quantum efficiency** $\eta_D$ is the number of photons leaving the device per additional injected electron:

$$\eta_D \;=\; \eta_i \cdot \dfrac{\alpha_{mir}}{\alpha_i + \alpha_{mir}}$$

## 2.3 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|--------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | J, eV | $1\text{ eV} = 1.602\times10^{-19}\text{ J}$ |
| 2 | Bandgap–wavelength | $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$ | $\mu$m | $E_g$ in eV |
| 3 | Critical escape angle | $\sin\theta_c = n_{air}/n_s$ | rad | $n_s \approx 3.5$ for III–V |
| 4 | Internal QE | $\eta_{int} = \tau_{nr}/(\tau_r+\tau_{nr})$ | — | $0\le\eta_{int}\le 1$ |
| 5 | External QE | $\eta_{ext} \approx \eta_{int}/(2n_s^2)$ | — | $\sim 1{-}3\%$ |
| 6 | Facet reflectivity | $R = \big((n_s-1)/(n_s+1)\big)^2$ | — | $R\approx 0.32$ for GaAs |
| 7 | Resonance condition | $m\lambda = 2n_{eff}L$ | m | Longitudinal modes |
| 8 | Mode spacing | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2/(2n_{eff}L)$ | m | For $L=300\,\mu$m, $\sim 1$ nm |
| 9 | Threshold gain | $g_{th} = \alpha_i + (1/2L)\ln(1/R_1R_2)$ | cm⁻¹ | Steady-state lasing |
| 10 | Threshold current | $J_{th} = J_0\exp(T/T_0)$ | A/cm² | Thermal roll-over |
| 11 | Output power | $P = \eta_D(I - I_{th})$ | W | Above-threshold linear |
| 12 | Differential QE | $\eta_D = \eta_i \cdot \alpha_{mir}/(\alpha_i+\alpha_{mir})$ | — | Often $\eta_D \approx 50{-}60\%$ |
| 13 | Linewidth (Schawlow–Townes) | $\Delta\nu = (h\nu\,n_{sp})/(4\pi P)\,\cdot\,\Delta\nu_{cav}$ | Hz | $\Delta\nu_{cav}=$ cavity linewidth |
| 14 | Recombination rate | $R = N/\tau$ | cm⁻³ s⁻¹ | $\tau^{-1} = \tau_r^{-1}+\tau_{nr}^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** LEDs power **short-reach, low-cost LAN/PON links** (e.g., Ethernet, Plastic Optical Fiber, ONUs in 1G-PON), while laser diodes enable **long-haul DWDM trunks** (100 Gbps, 80+ km spans) and **coherent optical networks**. Mastering the $P$–$I$ curve, threshold, and slope efficiency is essential for **link-budget design**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photon Energy and the Bandgap–Wavelength Relation

**Goal:** Show that the emission wavelength of a direct-bandgap LED is $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$ micrometres.

**Step 1 — Energy of a single photon (Planck–Einstein):**
The energy carried by one photon of frequency $\nu$ is

$$E_{ph} \;=\; h\nu \;=\; \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the source of the photon with the semiconductor bandgap:**
In a direct-bandgap semiconductor, the most efficient radiative transition is from the **bottom of the conduction band** to the **top of the valence band**, releasing an energy exactly equal to the bandgap:

$$E_{ph} \;=\; E_g \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} \;=\; E_g$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the wavelength:**

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical constants with $E_g$ expressed in eV:**
Constants: $h = 6.626\times 10^{-34}\text{ J·s}$, $c = 3\times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$, $1\text{ eV} = 1.602\times 10^{-19}\text{ J}$.

$$\lambda \;=\; \dfrac{6.626\times 10^{-34}\cdot 3\times 10^{8}}{1.602\times 10^{-19}\cdot E_g\,[\text{eV}]} \text{ [m]} \;=\; \dfrac{1.2407\times 10^{-6}}{E_g\,[\text{eV}]} \text{ [m]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to micrometres:**

$$\boxed{\;\lambda\,[\mu\text{m}] \;=\; \dfrac{1.24}{E_g\,[\text{eV}]}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example for GaAs ($E_g = 1.42$ eV):**
$\lambda = 1.24 / 1.42 = 0.873\,\mu\text{m} = 873\text{ nm}$. This is precisely the peak emission of a GaAs LED used in the first optical communication window.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Condition of a Fabry–Pérot Laser

**Goal:** Derive the round-trip threshold condition $R_1 R_2\,e^{2\Gamma g_{th} L} = e^{2\alpha_i L}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the photon flux in a single pass:**
Let $I_0$ be the optical intensity at one mirror. After traversing the active region of length $L$, the field experiences **material gain** $g$ (cm⁻¹) and **internal loss** $\alpha_i$:

$$I_1 \;=\; I_0\,\exp\!\big[(\Gamma g - \alpha_i)\,L\big]$$

**Step 2 — Reflection from the two facets:**
The light is reflected by mirror $R_2$, traverses the cavity back, and is reflected by $R_1$. The intensity after a complete round trip is:

$$I_2 \;=\; I_0\,\exp\!\big[2(\Gamma g - \alpha_i)\,L\big]\;R_1\,R_2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the steady-state (sustained-oscillation) condition:**
For a self-sustaining laser, the round-trip intensity must reproduce itself: $I_2 = I_0$.

$$R_1 R_2\,\exp\!\big[2(\Gamma g - \alpha_i)\,L\big] \;=\; 1$$

**Step 4 — Identify the threshold:**
At threshold the gain exactly cancels all losses, so $g = g_{th}$:

$$R_1 R_2\,\exp\!\big[2(\Gamma g_{th} - \alpha_i)\,L\big] \;=\; 1$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $g_{th}$ by taking the natural logarithm:**

$$2(\Gamma g_{th} - \alpha_i)\,L \;=\; -\ln(R_1 R_2) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $g_{th}$:**

$$\Gamma g_{th} - \alpha_i \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;g_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\Gamma}\left[\alpha_i \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)\right]\;}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation of each term:**
- $\alpha_i$ = bulk scattering + free-carrier absorption (cannot be eliminated by design below a few cm⁻¹).
- $\dfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ = **mirror loss**; vanishing if $R_1 R_2 \to 1$ (no useful output).
- $\Gamma$ = overlap integral — typically 0.03–0.05 for a thin active layer; a quantum-well structure enhances it.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Threshold Current Density $J_{th}$ and the $P$–$I$ Characteristic

**Step 1 — Relate the threshold gain to the threshold carrier density:**
The semiconductor gain is approximately linear in carrier density above transparency:

$$g(N) \;=\; a\,(N - N_0)$$

where $a$ is the differential gain (cm²) and $N_0$ is the transparency carrier density. Hence at threshold, $N_{th} = N_0 + g_{th}/a$.

**Step 2 — Carrier-density vs current-density relation:**
The steady-state carrier continuity gives:

$$N_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{J_{th}\,\tau}{q\,d}$$

where $d$ is the active layer thickness. Substituting:

$$J_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{q\,d}{\tau}\,\left(N_0 + \dfrac{g_{th}}{a}\right) \;=\; \dfrac{q\,d\,N_0}{\tau} \;+\; \dfrac{q\,d}{\tau\,a}\left[\alpha_i + \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)\right]\cdot\dfrac{1}{\Gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Above-threshold, the photon density grows until the rate equations balance:**
For $I > I_{th}$, $N$ is pinned at $N_{th}$ and the extra current feeds stimulated emission. The slope of $P$ vs $I$ is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dI} \;=\; \eta_D \;=\; \eta_i\,\dfrac{h\nu}{q}\,\dfrac{\alpha_{mir}}{\alpha_i + \alpha_{mir}}$$

**Step 4 — Total emitted output power (one facet):**

$$\boxed{\;P_{out} \;=\; \eta_D\,(I - I_{th})\;}$$

This is the **linear portion of the L-I curve** — the operating regime of a directly modulated laser.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linewidth of a Single-Mode Laser (Schawlow–Townes)

**Step 1 — Phase noise from spontaneous emission into the lasing mode:**
Spontaneous events add random phase kicks to the cavity field, broadening the line. The Schawlow–Townes–Henry formula is:

$$\Delta\nu_{ST} \;=\; \dfrac{h\nu\,n_{sp}}{4\pi\,P}\,\left(\Delta\nu_{cav}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Cavity linewidth:**
From the resonance condition, $\Delta\nu_{cav} = c/(4\pi n_{eff} L)\cdot\ln(1/R_1 R_2) + \text{(loss term)}$.

**Step 3 — Plugging in numerical values (1550 nm, $P=1$ mW, $L=300\,\mu$m):**
$\Delta\nu_{ST} \approx 100\text{ kHz}$, equivalent to $\Delta\lambda \approx 8\times 10^{-4}$ pm — far narrower than an LED ($\Delta\lambda \approx 30$–$60$ nm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical scale check (KTU-friendly):**
> - LED linewidth $\sim 10^{13}\text{ Hz}$ (incoherent).
> - FP laser $\sim 10^{11}\text{ Hz}$ (multi-mode).
> - DFB/DBR single-mode laser $\sim 10^{6}\text{–}10^{8}\text{ Hz}$.
> - Coherent source $\sim 10^{4}\text{–}10^{5}\text{ Hz}$.
> This $10^{8}$ factor difference in linewidth is precisely why laser diodes enable **DWDM** and LEDs do not.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards applied:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `led1`, `laser1`). Labels contain clean uppercase text only, no markdown or HTML formatting.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Source Sub-system in a Fiber Link

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["DRIVE CURRENT I"] --> B["SEMICONDUCTOR OPTICAL SOURCE"]
    B --> C["LED OR LASER DIODE"]
    C --> D["OPTICAL COUPLING LENS"]
    D --> E["OPTICAL FIBER"]
    E --> F["PHOTODETECTOR"]
    F --> G["RECEIVER ELECTRONICS"]
    C --> H["THERMAL SINK"]
    H --> I["TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER TEC"]
    I --> C
```

## 4.2 Internal Block Architecture — LED and Laser Diode Compared

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LED_BLOCK["LED STRUCTURE"]
        led1["P CONTACT METAL"] --> led2["P TYPE CLADDING LAYER"]
        led2 --> led3["ACTIVE P TYPE REGION"]
        led3 --> led4["N TYPE SUBSTRATE"]
        led4 --> led5["N CONTACT METAL"]
        led1 --> led6["LIGHT EXIT TOP OR EDGE"]
        led5 --> led6
    end
    subgraph LASER_BLOCK["LASER DIODE STRUCTURE"]
        laser1["P CONTACT METAL"] --> laser2["P TYPE CLADDING ALGAAS HIGH BANDGAP"]
        laser2 --> laser3["ACTIVE REGION GAAS LOW BANDGAP QUANTUM WELL"]
        laser3 --> laser4["N TYPE CLADDING ALGAAS HIGH BANDGAP"]
        laser4 --> laser5["N CONTACT METAL"]
        laser6["LEFT CLEAVED FACET REFLECTOR R1"] --- laser3
        laser7["RIGHT CLEAVED FACET REFLECTOR R2"] --- laser3
        laser3 --> laser8["COHERENT OUTPUT BEAM LAMBDA 1550 NM"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — LED Working Principle (Spontaneous Emission)

```mermaid
graph TD
    flow1["FORWARD BIAS APPLIED V GREATER THAN EG OVER Q"] --> flow2["ELECTRONS INJECTED FROM N REGION INTO ACTIVE LAYER"]
    flow2 --> flow3["HOLES INJECTED FROM P REGION INTO ACTIVE LAYER"]
    flow3 --> flow4["ELECTRON HOLE PAIRS FORM EXCITONS"]
    flow4 --> flow5["SPONTANEOUS RECOMBINATION OCCURS RANDOM PHASE"]
    flow5 --> flow6["PHOTON EMITTED WITH ENERGY HNU APPROX EG"]
    flow6 --> flow7{"ANGLE LESS THAN CRITICAL ANGLE THETA C"}
    flow7 -->|"YES"| flow8["PHOTON ESCAPES TO AIR OR FIBER"]
    flow7 -->|"NO"| flow9["PHOTON TRAPPED BY TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION"]
    flow9 --> flow10["REABSORBED OR LOST AS HEAT"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow — Laser Diode Working Principle (Stimulated Emission)

```mermaid
graph TD
    lf1["FORWARD BIAS APPLIED HIGH INJECTION LEVEL"] --> lf2["CARRIER DENSITY N EXCEEDS TRANSPARENCY N0"]
    lf2 --> lf3["POPULATION INVERSION ACHIEVED IN ACTIVE REGION"]
    lf3 --> lf4["SPONTANEOUS PHOTON TRIGGERS STIMULATED EMISSION"]
    lf4 --> lf5["AMPLIFIED LIGHT TRAVELS TO FACET R1"]
    lf5 --> lf6["PARTIAL REFLECTION R1 PART TRANSMISSION OUTPUT"]
    lf6 --> lf7["REMAINING PHOTON TRAVELS BACK TO FACET R2"]
    lf7 --> lf8["PARTIAL REFLECTION R2 PART TRANSMISSION OUTPUT"]
    lf8 --> lf9{"GAIN GREATER THAN LOSS AT THRESHOLD"}
    lf9 -->|"YES BELOW THRESHOLD AMPLIFIED SPONTANEOUS EMISSION ONLY"| lf10["SUPERLUMINESCENT BROAD OUTPUT"]
    lf9 -->|"ABOVE THRESHOLD GTH ACHIEVED"| lf11["COHERENT MONOCHROMATIC STIMULATED OUTPUT LAMBDA M"]
    lf11 --> lf12["OUTPUT POWER P EQUALS ETA D TIMES I MINUS ITH"]
```

## 4.5 Energy-Band Diagram — Direct-Bandgap Active Region

```mermaid
graph LR
    eb1["CONDUCTION BAND EC"] --- eb2["FERMI LEVEL IN CONDUCTION EFN HIGH INJECTION"]
    eb2 --- eb3["QUASI FERMI LEVEL EFN"]
    eb3 --- eb4["ACTIVE REGION THIN LAYER"]
    eb4 --- eb5["QUASI FERMI LEVEL EFP"]
    eb5 --- eb6["FERMI LEVEL IN VALENCE EFP HIGH INJECTION"]
    eb6 --- eb7["VALENCE BAND EV"]
    eb4 --> eb8["RADIATIVE TRANSITION HNU EQUALS EFN MINUS EFP"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The diagram captures the **quasi-Fermi level separation** inside the active region. Lasing requires $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} \geq h\nu \geq E_g$. This is the **Bernard–Duraffourg condition** for population inversion in a semiconductor.

## 4.6 P–I and I–V Characteristic Schematic (Textual Plot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pi1["CURRENT I MA"] --> pi2["BELOW ITH FLUORESCENT REGION SLOPE LOW"]
    pi2 --> pi3["AT ITH KNEE POINT LASER EMISSION BEGINS"]
    pi3 --> pi4["ABOVE ITH LINEAR COHERENT REGION SLOPE ETA D HIGH"]
    pi4 --> pi5["AT IMAX THERMAL ROLLOVER SLOPE DROPS"]
```

| Region | I range | Output behaviour | Spectral width |
|--------|--------|------------------|----------------|
| Sub-threshold | $I < I_{th}$ | Spontaneous emission (LED-like) | 30–60 nm |
| At threshold | $I = I_{th}$ | Mode competition begins | multi-mode |
| Above threshold | $I_{th} < I < I_{max}$ | Coherent stimulated | 1–3 nm (FP) / <0.01 nm (DFB) |
| Thermal roll-off | $I > I_{max}$ | Saturation | broadened |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of spontaneous emission in a semiconductor LED. Why is the LED output incoherent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- In a forward-biased direct-bandgap p–n junction, electrons injected from the n-side recombine with holes in the active region. **(1 mark)**
- This recombination is **spontaneous** — it occurs randomly, without any external trigger; each event is statistically independent of the others. **(1 mark)**
- Because the emission events are random, the photons have **random phase, random direction, and a wide spread in wavelength (broad spectrum, 30–60 nm)**. This is the definition of **incoherent** radiation. **(1 mark)**

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define "population inversion" and state its role in laser action.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- Population inversion is the non-equilibrium state in which the **density of electrons in the excited (conduction) state exceeds that in the lower (valence) state** at the lasing photon energy. **(1 mark)**
- It is achieved in a semiconductor laser by **heavy forward-bias injection** that pushes the quasi-Fermi level separation above the bandgap: $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} > E_g$ (Bernard–Duraffourg condition). **(1 mark)**
- It is the **necessary precondition for stimulated emission to dominate over absorption**, allowing optical amplification and therefore lasing. Without inversion, no net gain is possible. **(1 mark)**

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer ONE of the two choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional sketch, describe the **structure and working of a double-heterojunction (DH) semiconductor laser diode**. Explain the role of the two heterojunctions in carrier confinement and optical guiding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **threshold condition** for a Fabry–Pérot semiconductor laser. Define the **threshold current density** and explain how it varies with temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Structure and Working (7 marks)**

1. **Layered structure (2 marks):** A DH laser consists of a thin **p-type GaAs active layer** (low bandgap, $E_{g1}$) sandwiched between a **p-type AlGaAs cladding** (top, higher bandgap $E_{g2}$) and an **n-type AlGaAs cladding** (bottom, $E_{g2}$). Stripe contacts confine the current; cleaved facets form the resonator.

2. **Energy-band diagram at equilibrium (1 mark):** The conduction band has a "notch" and the valence band has a "step" at each heterojunction. Injected electrons and holes are **trapped inside the active layer** because the wider-bandgap claddings act as potential barriers — this is **carrier confinement**.

3. **Refractive-index profile (1 mark):** Since $E_{g2} > E_{g1}$, the cladding has a **lower refractive index** than the active layer. The active region thus behaves as a **planar dielectric waveguide**, confining the optical mode — this is **optical confinement**.

4. **Working — stimulated emission under forward bias (2 marks):** Above $I_{th}$, the carrier density in the active layer exceeds the transparency value, producing population inversion. Photons spontaneously emitted along the cavity axis are reflected by the cleaved facets; each round trip amplifies the field. The output is a **coherent, narrow-linewidth beam** at $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$.

5. **One-sentence summary (1 mark):** The DH design provides **simultaneous electrical and optical confinement in a single thin layer**, dramatically reducing $I_{th}$ and improving efficiency.

**Part (b) — Threshold Condition and Thermal Behaviour (7 marks)**

1. **Round-trip condition (3 marks):** As derived in §3.2 above, the steady-state threshold is

$$R_1 R_2\,\exp\!\big(2\,\Gamma\,g_{th}\,L\big) \;=\; \exp\!\big(2\,\alpha_i L\big)$$

which gives

$$g_{th} \;=\; \alpha_i \;+\; \dfrac{1}{2L}\,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$$

2. **Threshold current density definition (1 mark):** $J_{th}$ is the minimum drive current density at which the round-trip gain equals the total loss and lasing begins.

3. **Carrier-density relation (1 mark):** Using $g = a(N - N_0)$ and $N_{th} = J_{th}\tau/(qd)$,

$$J_{th} \;=\; \dfrac{q\,d}{\tau}\!\left(N_0 + \dfrac{g_{th}}{a}\right)$$

4. **Temperature dependence (2 marks):** $J_{th}$ increases exponentially with temperature:

$$J_{th}(T) \;=\; J_0\,\exp\!\left(\dfrac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

For GaAs/AlGaAs DH lasers $T_0 \approx 120{-}200$ K; for InGaAsP lasers $T_0 \approx 60{-}90$ K. Cooling (via a thermoelectric cooler) is therefore essential for stable operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> - Students often **omit the confinement factor $\Gamma$** from the threshold gain expression. For a thin active layer, $\Gamma \ll 1$, and forgetting it inflates $g_{th}$ by a factor of $20$–$30$. **Always include $\Gamma$.**
> - A common mistake is to write $g_{th} = \alpha_i$ only. Mirror loss **must** be added.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **structure and working of a surface-emitting LED (SLED)**. Compare its output characteristics with an **edge-emitting LED (ELED)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **internal** and **external quantum efficiency** of an LED. Derive the **relation between the two**, starting from the critical-angle condition. Why is $\eta_{ext}$ so small? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — SLED and ELED Structure (7 marks)**

1. **SLED structure (2 marks):** The active junction is parallel to the wafer surface. A **well (or hemisphere) is etched into the substrate** so that light emitted upward suffers only one refractive-index step. A **metal contact ring** in the centre provides current injection while leaving a large optical aperture.

2. **ELED structure (1 mark):** The active layer is a narrow stripe; light is emitted from the **cleaved edge** of the chip into a fibre butt-coupled at the end face.

3. **SLED working (2 marks):** Electrons injected through the central contact recombine in the active region. The etched well increases the **escape cone** and reduces internal reflection, so more photons enter the coupled fibre.

4. **Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | SLED | ELED |
|-----------|------|------|
| Emission direction | Normal to surface | Edge (in-plane) |
| Output pattern | **Lambertian** (broad) | More directional |
| Modulation bandwidth | Low (~50–100 MHz) | Moderate (~200 MHz) |
| Spectral width | 30–60 nm | 60–100 nm |
| Coupling to fibre | Poor (multimode only) | Slightly better |
| Cost | Very low | Low |

5. **Application (1 mark):** SLEDs are used in **short-reach POF links and optical printers**; ELEDs are used in **first-window LANs at 850 nm**.

**Part (b) — Internal and External Quantum Efficiency (7 marks)**

1. **Definitions (1 mark each):**
 - $\eta_{int}$ = (rate of photons generated inside the device)/(rate of electrons injected) = $R_r/(R_r+R_{nr}) = \tau_{nr}/(\tau_r+\tau_{nr})$.
 - $\eta_{ext}$ = (rate of photons emitted into free space)/(rate of electrons injected).

2. **Escape-cone argument (2 marks):** A photon generated at the semiconductor–air interface escapes only if its angle of incidence is below the **critical angle** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n/n_s)$. The fraction of isotropically emitted photons within the solid-angle escape cone is:

$$F \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\big(1 - \cos\theta_c\big) \;\approx\; \dfrac{\sin^2\theta_c}{2} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{2n_s^2}$$

(using $\sin\theta_c = 1/n_s$). For $n_s = 3.5$, $F \approx 1/24.5 \approx 4\%$.

3. **Relation (2 marks):** Combining the radiative fraction with the escape fraction:

$$\eta_{ext} \;=\; \eta_{int}\;\cdot\;F \;\approx\; \dfrac{\eta_{int}}{2\,n_s^2}$$

For $\eta_{int} = 0.5$ and $n_s = 3.5$: $\eta_{ext} \approx 0.5/24.5 \approx 2\%$.

4. **Why $\eta_{ext}$ is small (2 marks):**
 - The high refractive index of semiconductors ($n_s \approx 3$–$3.6$) limits the escape cone to a small fraction of the total solid angle.
 - Photons emitted beyond the critical angle undergo **total internal reflection** and are eventually reabsorbed.
 - A **hemispherical dome** or **non-imaging concentrator** can double or triple $\eta_{ext}$ in practical LEDs, but the underlying physics limits it to a few percent without resonant-cavity (RC-LED) design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> - Students frequently write $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int}/(2n_s)$ instead of $\eta_{int}/(2n_s^2)$. The factor is **$2 n_s^2$** in the denominator, not $2 n_s$. Use the **solid-angle integral** carefully.
> - Many forget to state that $\eta_{int} \le 1$, and present $\eta_{ext}$ values that violate the bound.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- [x] **Two optical sources:** LED (spontaneous, incoherent) and Laser Diode (stimulated, coherent).
