- [x] **Direct bandgap** is mandatory — GaAs, InGaAs, InGaAsP. Indirect bandgap (Si, Ge) → very poor emitters.
- [x] **Bandgap–wavelength formula:** $\lambda\,[\mu\text{m}] = 1.24 / E_g\,[\text{eV}]$.
- [x] **LED critical angle:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n_s) \approx 17°$ for GaAs; only $\sim 2{-}4\%$ of photons escape.
- [x] **Internal vs external QE:** $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int}/(2n_s^2)$.
- [x] **Double-heterojunction** laser provides simultaneous **carrier confinement** (bandgap step) and **optical confinement** (refractive-index step) in the active layer.
- [x] **Fabry–Pérot cavity** formed by cleaved facets with $R \approx 0.32$ (uncoated GaAs).
- [x] **Resonance condition:** $m\lambda = 2 n_{eff} L$. Mode spacing $\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2/(2n_{eff}L)$.
- [x] **Threshold gain:** $g_{th} = \alpha_i + (1/2L)\ln(1/R_1R_2)$. **Do not forget the confinement factor $\Gamma$ in the mode equation.**
- [x] **Threshold current:** $J_{th}(T) = J_0\exp(T/T_0)$ — temperature-sensitive; $T_0$ is a key device figure of merit.
- [x] **Above-threshold P–I:** $P = \eta_D (I - I_{th})$; slope efficiency $\eta_D = \eta_i \cdot \alpha_{mir}/(\alpha_i + \alpha_{mir})$.
- [x] **LED output:** broad spectrum (30–60 nm), Lambertian pattern, low modulation bandwidth.
- [x] **LD output:** narrow spectrum (<1 nm FP; <0.01 nm DFB), directional, high modulation bandwidth (tens of GHz).
- [x] **Bernard–Duraffourg condition:** $E_{Fn} - E_{Fp} \ge h\nu \ge E_g$ — defines the lasing threshold in terms of quasi-Fermi levels.
- [x] **Schawlow–Townes linewidth:** $\Delta\nu_{ST} = (h\nu\,n_{sp})/(4\pi P)\,\Delta\nu_{cav}$ — inversely proportional to output power.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** LEDs are for **short, low-cost, low-bit-rate** links; laser diodes are for **long-haul, high-bit-rate, DWDM** systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Board Examiner Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, examiners allocate marks for: **(i) a labelled cross-sectional diagram, (ii) the energy-band explanation, (iii) the correct algebraic derivation with the confinement factor, and (iv) numerical examples with proper units.** Skipping any one of these four typically costs 2–3 marks. Practise drawing the DH laser cross-section from memory and writing the threshold equation with all symbols defined — this single act can secure an easy 8–10 out of 14.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Raman Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Raman Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raman Amplifier (RA):** A Raman amplifier is an optical amplifier that operates on the principle of **Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS)**, an inelastic third-order nonlinear optical process in which energy from a high-power optical **pump** is coherently transferred to a lower-frequency **signal** (Stokes wave) through the interaction with the vibrational modes (optical phonons) of the silica ($\text{SiO}_2$) host medium.

In the context of KTU's 2024 Scheme Optical Communication syllabus, Raman amplifiers are classified as:

| Type | Physical Location | Pump Configuration | Primary Use |
|------|-------------------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Distributed Raman Amplifier (DRA)** | Transmission fiber itself | Co-, Counter-, or Bi-directional | Long-haul DWDM |
| **Lumped Raman Amplifier (LRA)** | External spool of specialty fiber | Counter-propagating (typical) | High-gain booster |
| **Hybrid Raman / EDFA** | Combination of both | Combined | Ultra-long-haul systems |

### 1.2 The Physical Process — Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS)

When an intense pump photon of frequency $\nu_p$ interacts with a silica molecule in its vibrational ground state, the molecule is excited to a virtual intermediate state and immediately drops to a real vibrational state, emitting a **Stokes photon** at a lower frequency $\nu_s$. The frequency difference is the **Stokes shift**:

$$
\Delta\nu = \nu_p - \nu_s = \nu_{\text{phonon}}
$$

For standard silica fiber, the dominant Stokes shift is approximately **13.2 THz** (≈ 100 nm around 1550 nm).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ball on a Spring" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English):** Imagine a strong **bass drum** (pump) vibrating in a hall full of wine glasses. The vibration shakes the glass molecules at a natural resonance. A faint **violin note** (signal) at a slightly lower pitch can now borrow energy from the bass drum and grow louder — but only if the violin is tuned to a specific pitch *lower* than the drum by the glass's natural vibration frequency. In silica, the "glass" is silicon dioxide, the natural "ring" is at ~13.2 THz, and this energy transfer amplifies the signal light.

This frequency-degenerate, **broadband, and tunable** nature distinguishes Raman amplification from rare-earth (EDFA) amplification, which is locked to fixed atomic transitions.

### 1.4 Why Raman Amplifiers Matter in Modern DWDM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Bandwidth:** The Raman gain spectrum of silica is extremely broad (≈ 30–40 THz), covering the entire C, L, and S bands.
> - **Low Noise Figure (NF):** Distributed Raman amplification has a theoretical quantum-limited NF approaching **3 dB**, lower than EDFA (~5 dB).
> - **Flexible Wavelength:** Any signal wavelength can be amplified simply by tuning the pump wavelength.
> - **Distributed Gain:** The transmission fiber itself becomes the gain medium → improved OSNR for long-haul links.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Pump injection:** A high-power CW laser (typically at ~1450 nm for amplifying 1550 nm signals) is launched into the fiber.
2. **Phonon generation:** The pump photons excite the Si–O bond vibrational modes (optical phonons) of silica.
3. **Stimulated Stokes emission:** A signal photon at $\nu_s = \nu_p - 13.2$ THz stimulates the release of a second, coherent photon at $\nu_s$, amplifying the signal.
4. **Energy transfer:** Each pump photon lost corresponds to exactly one signal photon gained — energy is conserved through the phonon.
5. **Gain buildup:** As pump and signal co-propagate, the signal grows exponentially with distance, while the pump is depleted.

### 2.2 The Raman Gain Coefficient $g_R(\Delta\nu)$

The Raman gain coefficient is a material property that depends on the frequency offset $\Delta\nu$ between pump and signal.

- Peak value for silica: $g_R^{\text{peak}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-13}$ m/W at $\Delta\nu = 13.2$ THz (around 1550 nm).
- FWHM of the gain spectrum: ~6–8 THz.
- $g_R$ is **polarization-dependent**; for randomly polarized light, an effective value $g_R/2$ is used.

### 2.3 Coupled Wave Equations (Steady-State, CW Pumps)

For CW pump and signal in a single-mode fiber, ignoring higher-order terms:

$$
\frac{dP_s}{dz} = -\alpha_s P_s + \frac{g_R}{A_{\text{eff}}} P_p P_s
$$

$$
\frac{dP_p}{dz} = -\alpha_p P_p - \frac{\nu_p}{\nu_s} \frac{g_R}{A_{\text{eff}}} P_s P_p
$$

where:
- $P_s(z)$, $P_p(z)$ are signal and pump powers along $z$.
- $\alpha_s$, $\alpha_p$ are fiber attenuation coefficients (m⁻¹).
- $A_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective mode area.
- The negative sign in the pump equation represents **pump depletion** by the signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why":** The first equation says signal power grows wherever pump power is high. The second says pump power is consumed by both linear fiber loss and stimulated Stokes transfer. This bidirectional coupling is the heart of Raman physics.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Raman Amplifiers**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Boundary / Units |
|---|---------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $G_R = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{g_R \, P_p(0) \, L_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{eff}}}\right)$ | Small-signal on-off Raman gain | Dimensionless (linear) |
| 2 | $L_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1 - e^{-\alpha_p L}}{\alpha_p}$ | Effective interaction length | meters (m) |
| 3 | $P_{\text{th}} \approx \dfrac{16 \, A_{\text{eff}}}{g_R \, L_{\text{eff}}}$ | Raman threshold pump power (CW) | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $G_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(G_R)$ | Gain in decibels | dB |
| 5 | $\text{OSNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{P_s^{\text{out}}}{N_{\text{ASE}} \, B_n}$ | Output optical SNR | dB |
| 6 | $\text{NF}_{\text{Raman}} = 2 \, n_{sp} \approx 2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{e^{h\Delta\nu/kT}-1}\right)$ | Noise figure (quantum limit → 3 dB) | dB |
| 7 | $\text{NF}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{NF})$ | Noise figure in dB | dB |
| 8 | $\Delta\nu_{\text{Stokes}} \approx 13.2~\text{THz}$ | Peak Stokes shift in silica | THz |

> **Critical Reminder:** When writing any modulus in KTU answer sheets, use $\vert x \vert$ (with `\vert` macro). In tables, always escape as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Submarine cable systems:** Distributed Raman pumping extends repeater spacing beyond the 80 km EDFA limit.
- **Ultra-long-haul terrestrial links (≥ 1000 km):** DRA + EDFA hybrids achieve > 100 nm usable bandwidth.
- **Low-noise pre-amplification:** Improves receiver sensitivity by ~2–3 dB in long links.
- **Broadband wavelength tunability:** Solves the "green gap" of EDFAs in the S-band and L-band.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — On-Off Raman Gain $G_R$ (Small-Signal Approximation)

**Starting Point:** Coupled wave equation for signal under undepleted pump ($P_p(z) \approx P_p(0) e^{-\alpha_p z}$).

**Step 1 — Substitute undepleted pump into signal equation:**

$$
\frac{dP_s}{dz} = -\alpha_s P_s + \frac{g_R}{A_{\text{eff}}} P_p(0) e^{-\alpha_p z} \, P_s
$$

**Step 2 — Factor $P_s$ and integrate over the fiber length $L$:**

$$
\int_{P_s(0)}^{P_s(L)} \frac{dP_s}{P_s} = \int_0^L \left[-\alpha_s + \frac{g_R \, P_p(0)}{A_{\text{eff}}} e^{-\alpha_p z}\right] dz
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the right-hand side:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_s(L)}{P_s(0)}\right) = -\alpha_s L + \frac{g_R \, P_p(0)}{A_{\text{eff}}} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha_p L}}{\alpha_p}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize $L_{\text{eff}}$ and form the gain expression:**

Since signal attenuation $\alpha_s \approx \alpha_p = \alpha$ for closely-spaced wavelengths, $-\alpha L$ cancels with the exponential, leaving:

$$
G_R = \frac{P_s(L)}{P_s(0)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{g_R \, P_p(0) \, L_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{eff}}}\right)
$$

where

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**

$$
G_{R,\text{dB}} = 4.343 \cdot \frac{g_R \, P_p(0) \, L_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{eff}}}
$$

> The factor **4.343 = 10/ln(10)** converts natural log to base-10 logarithm for dB-scale reporting.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Length $L_{\text{eff}}$ (Limiting Cases)

**Case 1: Short fiber** ($\alpha L \ll 1$): Use Taylor expansion $e^{-\alpha L} \approx 1 - \alpha L + \frac{(\alpha L)^2}{2} - \ldots$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} \approx \frac{1 - (1 - \alpha L)}{\alpha} = L
$$

> For short, low-loss fiber, $L_{\text{eff}} \approx L$.

**Case 2: Long fiber** ($\alpha L \gg 1$): $e^{-\alpha L} \to 0$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} \to \frac{1}{\alpha}
$$

> For a typical silica fiber with $\alpha = 0.2$ dB/km $= 0.046$ km⁻¹, $L_{\text{eff}} \approx 21.7$ km. Thus no benefit from fiber longer than ~20–25 km.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Raman Threshold Power

The threshold is defined as the pump power at which the **Stokes power equals the pump power** at the fiber output. Using the undepleted approximation and a critical condition $g_R P_p L_{\text{eff}} / A_{\text{eff}} \approx 16$:

**Step 1 — Apply threshold condition:** $G_R = e^{16}$ (≈ 10⁷ linear, ~70 dB).

$$
\frac{g_R P_{\text{th}} L_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{eff}}} = 16
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $P_{\text{th}}$:**

$$
P_{\text{th}} = \frac{16 \, A_{\text{eff}}}{g_R \, L_{\text{eff}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical estimate (silica, 1550 nm):**
- $A_{\text{eff}} \approx 80~\mu\text{m}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-12}~\text{m}^2$
- $g_R \approx 1 \times 10^{-13}~\text{m/W}$ (peak)
- $L_{\text{eff}} \approx 20~\text{km} = 2 \times 10^4~\text{m}$

$$
P_{\text{th}} = \frac{16 \times 80 \times 10^{-12}}{10^{-13} \times 2 \times 10^4} = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.64~\text{W} = 640~\text{mW}
$$

> This ~600 mW level explains why practical Raman pumps are high-power, and why co-propagating (forward) pumps can cause signal distortion at high data rates.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Raman vs. EDFA

| Parameter | EDFA | Raman (Distributed) | Raman (Lumped) |
|-----------|------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Gain medium | Er-doped silica | Standard SMF | Specialty fiber (e.g., DCF) |
| Pump wavelength | 980 nm or 1480 nm | Tunable (~ 100 nm below signal) | Tunable |
| Bandwidth | C-band: ~35 nm | Up to ~80–100 nm | Up to ~40 nm |
| Noise Figure | ~4.5–6 dB | ~3–4 dB (incl. double Rayleigh) | ~4.5–5 dB |
| Saturation power | High | Adjustable via pump | Adjustable via pump |
| Pump power required | ~50–200 mW | 200 mW – 1.5 W | 1–5 W |
| Pump direction | Co-pump / Counter-pump | Co / Counter / Bi | Mostly counter |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Stimulated Raman Scattering Energy Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Ground State of SiO2 Molecule] -->|Pump Photon Energy h times nu_p| B[Virtual Excited State]
    B -->|Stimulated by Signal h times nu_s| C[Vibrational Excited State plus Stokes Photon h times nu_s]
    C -->|Energy Delta h times Delta nu| A
    A -->|Incoming Signal h times nu_s amplifies| C
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of a Distributed Raman Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    TX[DWDM Transmitter] --> FIBER[Transmission Fiber SMF 80 to 100 km]
    FIBER --> EDFA[EDFA Booster In-line]
    EDFA --> FIBER2[Next Fiber Span]
    FIBER2 --> RX[DWDM Receiver]
    PUMP1[High Power Raman Pump 1450 nm] -->|Counter propagating via WDM Coupler| FIBER
    PUMP2[Optional Second Pump 1465 nm] -->|Bi directional| FIBER
    CTRL[Pump Power Controller and Monitor] --> PUMP1
    CTRL --> PUMP2
    PUMP3[EDFA Pump 980 nm 1480 nm] --> EDFA
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Co-Propagating vs Counter-Propagating Pumping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A[Co propagating Forward Pump]
        S1[Signal] -->|Same Direction| AMP1[Raman Gain in Fiber]
        P1[Pump 1450 nm] --> AMP1
    end
    subgraph B[Counter propagating Backward Pump]
        S2[Signal Input] --> AMP2[Raman Gain in Fiber]
        P2[Pump 1450 nm] -->|Opposite Direction| AMP2
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Noise Sources in a Raman Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    SIG[Input Signal Photons] --> AMP[Raman Gain Process]
    PUMP[Pump Photons] --> AMP
    AMP --> OUT[Output Signal plus ASE]
    NOISE1[Spontaneous Raman Scattering] --> OUT
    NOISE2[Double Rayleigh Backscattering DRB] --> OUT
    NOISE3[Pump Relative Intensity Noise RIN Transfer] --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure Notes for KTU 2024 Examiners:**
> - The counter-propagating configuration is preferred in high-bit-rate (≥ 10 Gbps/ch) DWDM systems because RIN transfer from pump to signal is averaged out over the bit period.
> - Bi-directional pumping gives the most uniform gain distribution but requires two pump lasers and adds cost.
> - The two main excess noise penalties unique to Raman are **DRB** (multipath interference) and **RIN transfer**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is Stimulated Raman Scattering? How does it differ from ordinary Rayleigh scattering?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stimulated Raman Scattering (SRS) is an **inelastic** third-order nonlinear optical process in which a high-energy pump photon interacts with the vibrational modes (optical phonons) of the silica medium. Part of the pump energy is transferred to a lower-frequency Stokes photon, with the difference appearing as molecular vibration energy. SRS requires a **threshold pump power** (~ 600 mW in SMF) and produces a coherent, signal-amplified Stokes wave.

In contrast, **Rayleigh scattering** is an *elastic* process that scatters light at the *same* frequency in all directions. It is linear (no threshold), causes fiber attenuation, and does not transfer energy to a different optical frequency. Rayleigh scattering is the dominant loss mechanism (~ 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm), whereas SRS is the gain mechanism exploited in Raman amplifiers.

> **Mark Distribution (as per KTU 2024 valuation key):** 1.5 marks for SRS definition, 1 mark for threshold/phonon explanation, 0.5 mark for the Rayleigh contrast.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Stokes shift. What is its typical value in silica fiber, and why is it important for broadband amplification?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stokes shift** $\Delta\nu$ is the frequency (or wavelength) difference between the pump photon and the emitted Stokes (signal) photon in the SRS process, given by $\Delta\nu = \nu_p - \nu_s = \nu_{\text{phonon}}$.

For fused silica ($\text{SiO}_2$) optical fiber, the dominant Stokes shift is approximately **13.2 THz** (≈ 100 nm in the 1550 nm region).

It is important because:
1. The Raman gain spectrum is centered at this offset; placing the signal ~13.2 THz below the pump wavelength maximizes gain.
2. The broadband (~ 6 THz FWHM) nature of the Raman gain peak around this shift allows **simultaneous amplification of many DWDM channels** across the C- and L-bands by simply tuning multiple pump wavelengths.
3. It determines the choice of pump wavelength — for 1550 nm signals, the most efficient pump is around 1450 nm.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the small-signal on-off Raman gain $G_R$ in terms of pump power $P_p(0)$, Raman gain coefficient $g_R$, effective area $A_{\text{eff}}$, and effective length $L_{\text{eff}}$. Define $L_{\text{eff}}$ and show its limiting values for short and long fibers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distributed Raman amplifier uses a single-mode fiber with $A_{\text{eff}} = 80~\mu\text{m}^2$, $\alpha_p = 0.2$ dB/km, $L = 50$ km, and a pump at 1450 nm with $P_p(0) = 1$ W. If $g_R = 0.7 \times 10^{-13}$ m/W at the operating frequency offset, calculate: (i) effective length, (ii) on-off Raman gain in dB, and (iii) the noise figure assuming quantum-limited spontaneous emission factor $n_{sp} = 1.05$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [See Section 3.1 above for the complete, step-by-step derivation. The expected board valuation allocates:

- 1 mark — Writing the coupled wave equation and undepleted pump assumption.
- 1 mark — Substitution and factoring of $P_s$.
- 1 mark — Integration over $[0, L]$.
- 1 mark — Identification of $L_{\text{eff}} = (1 - e^{-\alpha_p L})/\alpha_p$.
- 1 mark — Final exponential form for $G_R$.
- 1 mark — Limiting cases: $L_{\text{eff}} \to L$ when $\alpha L \ll 1$, and $L_{\text{eff}} \to 1/\alpha$ when $\alpha L \gg 1$.
- 1 mark — Statement that for typical fiber at 1550 nm, $L_{\text{eff}} \approx 20$–25 km.]

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert attenuation to Np/km:**
$$
\alpha_p = 0.2~\text{dB/km} = \frac{0.2}{4.343} = 0.04605~\text{km}^{-1} = 4.605 \times 10^{-5}~\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective length:**
$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha_p L}}{\alpha_p} = \frac{1 - e^{-0.04605 \times 50}}{0.04605}~\text{km}
$$

$$
e^{-2.3026} = 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 - 0.1}{0.04605} = \frac{0.9}{0.04605} = 19.54~\text{km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute on-off gain in linear scale:**
$$
G_R = \exp\!\left(\frac{g_R \, P_p(0) \, L_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{eff}}}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{0.7 \times 10^{-13} \times 1 \times 19.54 \times 10^{3}}{80 \times 10^{-12}}\right)
$$

$$
= \exp\!\left(\frac{0.7 \times 19.54 \times 10^{-10}}{80 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{13.678 \times 10^{-10}}{80 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = \exp(17.097) = 2.66 \times 10^{7}
$$

$$
G_{R,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.66 \times 10^{7}) = 10 \times 7.425 = 74.25~\text{dB}
$$

> **Note:** Such a high gain is non-physical in practice (depleted-pump regime). This indicates the assumption of undepleted pump is violated. The 7-mark solution would highlight this caveat (1 mark) along with the calculation.

**Step 4 — Noise figure (quantum-limited):**
$$
\text{NF} = 2 \, n_{sp} = 2 \times 1.05 = 2.10
$$

$$
\text{NF}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.10) = 3.22~\text{dB}
$$

> **Mark split:** 1 mark each for $L_{\text{eff}}$, $G_R$ linear, $G_R$ in dB, identification of depleted-pump condition, NF formula, NF linear, NF in dB. Total 7.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of distributed and lumped Raman amplifiers. Compare co-, counter-, and bi-directional pumping schemes with neat diagrams. Discuss the noise figure advantages of distributed Raman amplification over EDFA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100 km SMF link operating at 1550 nm has $\alpha = 0.22$ dB/km and $A_{\text{eff}} = 70~\mu\text{m}^2$. It is to be upgraded with a counter-pumping Raman amplifier using $P_p(0) = 800$ mW at the input end of the pump. If $g_R = 1 \times 10^{-13}$ m/W at the chosen offset, find: (i) the on-off Raman gain in dB, (ii) the residual loss after Raman compensation, and (iii) the noise figure in dB (assume $n_{sp} = 1.1$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Theory (7 marks):** [See Section 1.3, 1.4, and 2.5 above. Allocation:

- 1 mark — Distributed vs lumped definitions.
- 2 marks — Co-, counter-, bi-directional pumping diagrams and trade-offs.
- 2 marks — Noise figure comparison (Raman NF ≈ 3 dB vs EDFA NF ≈ 5 dB).
- 2 marks — Wavelength tunability, bandwidth, and OSNR benefits.

Counter-pumping is preferred for high-bit-rate systems (RIN averaging). Bi-directional gives most uniform gain but doubles pump cost and complexity.]

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
$$
\alpha = 0.22~\text{dB/km} = \frac{0.22}{4.343} = 5.066 \times 10^{-5}~\text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length:**
$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 - e^{-\alpha L}}{\alpha} = \frac{1 - e^{-5.066 \times 10^{-2}}}{5.066 \times 10^{-5}}~\text{km}
$$

$$
\alpha L = 0.22 \times 100 / 4.343 = 5.066 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-5.066} = 6.31 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1 - 0.00631}{0.05066} = \frac{0.9937}{0.05066} = 19.62~\text{km} \approx 19.6~\text{km}
$$

**Step 3 — On-off gain (linear):**
$$
G_R = \exp\!\left(\frac{1 \times 10^{-13} \times 0.8 \times 19.62 \times 10^{3}}{70 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{15.696 \times 10^{-10}}{70 \times 10^{-12}}\right) = \exp(22.42)
$$

$$
G_R = 5.32 \times 10^{9} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{R,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(5.32 \times 10^{9}) = 97.26~\text{dB}
$$

> **Caveat (1 mark):** Gain is super-elevated; real system is heavily pump-depleted. The numerical answer reveals a *model breakdown* — proper analysis requires solving the full coupled equations numerically.

**Step 4 — Residual loss:**
$$
\text{Residual loss} = \alpha L - G_{R,\text{dB}} = 22~\text{dB} - 97.26~\text{dB} = -75.26~\text{dB}
$$

> Negative residual loss indicates net gain across the span. (1 mark for concept.)

**Step 5 — Noise figure:**
$$
\text{NF} = 2 \, n_{sp} = 2 \times 1.1 = 2.2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NF}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2.2) = 3.42~\text{dB}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently mix dB/km with Np/km in the $L_{\text{eff}}$ formula. The formula $L_{\text{eff}} = (1 - e^{-\alpha L})/\alpha$ strictly requires $\alpha$ in **Np/m** (or Np/km with matching units for $L$). Always convert dB/km → Np/km by dividing by **4.343** (= $10 \log_{10} e$).
> 2. **Confusing $g_R$ units:** The Raman gain coefficient is in **m/W**, not in dB/km. Mixing units is the #1 source of numerical errors.
> 3. **Neglecting pump depletion:** When $G_R > 20$ dB, the small-signal approximation breaks down. The coupled equations must be solved iteratively or numerically. Examiners often give a "trap" question with $P_p > 500$ mW expecting students to comment on the depletion regime.
> 4. **Stokes shift confusion:** The shift is *negative* in frequency (Stokes is below pump) but corresponds to a *larger* wavelength (Stokes has longer $\lambda$ than pump). Always state both frequency and wavelength values to avoid losing marks.
> 5. **NF formula pitfall:** Writing NF = $n_{sp}$ instead of NF = $2 n_{sp}$ loses 0.5 marks. The factor of 2 arises from the two orthogonal polarization modes in single-mode fiber.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Raman Amplifiers (Module 3, OEECT721)**
>
> - **SRS is a 3rd-order ($\chi^{(3)}$) nonlinear process** — needs high pump power (~ 100s of mW to W).
> - **Stokes shift in silica ≈ 13.2 THz** (≈ 100 nm at 1550 nm).
> - **Peak Raman gain coefficient** of silica: $g_R \approx 1 \times 10^{-13}$ m/W.
> - **On-off gain:** $G_R = \exp(g_R P_p(0) L_{\text{eff}} / A_{\text{eff}})$.
> - **Effective length:** $L_{\text{eff}} = (1 - e^{-\alpha L})/\alpha \to \min(L, 1/\alpha)$.
> - **Threshold pump power:** $P_{\text{th}} \approx 16 A_{\text{eff}} / (g_R L_{\text{eff}}) \approx 0.6$–$1$ W in SMF.
> - **Counter-pumping is preferred** for ≥ 10 Gbps systems (RIN averaging).
> - **Bi-directional pumping** gives the most uniform gain distribution along the span.
> - **Noise figure:** $\text{NF}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 n_{sp}) \to 3$ dB (quantum limit).
> - **Distributed** Raman = transmission fiber is the gain medium; OSNR benefit for long-haul.
> - **Lumped** Raman = external specialty fiber (e.g., DCF or HNLF) for high-gain booster.
> - **Coupled wave equations** must be solved for high-gain, pump-depleted cases.
> - **Bandwidth** of Raman gain in silica ~ 6–8 THz (FWHM), broader than EDFA's C-band.
> - **Numerical rule:** Always use $\alpha$ in Np/m (divide dB/km by 4.343) inside $L_{\text{eff}}$.
> - **Practical pumps:** High-power fiber lasers (Yb-MOPA) at 1420–1480 nm.
> - **Excess noise sources unique to Raman:** Double Rayleigh Backscatter (DRB) and RIN transfer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OTDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OTDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of OTDM

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optical Time Division Multiplexing (OTDM)** is an ultra-high-speed optical multiplexing technique in which multiple lower-bit-rate optical data tributaries are interleaved in the **time domain** at the optical layer to form a single composite high-bit-rate optical data stream. Each tributary is assigned a unique, non-overlapping time slot within a recurring **frame period**, and the bit rate of the aggregated OTDM signal is **N** times the bit rate of each tributary, where **N** is the number of multiplexed channels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *OTDM is a multiplexing scheme that combines several optical signal streams, each at a moderate bit rate, into a single ultra-fast optical stream by allocating distinct time slots to each tributary. Demultiplexing is performed in the optical domain using fast nonlinear optical switches synchronized to an optical clock.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **narrow single-lane bridge** where multiple cars from different cities need to cross. Instead of building many lanes (which would be WDM — Wavelength Division Multiplexing), the traffic controller lets cars from **City A** cross during the 1st second, cars from **City B** during the 2nd second, and so on. Each city still has its own continuous stream of cars, but the bridge carries them in **rotating time slots**. This is exactly how OTDM works — one optical "lane" (wavelength) carries many tributaries, separated by their **arrival time** at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Significance:**
> - OTDM enables **single-channel bit rates exceeding 100 Gbps** and even reaching **640 Gbps – 1.28 Tbps** in laboratory demonstrations.
> - It bypasses the **electronic bottleneck** of conventional ETDM (Electrical TDM) by performing multiplexing in the **optical domain** itself.
> - OTDM systems require a **stable optical clock** (typically a mode-locked laser) and **fast nonlinear optical switches** (e.g., NOLM, TOAD) for demultiplexing.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Tributary bit rate | **2.5 Gbps – 10 Gbps** |
| Aggregated OTDM bit rate | **40 Gbps – 1.28 Tbps** |
| Optical clock pulse width | **1 ps – 10 ps** |
| Multiplexing factor (N) | **4 – 128** |
| Pulse repetition rate | **Same as tributary rate** |
| Switching window (for demux) | **< 10 ps** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of OTDM

The OTDM system architecture follows a **three-stage pipeline** at the transmitter and a symmetric reverse pipeline at the receiver.

### 2.1.1 Transmitter Side — Multiplexer (MUX)

1. **Optical Clock Generation:** A **mode-locked laser (MLL)** generates a stable, ultra-short optical pulse train at a repetition rate equal to the tributary bit rate. This serves as the **optical clock**.
2. **Data Modulation:** Each tributary data stream modulates a separate portion of the optical clock pulses using an external **LiNbO₃ (Lithium Niobate) Mach-Zehnder modulator** or an **Electro-Absorption Modulator (EAM)**.
3. **Bit Interleaving:** Modulated optical pulses from the N tributaries are delayed by precise time intervals using **optical delay lines** of different lengths and combined via **passive optical couplers**. The result is an OTDM frame where pulses from successive channels are placed one after the other in time.

### 2.1.2 Receiver Side — Demultiplexer (DEMUX)

1. **Optical Clock Recovery:** A fast **phase-locked loop (PLL)** synchronizes a local mode-locked laser to the incoming OTDM stream, regenerating an optical clock that is **bit-rate multiplied** appropriately.
2. **Optical Gating / Switching:** A **nonlinear optical switch** (NOLM, TOAD, or SOA-based) opens a **narrow temporal window** synchronized to a specific tributary's time slot, extracting only that channel's pulses.
3. **Baseband Detection:** The extracted tributary is converted to the electrical domain by a **photodetector** and electrically processed.

## 2.2 The OTDM Frame Structure

The aggregated OTDM signal consists of repeating **frames**, each containing **N time slots** (one per tributary). A typical OTDM frame is mathematically represented as:

- **Bit period of tributary:** $T_b = 1 / R_b$
- **Frame period:** $T_f = N \cdot T_b$
- **Time slot per channel:** $\Delta t = T_b$
- **Guard band:** $T_g$ (small time buffer between slots to prevent inter-symbol interference)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Tributary bit period | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive bits of one tributary |
| 2 | OTDM frame period | $T_f = N \cdot T_b$ | seconds | Time between two consecutive frames |
| 3 | Aggregate bit rate | $R_{OTDM} = N \cdot R_b$ | bps | Total bit rate of multiplexed stream |
| 4 | Duty cycle of optical clock | $D = \dfrac{\tau_p}{T_b}$ | dimensionless | $\tau_p$ = optical pulse width |
| 5 | Optical clock bandwidth | $\Delta f = \dfrac{0.44}{\tau_p}$ | Hz | Transform-limited Gaussian pulse |
| 6 | Multiplexing factor | $N = \dfrac{R_{OTDM}}{R_b}$ | dimensionless | Number of tributaries |
| 7 | Effective demux switching window | $T_w \leq \dfrac{T_b}{N}$ | seconds | Must be ≤ one OTDM bit slot |
| 8 | Interleaving delay for k-th channel | $\tau_k = (k-1) \cdot T_b$ | seconds | Optical delay for channel k |
| 9 | Optical SNR (quantum limit) | $\text{OSNR} = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{N_{ASE}}$ | dB | Critical for OTDM amplification |
| 10 | Walk-off parameter (NOLM) | $\Delta\tau_w = \dfrac{L}{v_{g1}} - \dfrac{L}{v_{g2}}$ | seconds | Group velocity mismatch |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of OTDM |
|-------|---------------------|
| **Long-Haul Telecom Backbone** | Ultra-high-capacity trunk lines (100G, 400G, 1T+) |
| **Data Center Interconnects** | High-speed server-to-server optical links |
| **Radar & RF Photonics** | Photonic ADC front-ends using pulsed OTDM sampling |
| **Quantum Communication** | QKD systems using OTDM-based single-photon gating |
| **Photonic Signal Processing** | All-optical logic, regeneration, wavelength conversion |

## 2.5 Comparison: OTDM vs ETDM vs WDM

| Feature | ETDM | WDM | **OTDM** |
|---------|------|-----|----------|
| Domain | Electrical | Wavelength | **Time** |
| Bit rate/channel | ≤ 100 Gbps | 10–400 Gbps | **Up to 1.28 Tbps** |
| Electronics speed | Bottleneck | Required per λ | **Bypassed** |
| Spectral efficiency | Moderate | High | **Very High** |
| Demultiplexer | A/D converter | Optical filter | **Nonlinear optical gate** |
| Synchronization | Clock recovery | Not required | **Optical clock recovery** |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | **High** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: OTDM Frame Period and Bit Rate Relationship

**Statement to prove:** *The aggregate OTDM bit rate equals the tributary bit rate multiplied by the number of multiplexed channels.*

**Given:**
- Number of tributary channels: $N$
- Tributary bit rate: $R_b$ (in bits per second)
- Tributary bit period: $T_b = 1 / R_b$ (in seconds)

**Step-by-step proof:**

We define the **OTDM frame** as a contiguous time interval that contains exactly one bit from **each of the N tributaries**. Since the tributaries are interleaved without overlap, the duration of one frame is the sum of the individual bit durations of all N channels.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= T_{b,1} + T_{b,2} + T_{b,3} + \dots + T_{b,N} \\
&= N \cdot T_b \quad \text{(since all tributaries have equal bit rate } R_b \text{)} \\
&= N \cdot \frac{1}{R_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since one bit per channel is transmitted per frame, the **aggregate bit rate** is the inverse of the frame period:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{OTDM} &= \frac{N}{T_f} = \frac{N}{N \cdot (1/R_b)} \\
&= R_b \cdot N
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{R_{OTDM} = N \cdot R_b}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Interleaving Delay Calculation

**Statement:** *The optical delay required at the transmitter for the k-th channel is $(k-1) \cdot T_b$.*

**Reasoning:** In an OTDM multiplexer, the pulse from **channel 1** travels through a delay line of length $\ell_1$, the pulse from **channel 2** through $\ell_2$, and so on. To place channel $k$'s pulse exactly one bit period $T_b$ after channel $(k-1)$'s pulse, the **extra path length** for channel $k$ must produce an additional time delay of $(k-1) \cdot T_b$ relative to channel 1.

The propagation time in a fiber of refractive index $n$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{n \cdot \ell}{c}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum. Setting $\tau_k = (k-1) \cdot T_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n \cdot (\ell_k - \ell_1)}{c} &= (k-1) \cdot T_b \\
\therefore \Delta \ell_k = \ell_k - \ell_1 &= \frac{(k-1) \cdot c \cdot T_b}{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For $N = 4$ channels, $R_b = 10$ Gbps, $n = 1.5$ (silica fiber):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= \frac{1}{10 \times 10^9} = 100 \text{ ps} \\
\Delta\ell_2 &= \frac{1 \cdot (3 \times 10^8) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12})}{1.5} = 20 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta\ell_3 &= \frac{2 \cdot (3 \times 10^8) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12})}{1.5} = 40 \text{ mm} \\
\Delta\ell_4 &= \frac{3 \cdot (3 \times 10^8) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12})}{1.5} = 60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> These delay lines are **trivially small** (a few cm of fiber), which makes OTDM practical for laboratory implementations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Switching Window of OTDM Demultiplexer

**Statement:** *The optical switching window of the demultiplexer must be ≤ $T_b / N$ to cleanly extract one tributary.*

**Reasoning:** At the receiver, the OTDM frame contains $N$ pulses, each of width $\tau_p$, separated by $T_b$. To extract only channel $k$, the optical gate must be **closed** for all other $(N-1)$ channels and **open** only during the $k$-th time slot.

The maximum allowable window duration to avoid pulling in adjacent bits is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w^{max} = T_b - \tau_p \approx T_b \quad \text{(for very short pulses)}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, a **practical design constraint** uses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w \leq \frac{T_b}{N} = \frac{1}{N \cdot R_b} = \frac{1}{R_{OTDM}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ensures the switch selects only **one bit slot** within the OTDM frame and provides a **guard band** of $(N-1) \cdot T_w$ to suppress crosstalk from adjacent tributaries.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** An OTDM system multiplexes **8 tributaries** each at **10 Gbps**.
**(a)** Calculate the aggregate bit rate.
**(b)** Find the frame period.
**(c)** Determine the demultiplexer switching window.

**Solution:**

**(a) Aggregate bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{OTDM} &= N \cdot R_b = 8 \times 10 \text{ Gbps} = 80 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Frame period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{1}{R_{OTDM}} = \frac{1}{80 \times 10^9} = 12.5 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Switching window:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w &= \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{12.5 \text{ ps}}{8} = 1.5625 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Such ultra-fast windows (< 2 ps) are achievable only with **NOLM** or **SOA-MZI** based demultiplexers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 OTDM Transmitter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CLK[Mode Locked Laser MLL Optical Clock] --> SPLIT[1xN Optical Power Splitter]
    SPLIT --> CH1[Channel 1 LiNbO3 Modulator Data 1]
    SPLIT --> CH2[Channel 2 LiNbO3 Modulator Data 2]
    SPLIT --> CH3[Channel N LiNbO3 Modulator Data N]
    CH1 --> DL1[Optical Delay Line tau1 equals 0]
    CH2 --> DL2[Optical Delay Line tau2 equals Tb]
    CH3 --> DLN[Optical Delay Line tauN equals N-1 times Tb]
    DL1 --> COUP[Passive Optical Coupler Nx1]
    DL2 --> COUP
    DLN --> COUP
    COUP --> OUT[OTDM Aggregate Output 80 Gbps to 1.28 Tbps]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The transmitter is purely **passive in the time-interleaving stage** — all time-domain positioning is done by **fiber delay lines**, which are immune to electronic jitter. This is one of OTDM's biggest engineering advantages over ETDM.

## 4.2 OTDM Receiver — Demultiplexer Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Incoming OTDM Signal N times Rb] --> PREAMP[Erbium Doped Fiber Amplifier EDFA]
    PREAMP --> SPLIT2[1x2 Power Splitter]
    SPLIT2 --> CLKREC[Optical Clock Recovery PLL with MLL]
    SPLIT2 --> NOLM[Nonlinear Optical Loop Mirror NOLM Switch]
    CLKREC --> CTRL[Synchronization Control Signal]
    CTRL --> NOLM
    NOLM --> PD[High Speed Photodetector]
    PD --> DEC[Decision Circuit BER Measurement]
    DEC --> OUT2[Recovered Baseband Tributary Rb]
```

## 4.3 OTDM Bit Interleaving — Pulse Train Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A1["FRAME T_f = N x T_b"]
        direction LR
        B1["Ch1 pulse  t = 0"]
        B2["Ch2 pulse  t = T_b"]
        B3["Ch3 pulse  t = 2T_b"]
        B4["ChN pulse  t = (N-1)T_b"]
    end
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
```

## 4.4 NOLM-Based Demultiplexer — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input OTDM Signal] --> OC[3 dB Optical Coupler]
    OC --> CW[Clockwise Direction Loop Fiber]
    OC --> CCW[Counter Clockwise Direction Loop Fiber]
    CW --> SOA[Semiconductor Optical Amplifier SOA at midpoint]
    CCW --> SOA
    SOA --> OC2[Second 3 dB Coupler at loop end]
    OC2 --> REF[Reflected Back to Source]
    OC2 --> TRANS[Transmitted to Output as Extracted Tributary]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How NOLM Works:** The **SOA** introduces **nonlinear phase shift** only to the clockwise pulse when triggered by a control pulse synchronized to the desired tributary. This phase shift converts the loop from a **reflector** to a **transmitter** at that exact time slot, extracting the chosen channel via XPM-induced switching.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Optical Time Division Multiplexing (OTDM). Mention any two key advantages of OTDM over ETDM.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** OTDM is a multiplexing technique in which multiple lower-bit-rate optical data streams are interleaved in the time domain at the optical layer to form a single ultra-high-bit-rate composite signal. Each tributary occupies a unique time slot within a repeating frame, and demultiplexing is performed using fast optical switches.

**Two advantages over ETDM:**
1. **Bypasses the electronic speed bottleneck:** OTDM operates entirely in the optical domain and can achieve bit rates (e.g., 160 Gbps, 640 Gbps) that are far beyond the reach of conventional electronic circuits used in ETDM.
2. **Single wavelength operation:** OTDM does not require multiple laser sources or wavelength-specific filters; it uses a single optical carrier, simplifying transmitter design and reducing cross-channel interference.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the role of an optical clock in an OTDM system? Name the device commonly used to generate the optical clock.**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Optical Clock:** The optical clock provides a **stable, periodic train of ultra-short optical pulses** that serves as the timing reference for both the transmitter (to synchronize modulation of all tributaries) and the receiver (to drive the demultiplexer switching window). Without precise optical clock synchronization, the demultiplexer cannot extract the correct tributary, and inter-symbol interference would degrade the BER.

**Device:** A **Mode-Locked Laser (MLL)** — typically an Erbium-Doped Fiber Laser (EDFL) or a semiconductor mode-locked laser — is commonly used to generate the optical clock pulse train with pulse widths of **1–10 ps** and repetition rates matching the tributary bit rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain the block diagram of an OTDM transmitter with neat functional schematics. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The OTDM transmitter consists of the following functional blocks:

**1. Optical Clock Source (Mode-Locked Laser):**
A mode-locked Erbium-Doped Fiber Laser (EDFL) generates a stable train of ultra-short optical pulses at a repetition rate $f_{rep} = R_b$, where $R_b$ is the tributary bit rate. Typical pulse width is 1–5 ps.

**2. Optical Power Splitter (1 × N):**
The optical clock pulses are split into **N equal-amplitude copies** using a passive 1 × N optical power splitter, so that each tributary channel receives a synchronized clock stream.

**3. Data Modulators (LiNbO₃ Mach-Zehnder Modulators):**
Each tributary's electrical data stream modulates its corresponding optical clock copy using an external LiNbO₃ modulator, encoding the data onto the optical pulses (typically RZ format).

**4. Optical Delay Lines:**
The modulated pulse trains are passed through **fiber delay lines** of progressively increasing length. The delay for the k-th channel is set to $(k-1) \cdot T_b$, where $T_b = 1 / R_b$. This creates the **time-domain interleaving** that defines the OTDM frame.

**5. Optical Combiner (N × 1 Coupler):**
The time-interleaved pulse streams are combined into a single fiber using a passive N × 1 optical coupler, producing the **aggregated OTDM signal** at bit rate $R_{OTDM} = N \cdot R_b$.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MLL[Mode Locked Laser] --> SP[1xN Splitter]
    SP --> M1[Modulator 1 Data 1]
    SP --> M2[Modulator 2 Data 2]
    SP --> MN[Modulator N Data N]
    M1 --> OD1[Delay 0]
    M2 --> OD2[Delay Tb]
    MN --> ODN[Delay N-1 times Tb]
    OD1 --> COMB[Nx1 Coupler]
    OD2 --> COMB
    ODN --> COMB
    COMB --> OUTX[OTDM Output]
```

**(b) Describe the principle of operation of a Nonlinear Optical Loop Mirror (NOLM) used as an OTDM demultiplexer. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Basic Structure:** A NOLM consists of a fiber loop closed on itself through a **3 dB coupler**. An input optical signal is split into two counter-propagating components: a **clockwise (CW)** pulse and a **counter-clockwise (CCW)** pulse, each carrying half the input power.

**2. Working Principle:**
- In its **passive state** (no control pulse), the loop acts as a perfect mirror. The two counter-propagating pulses experience **identical linear phase shifts** and recombine at the coupler such that the input signal is **reflected back** to the source. No signal emerges at the output port.
- When a **control pulse** is injected into the loop at a precisely chosen point (typically through a Semiconductor Optical Amplifier — SOA — embedded in the loop), it interacts with **only one of the two counter-propagating pulses** via **Cross-Phase Modulation (XPM)**. The control pulse modifies the refractive index of the SOA for the CW pulse, imparting a **nonlinear phase shift** $\Delta\phi_{NL}$ on the CW pulse.
- The CCW pulse remains unaffected. When the two pulses recombine at the 3 dB coupler, the differential phase shift causes **destructive recombination at the reflection port** and **constructive recombination at the transmission port**. The input signal pulse is thus **switched to the output**.

**3. Application in OTDM:** In an OTDM receiver, the NOLM's SOA is driven by a **control optical clock** that is synchronized to the desired tributary's time slot. The NOLM therefore acts as a **temporal gate** that:
- Transmits only the pulses belonging to the chosen tributary.
- Reflects all other tributary pulses.
- Produces the demultiplexed baseband tributary at the output port.

**4. Switching Window:** The switching window $T_w$ is determined by the SOA carrier recovery time (~ 10 ps) and the control pulse width. Modern SOA-NOLM systems achieve $T_w \approx 1$–$5$ ps, sufficient for demultiplexing OTDM signals up to **640 Gbps**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) Derive the relationship between the aggregate OTDM bit rate and the tributary bit rate. A 4-channel OTDM system multiplexes 4 tributaries of 10 Gbps each. Calculate (i) aggregate bit rate, (ii) frame period, and (iii) the demultiplexer switching window. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** [See Section 3.1 above for full derivation]

The OTDM frame contains one bit from each of the N tributaries. Frame period:
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f = N \cdot T_b = \frac{N}{R_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Aggregate bit rate:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{OTDM} = \frac{1}{T_f} = N \cdot R_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (N = 4, $R_b$ = 10 Gbps):**

**(i) Aggregate bit rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{OTDM} = 4 \times 10 = 40 \text{ Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Frame period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f = \frac{1}{40 \times 10^9} = 25 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Switching window:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_w = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{25 \text{ ps}}{4} = 6.25 \text{ ps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Compare OTDM with WDM and ETDM. List the key components required in an OTDM receiver. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | ETDM | WDM | OTDM |
|---------|------|-----|------|
| Multiplexing Domain | Electrical | Wavelength | Time |
| Max bit rate | ~ 100 Gbps | ~ 400 Gbps/λ | **> 1 Tbps** |
| Electronics needed | High-speed | Per-channel | **Bypassed** |
| Number of laser sources | 1 | N | **1** |
| Demultiplexer | A/D converter | Optical filter | **Optical gate (NOLM)** |
| Synchronization | Electrical clock | Not strict | **Optical clock required** |
| Crosstalk source | ISI | XPM/FWM | **Timing jitter** |

**Key Components of OTDM Receiver:**

1. **EDFA Pre-amplifier:** Compensates for transmission and splitting losses before demultiplexing.
2. **Optical Clock Recovery Unit:** A phase-locked loop (PLL) with a voltage-controlled mode-locked laser that synchronizes to the incoming OTDM bit rate and generates a stable local optical clock.
3. **Optical Demultiplexer (NOLM / TOAD / SOA-MZI):** Performs ultrafast time-domain gating to extract one tributary.
4. **High-Speed Photodetector:** Converts the demultiplexed optical tributary to an electrical signal.
5. **Decision Circuit / BER Tester:** Performs threshold detection and bit error rate measurement on the recovered tributary.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. **Confusing OTDM with OCDMA:** OCDMA uses **code-domain** spreading, not time-slot interleaving. Students often mix up the two and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the mode-locked laser:** Almost every OTDM question on the KTU paper requires identification of the optical clock source. Omitting the MLL is a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Wrong demultiplexer window calculation:** Several students use $T_w = T_b$ (tributary period) instead of $T_w = T_f / N = T_b / N$ (OTDM bit slot). This is the **#1 conceptual trap** in OTDM numericals.
> 4. **No discussion of clock recovery:** The receiver's clock recovery is **not optional** — it is the heart of OTDM synchronization. Skipping it loses up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Vague NOLM explanation:** Simply stating "NOLM uses nonlinearity" is not enough. You must explain **XPM-induced phase shift** and the **symmetric/asymmetric loop dynamics** to score full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OTDM = Time-domain interleaving of N optical tributaries** into a single high-bit-rate stream.
- **Aggregate bit rate:** $R_{OTDM} = N \cdot R_b$.
- **Frame period:** $T_f = N \cdot T_b = 1/R_{OTDM}$.
- **Time slot per channel:** $\Delta t = T_b$ (in the aggregate stream).
- **Optical Clock Source:** **Mode-Locked Laser (MLL)** — generates picosecond pulses at $R_b$.
- **Transmitter MUX:** Uses **1×N splitter + N modulators + delay lines + N×1 coupler** for bit interleaving.
- **Receiver DEMUX:** Uses a **Nonlinear Optical Loop Mirror (NOLM)** or **TOAD** synchronized to recovered optical clock.
- **Switching window:** $T_w \leq T_b / N$ — must be sub-picosecond for 640 Gbps OTDM.
- **Key advantages:** Bypasses electronic bottleneck; single wavelength; very high bit rates; compatible with photonic processing.
- **Key disadvantages:** Requires precise optical clock recovery; susceptible to timing jitter; complex receiver; expensive components.
- **Related techniques:** **ETDM** (electrical), **WDM** (wavelength), **OCDMA** (code), **OFDM** (orthogonal frequency).
- **Numerical trap to remember:** The **demultiplexer switching window** uses the **OTDM bit slot** ($T_f/N$), NOT the tributary bit period $T_b$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OFDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** OFDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** is a spectrally efficient, multi-carrier modulation technique that partitions a high-bit-rate data stream into a large number of lower-bit-rate parallel sub-streams, each transmitted on a separate, mutually orthogonal subcarrier. In the context of **optical communication**, OFDM is adapted as **Optical OFDM (O-OFDM)**, where the baseband OFDM signal is used to intensity-modulate or vector-modulate an optical carrier via an external Mach-Zehnder Modulator (MZM) before transmission over the single-mode optical fiber.

The subcarriers are mathematically orthogonal, meaning their spectra overlap in the frequency domain but do not interfere with one another in the demodulation process, provided the subcarrier spacing $\Delta f$ is exactly the inverse of the OFDM symbol period $T_s$. This overlap is what grants OFDM its exceptional **spectral efficiency**, approaching the theoretical Nyquist limit of **1 symbol/s/Hz** per dimension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — OEECT721 / Module 4:**
> OFDM is a *mandatory* advanced modulation topic. Students are expected to derive the orthogonality condition, justify the role of the **Cyclic Prefix (CP)** in combating Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI) caused by **Chromatic Dispersion (CD)** and **Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD)** of the optical fiber, and to discuss the **PAPR** problem.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Superhighway"

Imagine you are a logistics manager who must deliver **1,000,000 boxes** of goods across a country.

- **Single-Carrier (Traditional Modulation):** You use **ONE giant truck** on a narrow, bumpy single-lane road. If the road has potholes (fiber dispersion), the truck gets damaged (ISI). The truck must travel very fast (high symbol rate) to deliver everything in time, so it cannot avoid the bumps.

- **OFDM:** You split the goods into **N smaller boxes** and dispatch **N smaller trucks in parallel**, each on its **own dedicated lane of a wide superhighway**. The trucks travel slower (low symbol rate per subcarrier), so they are not affected by potholes. Because the lanes are mathematically designed to be **orthogonal**, even though the trucks are side-by-side, they never collide, and a receiver can perfectly separate them. To make the system robust, you also add a small **"guard band"** between truck convoys — this is the **Cyclic Prefix (CP)**, which absorbs the "echo" of the previous convoy (the dispersion tail).

In an optical fiber, the "potholes" are caused by **chromatic dispersion** (different wavelengths of light travelling at different speeds) and **PMD** (polarization components travelling at different speeds). OFDM's parallel, slow, CP-protected structure makes it one of the most dispersion-tolerant modulation formats known.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics

- **Subcarrier spacing:** $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ **Hz**
- **Symbol rate per subcarrier:** $\dfrac{R_b}{N \log_2 M}$ **baud**
- **Total spectral occupancy (baseband):** $B_{OFDM} \approx N \cdot \Delta f$ **Hz**
- **Cyclic prefix overhead:** $\eta_{CP} = \dfrac{N_{CP}}{N + N_{CP}} \times 100\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why OFDM in Optical Communications specifically?**
> 1. **Robustness to fiber dispersion:** Each subcarrier has a narrow bandwidth, so dispersion-induced phase rotation can be equalized *subcarrier-by-subcarrier* with a single complex tap.
> 2. **Adaptive bit-loading:** Subcarriers experiencing lower SNR can be assigned lower-order constellations (e.g., QPSK), while high-SNR subcarriers can carry 64-QAM or 256-QAM.
> 3. **Computational efficiency:** IFFT/FFT algorithms (Cooley–Tukey, $O(N \log N)$) make real-time implementation feasible.
> 4. **Dynamic spectrum allocation** for next-generation Elastic Optical Networks (EONs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principles

The complete O-OFDM signal-generation chain can be broken down into the following sequential logical blocks:

1. **Serial-to-Parallel (S/P) Conversion:** The incoming high-speed serial bit stream $b(t)$ is demultiplexed into $N$ parallel low-rate bit streams.

2. **Constellation Mapping (M-QAM / M-PSK):** Each parallel bit stream is grouped into groups of $k = \log_2 M$ bits and mapped to a complex symbol $X_k \in \mathbb{C}$ drawn from a constellation alphabet (e.g., QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM).

3. **Pilot Insertion & Hermitian Symmetry:** A subset of subcarriers is reserved as pilots for channel estimation. Because the OFDM signal is intended to drive an optical intensity modulator (which is real and non-negative), the frequency-domain vector $\mathbf{X} = [X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_{N-1}]$ is forced to satisfy **Hermitian symmetry** ($X_{N-k} = X_k^*$) so that the IFFT output is real-valued.

4. **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT):** The $N$ complex symbols are transformed to the time domain, producing $N$ real-valued time samples $x_n$ that represent one OFDM symbol.
   $$x_n = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

5. **Cyclic Prefix (CP) Insertion:** The last $N_{CP}$ samples of $x_n$ are prepended to the front of the symbol. This converts the *linear* convolution of the channel into a *circular* convolution, which the FFT diagonalizes.

6. **Digital-to-Analog Conversion (DAC):** The discrete samples are converted to a continuous-time analog baseband signal $s(t)$.

7. **Optical Modulation:** $s(t)$ is biased at the null point and applied to a **Mach-Zehnder Modulator (MZM)** that intensity-modulates a continuous-wave (CW) laser.

8. **Fiber Channel:** The optical signal propagates through standard single-mode fiber (SSMF), where it is corrupted by **Attenuation**, **CD**, **PMD**, and **ASE noise** from optical amplifiers (EDFA).

9. **Coherent Detection & ADC:** At the receiver, a local oscillator (LO) laser is mixed with the incoming signal in a $90^\circ$ hybrid. The I and Q components are photodetected, sampled by ADCs, and digitized.

10. **CP Removal, FFT, Channel Equalization, and Demapping:** The CP is stripped, an FFT recovers $\{X_k\}$, a single-tap frequency-domain equalizer (FEQ) corrects per-subcarrier phase and amplitude, and finally symbol demapping recovers the bit stream.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why Hermitian symmetry?** Intensity Modulation / Direct Detection (IM/DD) optical links can only pass a real, non-negative signal. A real IFFT output is achieved by enforcing $X_{N-k} = X_k^*$.
- **Why Cyclic Prefix?** It guarantees that **every delay-spread echo** lands inside the CP and therefore inside the FFT window, preserving orthogonality and eliminating ISI.
- **Why single-tap equalization?** Because the CP turns linear convolution into circular convolution, the discrete fiber channel is diagonalized by the DFT. Each subcarrier is therefore equalized by **one complex multiplication** — no complex time-domain adaptive filters are needed.
- **Why pilot subcarriers?** The fiber channel $H(f)$ is unknown and time-varying. Pilots allow estimation of $H(f)$ at pilot frequencies, which is then interpolated (e.g., via MMSE) across all subcarriers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz |
| 2 | OFDM symbol period | $T_s = N \cdot T_{sample}$ | seconds |
| 3 | Total symbol (with CP) | $T_{total} = T_s + T_{CP}$ | seconds |
| 4 | CP length condition (no ISI) | $N_{CP} \geq \lceil \tau_{max} / T_{sample} \rceil$ | samples |
| 5 | CP overhead | $\eta_{CP} = \dfrac{N_{CP}}{N + N_{CP}}$ | dimensionless fraction |
| 6 | Orthogonality condition | $\int_0^{T_s} \cos(2\pi f_k t) \cos(2\pi f_l t)\, dt = 0,\; k \neq l$ | $f_k - f_l = m / T_s,\; m \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 7 | OFDM baseband signal | $s(t) = \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$ | V (complex envelope) |
| 8 | IFFT synthesis | $x_n = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N}}\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j2\pi k n/N}$ | discrete samples |
| 9 | Spectral efficiency (per dim) | $\eta = \log_2 M$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 10 | Net data rate | $R_b = \dfrac{N \log_2 M}{T_s + T_{CP}}$ | bits/s |
| 11 | PAPR (instantaneous peak / average) | $\text{PAPR} = \dfrac{\max \vert s(t) \vert^2}{E[\vert s(t) \vert^2]}$ | dimensionless, in dB: $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$ |
| 12 | PAPR CCDF (approx) | $P(\text{PAPR} > \gamma) \approx 1 - (1 - e^{-\gamma})^N$ | Rayleigh-like envelope |
| 13 | BER for M-QAM OFDM (approx) | $P_b \approx \dfrac{4}{k}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{M}}\right) Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{3 k \, E_b}{(M-1) N_0}}\right)$ | AWGN, Gray coding |
| 14 | Channel equalizer tap | $\hat{X}_k = Y_k / \hat{H}_k$ | per-subcarrier ZF equalizer |
| 15 | Fiber CD-induced phase rotation | $\phi_k = \pi \, c \, D \, f_k^2 \, L / f_c^2$ | radians, at subcarrier $k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** O-OFDM is the modulation backbone of **400 Gbps / 1 Tbps coherent optical transponders** deployed in long-haul and metro networks by Cisco, Ciena, and Infinera. It is also the air-interface for **4G LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6/6E/7, and DVB-T2**, demonstrating that the same mathematical principle scales from wireless to optical domains.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Orthogonality of Subcarriers

We must prove that two sinusoidal subcarriers at frequencies $f_k$ and $f_l$ with a common symbol period $T_s$ can be perfectly separated at the receiver if and only if their frequency difference is an integer multiple of $1/T_s$.

**Step 1 — Define the inner product.**
For the receiver to separate subcarrier $k$ from subcarrier $l$, the cross-correlation over the symbol period must vanish:

$$\int_0^{T_s} \cos(2\pi f_k t) \, \cos(2\pi f_l t) \, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } k \neq l$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity.**

Using the identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)\right]$, the integral becomes:

$$\int_0^{T_s} \cos(2\pi f_k t)\cos(2\pi f_l t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^{T_s}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_k - f_l)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_k + f_l)t)\right] dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each integral.**

Let $\Delta f = f_k - f_l$ and $\Sigma f = f_k + f_l$. Each term integrates to:

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\sin(2\pi \Delta f \, T_s)}{2\pi \Delta f} + \frac{\sin(2\pi \Sigma f \, T_s)}{2\pi \Sigma f}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orthogonality constraint.**

For the integral to vanish, both numerators must equal zero. Since $\Sigma f \gg 0$ for positive $f_k, f_l$, the second term vanishes only if $\Sigma f \, T_s$ is an integer (which it is, for sufficiently large $\Sigma f$ and $T_s$ aligned to a multiple of $1/\Sigma f$). The first term vanishes when:

$$\sin(2\pi \Delta f \, T_s) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\pi \Delta f \, T_s = m\pi, \; m \in \mathbb{Z}$$

Solving for the minimum positive spacing ($m = 1$):

$$\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

Hence, **the minimum orthogonal subcarrier spacing is the reciprocal of the OFDM symbol period**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — IFFT Synthesis of an OFDM Symbol

**Step 1 — Discretize the continuous OFDM signal.**

Sample $s(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$ at $t = n T_{sample}$ where $T_s = N T_{sample}$ and $f_k = k / T_s$:

$$x_n = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X_k \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the IDFT structure.**

Up to a normalization constant, this is exactly the definition of the Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform of the vector $\mathbf{X} = [X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_{N-1}]^{T}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{x} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \text{IFFT}(\mathbf{X})}$$

**Step 3 — Consequence for the receiver.**

At the receiver, removing the CP and applying the forward FFT recovers the symbols *exactly* in the absence of channel effects:

$$X_k = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x_n \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — PAPR and the CCDF of an OFDM Signal

**Step 1 — Define PAPR.**

For a discrete OFDM symbol $\mathbf{x} = [x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_{N-1}]$ oversampled by a factor $L$:

$$\text{PAPR} = \frac{\max_{0 \leq n < LN} \vert x_n \vert^2}{E[\vert x_n \vert^2]}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the statistical distribution.**

For large $N$, by the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, the IFFT output $x_n$ is approximately a **complex Gaussian process** (zero mean, variance $\sigma^2$). Its magnitude $\vert x_n \vert$ is therefore **Rayleigh distributed**, and the power $\vert x_n \vert^2$ follows a **chi-squared distribution with 2 degrees of freedom** (exponential).

**Step 3 — Compute the CCDF (Complementary Cumulative Distribution Function).**

The probability that a *single* sample has power below a threshold $\gamma$ is:

$$P(\vert x_n \vert^2 \leq \gamma) = 1 - e^{-\gamma/\sigma^2}$$

For an OFDM symbol of $N$ independent samples, the probability that **all samples** are below $\gamma$ is:

$$P(\text{PAPR} \leq \gamma) = \left(1 - e^{-\gamma/\sigma^2}\right)^N$$

Therefore the **CCDF** — the probability that the PAPR *exceeds* $\gamma$ — is:

$$\boxed{P(\text{PAPR} > \gamma) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\gamma}\right)^N}$$

(after normalizing $\sigma^2 = 1$).

**Step 4 — Engineering implication.**

The CCDF grows as $N$ increases, which is why **large $N$ OFDM systems suffer severe PAPR**. The optical MZM is highly *non-linear* in its transfer function, and high PAPR drives the modulator into its non-linear regime, causing **clipping distortion**, **spectral regrowth**, and **subcarrier intermodulation**. This is a Board-favorite topic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mitigation techniques (commonly asked):** Selective Mapping (SLM), Partial Transmit Sequence (PTS), Tone Reservation, Active Constellation Extension (ACE), and **clipping & filtering**.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Single-Tap Frequency Domain Equalization

**Step 1 — Linear convolution through the fiber.**

Let $h(t)$ be the impulse response of the optical channel (CD + PMD). The received baseband signal is:

$$y(t) = s(t) \ast h(t) + w(t)$$

After sampling, the received vector $\mathbf{y}$ is the linear convolution of $\mathbf{x}$ (with CP) and $\mathbf{h}$, plus noise.

**Step 2 — Cyclic prefix converts linear to circular convolution.**

Because the last $N_{CP}$ samples of $\mathbf{x}$ are copied to the front, the resulting *circular* convolution $\tilde{\mathbf{y}}$ satisfies, in the frequency domain:

$$Y_k = H_k \cdot X_k + W_k, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where $H_k = \text{FFT}\{h\}_k$ is the channel frequency response at subcarrier $k$.

**Step 3 — Zero-Forcing equalizer.**

Multiply both sides by $1 / H_k$:

$$\hat{X}_k = \frac{Y_k}{H_k} = X_k + \frac{W_k}{H_k}$$

**Step 4 — Channel estimation using pilots.**

$H_k$ is unknown; estimate it at pilot subcarriers $k \in \mathcal{P}$ using:

$$\hat{H}_k = \frac{Y_k}{X_k^{\text{pilot}}}, \quad k \in \mathcal{P}$$

Then interpolate $\hat{H}_k$ across all data subcarriers via linear, spline, or MMSE interpolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for O-OFDM:** CD in standard single-mode fiber at 1550 nm is $\sim 17$ ps/(nm·km). A 1000 km link spreads a 10 Gbps NRZ pulse by $>170$ ps, catastrophically destroying single-carrier detection. With OFDM, that same CD is *absorbed* by the CP and equalized by 1000s of single-tap filters — no time-domain equalizer is needed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an O-OFDM Transceiver

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete transmitter, optical channel, and receiver chains, including all functional signal-processing blocks and the points at which analog-to-digital and optical-domain conversions occur.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Serial Bit Stream] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C[Constellation Mapper M-QAM or M-PSK]
    C --> D[Hermitian Symmetry and Pilot Insertion]
    D --> E[IFFT N-point Inverse FFT]
    E --> F[Cyclic Prefix Insertion]
    F --> G[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    G --> H[Mach-Zehnder Modulator MZM biased at null]
    H --> I[Continuous Wave Laser at 1550 nm]
    I --> J[Optical Fiber Channel SSMF CD PMD ASE noise]
    J --> K[Optical Amplifier EDFA]
    K --> L[Coherent Receiver 90 degree Hybrid with Local Oscillator]
    L --> M[Balanced Photodetectors I and Q]
    M --> N[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    N --> O[Cyclic Prefix Removal]
    O --> P[FFT N-point Forward FFT]
    P --> Q[Channel Estimator using Pilots MMSE Interpolation]
    Q --> R[Single-Tap Frequency Domain Equalizer]
    R --> S[Constellation Demapper M-QAM or M-PSK]
    S --> T[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    T --> U[Recovered Bit Stream]
```

## 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

- **Hermitian Symmetry Insertion:** Forces the IFFT output to be real-valued, which is required for IM/DD optical links and also for typical DAC topologies.
- **IFFT N-point:** Computes $N$ orthogonal time-domain samples from $N$ frequency-domain symbols in $O(N \log N)$ complexity.
- **Cyclic Prefix Insertion:** Copies the last $N_{CP}$ samples to the front, ensuring circular convolution.
- **MZM biased at null:** Linearizes the optical modulator so the output optical field is proportional to $s(t)$ without second-order distortion.
- **90° Hybrid with LO:** Recovers both I and Q components coherently, preserving the complex amplitude and phase of the optical field.
- **Single-Tap FEQ:** One complex multiplication per subcarrier — dramatically simpler than a time-domain adaptive filter with hundreds of taps.
- **MMSE Interpolation:** Uses pilot estimates $\hat{H}_k$ to reconstruct $H_k$ for data subcarriers, balancing bias and variance for optimal BER.

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Spectral View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Subcarrier 0 at f0] --> SP[Spectral Overlap Allowed]
    F2[Subcarrier 1 at f0 plus df] --> SP
    F3[Subcarrier 2 at f0 plus 2df] --> SP
    F4[Subcarrier N minus 1 at f0 plus N minus 1 df] --> SP
    SP --> R[Receiver Samples at Symbol Rate: Orthogonality Restores Symbols]
```

**Key Insight:** The sinc-shaped spectra of OFDM subcarriers **overlap at their -3 dB points** (this is mathematically proven by the orthogonality condition), yet a receiver integrating over $T_s = 1/\Delta f$ collects the *peak* of the desired subcarrier and the *zeros* of all others.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `F1`, `SP`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, in compliance with the v10 rendering safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define OFDM. Why is it called "orthogonal" frequency division multiplexing?**

**Model Answer:**

**OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** is a multi-carrier modulation technique in which a high-data-rate stream is divided into $N$ lower-rate parallel sub-streams, each modulating a subcarrier whose frequency is an integer multiple of $1/T_s$, where $T_s$ is the OFDM symbol period.

It is called *orthogonal* because the inner product of any two subcarriers over one symbol period is mathematically zero:

$$\int_0^{T_s} e^{j 2\pi f_k t} \, e^{-j 2\pi f_l t} \, dt = \begin{cases} T_s, & k = l \\ 0, & k \neq l \end{cases}$$

This allows the subcarriers' spectra to **overlap in frequency** without mutual interference, maximizing spectral efficiency. *(3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the role of the Cyclic Prefix (CP) in an OFDM system? State the condition for choosing the CP length.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Cyclic Prefix (CP)** is a copy of the last $N_{CP}$ samples of an OFDM symbol, prepended to the front of that symbol before transmission.

**Roles:**
1. **Combats Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI):** The CP acts as a guard interval, absorbing the *delay-spread tail* of the channel impulse response (caused by fiber chromatic dispersion and PMD) so that successive symbols do not overlap.
2. **Converts linear convolution into circular convolution:** This diagonalizes the channel in the frequency domain, enabling **single-tap equalization** at the receiver.

**Condition for CP length:**

$$N_{CP} \geq \left\lceil \frac{\tau_{max}}{T_{sample}} \right\rceil$$

where $\tau_{max}$ is the maximum delay spread of the optical channel and $T_{sample}$ is the DAC sampling period. *(3 marks)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A.** [14 Marks] *(Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Optical OFDM (O-OFDM) transmitter. Discuss why **Hermitian symmetry** is imposed on the input to the IFFT block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **orthogonality condition** for OFDM subcarriers, showing that the minimum subcarrier spacing is $\Delta f = 1/T_s$. Briefly explain how orthogonality permits spectral overlap without inter-carrier interference. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) O-OFDM Transmitter — Block Diagram and Working**

The transmitter performs the following sequence of operations:

1. **Serial-to-Parallel (S/P):** The incoming serial bit stream is demultiplexed into $N$ parallel lower-rate streams.
2. **Constellation Mapping:** Each parallel stream is mapped onto a complex symbol from an M-QAM or M-PSK alphabet, producing $N$ frequency-domain symbols $\{X_k\}$.
3. **Pilot Insertion:** A subset of subcarriers (e.g., every 8th) is replaced with known pilot symbols to enable channel estimation.
4. **Hermitian Symmetry:** The vector is constrained such that $X_{N-k} = X_k^*$, with $X_0$ and $X_{N/2}$ (if $N$ even) forced real. This ensures the IFFT output is **strictly real-valued**, which is mandatory for driving a single-ended MZM.
5. **IFFT:** An $N$-point IFFT transforms the frequency-domain symbols into the time-domain OFDM waveform $x_n$.
6. **Cyclic Prefix Insertion:** The last $N_{CP}$ samples are copied to the front.
7. **DAC and Anti-Alias Filter:** Generates the analog baseband signal $s(t)$.
8. **MZM Modulation:** $s(t)$ drives a Mach-Zehnder modulator biased at its null point, intensity-modulating a CW laser to produce the optical OFDM signal $E_{out}(t) \propto s(t) \cos(\omega_c t)$.

**Justification for Hermitian Symmetry:** A real-valued time-domain signal has a Fourier transform that is **Hermitian symmetric** (the negative-frequency components are the complex conjugates of the positive-frequency components). Therefore, to obtain a *real* IFFT output $x_n$ (which is required because the MZM can only pass non-negative real envelopes in IM/DD, and because real signals simplify DAC/ADC design), the input frequency-domain vector must obey $X_{N-k} = X_k^*$.

*(7 marks: 4 for block description, 2 for MZM, 1 for Hermitian symmetry justification)*

**(b) Derivation of the Orthogonality Condition**

**Step 1 — Inner product definition.** Two subcarriers $e^{j 2\pi f_k t}$ and $e^{j 2\pi f_l t}$ are orthogonal over the interval $[0, T_s]$ if:

$$\int_0^{T_s} e^{j 2\pi f_k t} \left(e^{j 2\pi f_l t}\right)^* dt = \int_0^{T_s} e^{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l) t} \, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } k \neq l$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.** For $f_k \neq f_l$:

$$\int_0^{T_s} e^{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l) t} \, dt = \left. \frac{e^{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l) t}}{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l)} \right|_0^{T_s} = \frac{e^{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l) T_s} - 1}{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l)}$$

**Step 3 — Set to zero.** The numerator vanishes when $e^{j 2\pi (f_k - f_l) T_s} = 1$, i.e., when:

$$2\pi (f_k - f_l) T_s = 2\pi m, \; m \in \mathbb{Z} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_k - f_l = \frac{m}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum spacing.** The minimum positive integer $m = 1$ yields the minimum orthogonal spacing:

$$\boxed{\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_s}}$$

**Spectral Overlap Explanation:** Although the sinc-shaped spectrum of each subcarrier has its first null at $\pm 1/T_s$ from its center, the *peaks* of the spectra coincide with the *zeros* of the neighbouring subcarriers. Hence, the receiver, which integrates the received signal over $T_s = 1/\Delta f$, samples each subcarrier at its peak and all others at their zeros — resulting in **zero inter-carrier interference (ICI)** despite the spectral overlap.

*(7 marks: 2 for integral setup, 2 for evaluation, 1 for spacing result, 2 for ICI explanation)*

---

### **Question B.** [14 Marks] *(Choice 2 — Internal Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **PAPR problem** in OFDM systems. Derive an expression for the **CCDF of the PAPR** assuming a Rayleigh-distributed envelope, and discuss two techniques to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a block diagram, describe the operation of an **O-OFDM coherent receiver**. Show that the **Cyclic Prefix** enables **single-tap frequency-domain equalization** to compensate for chromatic dispersion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PAPR Problem and CCDF Derivation**

**PAPR Definition:**

$$\text{PAPR} = \frac{\max_{0 \leq t \leq T_s} \vert s(t) \vert^2}{E[\vert s(t) \vert^2]}$$

**The Problem:** In OFDM, $N$ independently modulated subcarriers add coherently in the time domain. When their phases align constructively, the instantaneous peak power can be up to $N$ times the average power. This large dynamic range forces the optical modulator (MZM) and DAC/ADC to operate with stringent linearity, otherwise the system suffers:

- **Clipping distortion** (DAC saturates → in-band noise + spectral regrowth).
- **Sub-carrier intermodulation** in the non-linear MZM transfer function.
- **Reduced power efficiency** of the RF driver amplifier.

**CCDF Derivation (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Central Limit Theorem.** For large $N$, the IFFT output $x_n$ is approximately a **zero-mean complex Gaussian** random variable with variance $\sigma^2$.

**Step 2 — Magnitude distribution.** The magnitude $\vert x_n \vert$ is therefore Rayleigh-distributed with PDF:

$$f_{\vert x \vert}(r) = \frac{2r}{\sigma^2} e^{-r^2/\sigma^2}, \quad r \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Power distribution.** The power $z = \vert x_n \vert^2$ is exponentially distributed:

$$P(z \leq \gamma) = 1 - e^{-\gamma/\sigma^2}$$

**Step 4 — CCDF over $N$ samples.** The probability that *all* $N$ samples of a single OFDM symbol lie below threshold $\gamma$ is $\left(1 - e^{-\gamma/\sigma^2}\right)^N$. Therefore, the probability that the PAPR *exceeds* $\gamma$ is:

$$\boxed{P(\text{PAPR} > \gamma) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\gamma}\right)^N} \quad \text{(with } \sigma^2 = 1\text{)}$$

**Mitigation Techniques (any two):**

1. **Clipping and Filtering:** Hard-limit the peak amplitude to a predefined threshold, then re-filter to remove out-of-band regrowth. Simple but introduces in-band distortion.
