This is the **primary disadvantage** of FBT for multi-wavelength PON systems (e.g., GPON which uses 1490 nm down + 1310 nm up). ### Solution to Part (b): **Comparison Table: FBT vs PLC Splitters for 1×16 Configuration** | Performance Parameter | **FBT Splitter (1×16)** | **PLC Splitter (1×16)** | |------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | **Excess Loss** | 0.8 – 2.5 dB (high, due to cascading multiple 1×2 units) | 0.5 – 1.2 dB | | **Uniformity (port-to-port)** | ±1.5 – 2.0 dB (poor) | < 0.8 dB (excellent) | | **Wavelength Dependence** | High (designed for a specific λ) | Low (broadband 1260–1650 nm) | | **Cost (1×16)** | Higher (cascading 4 stages of 1×2 units = expensive labor) | **Lower** (single chip) | | **Size** | Bulky (cascaded packaging) | Compact (single chip, ribbon fiber output) | | **Reliability** | Good (mature technology) | Excellent (no moving parts) | | **Temperature Stability** | Moderate | Excellent | | **Polarization Dependent Loss** | < 0.1 dB | < 0.3 dB | | **Return Loss** | > 55 dB | > 55 dB | **Preferred Choice for GPON FTTH: PLC Splitter** The **PLC splitter is strongly preferred** for GPON FTTH deployments for the following reasons: 1. **Wavelength Insensitivity:** GPON uses three wavelengths — 1310 nm (upstream), 1490 nm (downstream), and 1550 nm (video overlay in some networks). A broadband PLC splitter handles all three with negligible performance variation, whereas an FBT splitter would require three separate optimized devices. 2. **Superior Uniformity:** With a 1×16 PLC splitter, uniformity is typically < 0.8 dB. This ensures **fair service to all 16 ONUs** without the strongest ONU hogging the budget. FBT splitters cascade four 1×2 units, each introducing its own excess loss asymmetry, leading to ±1.5 dB or worse. 3. **Lower Cost at Scale:** Although the **initial tooling** (photolithographic mask) is expensive, the **per-unit cost** for a 1×16 PLC chip is significantly lower than cascading 4 FBT 1×2 units with 4 fusion splices + 4 fiber packages. 4. **Compactness:** A single PLC chip can be packaged in a miniature module with a fiber ribbon output, ideal for space-constrained street cabinets and splice closures. **Verdict:** PLC splitters are the **industry standard** for FTTH/PON deployments with N ≥ 4. FBT splitters remain relevant only for **1×2 and 1×4** configurations in low-cost or wavelength-specific applications. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing "excess loss" with "insertion loss"** in numerical problems. Always write the relation $L_{ins} = L_{split} + L_{excess}$ explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting the minus sign in dB subtraction.** When computing $L_{excess} = P_{in} - \sum P_{out}$, use absolute dBm values and remember that dB loss = positive number. > 3. **Skipping the derivation** in Part (a) of 7-mark questions. KTU examiners allocate ~3–4 marks specifically to the **derivation steps**, not just the final formula. Always show the substitution of $P_{out} = P_{in}/N$ explicitly. > 4. **Not drawing the block diagram** for PLC splitter questions. A neatly labeled cascaded Y-junction diagram is **mandatory** and carries 2 marks. > 5. **Mixing up tree and star coupler definitions** in Part (a) of differentiation questions. Memorize: tree = 1 input → N outputs; star = M inputs → N outputs with equal distribution of summed power. --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ An **optical splitter** is a passive device that divides (or combines) optical signals among multiple fibers. It is the heart of **PON/FTTH** networks. - ✅ **Splitting Loss** is theoretical minimum: $L_{split} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB. For N = 32, this is **15.05 dB**. - ✅ **Insertion Loss** = Splitting Loss + Excess Loss. Always greater than splitting loss alone. - ✅ **Excess Loss** arises from scattering, absorption, and radiation at the coupling region. - ✅ **Two main technologies:** **FBT** (fused biconical tapered) and **PLC** (planar lightwave circuit). PLC dominates for N ≥ 4 in modern deployments. - ✅ **Tree couplers** (1×N) are used for downstream PON splitting. **Star couplers** (M×N) are used in optical cross-connects and broadcast networks. - ✅ **Uniformity** is the worst-case power variation across N outputs. PLC has excellent uniformity; FBT does not (especially for large N). - ✅ **Wavelength dependence** is the Achilles' heel of FBT; PLC is broadband (1260–1650 nm) — crucial for triple-wavelength GPON. - ✅ **Reciprocity** of passive splitters means the same device works as a combiner in the upstream direction (e.g., 1310 nm upstream from ONUs to OLT). - ✅ **Coupled-mode theory** governs FBT operation: $P_2(z) = P_1(0) \sin^2(\kappa z)$. The coupling coefficient κ depends on core separation, Δn, and wavelength. - ✅ For KTU 2024 numerical problems, always quote the **final answer with units** (dB) and a **physical interpretation** (suitability for the application). - ✅ **Class B+ GPON** budget is typically **28 dB** (OLT to ONU). Always verify that the computed total loss stays within this limit with adequate margin.

topics.title: Add/Drop Multiplexers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Add/Drop Multiplexers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM)** is a wavelength-selective photonic device deployed at intermediate nodes of a Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) optical network. Its primary function is to **drop** one or more selected optical channels (wavelengths) from a multi-wavelength composite signal traveling through the network, while simultaneously **adding** one or more new channels at the same nominal wavelength(s) into the outgoing fiber — all without the need for costly **Optical-Electrical-Optical (O-E-O)** conversion.

The OADM is a critical building block of **wavelength-routing networks** and is often considered the photonic analog of the **SONET/SDH Add-Drop Multiplexer (ADM)** used in classical digital transport networks. However, it operates entirely in the **optical domain**, preserving the **transparency** of the network to bit-rate, protocol, and modulation format.

### Formal Sub-Definitions

| Term | Academic Definition |
|------|---------------------|
| **Drop Function** | Extraction of one or more specific wavelength channels ($\lambda_d$) from the incoming WDM composite signal, routing them to a **local client** (e.g., a SONET ADM, IP router, or DWDM transponder). |
| **Add Function** | Insertion of one or more new local channels ($\lambda_a$) at a compatible wavelength into the outgoing WDM composite signal. |
| **Express-Through (Passthrough)** | Channels not selected for add/drop pass through the node optically unchanged. |
| **Wavelength Reuse** | The dropped wavelength $\lambda_d$ can be reused immediately at the same node for the add function, provided coherent interference is avoided. |
| **Optical Pass-Through (OPT) Band** | A spectral guard band in ROADMs that allows smooth channel migration between ports. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Toll Plaza" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a multi-lane highway where each lane carries cars of a unique color (Red, Blue, Green, Yellow). The highway represents the optical fiber, and each colored car represents an optical channel at a specific wavelength.**

**Consider a Toll Plaza (the OADM Node) in the middle of the highway:**

- **Blue cars** are instructed to **exit the highway** at this toll plaza (the **DROP** function — these channels are dropped to a local client).
- **New green cars** are inserted onto the highway at this toll plaza (the **ADD** function — a new local channel is added).
- **Red and Yellow cars** simply continue on the highway without stopping (the **EXPRESS/THROUGH** function).
- The toll plaza does **not** convert the cars into passengers and back into cars (no **O-E-O** conversion). The cars remain cars throughout — preserving their **transparency**.

This toll plaza analogy beautifully captures the essence of an OADM: a **selective, in-line, optically transparent node** that surgically manipulates specific wavelength channels while letting the rest flow through undisturbed.

## 1.3 Why OADMs Matter — The KTU Engineering Perspective

In long-haul WDM networks, terminating every channel at every node to perform add/drop (as was done in legacy O-E-O regenerators) is:

- **Prohibitively expensive** (each client requires a dedicated transponder pair).
- **Power-hungry**.
- **Bandwidth-limiting** (clients are locked to specific bit-rates).
- **Non-scalable** (adding a new wavelength requires new hardware at every intermediate node).

The OADM solves this by providing **wavelength-level granularity** with **bit-rate transparency** — enabling a scalable, future-proof, and cost-effective network architecture.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry-Recognized)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are the **canonical performance metrics** used by KTU examiners, ITU-T (G.672 / G.694.1), IEEE, and industry vendors (Ciena, Cisco, Nokia, Fujitsu) when evaluating OADM performance:

- **Insertion Loss (IL)**: typically **2.5 dB to 5 dB** per node.
- **Adjacent Channel Crosstalk**: **≤ −25 dB** (must be highly suppressed).
- **Non-Adjacent Channel Crosstalk**: **≤ −40 dB**.
- **Polarization Dependent Loss (PDL)**: **≤ 0.3 dB**.
- **Return Loss**: **≥ 40 dB**.
- **Channel Spacing**: **50 GHz / 100 GHz / 200 GHz** (ITU-T grid).
- **Number of Add/Drop Channels**: typically **1 to 96+** channels.
- **Switching Time** (for ROADMs): **< 50 ms** (latest MEMS-based WSS).
- **Optical Bandwidth per Channel**: **± 0.1 nm** (for 50 GHz spacing).

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes Addressed

| CO Code | Course Outcome | How This Topic Maps |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic OADM

The OADM, regardless of its physical implementation, performs **four fundamental operations** on the WDM signal:

1. **Demultiplexing** — separation of the incoming composite WDM signal into its constituent wavelength channels.
2. **Drop Switching** — routing of selected dropped channels to the local drop port.
3. **Add Multiplexing** — recombination of the locally added channels with the express channels.
4. **Express Switching** — passage of unselected channels to the output port.

A **generic functional block diagram** in logical terms:

```
[Input WDM Fiber] → [1×N WDM Demultiplexer] → {Channel Selector Matrix}
                                                          ↓
                                            [Drop Ports to Local Clients]
                                                          ↑
                                            [Add Ports from Local Clients]
                                                          ↓
[N×1 WDM Multiplexer] → [Output WDM Fiber]
```

The channel selector matrix is the **heart** of the OADM and is realized using different physical technologies (which we analyze in §2.3).

## 2.2 Two Principal Classes of OADMs

### 2.2.1 Fixed-Wavelength OADM (FOADM)

> [!NOTE]
> A **Fixed OADM** is hard-wired to drop and add a **predetermined set of wavelengths** at the time of network deployment. The add/drop wavelengths **cannot be remotely reconfigured** without physical intervention and manual rewiring.

**Characteristics:**
- Uses **passive components**: thin-film filters (TFF), fiber Bragg gratings (FBG), or arrayed waveguide gratings (AWG).
- **No electrical power** required at the node (fully passive).
- **Very low cost** (suited for access networks and metro edge).
- **Zero latency** for add/drop decisions.
- **Limited scalability** — adding new wavelengths requires new hardware.

### 2.2.2 Reconfigurable OADM (ROADM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Reconfigurable OADM (ROADM)** allows the network operator to **dynamically select** which wavelengths to add and drop at a node via **software control**, without any manual intervention or service interruption. ROADMs are the cornerstone of modern **software-defined optical networks (SDON)** and **elastic optical networks (EON)**.

**Characteristics:**
- Uses **active optical switches**: MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems), liquid crystal, or beam-steering.
- **Remote reconfigurability** via a **Network Management System (NMS)** or **SDN controller** (e.g., OpenDaylight, ONOS).
- Supports **wavelength-on-demand** service provisioning.
- **Higher cost** than FOADM but offers massive OPEX savings.
- **Architecture Generations**:
  - **1st Gen (Wavelength Selective Switch, WSS) ROADM** — 2-degree.
  - **2nd Gen (CDC ROADM)** — Colorless, Directionless, Contentionless.
  - **3rd Gen (CDC-F ROADM)** — adds Flexible-grid support (ITU-T G.694.1).

## 2.3 Physical Implementation Technologies

| Technology | Operating Principle | Typical Use Case | Key Advantage |
|------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Thin-Film Filter (TFF)** | Interference between dielectric layers; reflects one $\lambda$, transmits others. | Low-channel-count FOADMs (1–8 ch). | Lowest cost, fully passive. |
| **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) + Circulator** | FBG reflects target $\lambda$; circulator routes reflected light to drop port. | Channel-specific add/drop with sharp filtering. | Very narrow bandwidth, very low crosstalk. |
| **Arrayed Waveguide Grating (AWG)** | Diffraction through phase-controlled waveguide array. | High-channel-count OADM backplane. | Integrable, ITU-grid aligned. |
| **Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS)** | MEMS or LC steering of each wavelength beam to any output port. | Modern ROADM core. | Arbitrary wavelength-to-port mapping. |
| **Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF)** | Acoustic wave tunes the diffraction of a target wavelength. | Fast-tuning research OADMs. | Sub-microsecond tuning. |
| **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI)** | Two-coupler interferometer with phase-controlled arms. | Low-channel dual-wavelength add/drop. | Simple fabrication, integrable. |

## 2.4 The Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS) — Heart of the Modern ROADM

> [!NOTE]
> The **WSS** is a 1×N or N×M optical switch that can independently route each incoming wavelength to any of its N output ports. It is the **single most important photonic component** in any modern ROADM.

**Internal Architecture (Logical):**
1. **Input port** — receives the composite WDM signal.
2. **Demultiplexer** (e.g., free-space diffraction grating) — spatially separates wavelengths.
3. **Beam-steering array** (MEMS mirror array, LC phase array, or digital micromirror) — deflects each wavelength's beam toward the desired output port.
4. **Output ports (N)** — recombine the steered beams via a focusing lens.
5. **Control electronics** — drives the beam-steering array based on NMS commands.

**Key Specifications of State-of-the-Art WSS (2024):**
- **Port count**: 1×4, 1×8, 1×12, 1×16, 1×20, 1×32 (Ciena, Lumentum, Coherent).
- **Channel spacing**: 50 GHz, 100 GHz, or **flexible-grid** (6.25 GHz granularity).
- **Switching time**: **< 50 ms** (latest LCOS-based WSS).
- **Insertion loss**: **< 6 dB** (typical for 1×9 WSS).
- **Crosstalk**: **< −40 dB** (adjacent), **< −50 dB** (non-adjacent).
- **Optical bandwidth**: **C-band (1530–1565 nm)** standard; extended **L-band (1565–1625 nm)** available.

## 2.5 The Three Degrees of ROADM Architectural Evolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the evolution of ROADM architecture. Memorize the three generations:

### Generation 1 — Colorless ROADM
- **"Colorless"**: Any add/drop port can be tuned to **any wavelength** (no fixed color per port).
- Eliminates the need to physically patch transponders to specific ports.
- Implemented using a **WSS on the add/drop side**.

### Generation 2 — Colorless + Directionless (CD) ROADM
- **"Directionless"**: Any transponder can be connected to **any degree (direction)** of the node (East, West, North, South).
- Requires **M×N WSS** or a combination of splitters and WSS.

### Generation 3 — Colorless + Directionless + Contentionless (CDC) ROADM
- **"Contentionless"**: Two transponders can be assigned the **same wavelength** simultaneously without conflict, even if they share the same WSS.
- Implemented using **multi-cast switches (MCS)** or **CDCF WSS**.
- **CDC-F** (CDC + Flex-grid) is the current state-of-the-art in 2024.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **all key formulas** required for KTU 2024 numerical and analytical questions on OADMs. Pay special attention to the **crosstalk formulas** — they are examiner favorites.

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{IL}_{\text{node}} = \text{IL}_{\text{demux}} + \text{IL}_{\text{mux}} + \text{IL}_{\text{switch}}$ | **Node Insertion Loss**: sum of demux, mux, and switching matrix losses. | dB |
| 2 | $P_{\text{out},i} = P_{\text{in},i} - \text{IL}_{\text{node}}$ | **Output channel power** for the $i$-th express channel. | dBm |
| 3 | $X_{\text{adj}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{leak}}}{P_{\text{sig}}}\right)$ | **Adjacent channel crosstalk ratio** ($P_{\text{leak}}$ = leaked adjacent power, $P_{\text{sig}}$ = desired signal power). | dB |
| 4 | $X_{\text{non-adj}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum_{k \neq i, k \neq i\pm 1} P_k}{P_i}\right)$ | **Non-adjacent channel crosstalk** (sum over all non-adjacent leakages). | dB |
| 5 | $X_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum_{k \neq i} P_k}{P_i}\right)$ | **Total accumulated crosstalk** at channel $i$. | dB |
| 6 | $\text{OSNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{OSNR}_{\text{in}} - \text{IL}_{\text{node}}$ | **OSNR penalty** in dB (assuming noiseless passive OADM). | dB |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{eff}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{-\text{SNR}_{\text{sig}}/10} + 10^{X_{\text{total}}/10}\right)$ | **Effective SNR** including signal-crosstalk penalty. | dB |
| 8 | $\text{BER}_{\text{pen}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{eff}}}{2\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | **BER with crosstalk penalty** (Gaussian approximation, valid for high SNR). | dimensionless |
| 9 | $N_{\text{casc}} = \left\lfloor\dfrac{P_{\text{tx}} - P_{\text{rx,min}}}{\text{IL}_{\text{node}}}\right\rfloor$ | **Maximum cascaded OADMs** in a power-limited link. | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta f_{\text{FSR}} = \dfrac{c}{n_{\text{eff}} L}$ | **Free Spectral Range** of an FBG or AWG (for periodic filter design). | Hz |
| 11 | $\lambda_{\text{B}} = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$ | **Bragg wavelength** of an FBG ($\Lambda$ = grating period). | nm |
| 12 | $P_{\text{add, max}} = P_{\text{sat, EDFA}} - 3$ | **Maximum safe add power** to avoid nonlinear effects (rule of thumb). | dBm |
| 13 | $T_{\text{ROADM}} = N_{\lambda} \times t_{\text{port}}$ | **ROADM switching time** ($N_\lambda$ = number of wavelengths, $t_{\text{port}}$ = per-port switching time). | s |
| 14 | $B_{\text{opt,total}} = N_{\lambda} \times R_{\text{bit}} \times M$ | **Total optical capacity** of the WDM link ($M$ = bits per symbol for QAM). | bps |

> **Note:** All absolute-value or magnitude expressions (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) in the table above are written using LaTeX `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OADM is **not merely an academic curiosity** — it is the **operational backbone** of:
- **Long-haul DWDM transport** (e.g., Reliance Jio, Bharti Airtel, AT&T, Verizon backbones).
- **Metro aggregation rings** (e.g., Ciena 6500, Cisco NCS 2000 platforms).
- **Submarine cable systems** (e.g., MAREA, Dunant cable landing stations).
- **Data Center Interconnect (DCI)** (e.g., Google, Microsoft, Amazon hyperscaler networks).
- **5G X-haul (Fronthaul/Midhaul/Backhaul)** transport.

A single ROADM node in a Tier-1 carrier network can carry **terabits per second** of traffic across **80–192 wavelength channels**, each operating at **100 Gbps, 200 Gbps, 400 Gbps, or 800 Gbps** per the latest coherent optics standards (OIF 400ZR, 800G ZR+).

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Crosstalk at a Cascaded OADM Chain

> [!NOTE]
> One of the **most frequently asked** KTU numerical problems involves computing the **cumulative crosstalk** experienced by a signal traversing multiple cascaded OADM nodes. We derive this rigorously below.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a wavelength $\lambda_i$ that is **expressed through** $N$ cascaded OADM nodes before reaching its destination. At each node, **all other channels** generate a small leakage power into $\lambda_i$'s detection window. We assume:

- All $N-1$ interfering channels at each node have equal power $P_{\text{int}}$.
- The leaked fraction from each interferer is characterized by a **crosstalk coefficient** $\epsilon$ (linear, not dB).
- The signal power is $P_{\text{sig}}$.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the linear crosstalk coefficient.**

The adjacent-channel crosstalk in dB is $X_{\text{adj}}$ (typically $-25$ dB). The linear crosstalk coefficient is:

$$
\epsilon = 10^{\,X_{\text{adj}}/10}
$$

For $X_{\text{adj}} = -25$ dB:

$$
\epsilon = 10^{-25/10} = 10^{-2.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 2 — Single-node crosstalk power.**

At a single node, the leaked power from **one adjacent interferer** is:

$$
P_{\text{leak, single}} = \epsilon \cdot P_{\text{int}}
$$

If there are $M$ adjacent interferers contributing (in a 50 GHz grid, typically $M = 2$, one on each side), and an additional $K$ non-adjacent interferers with a smaller coefficient $\epsilon'$:

$$
P_{\text{crosstalk, single node}} = M \cdot \epsilon \cdot P_{\text{int}} + K \cdot \epsilon' \cdot P_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the leakage over $N$ cascaded nodes (worst-case).**

In the **worst-case (coherent) summation**, the leakage powers add **in phase** (amplitudes add, then square):

$$
P_{\text{crosstalk, total}} = \left(\sqrt{N \cdot M \cdot \epsilon \cdot P_{\text{int}}} + \sqrt{N \cdot K \cdot \epsilon' \cdot P_{\text{int}}}\right)^{2}
$$

In the **statistical (incoherent) summation** (more realistic for independent lasers), powers add directly:

$$
P_{\text{crosstalk, total}} = N \cdot M \cdot \epsilon \cdot P_{\text{int}} + N \cdot K \cdot \epsilon' \cdot P_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the total crosstalk in dB.**

$$
X_{\text{total, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{crosstalk, total}}}{P_{\text{sig}}}\right)
$$

If $P_{\text{int}} \approx P_{\text{sig}}$ (typical for equal-loaded WDM systems):

$$
X_{\text{total, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(N \cdot M \cdot \epsilon + N \cdot K \cdot \epsilon'\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (KTU-style).**

Given:
- $N = 10$ cascaded OADM nodes.
- $M = 2$ adjacent interferers per node.
- $K = 78$ non-adjacent interferers (for a 80-channel system).
- $X_{\text{adj}} = -25$ dB $\Rightarrow \epsilon = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}$.
- $X_{\text{non-adj}} = -40$ dB $\Rightarrow \epsilon' = 10^{-4}$.

Compute the total incoherent crosstalk:

$$
X_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10 \cdot 2 \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{-3} + 10 \cdot 78 \cdot 10^{-4}\right)
$$

Step 5a — First term:

$$
10 \cdot 2 \cdot 3.162 \times 10^{-3} = 63.24 \times 10^{-3} = 6.324 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Step 5b — Second term:

$$
10 \cdot 78 \cdot 10^{-4} = 780 \times 10^{-4} = 7.8 \times 10^{-2}
$$

Step 5c — Sum:

$$
X_{\text{total, linear}} = 6.324 \times 10^{-2} + 7.8 \times 10^{-2} = 1.4124 \times 10^{-1}
$$

Step 5d — Convert to dB:

$$
X_{\text{total, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.4124 \times 10^{-1}\right) = 10 \times (-0.850) = -8.50\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result**: After 10 cascaded OADM nodes, the effective crosstalk degrades from −25 dB (single-node) to **−8.5 dB** — a **catastrophic 16.5 dB penalty**! This is why modern ROADMs use **WSS-based architectures** with **crosstalk ≤ −40 dB** to limit cumulative degradation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Number of Cascadable OADMs (Power Budget Limit)

> [!NOTE]
> The second most common KTU problem: "How many OADMs can be cascaded before the receiver power drops below sensitivity?"

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

- Transmitter power: $P_{\text{tx}}$ (dBm).
- Receiver sensitivity: $P_{\text{rx, min}}$ (dBm).
- Each OADM node insertion loss: $\text{IL}_{\text{node}}$ (dB).
- In-line EDFA gain exactly compensates per-span loss (not node loss).

### 3.2.2 Derivation

The total power budget available for OADM losses:

$$
P_{\text{budget}} = P_{\text{tx}} - P_{\text{rx, min}}
$$

Each OADM consumes $\text{IL}_{\text{node}}$ dB of this budget. The maximum number of nodes:

$$
N_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{P_{\text{budget}}}{\text{IL}_{\text{node}}} \right\rfloor
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example

Given:
- $P_{\text{tx}} = 0$ dBm.
- $P_{\text{rx, min}} = -28$ dBm.
- $\text{IL}_{\text{node}} = 3.5$ dB.

Step 1 — Compute budget:

$$
P_{\text{budget}} = 0 - (-28) = 28\ \text{dB}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $N_{\text{max}}$:

$$
N_{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{28}{3.5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 8 \right\rfloor = 8\ \text{nodes}
$$

> **Conclusion**: Only **8 ROADMs** can be cascaded before the optical signal drops below the receiver sensitivity threshold. In practice, **OSNR-limited cascades** are even more restrictive — typically **6 to 10 nodes** is the engineering limit for 100G coherent systems.

## 3.3 Derivation: FBG-Based Single-Channel OADM Insertion Loss

> [!NOTE]
> A classic KTU derivation: the **circulator + FBG** OADM, where an FBG reflects the target $\lambda_d$ and a 3-port circulator routes it to the drop port.

### 3.3.1 Architecture

```
       ┌────[FBG at λ_d]────┐
       │                    │
Port 1 ●───→(Circulator 1)───→ Port 2 (Express out)
        │                    
        └──→ Port 3 (Drop out at λ_d)
```

### 3.3.2 Loss Analysis

- **Circulator insertion loss**: $\text{IL}_{\text{circ}} \approx 0.7$ dB.
- **FBG reflectivity**: $R = 0.99$ (99% reflective, 1% transmitted).
- **FBG insertion loss (through path)**: $\text{IL}_{\text{FBG, through}} \approx 0.05$ dB (low loss for non-resonant $\lambda$).

**Express channel loss** (non-resonant $\lambda$, passes through FBG and circulator):

$$
\text{IL}_{\text{express}} = \text{IL}_{\text{circ}} + \text{IL}_{\text{FBG, through}} = 0.7 + 0.05 = 0.75\ \text{dB}
$$

**Drop channel loss** (resonant $\lambda_d$, reflected by FBG):

$$
\text{IL}_{\text{drop}} = \text{IL}_{\text{circ}} - 10 \log_{10}(R) = 0.7 - 10 \log_{10}(0.99) \approx 0.7 + 0.04 = 0.74\ \text{dB}
$$

**Add channel loss** (similar to drop, but in reverse direction):

$$
\text{IL}_{\text{add}} \approx 0.75\ \text{dB}
$$

> **Conclusion**: FBG-based OADMs are **near-ideal** with sub-1 dB losses, making them excellent for **low-channel-count metro access networks**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of OADM Technologies

| Criterion | TFF-based | FBG + Circulator | AWG-based | WSS-based (ROADM) |
|-----------|-----------|------------------|-----------|-------------------|
| **Channel Count** | 1–8 | 1–4 (per unit) | 16–96 | 1–96+ |
| **Insertion Loss** | 0.5–1.5 dB | 0.5–1.0 dB | 3–6 dB | 4–7 dB |
| **Crosstalk** | −25 dB | −30 dB | −30 dB | −40 dB |
| **Tunability** | Fixed | Fixed | Fixed | **Fully reconfigurable** |
| **Cost per Channel** | Very low | Low | Medium | High |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Poor | Medium | **Excellent** |
| **Remote Control** | No | No | No | **Yes (SDN/NMS)** |
| **Use Case** | Access ring | Metro access | Backbone mux | Long-haul/metro core |
| **Power Consumption** | 0 W (passive) | 0 W (passive) | 0 W (passive) | 5–30 W |
