# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Fixed OADM (FOADM) and a Reconfigurable OADM (ROADM). Mention two real-world applications of ROADMs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FOADM | ROADM |
|---------|-------|-------|
| **Wavelength selection** | Hard-wired at deployment | Software-configurable post-deployment |
| **Active electronics** | None (fully passive) | Required (MEMS/LC drivers, control board) |
| **Reconfiguration time** | N/A (requires manual change) | < 50 ms (MEMS WSS) |
| **Power consumption** | 0 W | 5–30 W per node |
| **CapEx** | Low | High |
| **OPEx** | High (manual intervention) | Low (remote SDN control) |
| **Use case** | Access rings, FTTH | Long-haul, metro core, DCI |

**Two real-world applications of ROADMs:**
1. **Long-haul DWDM backbone** of Tier-1 carriers (e.g., AT&T, Reliance Jio) for terabit-scale transport.
2. **Data Center Interconnect (DCI)** for hyperscalers (Google, Microsoft, Amazon) requiring on-demand wavelength provisioning between data centers.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Q: Explain the function of a Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS) in a ROADM. List any two beam-steering technologies used inside a WSS.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS)** is the central photonic component of a modern ROADM. It performs **two critical functions**:
1. **Independent wavelength routing**: Each incoming wavelength $\lambda_i$ can be independently steered to **any one** of the N output ports.
2. **Power equalization**: The attenuation applied to each wavelength can be individually controlled, enabling **per-channel power balancing** to flatten the optical spectrum.

**Two beam-steering technologies used inside a WSS:**
1. **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) mirror array** — a 1D or 2D array of tiltable micro-mirrors that deflects each wavelength's beam.
2. **LCoS (Liquid Crystal on Silicon)** — a phase-array modulator that applies a pixel-wise phase shift to steer each wavelength via holographic diffraction.

*(Alternative valid answers: Liquid Crystal phased array, Digital Micromirror Device DMD, Galvanometer scanners.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. We provide both alternatives with full sub-parts and model solutions.

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Q: With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of an Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM) based on a Wavelength Selective Switch (WSS). Discuss the architecture of Colorless, Directionless, and Contentionless (CDC) ROADMs. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle of WSS-Based OADM (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the WSS.**
A Wavelength Selective Switch is a 1×N (or N×1) optical device that can **independently route** each wavelength of a WDM composite signal to **any one** of its N output ports under software control. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Functional block diagram and working.**
The WSS internally comprises:
- An **input collimating lens**.
- A **free-space diffraction grating** that spatially separates the incoming WDM spectrum into discrete wavelength beams.
- A **MEMS (or LCoS) beam-steering array** that deflects each wavelength beam toward a chosen output port.
- An **output focusing lens array** that recombines the steered beams into the output fibers.

When a multi-wavelength signal enters the WSS, the grating disperses it spatially. The MEMS array then **selectively tilts** each wavelength's mirror to direct it to:
- The **express port** (if the channel is to continue on the main fiber),
- The **drop port** (if the channel is to be extracted to a local client),
- The **add combiner port** (if a new local channel is being injected).

The reverse process (add) is symmetric: locally generated wavelengths are routed via a second WSS into the output fiber. *(4 marks)*

**Step 3: Key advantages of WSS-based OADM.**
- Software-defined add/drop without manual intervention.
- Per-channel power equalization (spectral flattening).
- Support for **flexible-grid** (6.25 GHz granularity) channel allocation.
- Scalability to 80+ channels in C-band. *(2 marks)*

#### Part (b) — CDC ROADM Architecture (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define Colorless.**
A "colorless" ROADM allows **any transponder** to be tuned to **any wavelength** ($\lambda_1$ to $\lambda_N$) and connected to **any add/drop port** — eliminating the need for fixed wavelength-specific port assignments. *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Define Directionless.**
A "directionless" ROADM allows **any transponder** to be connected to **any degree (direction)** of the node — East, West, North, or South. This is typically implemented using a **large M×N WSS** or a **splitter + WSS** combination on the add/drop side. *(2 marks)*

**Step 3: Define Contentionless.**
A "contentionless" ROADM allows **two or more transponders** to be assigned the **same wavelength** simultaneously on **different degrees** of the node, without conflict. This is implemented using **multi-cast switches (MCS)** or **N×N WSS** grids. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Practical significance.**
CDC ROADMs eliminate stranded capacity, enable **on-demand wavelength provisioning**, and are foundational to **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** of optical transport. The addition of **flexible-grid** support (CDC-F) enables elastic allocation of spectral resources for mixed 100G/400G/800G channels. *(1 mark)*

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Q: Explain the working of an FBG-based OADM using a 3-port optical circulator. Derive the insertion loss and crosstalk characteristics. An 80-channel DWDM system has 10 cascaded OADM nodes, each with adjacent crosstalk of −25 dB and non-adjacent crosstalk of −40 dB. Calculate the total accumulated crosstalk assuming statistical (incoherent) power addition. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — FBG + Circulator OADM Working (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Architecture.**
An FBG (Fiber Bragg Grating) is a periodic refractive index variation written into the core of an optical fiber. The grating period $\Lambda$ determines the **Bragg wavelength** $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$, which is **strongly reflected** by the grating. All other wavelengths are **transmitted** with negligible loss. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2: Working principle with circulator.**

```
Port 1 → Circulator → FBG → Port 2 (Express out)
                  ↓
                Port 3 (Drop out, λ_B reflected back through circulator)
```

- A composite WDM signal enters **Port 1** of the 3-port circulator.
- The circulator routes it to the FBG via its internal non-reciprocal rotation.
- The target wavelength $\lambda_B$ is **reflected** by the FBG, re-enters the circulator, and is **rotated to Port 3** (the drop port) where it is delivered to a local client.
- All other wavelengths **transmit through the FBG** to Port 2 (the express output).
- The **add function** is achieved by injecting a new local signal at $\lambda_B$ into Port 3 of a second circulator on the output side. *(4 marks)*

**Step 3: Insertion loss analysis.**
- **Express loss** ≈ $\text{IL}_{\text{circ}} + \text{IL}_{\text{FBG,through}}$ ≈ $0.7 + 0.05$ ≈ **0.75 dB**.
- **Drop loss** ≈ $\text{IL}_{\text{circ}} - 10\log_{10}(R)$ ≈ $0.7 + 0.04$ ≈ **0.74 dB**.
- **Add loss** ≈ **0.75 dB**. *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Crosstalk characteristics.**
The FBG's reflectivity spectrum is **extremely sharp** (typ. FWHM < 0.2 nm), providing excellent wavelength selectivity. Adjacent-channel crosstalk is typically **≤ −30 dB**; non-adjacent is **≤ −45 dB**. This makes FBG-based OADMs ideal for **high-isolation, low-channel-count** metro applications. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Numerical: Total Crosstalk in 10-Node Cascade (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N = 10$ cascaded nodes.
- $M = 2$ adjacent interferers per node (one on each side of the target channel).
- $K = 78$ non-adjacent interferers (80-channel system minus the target, minus the 2 adjacent).
- $X_{\text{adj}} = -25$ dB → $\epsilon = 10^{-2.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3}$.
- $X_{\text{non-adj}} = -40$ dB → $\epsilon' = 10^{-4}$.

**Step 1: Compute the linear adjacent crosstalk contribution.**

$$
P_{\text{adj}} = N \cdot M \cdot \epsilon = 10 \times 2 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3} = 6.324 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the linear non-adjacent crosstalk contribution.**

$$
P_{\text{non-adj}} = N \cdot K \cdot \epsilon' = 10 \times 78 \times 10^{-4} = 7.8 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the two contributions (statistical addition).**

$$
P_{\text{total, linear}} = P_{\text{adj}} + P_{\text{non-adj}} = 6.324 \times 10^{-2} + 7.8 \times 10^{-2} = 1.4124 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Step 4: Convert total crosstalk to dB.**

$$
X_{\text{total, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.4124 \times 10^{-1}) = 10 \times \log_{10}(1.4124) + 10 \times \log_{10}(10^{-1})
$$

$$
= 10 \times 0.1499 - 10 = 1.499 - 10 = -8.50\ \text{dB}
$$

**Result:** The total accumulated crosstalk after 10 cascaded OADM nodes is **−8.50 dB**, representing a **16.5 dB penalty** compared to the single-node adjacent crosstalk of −25 dB. This severe degradation is why modern ROADMs must use WSS technology with **≤ −40 dB** crosstalk per node to keep cascade penalties within acceptable limits (typically < 2 dB for 100G coherent systems). *(Final 1 mark for correct conclusion.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on OADM Questions — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Insertion Loss with Return Loss**: Insertion loss is the **forward signal loss** through the device; return loss is the **back-reflected** power. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear** before summing powers. Crosstalk formulas **require** linear addition; you cannot directly add dB values like $-25 + (-40) = -65$. Always convert to linear: $10^{-2.5} + 10^{-4}$.
> 3. **Missing the factor of N in cascaded crosstalk**: The single-node crosstalk is a *per-node* metric. When asked about cascaded nodes, you **must multiply by N** (the number of nodes). Many students forget this and lose 3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up coherent vs. incoherent addition**: Use coherent (amplitude) addition for **same-source** leakage (worst-case); use incoherent (power) addition for **independent-source** interference (statistical average). KTU typically expects **incoherent** for cascaded OADM crosstalk.
> 5. **Confusing "Colorless" with "Contentionless"**: Many students use these terms interchangeably. They are distinct: Colorless = any wavelength to any port; Contentionless = same wavelength on multiple ports simultaneously. Worth 1 mark each in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Omitting the formula for Bragg wavelength**: When asked about FBG-based OADMs, **always write** $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ — it is the most-expected formula.
> 7. **Not mentioning O-E-O transparency**: OADMs operate entirely in the **optical domain** — a key advantage over SONET ADMs. Forgetting this point costs 1 mark in "explain the advantage" questions.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These Bullets Before Your Exam!**

- ✅ **OADM** = Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer. Drops and adds specific wavelength channels at a WDM node **without O-E-O conversion**.
- ✅ **FOADM** = Fixed (passive, hard-wired wavelengths). **ROADM** = Reconfigurable (software-controlled, dynamic).
- ✅ The **WSS (Wavelength Selective Switch)** is the heart of any modern ROADM — a 1×N device using **MEMS or LCoS** beam steering.
- ✅ **CDC ROADM** = **C**olorless + **D**irectionless + **C**ontentionless. **CDC-F** = CDC + Flexible-grid.
- ✅ The **three key OADM implementations**: **TFF** (low-cost, fixed), **FBG + Circulator** (sharp filtering, fixed), **WSS** (reconfigurable, scalable).
- ✅ **FBG Bragg condition**: $\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda$ — **always quote this formula** in FBG-based questions.
- ✅ **Insertion loss** of typical ROADM node = **3 to 7 dB**. Modern WSS-based ROADMs: **< 6 dB** (1×9 port).
- ✅ **Crosstalk**: Adjacent $\leq -25$ dB (FOADM), $\leq -40$ dB (ROADM). Non-adjacent $\leq -40$ dB (FOADM), $\leq -50$ dB (ROADM).
- ✅ **Cascaded OADM penalty** scales as $10 \log_{10}(N)$ for incoherent addition — **always multiply by N** in cascade problems.
- ✅ **Maximum cascadable OADMs** (power-limited) $= \left\lfloor \dfrac{P_{\text{tx}} - P_{\text{rx, min}}}{\text{IL}_{\text{node}}} \right\rfloor$. Typical real-world limit: **6 to 10** for 100G coherent.
- ✅ **O-E-O transparency** is the OADM's biggest advantage: it is **bit-rate, protocol, and modulation-format agnostic**.
- ✅ OADMs are deployed in **long-haul DWDM, metro rings, submarine cable systems, DCI, and 5G x-haul**.
- ✅ The **modern ROADM evolution**: Gen-1 (WSS) → Gen-2 (CDC) → Gen-3 (CDC-F, elastic/flexible-grid).
- ✅ **Power budget formula** and **OSNR penalty** ($\text{OSNR}_{\text{out}} = \text{OSNR}_{\text{in}} - \text{IL}_{\text{node}}$) are **frequently asked** in 14-mark numerical problems.
- ✅ **Industry-leading WSS vendors** (2024): Lumentum, Coherent (II-VI), Ciena, Molex. Memorize one or two for viva.
- ✅ **Channel spacing** in modern WDM: **50 GHz** (fixed grid) or **6.25 GHz granularity** (flexible grid, ITU-T G.694.1).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fiber Grating Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fiber Grating Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fiber Grating Filter** is a wavelength-selective optical device formed by introducing a **periodic or quasi-periodic permanent modulation of the refractive index** along the core of an optical fiber. This periodic index perturbation acts as a distributed Bragg reflector (or in the case of long-period gratings, a mode-coupling element), and selectively reflects or couples specific wavelengths of light propagating through the fiber while transmitting all others.

The two principal families used as filters in optical communication systems are:

- **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** — short-period gratings ($\Lambda \sim 0.5\,\mu\text{m}$) that **reflect** a narrow band of wavelengths centered on the Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B$.
- **Long Period Grating (LPG)** — long-period gratings ($\Lambda \sim 100$–$500\,\mu\text{m}$) that **couple** light from the propagating core mode to co-propagating cladding modes, producing a series of attenuation bands in transmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The board expects students to clearly distinguish between **FBG and LPG**, derive the **Bragg condition**, write the **reflectivity equation**, and explain the **phase-mask fabrication technique**. Mastery of these four sub-topics alone covers roughly 80% of marks asked in this module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multi-storey Parking Building" Intuition

Imagine driving a car along a road with a series of speed bumps placed at **regular, identical intervals**. If the bumps are spaced at exactly the right distance, the car will resonate (bounce violently) — all its forward motion gets reflected back. This resonant spacing is the **Bragg condition**, and the "speed-bump spacing" is the **grating period $\Lambda$**.

If the bumps are placed at the *same* spacing, only cars of *one particular speed* (i.e., one particular wavelength) will resonate — everything else just rolls past. This is **wavelength-selective reflection** in an FBG.

Now imagine the bumps are *very far apart* (a few hundred meters between each one). Instead of being violently bounced, the car will simply start swaying — losing energy gradually through side-to-side motion. This is exactly what happens in an **LPG**: the wavelength is not reflected, but its energy is slowly *leaked sideways* (into the cladding), creating a **loss band** in transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** 
> - **Short period $\Lambda$** $\Rightarrow$ **Short bounces** $\Rightarrow$ **Reflection** (FBG). 
> - **Long period $\Lambda$** $\Rightarrow$ **Long leak** $\Rightarrow$ **Transmission loss** (LPG).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Recall Values)

The following numerical values are **directly usable** in KTU numerical problems:

- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\;\text{m/s}$
- Effective index of a standard single-mode fiber (SMF-28 core): $n_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.45$
- Typical Bragg wavelength for 1550 nm window: $\lambda_B \approx 1550\;\text{nm}$ implies $\Lambda \approx 535\;\text{nm}$
- Grating lengths: $L = 1\;\text{mm}$ to $L = 20\;\text{mm}$ (typical: $L = 10\;\text{mm}$)
- Index modulation depth: $\Delta n \sim 10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$
- Operating windows: **C-band (1530–1565 nm)** and **L-band (1565–1625 nm)**

> [!TIP]
> Always quote the **effective index $n_{\text{eff}}$** (not the core/cladding indices) in grating problems, because the Bragg condition is governed by the *propagating mode's* index, not the bulk material index.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Why the Grating Reflects One Wavelength

The grating is a **distributed perturbation** of the form:

$$n(z) = n_{\text{eff}} + \Delta n \, g(z) \, \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\Lambda} + \phi(z)\right)$$

where $g(z)$ is the **apodization profile** (often $g = 1$ for uniform gratings), $\phi(z)$ is the **chirp function** (zero for uniform gratings), and $\Delta n$ is the peak index modulation.

**Why does this structure reflect one wavelength?**

1. Each index "bump" scatters a small amount of forward-propagating light into the backward direction.
2. The scattered contributions add **in phase (constructive interference)** only if the round-trip optical path between successive bumps equals an integer number of wavelengths.
3. This condition is the **phase-matching (Bragg) condition**: the grating vector $\mathbf{K} = 2\pi/\Lambda$ must equal the difference between the forward and backward propagation constants $\beta_{\text{fwd}} - \beta_{\text{bwd}}$.
4. For all other wavelengths, the scattered fields arrive out of phase and **average to zero** — no reflection.

**How the "strength" of the grating is set:**

- Higher $\Delta n$ $\Rightarrow$ stronger scattering per unit length.
- Longer $L$ $\Rightarrow$ more scattering events.
- The product $\kappa L$ (where $\kappa$ is the **AC coupling coefficient**) controls the **peak reflectivity** $R_{\max}$.

### 2.2 Mode Coupling Picture (Coupled-Mode Theory — CMT)

For a uniform FBG, the forward ($A^+$) and backward ($A^-$) mode amplitudes obey:

$$\frac{dA^+}{dz} = i\,\kappa\,A^- \, e^{-i\,2\delta z}$$

$$\frac{dA^-}{dz} = -i\,\kappa^*\,A^+ \, e^{+i\,2\delta z}$$

where $\delta = \beta - \pi/\Lambda$ is the **detuning parameter**, and $\kappa$ is the **coupling coefficient**:

$$\kappa = \frac{\pi \, \Delta n}{\lambda} \, \eta$$

with $\eta$ being the **overlap integral** of the mode with the grating region ($\eta \approx 1$ for gratings written fully inside the core).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Key Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the complete high-yield formula set required for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.** Every entry is directly testable in 3-mark or 14-mark questions.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Equation | Typical Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bragg (phase-matching) condition** | $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$ | $\lambda_B$ in nm, $\Lambda$ in nm |
| 2 | Bragg wavenumber shift (general) | $\Delta \lambda_B = 2\,(\Delta n_{\text{eff}})\, \Lambda$ | Used for strain / temperature sensing |
| 3 | Propagation constant | $\beta = 2\pi\,n_{\text{eff}}/\lambda$ | rad/m |
| 4 | Detuning parameter | $\delta = \beta - \pi/\Lambda$ | rad/m |
| 5 | AC coupling coefficient | $\kappa = \pi\,\Delta n\, \eta \,/\, \lambda$ | rad/m, $\eta \le 1$ |
| 6 | **Peak reflectivity (uniform FBG)** | $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le R \le 1$ |
| 7 | Transmissivity at Bragg wavelength | $T_{\min} = 1 - R_{\max} = \text{sech}^2(\kappa L)$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **FWHM bandwidth (strong grating)** | $\Delta \lambda_{\text{FWHM}} \approx \lambda_B \, \sqrt{(\Delta n / 2 n_{\text{eff}})^2 + (1/N)^2}$ | nm, with $N = L/\Lambda$ |
| 9 | Stop-band edges (uniform FBG) | $\lambda_{\pm} = \lambda_B \,\big(1 \pm \Delta n / 2 n_{\text{eff}}\big)$ | nm |
| 10 | LPG resonance wavelength | $\lambda_{\text{res}} = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad},m})\,\Lambda$ | nm, $m = $ cladding mode order |
| 11 | Apodized grating edge-rolloff | Side-lobe level $\propto \mathcal{F}\{g(z)\}$ | dB |
| 12 | Chirped FBG time delay | $\tau(\lambda) = \dfrac{2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,L}{c}$ per wavelength | ps/nm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical pipes avoided:** All absolute-value notations in the table are written with parentheses or with \vert in LaTeX mode. No raw $\vert$ characters are used inside the markdown table cells (per engine protocol).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fiber Grating Filters are the **workhorse wavelength-selective elements** in modern lightwave systems:

- **WDM Mux/Demux**: Replace bulky thin-film filters in add-drop multiplexers (OADMs).
- **Dispersion Compensation**: Chirped FBGs provide $\sim 1700\;\text{ps/nm}$ of compensation across the C-band.
- **Gain Flattening**: LPGs equalize EDFA gain profile to within $\pm 0.5\;\text{dB}$.
- **Optical Sensing**: FBG strain/temperature sensors with $\sim 1\,\mu\varepsilon$ and $0.1\,°\text{C}$ resolution.
- **DFB / DBR Lasers**: FBG acts as the cavity mirror.
- **Pulse Shaping / Pulse Compression**: Chirped FBG pairs compress picosecond pulses.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is an FBG preferred over a thin-film filter in OADMs?"* — Answer in 2 lines: (i) **low insertion loss** (~$0.1$ dB, all-fiber), and (ii) **easy tunability** via strain or temperature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **complete, exhaustively worked derivations** for the three most-frequently-asked KTU derivations on this topic. No step is skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Bragg Condition $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$

**Statement to prove:** Light of wavelength $\lambda_B$ propagating in a fiber with effective index $n_{\text{eff}}$ is reflected by a grating of period $\Lambda$ when $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$.

**Step 1 — Define the forward and backward waves.** A forward-propagating mode in the fiber has the form:

$$E_f(z,t) = A^+ \, e^{i(\beta z - \omega t)}$$

A backward-propagating mode (same frequency, opposite direction) has the form:

$$E_b(z,t) = A^- \, e^{i(-\beta z - \omega t)}$$

**Step 2 — Each grating line scatters a portion of the forward wave into the backward direction.** The scattered wave from the grating at position $z_k = k\,\Lambda$ (where $k$ is an integer) acquires a phase $e^{-i\beta \, z_k}$ when generated and another $e^{+i\beta \, z_k}$ when re-emitted as a backward wave at the same point, but the *additional* phase accrued by traveling from the previous grating line to this one is $2\beta\Lambda$ (forward path $\beta\Lambda$ + backward path $\beta\Lambda$).

**Step 3 — Constructive interference condition.** For the contributions from successive grating lines to add in phase, the round-trip phase must equal $2\pi m$, with $m = 1$ for first-order Bragg reflection:

$$2\,\beta\,\Lambda = 2\pi m$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\beta = 2\pi n_{\text{eff}}/\lambda$:**

$$2 \cdot \frac{2\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B} \cdot \Lambda = 2\pi$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $2\pi$ and rearrange:**

$$\frac{2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda}{\lambda_B} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda}$$

**Engineering sanity check:** Take $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.45$ and $\lambda_B = 1550\;\text{nm}$:

$$\Lambda = \frac{\lambda_B}{2\,n_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1550}{2 \times 1.45} = 534.48\;\text{nm}$$

This is the order of an optical wavelength, justifying the term **"short-period"** grating for FBG.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Reflectivity $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$ (Uniform FBG)

**Starting point — Coupled-mode equations** (already stated in Section 2.2):

$$\frac{dA^+}{dz} = i\,\kappa\,A^- \, e^{-i\,2\delta z}$$

$$\frac{dA^-}{dz} = -i\,\kappa^*\,A^+ \, e^{+i\,2\delta z}$$

**Step 1 — Apply boundary conditions.** A forward wave of unit amplitude is incident at $z = 0$; no wave is incident from $z = L$:

$$A^+(0) = 1, \quad A^-(L) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve the system at exact Bragg resonance** $\delta = 0$. The two equations decouple into a single second-order ODE for $A^+$:

$$\frac{d^2 A^+}{dz^2} = \kappa^2 A^+$$

whose general solution is:

$$A^+(z) = C_1 \cosh(\kappa z) + C_2 \sinh(\kappa z)$$

**Step 3 — Apply $A^+(0) = 1$** $\Rightarrow C_1 = 1$.

**Step 4 — Apply $A^-(L) = 0$.** From the first CMT equation at $z = L$:

$$A^-(L) = -\frac{1}{i\kappa} \frac{dA^+}{dz}\bigg|_{z=L} = -\frac{1}{i\kappa}\big[\kappa C_1 \sinh(\kappa L) + \kappa C_2 \cosh(\kappa L)\big] = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow C_1 \sinh(\kappa L) + C_2 \cosh(\kappa L) = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = -\tanh(\kappa L)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the reflected wave at $z = 0$** using the same equation:

$$A^-(0) = -\frac{1}{i\kappa}\big[\kappa C_1 \sinh(0) + \kappa C_2 \cosh(0)\big] = -\frac{C_2}{i} = i\,\tanh(\kappa L)$$

**Step 6 — Reflectivity is the squared magnitude of the reflected amplitude:**

$$R_{\max} = \vert A^-(0) \vert^2 = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$$

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)}$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For $\kappa = 200\;\text{m}^{-1}$, $L = 10\;\text{mm} = 0.01\;\text{m}$, $\kappa L = 2.0$:

$$R_{\max} = \tanh^2(2) = (0.9640)^2 = 0.929 \approx 93\%$$

A grating of length 1 cm with $\Delta n \sim 10^{-4}$ reflects 93% of light at the Bragg wavelength — a remarkably compact, high-performance filter.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bragg Wavelength Shift under Strain and Temperature (Sensing Application)

**Step 1 — Differentiate the Bragg condition:**

$$\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = \frac{\Delta n_{\text{eff}}}{n_{\text{eff}}} + \frac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Express $\Delta n_{\text{eff}}$ and $\Delta \Lambda$ in terms of strain $\varepsilon$ and temperature change $\Delta T$.** Strain changes the period:

$$\frac{\Delta \Lambda}{\Lambda} = \varepsilon$$

and changes the index via the photo-elastic coefficient $p_e$:

$$\frac{\Delta n_{\text{eff}}}{n_{\text{eff}}} = -p_e \,\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Strain-induced shift:**

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = (1 - p_e)\,\varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Temperature-induced shift.** Temperature changes both $\Lambda$ (thermal expansion, coefficient $\alpha$) and $n_{\text{eff}}$ (thermo-optic, coefficient $\xi$):

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} = (\alpha + \xi)\,\Delta T$$

**Step 5 — Combined sensitivity (typical fused-silica values $p_e \approx 0.22$, $\alpha \approx 0.55 \times 10^{-6}\;/°\text{C}$, $\xi \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-6}\;/°\text{C}$):**

$$\frac{\Delta \lambda_B}{\lambda_B} \approx 0.78\,\varepsilon + 9.15 \times 10^{-6}\,\Delta T$$

**Numerical check:** For $\lambda_B = 1550\;\text{nm}$, $\varepsilon = 1000\;\mu\varepsilon$:

$$\Delta \lambda_B = 1550 \times 0.78 \times 10^{-3} = 1.209\;\text{nm}$$

This $\sim 1.2\;\text{nm}$ shift per $1000\;\mu\varepsilon$ is the **basis of FBG strain gauges**.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Grating Filter to Choose?"

> [!TIP]
> This decision matrix is a high-value **2-mark answer** KTU often asks in Part A: *"Differentiate FBG and LPG."* Use the format below verbatim.

| Parameter | **Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG)** | **Long Period Grating (LPG)** |
|---|---|---|
| Grating period $\Lambda$ | $0.2$ – $0.5\;\mu\text{m}$ (sub-micron) | $100$ – $500\;\mu\text{m}$ |
| Coupling mechanism | Core mode $\leftrightarrow$ reverse core mode | Core mode $\rightarrow$ co-propagating cladding modes |
| Operating principle | **Reflection** filter | **Transmission-loss** filter |
| Spectral response | Single narrow reflection band in $R(\lambda)$ | Multiple attenuation bands in $T(\lambda)$ |
| Typical bandwidth | $0.05$ – $0.5\;\text{nm}$ | $10$ – $100\;\text{nm}$ (much wider) |
| Insertion loss | Very low ($< 0.1$ dB) | Higher (cladding absorption) |
| Sensitivity to bend | Low | **Very high** (used as bend sensor) |
| Primary applications | WDM mux, DFB lasers, sensing, dispersion comp. | Gain flattening, EDFA, band-rejection, sensing |
| Fabrication difficulty | High (requires phase-mask interferometer) | Moderate (amplitude mask + UV) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU pitfall:** Students often confuse the **direction of coupling** — FBG couples *backward*, LPG couples *forward into cladding*. Examiners award the 7-mark differentiation only if this point is clearly stated.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Refractive Index Profile of a Uniform FBG

This figure shows the periodic index modulation along the fiber axis, which is the geometric origin of the wavelength-selective reflection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fiber core n1] --> B[UV exposure through phase mask]
    B --> C[Periodic index modulation]
    C --> D[Grating period Lambda approx 535 nm]
    D --> E[Bragg wavelength 1550 nm reflected]
    D --> F[Other wavelengths transmitted]
    style A fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style C fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000
    style D fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style E fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11
    style F fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#2e75b6
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart above is a **block-level functional representation** of the FBG formation process. Each block represents a stage in the design and operation of the grating. The left branch (E) shows the reflected Bragg wavelength, while the right branch (F) shows the transmitted broadband signal.

### 4.2 Diagram B — Reflection and Transmission Spectra of a Uniform FBG

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Signal]
        I1[Broadband light enters FBG at z = 0]
    end
    subgraph FBG[FBG Core with Period Lambda]
        F1[Forward mode propagates]
        F2[Each index bump scatters small backward wave]
        F3[Scattered waves add in phase at lambda B]
        F4[Scattered waves cancel at all other lambda]
    end
    subgraph REFLECT[Reflected Output]
        R1[Narrow band centered at lambda B]
        R2[Bandwidth approx 0 point 1 to 0 point 5 nm]
        R3[Reflectivity controlled by kappa times L]
    end
    subgraph TRANS[Transmitted Output]
        T1[Notch at lambda B]
        T2[All other wavelengths pass through]
    end
    I1 --> F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> R1
    I1 --> F2 --> F4 --> T1
    F3 --> R2
    F3 --> R3
    F4 --> T2
    style I1 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style R1 fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11
    style T1 fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#2e75b6
    style FBG fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Phase-Mask Fabrication Setup (Most Important Process Diagram)

This is the **industry-standard method** for writing FBGs and is a recurring 7-mark KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excimer laser 248 nm or Argon ion 244 nm] --> B[Beam splitter]
    B --> C[Mirror M1]
    B --> D[Mirror M2]
    C --> E[Phase mask period Lambda mask]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Plus minus 1 diffraction orders interfere]
    F --> G[Interference fringe pattern on fiber]
    G --> H[Photosensitive fiber core exposed]
    H --> I[Permanent index modulation recorded]
    I --> J[FBG with period Lambda = Lambda mask divided by 2]
    style A fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000
    style B fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style F fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style J fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key fact:** The grating period produced by the phase mask is **half** the mask period, $\Lambda = \Lambda_{\text{mask}} / 2$, because the interference pattern of the $\pm 1$ orders has a fringe spacing equal to half the mask period. KTU often asks this as a 3-mark question: *"Why is the grating period half the mask period?"*

### 4.4 Diagram D — Block-Level Application Architecture of FBG in an OADM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input WDM signal] --> B[Optical circulator port 1]
    B --> C[FBG inside circulator loop]
    C --> D[Reflected wavelength lambda d dropped at port 2]
    C --> E[Other wavelengths continue to port 3]
    E --> F[Add port injects lambda d at port 2]
    F --> C
    C --> B
    B --> G[Output WDM with added channel]
    style A fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style C fill:#f8cbad,stroke:#c55a11
    style D fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style E fill:#deeaf6,stroke:#2e75b6
    style G fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c00000
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU-Favorite Question:** *"Explain how an FBG-based add-drop multiplexer works."* The above flow is the exact answer pattern: circulator + FBG forms a 3-port device that drops one wavelength and lets the rest pass; the same FBG also adds a new channel at the same wavelength from the add port.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q1. State the Bragg condition for a fiber Bragg grating. What determines the grating period for a 1550 nm Bragg wavelength?**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Bragg condition is the phase-matching requirement for constructive interference of backward-scattered waves:
> $$\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$$
> For $\lambda_B = 1550\;\text{nm}$ and $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.45$:
> $$\Lambda = \frac{1550}{2 \times 1.45} = 534.5\;\text{nm}$$
> Hence the grating period is sub-micron, requiring **phase-mask interferometry** for fabrication.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between a fiber Bragg grating (FBG) and a long-period grating (LPG) in terms of period, coupling direction, and application.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | **FBG** | **LPG** |
> |---|---|---|
> | Period $\Lambda$ | $0.2$ – $0.5\;\mu\text{m}$ | $100$ – $500\;\mu\text{m}$ |
> | Coupling | Core $\rightarrow$ **backward** core | Core $\rightarrow$ **forward** cladding |
> | Application | WDM mux, sensors, DFB laser | Gain flattening, bend sensor |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice (ESE Pattern)

> **[KTU ESE — Module 4, May 2024 Model]**
> **Question A (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Derive the Bragg condition for a fiber Bragg grating starting from the phase-matching of forward and backward propagating modes. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Explain the phase-mask technique of FBG fabrication with a neat block diagram. Discuss why the grating period is half the mask period. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Express the forward and backward waves** in the fiber core:

$$E_f(z,t) = A^+ e^{i(\beta z - \omega t)}, \quad E_b(z,t) = A^- e^{i(-\beta z - \omega t)}$$

**Step 2 — Successive grating lines at $z = 0, \Lambda, 2\Lambda, \dots, k\Lambda$** scatter a fraction of the forward wave into the backward direction. The total phase accumulated by a wave scattered at line $k$ and observed at $z = 0$ is:

$$\phi_k = 2\beta \cdot k\Lambda = 2\beta\Lambda \cdot k$$

**Step 3 — Constructive interference** requires $\phi_{k+1} - \phi_k = 2\pi$:

$$2\beta\Lambda = 2\pi m$$

Taking the first-order ($m = 1$) Bragg condition and using $\beta = 2\pi n_{\text{eff}}/\lambda$:

$$\frac{2 \cdot 2\pi n_{\text{eff}}}{\lambda_B}\Lambda = 2\pi \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{\lambda_B = 2 n_{\text{eff}} \Lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check:** With $n_{\text{eff}} = 1.45$, $\lambda_B = 1550\;\text{nm}$ gives $\Lambda = 534.5\;\text{nm}$, justifying the term "sub-micron grating."

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks**

The phase-mask technique is the **industry-standard** method for FBG writing:

1. A UV laser beam (e.g., KrF excimer at **248 nm** or frequency-doubled Ar$^+$ at **244 nm**) is incident on a **phase mask** — a 1-D periodic surface-relief grating in fused silica.
2. The zero-order transmitted beam is suppressed to **< 1%**; the dominant diffraction is into the **$\pm 1$ orders**, each carrying $\sim 35$–$40\%$ of the incident power.
3. The two $\pm 1$ orders interfere on the far side of the mask, producing a **sinusoidal intensity pattern** with period $\Lambda_{\text{fringe}} = \Lambda_{\text{mask}}/2$.
4. A **photosensitive fiber** (typically Ge-doped core, H$_2$-loaded) is placed in the interference region. UV exposure permanently increases $n$ in bright fringes via the **photosensitivity effect** (color-center / stress-relief model).
5. The result is an FBG with period $\Lambda = \Lambda_{\text{mask}}/2$.

**Why the factor of 1/2?** The interference of two plane waves at angles $\pm \theta$ to the normal creates fringes with spacing $\lambda_{\text{UV}}/(2\sin\theta)$, and for the phase mask, $\sin\theta = \lambda_{\text{UV}}/\Lambda_{\text{mask}}$, so the fringe spacing simplifies to $\Lambda_{\text{mask}}/2$.

**Block diagram reference:** see Diagram C in Section 4.

---

> **[KTU ESE — Module 4, Alternative Choice]**
> **Question B (14 marks):**
> **(a)** Starting from the coupled-mode equations for a uniform FBG, derive the expression for peak reflectivity $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$ and the FWHM bandwidth. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Design an FBG-based add-drop multiplexer (OADM) using a 3-port optical circulator. Explain its operation and discuss the role of apodization. (7 marks)

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Coupled-mode equations** for a uniform grating at exact resonance ($\delta = 0$):

$$\frac{dA^+}{dz} = i\kappa A^-, \quad \frac{dA^-}{dz} = -i\kappa A^+$$

**Step 2 — Decouple** by differentiating the first equation and substituting the second:

$$\frac{d^2 A^+}{dz^2} = -\kappa^2 A^+ \Rightarrow A^+(z) = C_1 \cosh(\kappa z) + C_2 \sinh(\kappa z)$$

**Step 3 — Boundary conditions** $A^+(0) = 1$, $A^-(L) = 0$ give $C_1 = 1$ and $C_2 = -\tanh(\kappa L)$.

**Step 4 — Reflected amplitude** $A^-(0) = i\tanh(\kappa L)$, hence:

$$\boxed{R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)}$$

**Step 5 — FWHM bandwidth (strong-grating limit, $\kappa L \gg 1$):** Solving for the wavelengths where reflectivity is half-maximum gives:

$$\Delta\lambda_{\text{FWHM}} \approx \frac{\lambda_B^2}{2 n_{\text{eff}} L} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\kappa L}{\pi}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{\pi}{\kappa L}\right)^2 }$$

In the strong-grating regime this reduces to the **stop-band width**:

$$\Delta\lambda_{\text{stop}} = \frac{\lambda_B \, \Delta n}{n_{\text{eff}}}$$

and the FWHM is approximately equal to the stop-band width. A grating with $\Delta n = 10^{-3}$ at $\lambda_B = 1550\;\text{nm}$ has $\Delta\lambda_{\text{FWHM}} \approx 1.07\;\text{nm}$.

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks**

**Operation of the FBG-OADM** (refer to Diagram D, Section 4):

1. The **input WDM signal** (e.g., $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \dots, \lambda_N$) enters port 1 of a 3-port optical circulator.
2. The circulator routes the signal to port 2, where the **FBG is connected**.
3. The FBG reflects one specific wavelength $\lambda_d$ back into port 2.
4. The circulator routes the reflected $\lambda_d$ to **port 3 — the drop port**.
5. All other wavelengths pass through the FBG and exit via port 2, continuing along the **express path** (back through the circulator to the next node, or directly to output).
6. A new channel at $\lambda_d$ can be **added** at the same port 3; the circulator sends it back to port 2, where the FBG reflects it into the express path.

**Role of apodization:** A uniform FBG has strong **sidelobes** (side-band reflection) due to the abrupt index change at the grating ends. These sidelobes cause **crosstalk** in WDM systems. **Apodization** (e.g., Gaussian or raised-cosine modulation of $\Delta n$ along $z$) tapers the index change smoothly to zero at the grating boundaries, suppressing sidelobes by 20–40 dB and ensuring that only the main Bragg peak reflects the desired channel.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks (compiled from KTU valuation keys):**
>
> 1. **Confusing effective index $n_{\text{eff}}$ with core index $n_1$** in the Bragg equation. Always use $n_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.45$ for SMF-28. *Penalty: −1 mark.*
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the phase-mask relation $\Lambda = \Lambda_{\text{mask}}/2$. *Penalty: −2 marks in 14-mark question.*
> 3. **Stating that LPG reflects light** — LPG *couples forward into cladding*; it does not produce backward reflection. *Penalty: −1 mark.*
> 4. **Not defining $\delta$ (detuning parameter)** before writing CMT equations. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this on derivations.
> 5. **Using $\tanh$ instead of $\tanh^2$** for reflectivity. Half the answer. Be precise.
> 6. **In FBG-OADM answers, omitting the circulator.** The circulator is essential to separate reflected and transmitted light. Without it, the reflected and transmitted signals travel back along the same fiber and cannot be separated. *Penalty: −2 marks.*
> 7. **Writing reflectivity $R$ instead of amplitude $A^-$** in the derivation. Always square the amplitude at the end.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and read it twice before entering the exam hall:**

- **Bragg condition:** $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,\Lambda$ — memorize first.
- **Peak reflectivity of uniform FBG:** $R_{\max} = \tanh^2(\kappa L)$.
- **Transmissivity at Bragg wavelength:** $T_{\min} = 1 - R_{\max} = \text{sech}^2(\kappa L)$.
- **Coupling coefficient:** $\kappa = \pi\,\Delta n\,\eta/\lambda$ — proportional to $\Delta n$ and inversely proportional to $\lambda$.
- **Phase-mask relation:** Grating period is **half** the mask period: $\Lambda = \Lambda_{\text{mask}}/2$.
- **Photosensitivity:** UV exposure of Ge-doped (or H$_2$-loaded) fiber creates permanent $\Delta n$.
- **Apodization:** Suppresses reflection sidelobes; uses $g(z) = $ Gaussian, raised-cosine, or tanh profile.
- **Chirped FBG:** Period $\Lambda(z)$ varies along $z$ — used for **dispersion compensation** (different wavelengths reflect at different positions, creating wavelength-dependent delay).
- **LPG vs FBG:** LPG = forward coupling to cladding, transmission loss, wide band; FBG = backward reflection, narrow band.
- **LPG resonance:** $\lambda_{\text{res}} = (n_{\text{core}} - n_{\text{clad},m})\,\Lambda$.
- **Strain sensitivity of FBG:** $\Delta\lambda_B / \lambda_B \approx 0.78\,\varepsilon$.
- **Temperature sensitivity of FBG:** $\Delta\lambda_B / \lambda_B \approx 9.15 \times 10^{-6}\,\Delta T$.
- **Strong grating limit:** $\kappa L \gg 1$ $\Rightarrow$ $R_{\max} \to 1$, bandwidth $\to \lambda_B \Delta n / n_{\text{eff}}$.
- **Weak grating limit:** $\kappa L \ll 1$ $\Rightarrow$ $R_{\max} \approx (\kappa L)^2$, narrow Lorentzian lineshape.
- **OADM function:** Circulator + FBG = 1-channel add-drop with low insertion loss and high isolation (> 30 dB).
- **Operating windows in C-band:** 1530–1565 nm is the densest WDM region; standard FBG gratings in this band use $\Lambda$ between **529 and 540 nm**.
- **Bandwidth units:** FBG bandwidths are typically expressed in **nm** or **GHz** (1 nm $\approx$ 125 GHz at 1550 nm).
- **Real FBG samples:** 1 cm long, $\Delta n \approx 10^{-4}$, $R_{\max} > 95\%$, FWHM $\approx 0.1$ – $0.3$ nm at 1550 nm.
- **Always quote numbers with units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit in numerical answers.
- **Three-line answer strategy** for 3-mark Part A: (i) state the formula, (ii) plug in numbers, (iii) interpret the result in one engineering sentence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunable Filters; Optical Networks: General Description of SONET/SDH; Free Space Optics: Principle of LiFi Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEECT721 - OPTICAL COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tunable Filters; Optical Networks: General Description of SONET/SDH; Free Space Optics: Principle of LiFi Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Tunable Filters

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Tunable Optical Filter** is a wavelength-selective photonic device whose passband (center wavelength $\lambda_c$) can be dynamically adjusted across a broad spectral range (typically the C-band: **1530–1565 nm** or the L-band: **1565–1625 nm**) using an external control stimulus (mechanical, thermal, electro-optic, or acousto-optic). They serve as the wavelength-selection engine inside optical add-drop multiplexers (OADMs), wavelength-routed cross-connects, optical spectrum analyzers, and tunable receivers in dense WDM (DWDM) networks.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a tunable filter as a "Radio Dial for Light."** Just like turning the knob on an old transistor radio lets you "lock onto" only one FM station while rejecting all others, a tunable optical filter lets you "lock onto" one specific wavelength (color) of light out of dozens traveling simultaneously down a single optical fiber. The "knob" can be a voltage, a temperature, a physical tilt, or a sound wave inside the device.

### Main Sub-Types of Tunable Filters

| Type | Tuning Mechanism | Tuning Range | Typical Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fabry-Perot (FP) Etalon | Piezoelectric cavity gap change | ~30–40 nm | 0.1–10 nm |
| Mach-Zehnder Interferometer (MZI) | Thermo-optic phase shift | ~10 nm | ~0.8 nm |
| Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) | Strain / Temperature | ~5–10 nm | 0.1–0.5 nm |
| Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF) | RF acoustic frequency | ~100 nm | 1–5 nm |
| Liquid Crystal (LC) | Birefringence via voltage | ~50–100 nm | 0.2–2 nm |

---

## 1.2 Optical Networks — General Description of SONET/SDH

### Formal Academic Definition
**SONET (Synchronous Optical Network)** is the ANSI/North American standard (T1.105) for high-speed optical transmission, while **SDH (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy)** is its ITU-T counterpart (G.707) used globally outside North America/Japan. Both define a **synchronous**, byte-interleaved, ring- or mesh-based transport framework that multiplexes lower-rate digital payloads into higher-rate optical frames (STS/STM) at standard bit rates of **51.84 Mbps (STS-1)** and multiples thereof, enabling interoperable, resilient, and manageable backbone networks.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a moving train platform with numbered seats.** In an *asynchronous* (PDH) system, you can only add a passenger by stopping the whole train. In SONET/SDH, the train moves at a fixed, perfectly timed speed, and every "seat" (time slot) has a fixed address — so adding/dropping traffic at any node is as easy as a passenger boarding at a specific seat number, without disturbing others. The **STS-1 frame is the "train"**, the **payload pointer** is the "seat map," and **overhead bytes** are the "conductor's signaling system."

### Key Frame Constants
- **STS-1 (SONET):** 9 rows × 90 columns = **810 bytes/frame** → **51.84 Mbps**
- **STM-1 (SDH):** 9 rows × 270 columns = **2430 bytes/frame** → **155.52 Mbps** (= 3 × STS-1)
- **Frame Period:** **125 µs** (regardless of bit rate)

---

## 1.3 Free Space Optics & LiFi Technology

### Formal Academic Definition
**Free Space Optics (FSO)** is a line-of-sight (LOS) optical communication technology that uses **infrared (IR, 780–1600 nm)** or **visible-light** laser/LED beams propagating through atmosphere (air, vacuum, or water) as the transmission medium. **LiFi (Light Fidelity, IEEE 802.11bb)** is a specific bidirectional, high-speed, networked subset of FSO/Visible Light Communication (VLC, IEEE 802.15.7) that uses rapid intensity modulation of **LEDs/LDs** in the **400–800 THz (visible 380–750 nm)** band, with photodiodes (PDs) as receivers, achieving data rates from a few Mbps to >10 Gbps.

### Conceptual Analogy
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LiFi = "WiFi, but with light."** Imagine your LED ceiling bulb blinking millions of times per second — too fast for your eye to notice, but a photodiode reads it as a stream of 1s and 0s. While WiFi uses invisible radio waves that pass through walls, **LiFi uses the light already illuminating your room**. Wherever there is light, there is internet. Conversely, FSO is the long-range "invisible laser flash-light" connecting two rooftops kilometers apart.

### Standard Spectrum Allocations

| Band | Wavelength | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Visible (VLC/LiFi) | 380–750 nm | Indoor LiFi, IEEE 802.11bb |
| Near-IR (NIR) FSO | 780–1600 nm | Outdoor FSO links, 1550 nm preferred (eye-safe) |
| Telecom C-band | 1530–1565 nm | Hybrid FSO + Fiber |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU OEECT721 Module 4, the focus is on (a) the **principle** of LiFi, (b) the **SONET/SDH frame structure**, and (c) the **role and types** of tunable filters — not exhaustive ITU protocol stacks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fabry-Perot Tunable Filter — Deep Theory

A Fabry-Perot Interferometer (FPI) is the most common tunable filter architecture. It consists of two parallel, high-reflectivity mirrors separated by a cavity length $L$ filled with medium of refractive index $n$.

### Operational Logic
1. **Resonance Condition:** Constructive interference occurs when the round-trip phase equals an integer multiple of $2\pi$.
2. **Wavelength Selection:** The transmitted wavelength satisfies $m\lambda = 2nL \cos\theta$. For normal incidence ($\theta = 0$), this simplifies.
3. **Tuning:** Varying $L$ (via piezoelectric transducer) or $n$ (via thermo-optic effect) shifts the passband.
4. **Multi-Passband Issue:** Free Spectral Range (FSR) limits the unambiguous tuning range — only one channel can be selected per FSR.

### Key Performance Parameters

**Free Spectral Range (FSR):** Spectral separation between adjacent transmission peaks.

$$FSR = \frac{\lambda^2}{2nL}$$

**Finesse ($F$):** Ratio of FSR to the Full-Width-at-Half-Maximum (FWHM) bandwidth $\Delta\lambda_{FWHM}$ of a single peak.

$$F = \frac{FSR}{\Delta\lambda_{FWHM}} = \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R}$$

where $R$ is the mirror reflectivity.

**Cavity FWHM Bandwidth:**

$$\Delta\lambda_{FWHM} = \frac{FSR}{F} = \frac{\lambda^2 (1 - R)}{2\pi n L \sqrt{R}}$$

**Tuning Sensitivity (Cavity-Length Method):** For a target wavelength shift $\Delta\lambda$:

$$\Delta L = \frac{L \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda}$$

### KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| FSR | $FSR = \lambda^2 / (2nL)$ | nm | Distance between transmission peaks |
| Finesse | $F = \pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$ | dimensionless | Spectral selectivity "sharpness" |
| FWHM Bandwidth | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda^2(1-R)/(2\pi n L \sqrt{R})$ | nm | Width of one passband |
| Max Tunable Channels | $N_{ch} \approx FSR / \Delta\lambda_{channel}$ | channels | Within one FSR |
| Tuning Range (PZT) | $\Delta L = L \cdot \Delta\lambda / \lambda$ | nm | Cavity length change needed |
| AOTF Acoustic Tuning | $\lambda_c = v_a \cdot \Lambda / \Delta n$ | nm | Bragg matching condition |
| FBG Bragg Wavelength | $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ | nm | Reflected wavelength |
| SONET Bit Rate | $R_{STS-N} = N \times 51.84$ Mbps | Mbps | N times STS-1 |
| SDH Bit Rate | $R_{STM-N} = N \times 155.52$ Mbps | Mbps | N times STM-1 |
| SONET Frame Size | $9 \times 90 \times N$ bytes | bytes | N times STS-1 |
| SDH Frame Size | $9 \times 270 \times N$ bytes | bytes | N times STM-1 |
| Frame Period | $T_f = 125$ µs | µs | Same for all SONET/SDH |
| LiFi Data Rate Bound | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps | Shannon capacity |
| LED Modulation Bandwidth | $B_{LED} \approx 1/(2\pi R_{eq} C_j)$ | Hz | Limited by RC time constant |
| FSO Link Loss | $L_{FSO} = (4 R / \theta \cdot d)^2$ | dB | Geometric loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A tunable FP filter with $R = 0.95$ has $F \approx 61.2$, enabling selection of one channel out of 60+ DWDM channels with crosstalk $<-30$ dB. In a 100-GHz-spaced DWDM grid (C-band 1530–1565 nm), the FSR must be $\geq 35$ nm to select any of the ~40 channels unambiguously.

---

## 2.2 SONET/SDH Multiplexing — Deep Theory

### Synchronous Multiplexing Logic
1. **Payload Mapping:** Lower-rate tributaries (e.g., DS1 = 1.544 Mbps, E1 = 2.048 Mbps, DS3 = 44.736 Mbps) are *byte-mapped* into a **Virtual Container (VC)** in SDH or **Virtual Tributary (VT)** in SONET.
2. **Plesiochronous Justification:** Slight bit-rate differences between tributaries are absorbed by **pointer adjustments** (H1/H2 bytes) — this is the heart of SONET/SDH's elegance.
3. **Byte Interleaving:** The synchronous payload envelope (SPE) is byte-interleaved with **Transport Overhead (TOH)** bytes.
4. **Frame Assembly:** The complete STS-N/STM-N frame is scrambled and converted from electrical to optical (NRZ or optical duo-binary).

### The Synchronous Payload Envelope (SPE)
The SPE is a 9 × 87 column structure that "floats" inside the STS-1 frame. The **H1/H2 pointer bytes** (row 4, columns 1–2) indicate the SPE's offset, allowing it to be phase-shifted without demultiplexing the whole signal — a feature called **pointer processing**.

### Multiplexing Hierarchy

**SONET Hierarchy (ANSI):**
- STS-1 = 51.84 Mbps → OC-1
- STS-3 = 155.52 Mbps → OC-3
- STS-12 = 622.08 Mbps → OC-12
- STS-48 = 2488.32 Mbps → OC-48
- STS-192 = 9953.28 Mbps → OC-192
- STS-768 = 39813.12 Mbps → OC-768

**SDH Hierarchy (ITU-T):**
- STM-1 = 155.52 Mbps (= STS-3)
- STM-4 = 622.08 Mbps
- STM-16 = 2488.32 Mbps
- STM-64 = 9953.28 Mbps
- STM-256 = 39813.12 Mbps

### Why Synchronous?
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In PDH (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy), demultiplexing a high-rate signal required demultiplexing *all* lower-rate signals (because bit stuffing is asynchronous). In SONET/SDH, the synchronous nature allows **direct add-drop multiplexing (ADM)** of any tributary at any node — saving hardware, time, and cost.

---

## 2.3 LiFi — Deep Theory

### Operational Layers
1. **Physical Layer (PHY):** LED intensity modulation at 1–1000 MHz using **OOK, DCO-OFDM, ACO-OFDM, or VPPM**.
2. **MAC Layer:** Modified CSMA/CA adapted from IEEE 802.15.7 / 802.11bb.
3. **Optical Wireless Channel:** LOS (line-of-sight) and NLOS (diffuse) propagation via Lambertian reflection.

### Shannon Capacity of an LiFi Channel

$$C_{LiFi} = B_{optical} \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{(R \cdot P_{rx})^2}{N_0 B_{optical}}\right)$$

where:
- $B_{optical}$ = LED modulation bandwidth (typ. 20–200 MHz for phosphor-converted white LEDs)
- $R$ = photodiode responsivity (A/W)
- $P_{rx}$ = received optical power
- $N_0$ = noise spectral density (shot + thermal)

### FSO Link Equation
For an FSO link of range $d$ with transmitter beam divergence $\theta$ and receiver aperture diameter $D$:

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} \cdot \eta_t \cdot \eta_r \cdot \left(\frac{D}{d \cdot \theta}\right)^2 \cdot L_{atm}$$

where $L_{atm} = 10^{-\alpha d/10}$ accounts for atmospheric attenuation (fog, rain, scintillation). **Scintillation** is the dominant impairment — modeled as a log-normal distribution for weak turbulence.

### LiFi vs WiFi — Engineering Trade-offs

| Parameter | LiFi | WiFi |
|---|---|---|
| Spectrum | 400–800 THz (visible) | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz |
| Security | Confined to room (line of sight) | Passes through walls |
| EMI Immunity | Excellent (no RF) | Susceptible |
| Mobility | Lower (shadowing issues) | High |
| Max Data Rate (2024) | 10+ Gbps (lab) / 1.5 Gbps (802.11bb) | ~10 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6E/7) |
| Bandwidth Regulation | License-free (visible) | License-free (ISM) |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fabry-Perot FSR and Tuning Range

**Problem:** A Fabry-Perot tunable filter has a cavity length $L = 50\ \mu m$, refractive index $n = 1.5$, and operates at center wavelength $\lambda = 1550\ nm$. The mirror reflectivity is $R = 0.9$. Calculate the FSR, Finesse, FWHM bandwidth, and the cavity length change required to tune by 1 nm.

### Step 1 — Free Spectral Range (FSR)

The round-trip path difference in a FP cavity is $2nL$. Two adjacent transmission peaks are separated by an optical path difference of exactly one wavelength. Therefore:

$$\Delta\lambda_{FSR} = \frac{\lambda^2}{2nL}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta\lambda_{FSR} = \frac{(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2}{2 \times 1.5 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$= \frac{2.4025 \times 10^{-12}}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= 1.6017 \times 10^{-8}\ m = 16.02\ nm$$

> **Interpretation:** The FSR is approximately **16.02 nm**, which is large enough to cover a few DWDM channels (100-GHz spacing $\approx 0.8$ nm at 1550 nm). This means ~20 channels can be unambiguously selected.

### Step 2 — Finesse ($F$)

The finesse depends only on mirror reflectivity $R$:

$$F = \frac{\pi \sqrt{R}}{1 - R} = \frac{\pi \times \sqrt{0.9}}{1 - 0.9} = \frac{3.1416 \times 0.9487}{0.1} = \frac{2.9805}{0.1} = 29.81$$

### Step 3 — FWHM Bandwidth ($\Delta\lambda_{FWHM}$)

$$\Delta\lambda_{FWHM} = \frac{\Delta\lambda_{FSR}}{F} = \frac{16.02\ nm}{29.81} = 0.5373\ nm$$

> **Interpretation:** Each passband is ~0.54 nm wide. In a 100-GHz-spaced DWDM grid (0.8 nm), this is acceptable but not ideal — high-finesse ($R \to 1$) filters are needed for narrower channels.

### Step 4 — Cavity Length Change for $\Delta\lambda = 1$ nm Tuning

From $\lambda = 2nL/m$, differentiating with respect to $L$:

$$\frac{d\lambda}{dL} = \frac{2n}{m} = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$

So, for a small tuning $\Delta\lambda$:

$$\Delta L = L \cdot \frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times \frac{1 \times 10^{-9}}{1550 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$= 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 6.452 \times 10^{-4} = 3.226 \times 10^{-8}\ m = 32.26\ nm$$

> **Interpretation:** A piezoelectric actuator with sub-nanometer resolution can easily achieve this — making FP tunable filters practical for dense WDM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: SONET/SDH Frame and Payload Capacity

**Problem:** Determine the number of DS1 (1.544 Mbps) tributaries that can be carried in one STS-1 frame. Given: STS-1 line rate = 51.84 Mbps, frame period = 125 µs.

### Step 1 — STS-1 Frame Size in Bytes

The STS-1 frame is $9 \times 90 = 810$ bytes. Since it is transmitted in 125 µs:

$$\text{Bytes/second} = \frac{810\ bytes}{125 \times 10^{-6}\ s} = 6.48 \times 10^6\ bytes/s = 51.84\ Mbps \ \checkmark$$

### Step 2 — Synchronous Payload Envelope (SPE) Size

The SPE is $9 \times 87 = 783$ bytes (the first 3 columns are Transport Overhead, TOH). In 125 µs:

$$\text{Payload rate} = \frac{783 \times 8\ bits}{125 \times 10^{-6}\ s} = \frac{6264}{1.25 \times 10^{-4}} = 50.112\ Mbps$$

### Step 3 — Overhead Subtraction for Mapping

Of the 783 SPE bytes, 9 bytes (1 column) are **Path Overhead (POH)**, leaving:

$$\text{Payload capacity} = \frac{774 \times 8}{125 \times 10^{-6}} = 49.536\ Mbps$$

### Step 4 — Number of DS1 Tributaries

The synchronous DS1 mapping uses 27 bytes per DS1 (24 payload + 3 overhead), wrapped into a VT1.5 group. The standard answer is:

$$N_{DS1} = \frac{49.536}{1.544} = 28\ DS1\ signals$$

> **Interpretation:** One STS-1 carries **28 DS1s** (or 21 E1s in international mapping) — this is the *VT-mapped* synchronous capacity. This is significantly more efficient than PDH, where DS3 (44.736 Mbps) carries only 28 DS1s asynchronously.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Topic Comparison

| Feature | Tunable FP Filter | SONET/SDH | LiFi / FSO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Medium** | Fiber (in-line device) | Optical fiber / Wireless | Air / Vacuum / Water |
| **Key Equation** | $\lambda = 2nL/m$ | $R = N \times 51.84$ Mbps | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ |
| **Tunable?** | Yes (PZT/thermo) | No — fixed frame rate | Partially (modulation format) |
| **Limitation** | FSR ↔ Finesse tradeoff | Pointer jitter | LOS blockage, scintillation |
| **Application** | OADM, OSA, Tunable Rx | Backbone transport | Indoor networking, last-mile |
| **Standard Body** | ITU-T G.671 | ANSI T1.105 / ITU-T G.707 | IEEE 802.11bb / 802.15.7 |

## 3.4 Worked Example: LiFi Coverage Area

**Problem:** An LED ceiling lamp emits $P_{tx} = 10$ W optical power uniformly over a hemisphere (Lambertian pattern with order $m = 1$). A user holds a photodiode receiver at distance $d = 2$ m directly below. The photodiode has area $A_{PD} = 1\ cm^2$ and responsivity $R = 0.5$ A/W. Find the received optical power and photocurrent.

### Step 1 — Lambertian Irradiance

For a generalized Lambertian source of order $m$:

$$E_{horiz} = \frac{(m+1) P_{tx}}{2\pi d^2} \cos^m(\phi)$$

For $m = 1$ and $\phi = 0$ (directly below):

$$E_{horiz} = \frac{2 \times 10}{2\pi \times (2)^2} \times 1^1 = \frac{20}{25.133} = 0.7958\ W/m^2$$

### Step 2 — Received Power

$$P_{rx} = E_{horiz} \times A_{PD} = 0.7958 \times 1 \times 10^{-4} = 7.958 \times 10^{-5}\ W = 79.58\ \mu W$$

### Step 3 — Photocurrent

$$I_{PD} = R \times P_{rx} = 0.5 \times 79.58 \times 10^{-6} = 39.79\ \mu A$$

> **Interpretation:** A 10-W LED lamp delivers ~40 µA photocurrent at 2 m — easily detectable with a low-noise transimpedance amplifier, supporting Mbps-class LiFi.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]
**Define a tunable optical filter. List any two types of tunable filters used in WDM systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A tunable optical filter is a wavelength-selective device whose passband center wavelength can be dynamically adjusted over a range of wavelengths (typically across the C-band 1530–1565 nm) using an external control input (voltage, temperature, mechanical, or acoustic). They are essential building blocks of OADMs, OSAs, and tunable receivers in DWDM networks.

**Two Types:**
1. **Fabry-Perot (FP) Tunable Filter:** Uses a piezo-electrically tuned air/dielectric cavity between two high-reflectivity mirrors. Tuning range ~30 nm, FWHM ~0.1–1 nm.
2. **Acousto-Optic Tunable Filter (AOTF):** Uses RF acoustic waves in a TeO$_2$ crystal to diffract selected wavelength via Bragg diffraction. Tuning range ~100 nm, very fast ($\mu$s switching).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]
**Differentiate between SONET and SDH in terms of frame size and basic bit rate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | SONET (STS-1) | SDH (STM-1) |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Bit Rate | 51.84 Mbps | 155.52 Mbps |
| Frame Size | 9 rows × 90 columns = 810 bytes | 9 rows × 270 columns = 2430 bytes |
| Payload Unit | VT (Virtual Tributary) / SPE | VC (Virtual Container) |
| Standard Body | ANSI T1.105 | ITU-T G.707 |
| Region | North America, Japan | Europe, rest of world |
| Multiplexing Ratio | STS-1 is the base | STM-1 = 3 × STS-1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** STM-1 is exactly **3 × STS-1**, so STM-4 = STS-12, STM-16 = STS-48, etc.
